## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Example — DFS on a Sample VLSI Netlist Graph

Consider a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with:
$$V = \{s, a, b, c, d, e\}$$
$$E = \{(s,a), (s,b), (a,c), (a,d), (b,d), (c,e), (d,e), (e,s)\}$$

This represents a small gate-level netlist with a feedback edge $(e, s)$ — a suspected combinational loop. We will execute DFS starting from $s$ using **alphabetical tie-breaking** for neighbor ordering (a standard KTU assumption).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Used:** Time counter $T$ increments *before* recording $d[v]$ and *after* recording $f[v]$. Neighbors are processed in alphabetical order. The graph is directed.

#### Step 1: Initialization

All vertices colored WHITE. Global time $T = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[v] &= \text{WHITE} \quad \forall v \in V \\
T &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Visit Source Vertex $s$

We begin DFS at $s$. Its color transitions to GRAY, and we record its discovery time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[s] &\leftarrow \text{GRAY} \\
d[s] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 1 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

State snapshot after Step 2:

| Vertex | Color | $d[v]$ | $f[v]$ | Parent |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $s$ | GRAY | 1 | — | NIL |
| $a$ | WHITE | — | — | — |
| $b$ | WHITE | — | — | — |
| $c$ | WHITE | — | — | — |
| $d$ | WHITE | — | — | — |
| $e$ | WHITE | — | — | — |

#### Step 3: Recursive Descent from $s$ to $a$

Neighbors of $s$ in alphabetical order: $\{a, b\}$. The first WHITE neighbor is $a$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[a] &\leftarrow \text{GRAY} \\
d[a] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 2 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 2 \\
p[a] &\leftarrow s
\end{aligned}
$$

Edge $(s, a)$ is classified as a **Tree Edge**.

#### Step 4: Recursive Descent from $a$ to $c$

Neighbors of $a$: $\{c, d\}$. First WHITE neighbor is $c$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[c] &\leftarrow \text{GRAY} \\
d[c] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 3 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 3 \\
p[c] &\leftarrow a
\end{aligned}
$$

Edge $(a, c)$ is a **Tree Edge**.

#### Step 5: Recursive Descent from $c$ to $e$

Neighbors of $c$: $\{e\}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[e] &\leftarrow \text{GRAY} \\
d[e] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 4 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 4 \\
p[e] &\leftarrow c
\end{aligned}
$$

Edge $(c, e)$ is a **Tree Edge**.

#### Step 6: Critical Step — Back Edge Detection at $e$

Neighbors of $e$: $\{s\}$. Inspect $s$:

$$
\text{color}[s] = \text{GRAY}
$$

Since $s$ is GRAY (discovered but not finished), the edge $(e, s)$ is a **Back Edge**. This confirms the presence of a **directed cycle**: $s \rightarrow a \rightarrow c \rightarrow e \rightarrow s$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly mark a cycle when they encounter a BLACK vertex with $d[v] < d[u]$. This is wrong. A back edge — and hence a directed cycle — is signaled *exclusively* by a GRAY target vertex. BLACK targets only indicate forward or cross edges.

#### Step 7: Finish $e$

No more unvisited neighbors of $e$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[e] &\leftarrow \text{BLACK} \\
f[e] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 5 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 8: Backtrack and Continue — Visit $d$ from $a$

Backtrack to $a$. Remaining neighbor $d$ is still WHITE.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[d] &\leftarrow \text{GRAY} \\
d[d] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 6 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 6 \\
p[d] &\leftarrow a
\end{aligned}
$$

Edge $(a, d)$ is a **Tree Edge**. Neighbor of $d$ is $e$, which is BLACK, so $(d, e)$ is a **Cross Edge** (since $d[e] = 4 < d[d] = 6$ and $e$ is not an ancestor of $d$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[d] &\leftarrow \text{BLACK} \\
f[d] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 7 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 9: Finish $a$ and Continue to $b$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[a] &\leftarrow \text{BLACK} \\
f[a] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 8 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

Backtrack to $s$. Visit neighbor $b$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[b] &\leftarrow \text{GRAY} \\
d[b] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 9 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 9 \\
p[b] &\leftarrow s
\end{aligned}
$$

Neighbor of $b$ is $d$, which is BLACK. Edge $(b, d)$ is a **Cross Edge**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[b] &\leftarrow \text{BLACK} \\
f[b] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 10 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 10: Finish $s$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{color}[s] &\leftarrow \text{BLACK} \\
f[s] &\leftarrow T + 1 = 11 \\
T &\leftarrow T + 1 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Analytical Matrix

| Vertex | Color (Final) | $d[v]$ | $f[v]$ | Parent | Tree Path |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $s$ | BLACK | **1** | **11** | NIL | ROOT |
| $a$ | BLACK | **2** | **8** | $s$ | $s \rightarrow a$ |
| $b$ | BLACK | **9** | **10** | $s$ | $s \rightarrow b$ |
| $c$ | BLACK | **3** | **5** | $a$ | $s \rightarrow a \rightarrow c$ |
| $d$ | BLACK | **6** | **7** | $a$ | $s \rightarrow a \rightarrow d$ |
| $e$ | BLACK | **4** | **5** | $c$ | $s \rightarrow a \rightarrow c \rightarrow e$ |

### Verification of the Time Invariant

For every vertex $v$:

$$
d[v] < f[v]
$$

The check yields: $1<11$, $2<8$, $9<10$, $3<5$, $6<7$, $4<5$. **All invariants hold.** DFS has terminated correctly.

### Derived Cycle Confirmation

The back edge $(e, s)$ implies the cycle:

$$
C = s \rightarrow a \rightarrow c \rightarrow e \rightarrow s
$$

This is the smallest directed cycle in $G$ and corresponds to a **combinational feedback loop** in the VLSI netlist — a critical design error that must be flagged to the place-and-route engine.

### Topological Sort Validity Check

For a true DAG, topological order requires $f[u] > f[v]$ for every edge $(u, v) \in E$. Here, edge $(e, s)$ has $f[s] = 11 > f[e] = 5$, which *fails* the topological ordering — further confirmation that $G$ is **not a DAG**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: DFS Recursive State Machine (Mermaid Flowchart)

This diagram captures the canonical state transition logic of the DFS algorithm, directly mapping the WHITE/GRAY/BLACK lifecycle to a flow the EDA tool can execute.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Initialize all vertices WHITE, T = 0] --> B{Pick next WHITE vertex s}
    B -- Found --> C[VISIT s: color GRAY, d s = T plus 1, T increment]
    B -- None --> Z[TERMINATE: DFS Forest Complete]
    C --> D{For each neighbor v of s in order}
    D -- color v is WHITE --> E[Tree Edge: set parent v = s, recurse DFS v]
    D -- color v is GRAY --> F[BACK EDGE DETECTED: Record cycle]
    D -- color v is BLACK --> G[Forward or Cross Edge: Continue]
    E --> D
    F --> D
    G --> H{All neighbors processed}
    H -- No --> D
    H -- Yes --> I[FINISH s: color BLACK, f s = T plus 1, T increment]
    I --> J{Return to caller or backtrack}
    J --> B
```

### Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of DFS in a VLSI CAD Tool

This diagram illustrates how DFS is embedded within the broader VLSI design verification framework.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Design Input Layer]
        A1[Gate Level Netlist V and E]
        A2[HDL Source VHDL or Verilog]
        A3[Library Cells .lib files]
    end

    subgraph PARSER[Netlist Parser]
        B1[Elaboration Engine]
        B2[Graph Builder: cells become vertices, wires become edges]
    end

    subgraph DFSENGINE[DFS Search Engine]
        C1[Vertex State Array WHITE GRAY BLACK]
        C2[Time Counter T]
        C3[Recursion or Explicit Stack]
        C4[Edge Classifier: Tree, Back, Forward, Cross]
    end

    subgraph ANALYSIS[Analysis Modules]
        D1[Cycle Detector: reports back edges]
        D2[Reachability Analyzer: input to output paths]
        D3[Topological Sorter: netlist schedule]
        D4[Critical Path Finder: longest path]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Reporting]
        E1[Combinational Loop Report]
        E2[Unreachable Cell Report]
        E3[Synthesis Constraint File]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C4 --> D3
    C4 --> D4
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E3
```

### Diagram 3: DFS Traversal Order on the Sample Graph

This diagram visualizes the actual DFS forest produced by the worked example.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s[Vertex s, d=1, f=11, ROOT] --> a[Vertex a, d=2, f=8]
    s --> b[Vertex b, d=9, f=10]
    a --> c[Vertex c, d=3, f=5]
    a --> d[Vertex d, d=6, f=7]
    c --> e[Vertex e, d=4, f=5]
    e -. BACK EDGE CYCLE .-> s
    b -. Cross Edge .-> d
    d -. Cross Edge .-> e
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Solid arrows** represent Tree Edges (the DFS forest itself). **Dotted arrows** represent Non-Tree Edges (Back Edges and Cross Edges). The single dotted arrow from $e$ back to $s$ is the cycle detected in the netlist.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Define Depth First Search. Explain the three-color scheme used in DFS with the significance of each color.**

**Model Answer:**

Depth First Search is a recursive graph traversal algorithm that explores a graph by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. It uses a LIFO (Last In First Out) stack structure, either through recursion or an explicit stack, to manage the search frontier.

The three-color scheme used in DFS is:
- **WHITE:** Represents a vertex that has not yet been discovered by the search. All vertices are initially WHITE.
- **GRAY:** Represents a vertex that has been discovered (placed on the search stack) but whose descendants have not all been explored. It is an "active" vertex.
- **BLACK:** Represents a vertex whose entire subtree has been fully explored. The vertex is "finished" and removed from the active search stack.

**Significance:** The color scheme enables O(1) classification of edges during traversal. Detection of a GRAY target vertex during edge inspection immediately reveals a back edge, which in turn signals a directed cycle in the graph — a critical operation in VLSI combinational loop detection.

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]`
**What is a back edge in DFS? How can the existence of a back edge in a directed graph be used to detect a cycle?**

**Model Answer:**

A **back edge** $(u, v)$ in DFS is an edge where $v$ is an ancestor of $u$ in the DFS tree. In terms of the three-color scheme, a back edge is identified when, during the exploration of vertex $u$, an adjacent vertex $v$ is found with $\text{color}[v] = \text{GRAY}$ — meaning $v$ is currently on the recursion stack.

**Cycle Detection Principle:** If a back edge $(u, v)$ exists, then there is already a tree path from $v$ to $u$ (through the DFS tree ancestors), and the back edge $(u, v)$ closes this path into a cycle:

$$
v \rightsquigarrow u \quad \text{(tree path)} \quad \cup \quad (u, v) \quad \text{(back edge)} \quad = \quad \text{directed cycle}
$$

**Theorem:** A directed graph $G$ contains a directed cycle *if and only if* its DFS forest contains at least one back edge. This equivalence is the foundation of all cycle-detection algorithms in VLSI netlist verification.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students must answer exactly ONE full question (both sub-parts) from the choice provided.

---

#### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Apply Depth First Search to the following directed graph. Start DFS from vertex $A$ and process neighbors in alphabetical order. For each vertex, record the discovery time $d[v]$ and finish time $f[v]$. Draw the resulting DFS forest and classify every edge as Tree, Back, Forward, or Cross. **(7 Marks)**

Vertices: $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$
Edges: $E = \{(A, B), (A, C), (B, D), (B, E), (C, E), (D, F), (E, F), (F, C)\}$

**(b)** Based on the DFS trace from part (a), determine whether the graph contains a directed cycle. If yes, list the cycle and explain how the back edge confirms it. If no, produce a topological ordering of the vertices. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Following the procedure from the worked example in Section 3, we execute DFS from $A$ with alphabetical tie-breaking. The execution yields:

| Vertex | $d[v]$ | $f[v]$ | Parent | Status |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $A$ | 1 | 12 | NIL | ROOT |
| $B$ | 2 | 9 | $A$ | Tree Child |
| $D$ | 3 | 6 | $B$ | Tree Child |
| $F$ | 4 | 5 | $D$ | Tree Child |
| $E$ | 7 | 8 | $B$ | Tree Child |
| $C$ | 10 | 11 | $A$ | Tree Child |

**Edge Classification:**
- $(A, B)$, $(A, C)$, $(B, D)$, $(B, E)$, $(D, F)$ — **Tree Edges** (form the DFS forest).
- $(F, C)$ — **Cross Edge** (from $F$ to $C$ where $d[F] = 4 < d[C] = 10$ and $C$ is not an ancestor of $F$).
- $(C, E)$ — **Back Edge** (when exploring $C$, neighbor $E$ is GRAY, ancestor of $C$).
- $(E, F)$ — **Cross Edge** (when exploring $E$, neighbor $F$ is BLACK with $d[F] = 4 < d[E] = 7$, and $F$ is not an ancestor of $E$).

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

The edge $(C, E)$ is a **Back Edge** because at the moment DFS processes $C$ (colored GRAY), vertex $E$ is also GRAY and is an ancestor of $C$ in the DFS tree.

By the DFS cycle equivalence theorem, this back edge guarantees the existence of a directed cycle. The cycle is constructed as:

$$
E \rightarrow F \rightarrow D \rightarrow B \rightarrow E \quad \text{(via back edge } (C, E) \text{, using tree path } E \leftarrow B \leftarrow \cdots)
$$

More explicitly, tracing the cycle using the back edge $(C, E)$:

$$
C \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow D \rightarrow B \rightarrow \cdots \text{ (tree path back up to } A \text{) } \cdots
$$

The actual minimal cycle is:

$$
C \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow D \rightarrow B \rightarrow A \rightarrow C
$$

Wait — let us re-examine. The back edge is $(C, E)$, meaning $C$ points to $E$. The tree path from $E$ up to the root passing through $C$'s ancestor gives:

$$
E \xrightarrow{\text{tree}} B \xrightarrow{\text{tree}} A \xrightarrow{\text{tree}} \cdots
$$

Combining with the back edge $(C, E)$, and using the fact that $C$ is reached from $A$ via tree edge $(A, C)$, the cycle is:

$$
C \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow D \rightarrow B \rightarrow A \rightarrow C
$$

This is a **6-vertex directed cycle** in the VLSI netlist — flagged as a critical combinational feedback loop.

Since the graph contains a cycle, **no valid topological ordering exists**.

---

#### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**(a)** Define the DFS spanning forest. Explain with proper justification why the time complexity of DFS on a graph with $\vert V \vert$ vertices and $\vert E \vert$ edges (represented using adjacency lists) is $\Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a gate-level netlist of an 8-bit ripple-carry adder with $\vert V \vert = 64$ gates and $\vert E \vert = 98$ wires. Estimate the DFS execution time if the EDA tool processes 10 million operations per second. Next, explain how DFS can be applied to verify that the carry output of the most significant bit is reachable from the primary carry input. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

A **DFS spanning forest** is a collection of rooted trees $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k$ (where $k$ is the number of connected components in the undirected case, or the number of DFS source vertices in the directed case) such that:
- The vertex set of all $T_i$ together equals $V$.
- Every edge of the forest is either a tree edge discovered during DFS, or a non-tree edge (back, forward, or cross).
- The forest is *spanning* because it covers all reachable vertices from each DFS source.

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

The DFS procedure performs two main kinds of operations on each vertex and each edge:

**1. Vertex Processing Cost:** Each vertex $v \in V$ is discovered exactly once and finished exactly once. Each of these events costs $O(1)$:
- White $\rightarrow$ Gray transition,
- Record $d[v]$,
- Gray $\rightarrow$ Black transition,
- Record $f[v]$.

Total cost over all vertices:
$$C_V = \sum_{v \in V} O(1) = O(\vert V \vert)$$

**2. Edge Processing Cost:** For each edge $(u, v) \in E$, DFS inspects the edge exactly once (in the adjacency list of either $u$ or $v$, depending on the implementation). The inspection operation is $O(1)$ — it checks the color of the neighbor and classifies the edge.

Total cost over all edges:
$$C_E = \sum_{e \in E} O(1) = O(\vert E \vert)$$

**3. Total Time:**
$$T(V, E) = C_V + C_E = O(\vert V \vert) + O(\vert E \vert) = \Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$$

This bound is *tight* ($\Theta$ rather than $O$) because in the worst case — for example, a connected graph — every vertex and every edge must indeed be visited.

> [!NOTE]
> **Adjacency List Justification:** The total length of all adjacency lists in a graph is exactly $2 \vert E \vert$ (for undirected) or $\vert E \vert$ (for directed). Iterating through adjacency lists of all vertices therefore costs $\Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$, not $\Theta(\vert V \vert^2)$ as in the matrix representation.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Execution Time Estimate:**

Given:
- $\vert V \vert = 64$ gates,
- $\vert E \vert = 98$ wires,
- EDA tool throughput: $10^7$ operations per second.

Total DFS operations:
$$N_{\text{ops}} = c \cdot (\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$$

where $c$ is the per-vertex/edge operation count (typically $c \approx 5$ to $10$ for a well-optimized EDA implementation). Using $c = 10$ as a conservative upper bound:

$$N_{\text{ops}} = 10 \times (64 + 98) = 10 \times 162 = 1620 \text{ operations}$$

Execution time:
$$t = \frac{N_{\text{ops}}}{10^7} = \frac{1620}{10^7} = 1.62 \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds} = 162 \, \mu s$$

This sub-millisecond execution time demonstrates why DFS is the *industry default* for netlist traversal — even for designs with millions of gates, DFS completes in seconds.

**Reachability Verification via DFS:**

To verify that the carry output $C_{out}[7]$ of the most significant bit is reachable from the primary carry input $C_{in}$:

1. **Build the netlist graph** $G = (V, E)$ with logic gates as vertices and signal wires as directed edges.

2. **Execute DFS from $C_{in}$:** Begin the search with $C_{in}$ as the source vertex. DFS will traverse forward through every gate and wire reachable from $C_{in}$.

3. **Check vertex status of $C_{out}[7]$:** After DFS terminates, inspect the state of vertex $C_{out}[7]$:
   - If $\text{color}[C_{out}[7]] = \text{BLACK}$ (or equivalently, $d[C_{out}[7]] \neq \text{undefined}$), then $C_{in}$ can reach $C_{out}[7]$ through the netlist — **reachability confirmed**.
   - If $\text{color}[C_{out}[7]] = \text{WHITE}$, then $C_{out}[7]$ is unreachable from $C_{in}$ — there is a **design connectivity bug** (broken wire, missing buffer, or incorrectly connected pin).

4. **Diagnostic Output:** The EDA tool generates a reachability report listing all unreachable cells, allowing the designer to inspect the broken net.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — The Reachability Trap:** Many students confuse *reachability* with *functional correctness*. DFS reachability only confirms that a **path exists** in the netlist — it does **not** prove that the logic produces the correct value. A 8-bit ripple-carry adder may be *reachable* yet still compute the wrong sum if gates are mis-typed (e.g., AND instead of XOR). DFS complements, but does not replace, formal equivalence checking.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in DFS Questions (Module 1):**
> 1. **Wrong cycle indicator:** Marking a cycle when a BLACK target vertex is found (should only mark on GRAY target for *directed* graphs).
> 2. **Time counter convention:** Some textbooks increment $T$ *after* recording, others *before*. Always state your convention explicitly at the start of the solution.
> 3. **Edge classification confusion:** Distinguishing Forward vs Cross Edges requires checking ancestry, not just color. Forward = descendant in tree; Cross = non-ancestor, non-descendant.
> 4. **Forgotten DFS forest:** In disconnected graphs, the DFS forest contains multiple trees. A single starting vertex will not visit all vertices.
> 5. **Parenthesis Theorem violation:** If your $d$ and $f$ intervals do not nest or remain disjoint as in the theorem, you have a computation error — recompute carefully.
> 6. **Adjacency list vs matrix confusion:** Reporting $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ for an adjacency-list DFS loses 2 marks immediately.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DFS = depth before breadth**, uses LIFO (stack/recursion), color-scheme = WHITE/GRAY/BLACK.
- **Time complexity** (adjacency list): $\Theta(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. **Space**: $\Theta(\vert V \vert)$.
- **Back edge = cycle signal** in directed graphs (detected via GRAY target vertex only).
- **Parenthesis Theorem:** $d/f$ intervals are either disjoint or strictly nested — never partially overlapping.
- **DFS Forest** spans all reachable vertices from every DFS source; covers entire graph.
- **Edge Classification:** Tree (parent-child in DFS), Back (GRAY ancestor), Forward (BLACK descendant), Cross (BLACK non-ancestor, non-descendant).
- **Topological Sort** valid *iff* $f[u] > f[v]$ for *all* edges $(u, v)$ — *iff* graph is a DAG.
- **VLSI Applications:** combinational loop detection, reachability analysis, netlist traversal, timing graph exploration, layout connectivity verification.
- **Recursive DFS** uses call stack; **Iterative DFS** uses explicit stack — same logical behavior, different implementation strategy.
- **Time Invariant:** $1 \leq d[v] < f[v] \leq 2\vert V \vert$ for every vertex $v$.
- **Alphabetical tie-breaking** is the standard KTU assumption unless otherwise specified.
- **Reachability $\neq$ Correctness:** DFS confirms path existence, not logical function.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Topological Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Topological Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: TOPOLOGICAL SORT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topological Sort (Topological Ordering)** is a linear ordering of the vertices of a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** such that for every directed edge $(u \rightarrow v)$, the vertex $u$ appears *before* $v$ in the ordering. In the context of **VLSI CAD**, topological sort provides a valid **evaluation/scheduling sequence** for a circuit netlist, a logic network, or a design-flow task graph where data/control dependencies must be respected.

A Directed Acyclic Graph $G = (V, E)$ admits a topological ordering **if and only if** it contains no directed cycles. A DAG may have **multiple valid topological orderings**; the number of orderings is given by the determinant of a certain poset-related matrix (a non-trivial combinatorial quantity).

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are a **VLSI design engineer** scheduling a day's tasks:

1. You **cannot synthesize** a circuit before **HDL is written**.
2. You **cannot place-and-route** before **synthesis** is complete.
3. You **cannot perform Static Timing Analysis (STA)** before **placement** is done.

Each task has **prerequisite tasks**. A topological sort is simply the act of writing these tasks on a whiteboard in a sequence such that every prerequisite appears before the task that depends on it. **If there is a cyclic dependency** (e.g., "Task A needs B, B needs C, C needs A") — you are stuck forever! That's why the graph must be acyclic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for VLSI**: A combinational logic circuit (without feedback loops) is a DAG. Topological sorting lets the CAD tool determine **the order in which to evaluate signals** so that when a gate is evaluated, all its input signals are already known.

### 📐 Standard Metrics & Notation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $G = (V, E)$ | Directed Acyclic Graph with vertex set $V$ and edge set $E$ |
| $n = \vert V \vert$ | Number of vertices (gates/nodes/tasks) |
| $m = \vert E \vert$ | Number of edges (wires/dependencies) |
| $indeg(v)$ | **In-degree** of $v$ = number of incoming edges |
| $outdeg(v)$ | **Out-degree** of $v$ = number of outgoing edges |
| $\pi(v)$ | Position of $v$ in the topological order |
| $L$ | Resulting list (topological order) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Complexity Standard**: The optimal complexity of topological sort is $\Theta(n + m)$ — **linear in the size of the graph**. This is critical in VLSI CAD where netlists routinely contain **millions of gates**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔍 Conditions for Existence of Topological Sort

A topological ordering of a graph $G$ exists **if and only if** $G$ is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**.

> **Necessity:** If a topological order exists, suppose a cycle $v_1 \rightarrow v_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow v_k \rightarrow v_1$ exists. The ordering requires $v_1$ before $v_2$, $v_2$ before $v_3$, ..., $v_k$ before $v_1$ — a **contradiction**.

> **Sufficiency:** If $G$ is a DAG, then it has at least one vertex with in-degree **0** (a "source"). Removing it recursively yields a topological order.

## 🧮 Two Canonical Algorithms

### Algorithm A: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS / Source Removal Method)

**Core Idea:** Repeatedly pick a vertex with in-degree **0**, append it to the output, and "remove" it (decrease in-degrees of its successors).

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. Compute $indeg(v)$ for every vertex $v \in V$.
2. Initialize a queue $Q$ with all vertices having $indeg(v) = 0$.
3. While $Q$ is **not empty**:
    - Dequeue vertex $u$; append $u$ to output list $L$.
    - For each edge $(u \rightarrow v) \in E$: decrement $indeg(v)$. If $indeg(v)$ becomes $0$, enqueue $v$.
4. If $\vert L \vert < n$, the graph contains a cycle → **No valid ordering**.

### Algorithm B: DFS-Based Topological Sort

**Core Idea:** Run Depth-First Search. After all descendants of a vertex $v$ are finished (post-order), prepend $v$ to the output — or, equivalently, append in reverse post-order.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. For each unvisited vertex $u$, invoke `DFS(u)`.
2. In `DFS(u)`: mark $u$ visited; recursively visit all unvisited successors $v$ in edge $(u \rightarrow v)$.
3. After exploring all successors, **push $u$ onto a stack** (or prepend to output list).
4. Reverse the stack/list to obtain the topological order.
5. If a back-edge is detected during DFS, the graph contains a cycle.

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Formula / Expression | Application in VLSI CAD |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Total time complexity | $T(n, m) = \Theta(n + m)$ | Determines CAD tool scalability |
| Space complexity | $S(n, m) = \Theta(n + m)$ | Memory footprint for netlist |
| In-degree of $v$ | $indeg(v) = \sum_{u \in V} \mathbb{1}[(u \rightarrow v) \in E]$ | Fan-in cone size of a gate |
| Out-degree of $v$ | $outdeg(v) = \sum_{w \in V} \mathbb{1}[(v \rightarrow w) \in E]$ | Fan-out load of a gate |
| Number of edges | $m = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{v \in V} \left(indeg(v) + outdeg(v)\right)$ | Netlist edge count |
| Number of orderings (DAG) | $N_{order} \geq 1$ (multiple, combinatorial) | Scheduling flexibility |
| Longest path length (critical path) | $L_{cp} = \max_{v \in V} \pi_{depth}(v)$ | Determines clock period constraint |
| Depth of node $v$ | $depth(v) = 1 + \max_{(u \rightarrow v)} depth(u)$ | Used in PERT/CPM scheduling |

> [!NOTE]
> In VLSI CAD, the **critical path** through a combinational logic DAG dictates the **minimum clock period** of the design. Topological sort enables $O(n+m)$ critical path analysis.

## 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logic Simulation**: Event-driven simulators order gate evaluations via topological sort to propagate signal values correctly.
- **Static Timing Analysis (STA)**: Traversal order is determined topologically to propagate arrival times level-by-level.
- **High-Level Synthesis (HLS)**: Operation scheduling under resource/dependency constraints reduces to DAG-based topological reasoning.
- **Design-Flow Task Scheduling**: Makefiles/scripts for RTL → Synthesis → PnR → STA → Sign-off tasks form a DAG.
- **Technology Mapping**: Subject graphs and pattern DAGs are topologically matched for area/delay optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔬 Derivation 1: Time Complexity of Kahn's Algorithm

**Claim:** Kahn's algorithm runs in $\Theta(n + m)$ time.

**Proof by Step Counting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n, m) &= T_{\text{indeg compute}} + T_{\text{init queue}} + T_{\text{main loop}} + T_{\text{edge relaxation}} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{indeg compute}} &= \sum_{v \in V} \sum_{w:(w \rightarrow v)} \mathbb{1}[\text{edge processed}] = \Theta(m) \\[4pt]
T_{\text{init queue}} &= \sum_{v \in V} \mathbb{1}[indeg(v) = 0] \leq \Theta(n) \\[4pt]
T_{\text{main loop}} &= \sum_{u \in V} \mathbb{1}[u \text{ dequeued}] = \Theta(n) \\[4pt]
T_{\text{edge relaxation}} &= \sum_{(u \rightarrow v) \in E} \mathbb{1}[\text{indeg}(v) \text{ decremented}] = \Theta(m) \\[4pt]
\therefore T(n, m) &= \Theta(m) + \Theta(n) + \Theta(n) + \Theta(m) = \Theta(n + m)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every vertex is dequeued exactly once; every directed edge is examined exactly once during in-degree update. This is the **theoretical optimum** for a graph algorithm (any algorithm must at least read all edges and visit all vertices).

## 🔬 Derivation 2: Longest Path (Critical Path) on a DAG

**Problem:** Given a weighted DAG $G$ with edge weights $w(u \rightarrow v)$ representing, e.g., gate delay, find the longest path from a source to any vertex $v$. This is the **critical path** in timing analysis.

**Recurrence:**

$$
dist(v) = 
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } indeg(v) = 0 \\
\max\limits_{(u \rightarrow v) \in E} \left( dist(u) + w(u \rightarrow v) \right) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step-by-Step Computation (Topologically Ordered Traversal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } \sigma &= (v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n) \text{ be a topological order} \\[4pt]
\text{For } k = 1 \text{ to } n: \\[4pt]
\quad dist(v_k) &= 
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } k = 1 \\
\max\limits_{j < k, (v_j \rightarrow v_k) \in E} \left( dist(v_j) + w(v_j \rightarrow v_k) \right) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases} \\[4pt]
\text{Critical path length } L_{cp} &= \max_{v \in V} dist(v) \\[4pt]
\text{Time complexity} &= O(n + m) \text{ (one pass after topological sort)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This recurrence is the **backbone of Static Timing Analysis (STA)**. Tools like Synopsys PrimeTime and Cadence Tempus implement exactly this algorithm at massive scale.

## 🔬 Derivation 3: Cycle Detection via Kahn's Algorithm

**Claim:** A graph $G$ has a topological ordering **if and only if** Kahn's algorithm outputs a list of size $n$.

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forward direction (}\Rightarrow\text{):} & \quad \text{If } G \text{ is a DAG, then a source vertex with } indeg = 0 \text{ always exists.} \\
& \quad \text{By induction on } n, \text{ every source can be removed, yielding a full order.} \\[4pt]
\text{Backward direction (}\Leftarrow\text{):} & \quad \text{Suppose Kahn outputs } \vert L \vert = n \text{ (full order).} \\
& \quad \text{Suppose for contradiction } \exists \text{ cycle } C = (v_1 \rightarrow v_2 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow v_k \rightarrow v_1). \\
& \quad \text{Every } v_i \in C \text{ has } indeg(v_i) \geq 1 \text{ (within cycle).} \\
& \quad \text{None can ever be enqueued} \Rightarrow \text{None is added to } L. \\
& \quad \text{Contradiction with } \vert L \vert = n. \\[4pt]
\therefore G \text{ is a DAG} &\iff \vert L \vert = n
\end{aligned}
$$

## 🔬 Worked Example: Kahn's Algorithm on a VLSI Netlist

**Given DAG** with $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ representing a small logic netlist.

**Edges:** $A \rightarrow C$, $A \rightarrow D$, $B \rightarrow C$, $C \rightarrow E$, $D \rightarrow E$, $D \rightarrow F$, $E \rightarrow F$

**Step 1: Compute in-degrees**

$$
\begin{aligned}
indeg(A) &= 0 \quad (source)\\
indeg(B) &= 0 \quad (source)\\
indeg(C) &= 2 \quad (from A, B)\\
indeg(D) &= 1 \quad (from A)\\
indeg(E) &= 2 \quad (from C, D)\\
indeg(F) &= 2 \quad (from D, E)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Initial Queue** $Q = [A, B]$ (insertion order, say)

**Step 3: Iterative Dequeue**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dequeue } A &: \ L = [A];\ indeg(C) = 1,\ indeg(D) = 0 \Rightarrow Q = [B, D] \\
\text{Dequeue } B &: \ L = [A, B];\ indeg(C) = 0 \Rightarrow Q = [D, C] \\
\text{Dequeue } D &: \ L = [A, B, D];\ indeg(E) = 1,\ indeg(F) = 1 \Rightarrow Q = [C] \\
\text{Dequeue } C &: \ L = [A, B, D, C];\ indeg(E) = 0 \Rightarrow Q = [E] \\
\text{Dequeue } E &: \ L = [A, B, D, C, E];\ indeg(F) = 0 \Rightarrow Q = [F] \\
\text{Dequeue } F &: \ L = [A, B, D, C, E, F];\ Q = []
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Topological Order:** $\sigma = (A, B, D, C, E, F)$

Since $\vert L \vert = 6 = n$, the graph is **acyclic**. ✅

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🗺️ Diagram 1: Sample DAG for Topological Sort (VLSI Netlist View)

The following Mermaid diagram represents a small combinational logic netlist as a DAG. Each node is a gate; each directed edge is a wire carrying a signal.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[INPUT A] --> nodeC[GATE C]
    nodeA --> nodeD[GATE D]
    nodeB[INPUT B] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE[GATE E]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF[OUTPUT F]
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

**Reading the diagram:** The edges dictate data flow. Inputs $A$ and $B$ feed intermediate gates $C$ and $D$, which then feed $E$, finally driving output $F$. A valid evaluation order is: $A \to B \to D \to C \to E \to F$.

## 🗺️ Diagram 2: Kahn's Algorithm Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This block diagram shows the internal data-flow of Kahn's algorithm as it processes a VLSI netlist graph.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputG[DAG G = V,E] --> inDegBlock[Compute indeg of every vertex]
    inDegBlock --> initQ[Initialize Queue Q with indeg equals 0]
    initQ --> checkQ{Is Q empty?}
    checkQ -- No --> dequeueU[Dequeue vertex u]
    dequeueU --> appendL[Append u to Output List L]
    appendL --> relaxEdges[For each edge u to v decrement indeg of v]
    relaxEdges --> checkZero{indeg of v equals 0?}
    checkZero -- Yes --> enqueueV[Enqueue v into Q]
    checkZero -- No --> checkQ
    enqueueV --> checkQ
    checkQ -- Yes --> finalCheck{Length of L equals n?}
    finalCheck -- Yes --> success[Valid Topological Order Output]
    finalCheck -- No --> cycleDet[Cycle Detected - No Valid Order]
```

**Interpretation:** The flow enters a processing loop that consumes source vertices (in-degree zero) and "cascades" their removal, propagating new sources into the queue. If the loop terminates with a partial list, a cycle is flagged — critical feedback for VLSI tools handling feedback loops in sequential circuits.

## 🗺️ Diagram 3: DFS-Based Topological Sort (Recursion Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start DFS from any unvisited vertex u] --> markU[Mark u as visited]
    markU --> iterSucc[For each successor v of u]
    iterSucc --> checkVis{Is v visited?}
    checkVis -- No --> recurse[Recursive DFS on v]
    checkVis -- Yes --> checkBack{Is v in current recursion stack?}
    checkBack -- Yes --> cycleFound[BACK EDGE - CYCLE DETECTED]
    checkBack -- No --> iterSucc
    recurse --> iterSucc
    iterSucc -- Done --> pushU[Push u onto Stack]
    pushU --> moreUnvis{Any unvisited vertex?}
    moreUnvis -- Yes --> start
    moreUnvis -- No --> reverseStack[Reverse Stack to get Topological Order]
    reverseStack --> output[Output Topological Order]
```

**Interpretation:** DFS visits deep into successor chains first. Once a vertex's entire reachable subgraph is explored, the vertex is recorded. Reversing the recording order yields a topological order. A back-edge (an edge to a vertex on the current DFS stack) signals a directed cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## ✍️ Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define topological sort. State the necessary and sufficient condition for a directed graph to admit a topological ordering.**

**Model Answer:**

> Topological sort is a linear ordering of vertices of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ such that for every directed edge $(u \rightarrow v)$, $u$ appears before $v$ in the ordering.
>
> **Necessary and Sufficient Condition:** A directed graph admits a topological ordering **if and only if** it is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**, i.e., it contains **no directed cycles**.
>
> *Proof sketch:* If a topological order exists, a cycle would imply a contradiction ($v_1$ before $v_2$ before ... before $v_k$ before $v_1$). Conversely, a DAG always has a vertex with in-degree 0; removing it recursively yields a valid order.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for condition, 1 for proof sketch)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**State the time and space complexity of Kahn's algorithm for topological sort. Why is this complexity optimal for graph algorithms?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Time Complexity:** $T(n, m) = \Theta(n + m)$
> **Space Complexity:** $S(n, m) = \Theta(n + m)$ (for in-degree array, adjacency list, queue, output)
>
> **Why optimal:** Any correct algorithm that processes a graph must, in the worst case, examine every vertex ($n$) and traverse every edge ($m$) at least once. The lower bound for any graph algorithm is therefore $\Omega(n + m)$. Since Kahn's algorithm achieves this bound, it is **asymptotically optimal**.

*(3 marks: 1 each for time, space, optimality justification)*

---

## ✍️ Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain **Kahn's algorithm** for topological sort with its step-by-step procedure. Apply it on the following DAG and write the topological ordering. Show all intermediate states. **(7 marks)**

**DAG:** Vertices: $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$
**Edges:** $1 \rightarrow 3$, $1 \rightarrow 4$, $2 \rightarrow 3$, $2 \rightarrow 5$, $3 \rightarrow 6$, $4 \rightarrow 5$, $5 \rightarrow 6$

**(b)** What is the significance of topological sort in **VLSI CAD flow**? List at least four specific applications and explain one in detail. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Kahn's Algorithm Explained & Applied**

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Compute in-degree of every vertex.
2. Place all in-degree zero vertices in a queue.
3. While queue is non-empty:
    - Dequeue $u$, append to output.
    - For each successor $v$ of $u$, decrement $indeg(v)$; if it becomes 0, enqueue $v$.
4. If output has fewer than $n$ vertices, graph has a cycle.

**Application on Given DAG:**

**Step 1 — In-degree computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
indeg(1) &= 0 \\
indeg(2) &= 0 \\
indeg(3) &= 2 \quad (from\ 1, 2)\\
indeg(4) &= 1 \quad (from\ 1)\\
indeg(5) &= 2 \quad (from\ 2, 4)\\
indeg(6) &= 2 \quad (from\ 3, 5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Initial Queue:** $Q = [1, 2]$ (assuming insertion order)

**Step 3 — Iterative processing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pop 1:} \quad & L = [1]; \quad indeg(3): 2 \to 1;\ indeg(4): 1 \to 0 \Rightarrow Q = [2, 4] \\
\text{Pop 2:} \quad & L = [1, 2]; \ indeg(3): 1 \to 0 \Rightarrow Q = [4, 3] \\
\text{Pop 4:} \quad & L = [1, 2, 4]; \ indeg(5): 2 \to 1 \Rightarrow Q = [3] \\
\text{Pop 3:} \quad & L = [1, 2, 4, 3]; \ indeg(6): 2 \to 1 \Rightarrow Q = [] \text{ then } indeg(5): 1 \to 0 \Rightarrow Q = [5] \\
& \text{(Note: when pop 3, indeg(6) becomes 1, not 0; indeg(5) becomes 0, enqueue 5.)} \\
\text{Pop 5:} \quad & L = [1, 2, 4, 3, 5]; \ indeg(6): 1 \to 0 \Rightarrow Q = [6] \\
\text{Pop 6:} \quad & L = [1, 2, 4, 3, 5, 6]; \ Q = []
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Topological Order:** $\boxed{\sigma = (1, 2, 4, 3, 5, 6)}$

Since $\vert L \vert = 6 = n$, the graph is acyclic. ✅

*(Marks distribution: 3 for algorithm explanation, 2 for in-degree table, 2 for step-by-step trace)*

**Part (b) — Significance in VLSI CAD**

Topological sort is fundamental to VLSI CAD because most design tasks, netlists, and synthesis flows can be modeled as DAGs. Key applications:

1. **Logic Simulation:** Combinational gates form a DAG; topological order determines evaluation order so all inputs of a gate are known when it fires.
2. **Static Timing Analysis (STA):** Longest path (critical path) is computed in topological order to determine the maximum circuit delay.
3. **High-Level Synthesis (HLS):** Operations in a CDFG (Control-Data Flow Graph) are scheduled in topological order under resource constraints.
4. **Design-Flow Task Management:** Tools orchestrate RTL → Synthesis → Floorplanning → Placement → Routing → STA → Sign-off as a DAG of dependent tasks.

**Detailed explanation of Logic Simulation:**
A combinational circuit has no feedback, so signal flow forms a DAG. Each gate's output depends only on its inputs' *current* values. The simulator assigns values to primary inputs, then evaluates gates one by one in topological order — guaranteeing that when a gate is evaluated, all its predecessor gates have already produced correct output. This makes event-driven simulation $O(n + m)$ per cycle, enabling it to scale to multi-million-gate designs.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for listing, 3 for chosen detailed explanation, 2 for VLSI relevance)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **DFS-based topological sorting algorithm** with pseudocode. Prove its correctness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the DAG below. Perform DFS-based topological sort starting from vertex $S$. Show the DFS call stack at each step and the final topological order. **(7 marks)**

**DAG:** Vertices: $\{S, A, B, C, D, E, T\}$
**Edges:** $S \rightarrow A$, $S \rightarrow B$, $A \rightarrow C$, $B \rightarrow C$, $B \rightarrow D$, $C \rightarrow E$, $D \rightarrow E$, $E \rightarrow T$

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — DFS-Based Topological Sort**

**Pseudocode (structured):**

```
TOPOLOGICAL-SORT-DFS(G)
    for each vertex u in V:
        color[u] = WHITE
        parent[u] = NIL
    stack = empty
    for each vertex u in V:
        if color[u] == WHITE:
            DFS-VISIT(u, stack)
    return reverse(stack)

DFS-VISIT(u, stack)
    color[u] = GRAY
    for each v in Adj[u]:
        if color[v] == GRAY:
            print "CYCLE DETECTED at edge (u, v)"
        if color[v] == WHITE:
            parent[v] = u
            DFS-VISIT(v, stack)
    color[u] = BLACK
    stack.push(u)
```

**Correctness Proof:**

*Claim:* When `DFS-VISIT(u)` finishes (vertex $u$ is colored BLACK and pushed onto the stack), all vertices reachable from $u$ are already on the stack *above* $u$.

*Proof by induction on DFS finish time:*
- **Base case:** $u$ is a sink (no outgoing edges). It is pushed immediately. Trivially true.
- **Inductive step:** For each successor $v$ of $u$, `DFS-VISIT(v)` finishes before `DFS-VISIT(u)`. By induction, all vertices reachable from $v$ are pushed before $v$, and $v$ is pushed before $u$. Hence all vertices reachable from $u$ are pushed before $u$. ✅
- **Stack reversal:** When $u$ finishes, all its reachable vertices are above it in the stack. After reversing, $u$ appears *before* all of them — satisfying the topological property $u \rightarrow v \Rightarrow u$ before $v$.

**Part (b) — Worked Example**

Starting DFS from $S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{DFS-VISIT(S): visit S; recurse on A} \\
&\quad \text{DFS-VISIT(A): visit A; recurse on C} \\
&\quad\quad \text{DFS-VISIT(C): visit C; recurse on E} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{DFS-VISIT(E): visit E; recurse on T} \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{DFS-VISIT(T): visit T; no successors; PUSH T} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{finish E; PUSH E} \\
&\quad \text{finish C; PUSH C} \\
&\text{finish A; PUSH A} \\
&\text{back to S; recurse on B} \\
&\quad \text{DFS-VISIT(B): visit B; C already BLACK; recurse on D} \\
&\quad\quad \text{DFS-VISIT(D): visit D; E already BLACK; PUSH D} \\
&\text{finish B; PUSH B} \\
&\text{finish S; PUSH S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stack (push order):** $[T, E, C, A, D, B, S]$

**Final Topological Order (after reverse):**
$$
\boxed{\sigma = (S, B, D, A, C, E, T)}
$$

*Note:* An alternative valid order would be $S, A, B, C, D, E, T$ — confirming that DAGs admit **multiple valid topological orderings**.

*(Marks distribution: 3 pseudocode, 2 proof, 2 trace)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing reverse-postorder with postorder:** Many students forget to *reverse* the stack from DFS. Without reversal, the output is an *anti*-topological order. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to check for cycles:** Simply outputting a list without verifying $\vert L \vert = n$ loses credit for correctness. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Miscomputing in-degrees:** Off-by-one errors in in-degree table are the #1 reason for incorrect traces. **Double-check each edge.**
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps:** KTU valuators award step-marks. Always show queue state, in-degree updates, and the output list at *every iteration*.
> 5. **Mixing up Kahn's and DFS methods:** Clearly state which algorithm is being used and stick to its conventions.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Topological sort orders a **DAG** so that every directed edge $u \rightarrow v$ implies $u$ appears before $v$.
- ✅ **Existence Condition:** Graph must be a Directed Acyclic Graph (no directed cycles).
- ✅ **Kahn's Algorithm** uses **in-degree counting + BFS** with a queue. Complexity: $\Theta(n + m)$.
- ✅ **DFS-based method** uses **post-order + stack reversal**. Complexity: $\Theta(n + m)$.
- ✅ **Cycle detection:** Output size $< n$ (Kahn) or back-edge to GRAY node (DFS).
- ✅ In **VLSI CAD**: critical for logic simulation, STA, HLS scheduling, and design-flow task orchestration.
- ✅ **Optimality:** $\Theta(n + m)$ is the theoretical lower bound for any graph algorithm — both methods achieve it.
- ✅ A DAG has **$\geq 1$** valid topological order; the count is a combinatorial function of the partial order.
- ✅ **Critical path** (longest path) computation uses the recurrence $dist(v) = \max_{(u \rightarrow v)} (dist(u) + w(u \rightarrow v))$ after topological ordering.
- ✅ **Examiner's mantra:** Always show in-degree table, queue states, and intermediate output list — partial credit depends on visible working.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shortest Path Algorithms: Dijkstra's Shortest-Path Algorithm for Single Pair Shortest Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shortest Path Algorithms: Dijkstra's Shortest-Path Algorithm for Single Pair Shortest Path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dijkstra's Shortest-Path Algorithm** is a classic single-source shortest-path (SSSP) graph-search algorithm proposed by Edsger W. Dijkstra (1956). Given a weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with non-negative edge weights $w(u, v) \geq 0$ and a designated **source vertex** $s \in V$, the algorithm computes the minimum cumulative cost (path length) from $s$ to every other vertex $v \in V$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Pair vs. Single-Source (KTU Board Definition)**
> - **Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP)**: Computes the shortest path from *one* source vertex to **all** other vertices.
> - **Single-Pair Shortest Path (SPSP)**: Computes the shortest path from a *specific source* $s$ to a *specific target* $t$. Dijkstra's algorithm, when terminated early upon visiting $t$, is the standard solution for SPSP. Hence the KTU module groups it under the SPSP heading.

The algorithm belongs to the family of **Greedy Algorithms** and operates on the principle of **optimal substructure** combined with the **greedy choice property** — at every step it commits to the locally optimal (minimum distance) unvisited vertex, and this choice is provably globally optimal when all edge weights are non-negative.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Spilled-Water Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)**
> Imagine you are standing at node **A** in a city map. You pour water on node A. The water flows through every road (edge) at a speed inversely proportional to the road's length. The first time a drop of water reaches any intersection (node), it has travelled via the *shortest possible route*. The order in which nodes get "wet" is precisely the order in which Dijkstra finalises their shortest distances.
>
> - **Roads = Edges**
> - **Road lengths = Edge weights (cost)**
> - **First-arrival time at a node = Shortest distance from source**

In a more practical sense, **Google Maps** works on this exact principle (with several extensions): the moment you enter a source, it computes the minimum-time path to your destination by relaxing distances outward, exactly as Dijkstra prescribed.

## 1.3 Role in VLSI CAD — Why KTU Includes This in Module 1

In a VLSI physical-design flow, the chip is modelled as a **routing graph** (or **grid graph**) where:
- **Vertices** = routing tracks, vias, channels, or grid cells.
- **Edges** = wire segments with weights representing **wirelength, congestion, delay, or capacity usage**.

Dijkstra's algorithm underpins several core CAD tools:
- **Maze Routing** (Lee's algorithm uses BFS, but weighted maze routing uses Dijkstra)
- **Steiner-Tree construction** (heuristic extensions)
- **Timing-driven global placement**
- **Clock-tree synthesis** (finding low-skew routes)

| CAD Sub-Problem | Graph Vertices | Edge Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Maze Routing | Routing grid cells | Wirelength / Congestion |
| Global Routing | Rectilinear routing regions | Capacity overflow penalty |
| Clock-Tree Routing | Driver/load pins | Wire delay |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Whenever you see the words "find the minimum-cost path between two pins through a routing graph" — think **Dijkstra**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Operational Concept — Stepwise Logic

Dijkstra's algorithm maintains three data structures during execution:

1. **Distance Array $D[v]$** — current known shortest distance from source $s$ to vertex $v$ (initialised to $\infty$ for all $v \neq s$, with $D[s] = 0$).
2. **Predecessor Array $\pi[v]$** — the vertex from which the shortest path to $v$ is currently reached (used to reconstruct the actual path).
3. **Visited Set $S$** — the set of vertices whose shortest distances have been *finalised* (greedy commitment).

### Operational Steps (Logically Decomposed)

- **Step 1 — Initialisation:** Set $D[s] = 0$, $D[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$, $\pi[v] = \text{NIL}$, and $S = \emptyset$.

- **Step 2 — Greedy Selection:** From the unvisited set $V \setminus S$, select the vertex $u$ with the **minimum tentative distance**:
$$u = \arg\min_{v \in V \setminus S} D[v]$$
Add $u$ to $S$ (finalise its distance).

- **Step 3 — Edge Relaxation:** For every neighbour $v$ of $u$ (i.e., every edge $(u, v) \in E$) that is **not** in $S$, update:
$$D[v] = \min\bigl(D[v],\; D[u] + w(u, v)\bigr)$$
$$\pi[v] = u \quad \text{if relaxation improved } D[v]$$

- **Step 4 — Termination Check:** Repeat Steps 2 and 3 until $S = V$ (SSSP) or until the **target vertex $t$ is added to $S$** (SPSP — early termination).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Initialise $D[v] = \infty$?** Before any path is discovered, the distance is conceptually infinite.
- **Why pick the minimum-distance unvisited vertex?** Because all edge weights are non-negative, *no future relaxation can lower $D[u]$* once it is the smallest among unvisited vertices. This is the cornerstone of the **cut-based greedy proof**.
- **Why relax neighbours only?** Any shortest path from $s$ to $v$ that goes through a non-finalised vertex $u$ must equal $D[u] + w(u, v)$ at the moment $u$ is finalised.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 4 lines — they cover 90% of KTU exam marks for this topic.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relaxation Rule | $D[v] = \min(D[v],\; D[u] + w(u,v))$ | Updating tentative distance |
| 2 | Greedy Selection | $u = \arg\min_{v \in V \setminus S} D[v]$ | Choosing next vertex to finalise |
| 3 | Path Reconstruction | $\text{Trace back from } t \text{ using } \pi[v]$ | Recovering the actual SPSP route |
| 4 | Complexity (Array) | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | Worst-case time, no heap |
| 5 | Complexity (Binary Heap) | $O\bigl((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert\bigr)$ | Sparse graphs |
| 6 | Complexity (Fibonacci Heap) | $O\bigl(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert\bigr)$ | Theoretical optimum |
| 7 | Precondition | $w(u, v) \geq 0\; \forall (u, v) \in E$ | No negative edges |
| 8 | Invariant | $D[v] = \delta(s, v)\; \forall v \in S$ | Maintains optimality |

*(Note: $\delta(s, v)$ denotes the **true shortest path distance** from $s$ to $v$.)*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLSI Maze Routing:** Each grid cell is a vertex, the cost of crossing a cell encodes congestion and wirelength. Dijkstra yields the minimum-cost path between two pins.
- **Network Routing Protocols (OSPF, IS-IS):** Internet backbone routers use Dijkstra to compute the shortest-hop / lowest-latency forwarding tables.
- **GPS / Google Maps:** Real-time traffic is encoded as edge weights; Dijkstra (and its faster successor, A*) provides turn-by-turn navigation.
- **Robotics Path Planning:** Mobile robots use it for obstacle-free traversal on a discretised cost map.
- **Game AI:** NPC (Non-Player Character) navigation in grid-based video games.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — Dijkstra's Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **The example below is the standard KTU-board-style graph used in past university examinations. Trace it carefully — examiners award partial marks for every correct iteration.**

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Find the shortest path from **source vertex $S = 1$** to **target vertex $T = 5$** (and all other vertices) in the following undirected weighted graph:

- **Vertices:** $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$
- **Edges & Weights:**
  - $(1, 2) = 6$
  - $(1, 3) = 2$
  - $(2, 3) = 3$
  - $(2, 4) = 1$
  - $(3, 4) = 4$
  - $(3, 5) = 5$
  - $(4, 5) = 1$

### 3.1.2 Initialisation

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[1] &= 0, \quad \pi[1] = \text{NIL} \\
D[2] &= \infty, \quad \pi[2] = \text{NIL} \\
D[3] &= \infty, \quad \pi[3] = \text{NIL} \\
D[4] &= \infty, \quad \pi[4] = \text{NIL} \\
D[5] &= \infty, \quad \pi[5] = \text{NIL} \\
S &= \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Iteration 1 — Finalise Vertex 1

**Selection:** $D[1] = 0$ is the minimum among unvisited $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. So $u = 1$, $S = \{1\}$.

**Relaxation of neighbours of vertex 1** (vertices 2 and 3):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[2] &= \min\bigl(\infty,\; 0 + 6\bigr) = 6, \quad \pi[2] = 1 \\
D[3] &= \min\bigl(\infty,\; 0 + 2\bigr) = 2, \quad \pi[3] = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Snapshot after Iteration 1:**

| Vertex | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D[v]$ | **0** ✓ | 6 | 2 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\pi[v]$ | NIL | 1 | 1 | NIL | NIL |
| Status | Final | Tentative | Tentative | Tentative | Tentative |

### 3.1.4 Iteration 2 — Finalise Vertex 3

**Selection:** Among unvisited $\{2, 3, 4, 5\}$, the minimum $D[v]$ is $D[3] = 2$. So $u = 3$, $S = \{1, 3\}$.

**Relaxation of neighbours of vertex 3** (vertices 1, 2, 4, 5; skip 1 since finalised):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[2] &= \min\bigl(6,\; 2 + 3\bigr) = \min(6, 5) = 5, \quad \pi[2] = 3 \\
D[4] &= \min\bigl(\infty,\; 2 + 4\bigr) = 6, \quad \pi[4] = 3 \\
D[5] &= \min\bigl(\infty,\; 2 + 5\bigr) = 7, \quad \pi[5] = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Snapshot after Iteration 2:**

| Vertex | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D[v]$ | **0** ✓ | 5 | **2** ✓ | 6 | 7 |
| $\pi[v]$ | NIL | 3 | 1 | 3 | 3 |

### 3.1.5 Iteration 3 — Finalise Vertex 2

**Selection:** Minimum unvisited $D[v]$ is $D[2] = 5$. So $u = 2$, $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$.

**Relaxation of neighbours of vertex 2** (vertices 1, 3, 4; skip 1, 3 since finalised):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[4] &= \min\bigl(6,\; 5 + 1\bigr) = \min(6, 6) = 6 \quad \text{(no improvement, } \pi[4] \text{ remains } 3\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Snapshot after Iteration 3:**

| Vertex | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D[v]$ | **0** ✓ | **5** ✓ | **2** ✓ | 6 | 7 |
| $\pi[v]$ | NIL | 3 | 1 | 3 | 3 |

### 3.1.6 Iteration 4 — Finalise Vertex 4

**Selection:** Minimum unvisited $D[v]$ is $D[4] = 6$. So $u = 4$, $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.

**Relaxation of neighbours of vertex 4** (vertices 2, 3, 5; skip 2, 3 since finalised):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[5] &= \min\bigl(7,\; 6 + 1\bigr) = \min(7, 7) = 7 \quad \text{(no improvement, } \pi[5] \text{ remains } 3\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Snapshot after Iteration 4:**

| Vertex | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D[v]$ | **0** ✓ | **5** ✓ | **2** ✓ | **6** ✓ | 7 |
| $\pi[v]$ | NIL | 3 | 1 | 3 | 3 |

### 3.1.7 Iteration 5 — Finalise Vertex 5 (Target Reached → SPSP Terminates Here)

**Selection:** $D[5] = 7$ is the only unvisited vertex. So $u = 5$, $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$.

### 3.1.8 Final Result — Shortest Distances

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(1, 1) &= 0 \\
\delta(1, 2) &= 5 \\
\delta(1, 3) &= 2 \\
\delta(1, 4) &= 6 \\
\delta(1, 5) &= 7 \quad \text{(single-pair shortest path from 1 to 5)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.9 Path Reconstruction (for the Single Pair $1 \to 5$)

Trace back using $\pi[\cdot]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi[5] &= 3 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Edge } (3, 5) \\
\pi[3] &= 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Edge } (1, 3) \\
\pi[1] &= \text{NIL} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Stop at source}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Shortest path:** $1 \to 3 \to 5$ with total cost $2 + 5 = 7$.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification by inspection:** The alternative route $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5$ has cost $2 + 3 + 1 + 1 = 7$, which ties. Both are valid shortest paths. The algorithm correctly found one of them.

## 3.2 Proof Sketch — Why Greedy Choice Is Optimal

**Claim:** When vertex $u$ with minimum $D[u]$ is added to $S$, $D[u] = \delta(s, u)$ (the true shortest distance).

**Proof by contradiction:** Suppose at the moment $u$ is finalised, a shorter path $P: s \leadsto u$ exists that goes through some unvisited vertex $x$ (the first unvisited vertex on $P$). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[x] &\leq \text{cost of prefix } s \leadsto x \text{ on path } P \\
      &< \text{cost of full path } P \quad \text{(since edge weights are non-negative)} \\
      &< D[u]
\end{aligned}
$$

But this contradicts the choice of $u$ as the minimum-distance unvisited vertex. Hence no such shorter path exists, and $D[u] = \delta(s, u)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where the proof breaks for negative weights:** The strict inequality $D[x] < D[u]$ fails if some edge has negative weight, because a longer prefix could be offset by a negative edge and still yield a smaller total cost. **This is exactly why Dijkstra fails on graphs with negative edges.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid Compilation-Safe)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Input Graph G = V E w and Source s"] --> B["Initialise D s = 0, D v = infinity for v not s"]
    B --> C["Set pi v = NIL, S = empty set"]
    C --> D{"V minus S is empty?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["END: All shortest paths found"]
    D -- "No" --> F["Select u = argmin D v for v in V minus S"]
    F --> G["Add u to S finalise D u"]
    G --> H{"Is u equal to target t?"}
    H -- "Yes SPSP early exit" --> I["Reconstruct path using pi array and STOP"]
    H -- "No" --> J["For each neighbour v of u with v not in S"]
    J --> K["Compute tentative = D u plus w u v"]
    K --> L{"tentative less than D v?"}
    L -- "Yes relax" --> M["Set D v = tentative, pi v = u"]
    L -- "No skip" --> N["Continue to next neighbour"]
    M --> N
    N --> O{"All neighbours checked?"}
    O -- "No" --> J
    O -- "Yes" --> D
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dijkstra in VLSI Maze Router

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1["Netlist: Source Pin S and Target Pin T"]
        A2["Routing Grid Graph: vertices, edges, costs"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["DIJKSTRA ENGINE"]
        B1["Initialisation Module: D infinity, pi NIL"]
        B2["Priority Queue: min heap on D v"]
        B3["Greedy Selector: pop min distance vertex"]
        B4["Relaxation Unit: D v = min D v, D u + w"]
        B5["Path Traceback: pi chain reconstruction"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1["Shortest Wirelength Path S to T"]
        C2["Updated Congestion Map for Next Net"]
    end
    A1 --> CORE
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B2
    B3 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
```

## 4.3 Worked-Example Graph (Re-Drawn for Clarity)

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1["Vertex 1"]
    n2["Vertex 2"]
    n3["Vertex 3"]
    n4["Vertex 4"]
    n5["Vertex 5"]
    n1 ---|"6"| n2
    n1 ---|"2"| n3
    n2 ---|"3"| n3
    n2 ---|"1"| n4
    n3 ---|"4"| n4
    n3 ---|"5"| n5
    n4 ---|"1"| n5
    n1:::src
    n5:::tgt
    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tgt fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, **Vertex 1** (gold) is the source, and **Vertex 5** (green) is the target. The final shortest path $1 \to 3 \to 5$ with cost **7** is highlighted by tracing edges with weights 2 and 5.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]**

### Q1. State the Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm. Why is it not applicable to graphs with negative edge weights? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Dijkstra's algorithm is a greedy single-source shortest-path algorithm that works as follows:

1. Initialise $D[s] = 0$ and $D[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$. Mark all vertices as unvisited.
2. Repeatedly select the unvisited vertex $u$ with the **smallest** $D[u]$, mark it as visited (finalised), and **relax** all its outgoing edges using $D[v] = \min(D[v], D[u] + w(u, v))$.
3. Terminate when all vertices are finalised (SSSP) or when the target $t$ is reached (SPSP).

**Why it fails for negative edges:** The proof of optimality relies on the fact that once a vertex is finalised with minimum $D[u]$, no alternate path through an unvisited vertex can yield a smaller total cost. This relies on **non-negative** edge weights. With negative edges, a longer prefix path may still produce a smaller total cost, invalidating the greedy commitment.

---

### Q2. What is the difference between single-source and single-pair shortest-path problems? Which variant does Dijkstra solve by default? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

- **Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP):** Find the shortest path from one source vertex $s$ to **all** other vertices in the graph.
- **Single-Pair Shortest Path (SPSP):** Find the shortest path from a specific source $s$ to a specific target $t$ **only**.

Dijkstra's algorithm by default solves **SSSP** because it finalises every vertex. However, it can be trivially adapted to solve SPSP by **terminating early** the moment vertex $t$ is finalised (added to set $S$). Under KTU 2024 module grouping, the algorithm is studied under the SPSP heading because that is its most common application in VLSI maze routing (find shortest path between two pins).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Style, Module 1]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — *(Choose ONE of the two options)*

#### **Q A.1. Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the following graph. Find the shortest distance and path from vertex $S$ to all other vertices.** (14 Marks)

**Graph Data:**

| Edge | Weight |
|---|---|
| S – A | 4 |
| S – B | 2 |
| A – B | 1 |
| A – C | 5 |
| B – C | 8 |
| B – D | 10 |
| C – D | 2 |
| C – E | 6 |
| D – E | 3 |

#### **Part (a): Trace the algorithm in a tabular form showing the distance array update in every iteration. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Initialisation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[S] &= 0, \quad D[A] = D[B] = D[C] = D[D] = D[E] = \infty \\
\pi[v] &= \text{NIL for all } v, \quad S = \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1: Finalise $S$ (dist = 0)**

Relax neighbours of $S$ (A, B):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[A] &= \min(\infty, 0 + 4) = 4, \quad \pi[A] = S \\
D[B] &= \min(\infty, 0 + 2) = 2, \quad \pi[B] = S
\end{aligned}
$$

| Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D[v]$ | **0** ✓ | 4 | 2 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| $\pi[v]$ | NIL | S | S | NIL | NIL | NIL |

**Iteration 2: Finalise $B$ (dist = 2)**

Relax neighbours of $B$ (S, A, C, D; skip $S$ as finalised):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[A] &= \min(4, 2 + 1) = 3, \quad \pi[A] = B \\
D[C] &= \min(\infty, 2 + 8) = 10, \quad \pi[C] = B \\
D[D] &= \min(\infty, 2 + 10) = 12, \quad \pi[D] = B
\end{aligned}
$$

| Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D[v]$ | **0** ✓ | 3 | **2** ✓ | 10 | 12 | $\infty$ |
| $\pi[v]$ | NIL | B | S | B | B | NIL |

**Iteration 3: Finalise $A$ (dist = 3)**

Relax neighbours of $A$ (S, B, C; skip $S$, $B$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[C] &= \min(10, 3 + 5) = 8, \quad \pi[C] = A
\end{aligned}
$$

| Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D[v]$ | **0** ✓ | **3** ✓ | **2** ✓ | 8 | 12 | $\infty$ |
| $\pi[v]$ | NIL | B | S | A | B | NIL |

**Iteration 4: Finalise $C$ (dist = 8)**

Relax neighbours of $C$ (A, B, D, E; skip $A$, $B$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[D] &= \min(12, 8 + 2) = 10, \quad \pi[D] = C \\
D[E] &= \min(\infty, 8 + 6) = 14, \quad \pi[E] = C
\end{aligned}
$$

| Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D[v]$ | **0** ✓ | **3** ✓ | **2** ✓ | **8** ✓ | 10 | 14 |
| $\pi[v]$ | NIL | B | S | A | C | C |

**Iteration 5: Finalise $D$ (dist = 10)**

Relax neighbours of $D$ (B, C, E; skip $B$, $C$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D[E] &= \min(14, 10 + 3) = 13, \quad \pi[E] = D
\end{aligned}
$$

| Vertex | S | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $D[v]$ | **0** ✓ | **3** ✓ | **2** ✓ | **8** ✓ | **10** ✓ | 13 |
| $\pi[v]$ | NIL | B | S | A | C | D |

**Iteration 6: Finalise $E$ (dist = 13). Done.**

#### **Part (b): List the final shortest paths and distances from $S$ to all vertices. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Vertex $v$ | Shortest Distance $\delta(S, v)$ | Shortest Path | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|
| S | 0 | S | — |
| A | 3 | $S \to B \to A$ | $2 + 1 = 3$ ✓ |
| B | 2 | $S \to B$ | $2$ ✓ |
| C | 8 | $S \to B \to A \to C$ | $2 + 1 + 5 = 8$ ✓ |
| D | 10 | $S \to B \to A \to C \to D$ | $2 + 1 + 5 + 2 = 10$ ✓ |
| E | 13 | $S \to B \to A \to C \to D \to E$ | $2 + 1 + 5 + 2 + 3 = 13$ ✓ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Causing Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the predecessor $\pi[v]$** when a relaxation improves $D[v]$. This loses 1–2 marks because path reconstruction becomes impossible.
> 2. **Skipping the "no improvement" check** — even when $\min(D[v], D[u] + w) = D[v]$, you must still inspect and *declare* "no improvement" in the table. Examiners award marks for explicit justification.
> 3. **Picking a vertex with $\infty$ distance** when an unvisited finite-distance vertex exists. The greedy selector must always pick the **minimum finite** $D[v]$, not the first one encountered.
> 4. **Confusing SSSP and SPSP termination** — for SPSP, you stop when the **target $t$** enters $S$, not when all vertices are finalised.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

#### **Q B.1. With a suitable example, explain how Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm is used in VLSI maze routing. Discuss its limitations and the modifications required to handle congestion in modern routers. (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a): Explain the mapping from maze-routing problem to graph-search, and show Dijkstra's trace on a $4 \times 4$ routing grid. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mapping the Problem:**

- The **routing region** is discretised into a grid of cells (rows × columns). Each cell is a **vertex** in the graph $G$.
- Two cells are connected by an **edge** if they are 4-neighbour (or 8-neighbour for diagonal routing). Each edge is assigned a **cost** representing the wirelength contribution or congestion penalty.
- The **source pin** corresponds to one vertex, and the **target/sink pin** corresponds to another. The single-pair shortest path between them gives the **minimum-cost wire route**.

**Example: $4 \times 4$ routing grid with two pins**

Consider a $4 \times 4$ grid labelled $(r, c)$ with $r, c \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. Let the **source** be $S = (1, 1)$ and the **target** $T = (4, 4)$. Assume one congestion cell at $(2, 3)$ with cost 5 (all other cells have cost 1).

**Equivalent Graph (5 representative cells, others omitted for clarity):**

| Edge (cell-to-cell) | Cost |
|---|---|
| $(1,1) - (1,2)$ | 1 |
| $(1,2) - (1,3)$ | 1 |
| $(1,3) - (1,4)$ | 1 |
| $(1,4) - (2,4)$ | 1 |
| $(2,4) - (3,4)$ | 1 |
| $(3,4) - (4,4)$ | 1 |
| $(1,3) - (2,3)$ | 5 (congestion) |
| $(2,3) - (3,3)$ | 5 |

**Dijkstra's trace (compressed):**

- $D[(1,1)] = 0$. Finalise it.
- $D[(1,2)] = 1$, $D[(2,1)] = 1$. Finalise $(1,2)$.
- $D[(1,3)] = 2$, $D[(2,2)] = 2$. Finalise $(1,3)$.
- Relaxation via $(1,3) \to (2,3)$: $D[(2,3)] = 2 + 5 = 7$. But the path $(1,3) \to (1,4) \to (2,4) \to (3,4) \to (4,4)$ gives $D[(4,4)] = 4$ without touching the congested cell.
- Final shortest path: $(1,1) \to (1,2) \to (1,3) \to (1,4) \to (2,4) \to (3,4) \to (4,4)$ with total cost **6**, **avoiding the congestion cell**.

This demonstrates how Dijkstra's cost model inherently routes around high-cost (congested) regions.

#### **Part (b): Discuss the limitations and the modifications for handling congestion. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of Pure Dijkstra in Modern VLSI Routing:**

1. **Computational Cost on Full-Chip Grids:** A modern chip's global routing grid has $\sim 10^5 - 10^6$ cells. Pure Dijkstra per-net is too slow; the runtime must be amortised over millions of nets.
2. **Single-Net Optimality is Not Multi-Net Optimality:** Dijkstra finds the shortest path for *one* net ignoring that other nets also need routes. This leads to **overflow** when multiple shortest paths crowd the same edges.
3. **No Look-Ahead / Negotiation:** Pure Dijkstra does not consider future routing demand. It greedily commits and may block downstream routing.
4. **Sequential vs. Concurrent Routing:** Multi-net concurrent routing requires negotiation-based extensions (e.g., **Negotiated Congestion Routing (Negotiation-Based Dijkstra)**).

**Modifications Used in Modern VLSI Routers:**

| Modification | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Cost Function = Base + $\alpha \cdot$ Congestion** | Penalise edges near capacity, adapt $\alpha$ per iteration |
| **Rip-up and Re-route** | If overflow persists, remove offending wires and re-route with updated costs |
| **Negotiation-Based Routing** (e.g., PathFinder algorithm) | Multi-pass Dijkstra with history-cost adjustment |
| **A\* Search** | Use heuristic $h(n)$ to prune search space (Manhattan distance to target) |
| **Multi-Commodity Flow Formulation** | Global optimisation across all nets simultaneously |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Don't Confuse Dijkstra with Lee's Algorithm:** A common mistake is to state that "Lee's algorithm uses Dijkstra" — it does not. **Lee's algorithm** is a BFS-based maze router that handles unit-cost grids. **Dijkstra-based maze routing** is the *weighted* version that handles variable cost (e.g., congestion). Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Pass — Memorise these bullets before walking into the exam hall.**

- ☐ **Dijkstra's algorithm** is a **greedy** SSSP algorithm that can be adapted to **SPSP** via early termination.
- ☐ It requires **non-negative edge weights**; fails on negative edges.
- ☐ Core operations: **(1) Greedy selection** of minimum-distance unvisited vertex, **(2) Edge relaxation** $D[v] = \min(D[v], D[u] + w(u,v))$.
- ☐ Three data structures: **Distance array $D[\cdot]$**, **Predecessor array $\pi[\cdot]$**, **Visited set $S$**.
- ☐ **Time complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ with array, $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ with binary heap.
- ☐ **Space complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
- ☐ **Optimality invariant:** $D[v] = \delta(s, v)$ for all $v \in S$ throughout execution.
- ☐ **VLSI Application:** Maze routing on grid graphs; cost = wirelength + congestion penalty.
- ☐ **Path Reconstruction:** Trace $\pi[t] \to \pi[\pi[t]] \to \cdots \to s$ and reverse.
- ☐ **Comparison to remember:**
  - **Dijkstra** → non-negative weights, SSSP/SPSP, greedy.
  - **Bellman-Ford** → handles negative weights, SSSP, dynamic programming.
  - **Floyd-Warshall** → all-pairs shortest path, dynamic programming, $O(\vert V \vert^3)$.
  - **A\*** → Dijkstra + heuristic, faster in practice, used in VLSI detailed routing.
- ☐ **Common exam trap:** Always re-check the **predecessor update** in every iteration; an outdated $\pi$ produces a wrong path even if $D[\cdot]$ is correct.
- ☐ **Modern VLSI routers** extend Dijkstra with congestion-aware cost functions and negotiation-based rip-up/re-route (PathFinder-style).
- ☐ **Initial state:** $D[s] = 0$, all other $D[v] = \infty$, all $\pi[v] = \text{NIL}$, $S = \emptyset$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floyd Warshall Algorithm for All Pair Shortest Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Floyd Warshall Algorithm for All Pair Shortest Path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Floyd–Warshall Algorithm** is a classical **dynamic programming (DP)** algorithm used in VLSI Computer-Aided Design (CAD) to compute the **All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP)** problem on a weighted directed graph $G = (V, E, W)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$ vertices and edge weight matrix $W$. It incrementally improves an estimated shortest path matrix by considering each vertex as an *intermediate* node in succession.

In the context of **PEEVT524 – CAD for VLSI Design**, this algorithm is a cornerstone of:
- **Global Routing** in multi-net VLSI layouts
- **Maze Routing** graph construction
- **Steiner-Tree estimation** for wire-length minimization
- **Timing-driven placement** and **path-delay analysis**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The Floyd–Warshall algorithm is treated as a **fundamental graph theoretic primitive** that is reused across partitioning, placement, and routing stages of the VLSI design flow.

### 1.2 Mathematical Statement (Recurrence Relation)

Let $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ denote the shortest path distance from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ using only the intermediate vertices from the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$. The DP recurrence is:

$$
D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min \Big( D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \quad D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \Big)
$$

with the base condition $D^{(0)}[i][j] = W[i][j]$ (direct edge weight, $\infty$ if no edge).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kerala KSRTC Bus Network"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine Kerala's KSRTC bus network connecting 6 major cities: Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, Kozhikode, Thrissur, Kannur, and Kollam. You want to find the *cheapest (shortest) ticket cost* between **every pair of cities**.

- A **direct ticket** from city $A$ to city $B$ has a fixed price — this is your initial distance matrix $D^{(0)}$.
- Suppose the KSRTC discovers that **transit hubs** (e.g., Kochi) can be used as *intermediate stops* to reduce total fare. If going $A \rightarrow \text{Kochi} \rightarrow B$ is cheaper than a direct $A \rightarrow B$ ticket, you update the price.
- You then check *Thrissur* as a hub, then *Kozhikode*, and so on, sequentially.
- After checking **all 6 hubs**, your final price table holds the *cheapest* ticket for **every** city pair. This is exactly what Floyd–Warshall does — it incrementally allows **one more intermediate city** to participate in the path, eventually producing the optimal matrix.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (VLSI CAD Context)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of nets | $N$ | $\mathbf{10^4 - 10^6}$ in modern SoCs |
| Routing graph vertices | $\vert V \vert$ | Gcells or pins |
| Time complexity | $T(n)$ | $\mathbf{O(n^3)}$ |
| Space complexity | $S(n)$ | $\mathbf{O(n^2)}$ |
| Negative edge weights | Allowed? | **Yes**, but no negative cycles |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Recurrence Works — The "How" Behind Each Step

The algorithm operates on a beautiful dynamic-programming principle:

1. **Base case ($k=0$):** Paths are restricted to *no intermediate* vertices. So $D^{(0)}[i][j]$ is simply the direct edge weight (or $\infty$).

2. **Inductive step ($k$ → $k+1$):** When we allow vertex $k$ as an *additional* intermediate, every shortest path $i \leadsto j$ is one of two possibilities:
   - **Option A:** It does **not** pass through vertex $k$ → its length is $D^{(k-1)}[i][j]$.
   - **Option B:** It **does** pass through $k$ → then the path splits as $i \leadsto k$ followed by $k \leadsto j$, both using only $\{1, \ldots, k-1\}$ as intermediates. Length = $D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]$.

3. **Final answer ($k=n$):** All vertices are allowed as intermediates → $D^{(n)}$ is the true all-pairs shortest path matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Logic:** Each iteration *opens one more city as a possible transit hub*. By the time we have opened all $n$ hubs, every possible route — including multi-hop ones — has been considered.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Notation / Units |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **DP Recurrence** | $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\!\big(D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\big)$ | Distances, no unit |
| 2 | **Initial Matrix** | $D^{(0)}[i][j] = W[i][j]$, with $D^{(0)}[i][i] = 0$ | $\infty$ if no edge |
| 3 | **Time Complexity** | $T(n) = \Theta(n^3)$ | Operations |
| 4 | **Space Complexity** | $S(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ | Memory cells |
| 5 | **Path Reconstruction** | Use a successor matrix $S[i][j]$ updated when a shorter path via $k$ is found | Index |
