| 6 | **Negative Cycle Detection** | A negative cycle exists iff any $D^{(n)}[i][i] < 0$ | Diagonal check |
| 7 | **Transitive Closure** | Boolean variant: $T^{(k)}[i][j] = T^{(k-1)}[i][j] \lor (T^{(k-1)}[i][k] \land T^{(k-1)}[k][j])$ | $\{0,1\}$ |
| 8 | **VLSI Wire Length (Lower Bound)** | $L = \sum_{(i,j) \in \text{nets}} D[i][j]$ | Micrometers ($\mu m$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why no $\vert \cdot \vert$ symbols in tables?** The KTU marking system requires clean, parseable LaTeX. Use `\vert V \vert` (e.g., $\vert V \vert$) instead of `|V|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.3 Real-World VLSI Engineering Utility

- **Global Routing (Multi-Net):** The number of routing trees spanning pins equals the number of APSP queries. Floyd–Warshall (or its sparse variants like Johnson's) computes these in $O(n^3)$ per net, which is acceptable for small global-routing graphs ($n \le 10^3$).
- **Critical-Path Delay Estimation:** In static timing analysis (STA), the longest path in a timing graph from any input flip-flop to any output flip-flop is an APSP problem solved by a *max-plus* variant of Floyd–Warshall.
- **Test-Access Mechanism (TAM) Optimization:** Used to find shortest test-wire paths in scan-chain planning.
- **FPGA Routing Architecture:** Modern FPGA routers (e.g., VPR — *Versatile Place and Route*) use APSP as a pre-computation step.

### 2.4 Predecessor Matrix (Path Reconstruction)

To recover the *actual path*, not just the distance, maintain a predecessor matrix $\Pi[i][j]$:

$$
\Pi^{(k)}[i][j] = \begin{cases} \Pi^{(k-1)}[k][j] & \text{if } D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] < D^{(k-1)}[i][j] \\[4pt] \Pi^{(k-1)}[i][j] & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Full APSP Computation

**Problem:** Compute the all-pairs shortest path for the directed graph $G$ with $n = 4$ vertices. Edge weights (with $\infty$ denoting no direct edge):

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & 4 \\ 5 & 1 & 0 & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> We use the recurrence: $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\!\big(D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\big)$

---

#### **Step 0 — Initialization ($k = 0$)**

Set $D^{(0)} = W$, and $D^{(0)}[i][i] = 0$ for all $i$.

$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & 4 \\ 5 & 1 & 0 & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Reasoning:* No intermediate vertices allowed; only direct edges.

---

#### **Step 1 — $k = 1$ (Use vertex 1 as intermediate)**

Apply the recurrence column by column for every $i, j$ pair. We list the *changed* entries:

- $D^{(1)}[3][4] = \min(\infty,\; D^{(0)}[3][1] + D^{(0)}[1][4]) = \min(\infty,\; 5 + 7) = 12$
- $D^{(1)}[4][1] = \min(\infty,\; D^{(0)}[4][1] + D^{(0)}[1][1]) = \infty$ (no path via 1)

All other entries remain unchanged.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & 4 \\ 5 & 1 & 0 & 12 \\ \infty & \infty & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Reasoning:* Vertex 1 is allowed as a transit point. The only new shorter path is $3 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 4$ with total cost $5 + 7 = 12$.

---

#### **Step 2 — $k = 2$ (Use vertex 2 as intermediate)**

- $D^{(2)}[3][1] = \min(5,\; D^{(1)}[3][2] + D^{(1)}[2][1]) = \min(5,\; 1 + 2) = 3$
- $D^{(2)}[3][4] = \min(12,\; D^{(1)}[3][2] + D^{(1)}[2][4]) = \min(12,\; 1 + 4) = 5$

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & 4 \\ 3 & 1 & 0 & 5 \\ \infty & \infty & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Reasoning:* Adding vertex 2 as a hub drastically improves row 3. Path $3 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 1$ beats the direct edge $5$, and $3 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4$ beats the earlier $12$.

---

#### **Step 3 — $k = 3$ (Use vertex 3 as intermediate)**

- $D^{(3)}[4][1] = \min(\infty,\; D^{(2)}[4][3] + D^{(2)}[3][1]) = \min(\infty,\; 2 + 3) = 5$
- $D^{(3)}[4][2] = \min(\infty,\; D^{(2)}[4][3] + D^{(2)}[3][2]) = \min(\infty,\; 2 + 1) = 3$

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & \infty & 4 \\ 3 & 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 5 & 3 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Reasoning:* Vertex 3 unlocks row 4. From vertex 4, we can now reach everyone via the new path $4 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 1$ (cost $5$) and $4 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 2$ (cost $3$).

---

#### **Step 4 — $k = 4$ (Use vertex 4 as intermediate)**

- $D^{(4)}[1][3] = \min(\infty,\; D^{(3)}[1][4] + D^{(3)}[4][3]) = \min(\infty,\; 7 + 2) = 9$
- $D^{(4)}[2][3] = \min(\infty,\; D^{(3)}[2][4] + D^{(3)}[4][3]) = \min(\infty,\; 4 + 2) = 6$
- $D^{(4)}[3][1] = \min(3,\; D^{(3)}[3][4] + D^{(3)}[4][1]) = \min(3,\; 5 + 5) = 3$ (no change)
- $D^{(4)}[3][2] = \min(1,\; D^{(3)}[3][4] + D^{(3)}[4][2]) = \min(1,\; 5 + 3) = 1$ (no change)

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 9 & 7 \\ 2 & 0 & 6 & 4 \\ 3 & 1 & 0 & 5 \\ 5 & 3 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Reasoning:* The final iteration fills in all "previously infinite" entries. For example, the path $1 \rightarrow 3$ was not directly achievable, but $1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 3 = 7 + 2 = 9$ now succeeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $D^{(4)}$ is the all-pairs shortest-path distance matrix. The diagonal is zero (no negative cycles detected), and every off-diagonal entry holds the minimum cost.

### 3.2 Path Reconstruction Using Predecessor Matrix

Let us trace the path from **1 → 3** (cost 9):
- $\Pi[1][3] = 4$ (last hop came via 4)
- $\Pi[1][4] = 1$ (direct edge)
- $\Pi[4][3] = 4$ (direct edge)
- **Reconstructed path:** $1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 3$ ✓

Trace the path from **4 → 1** (cost 5):
- $\Pi[4][1] = 3$ (last hop via 3)
- $\Pi[3][1] = 2$ (last hop via 2)
- $\Pi[2][1] = 2$ (direct edge)
- **Reconstructed path:** $4 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 1$ ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Read adjacency matrix W of size n x n]) --> B[Initialize D0 = W, set D0 i,i = 0]
    B --> C[Set k = 1]
    C --> D{k <= n ?}
    D -- No --> Z([Output: D superscript n is the final APSP matrix])
    D -- Yes --> E[Set i = 1]
    E --> F{i <= n ?}
    F -- No --> G[k = k + 1]
    G --> D
    F -- Yes --> H[Set j = 1]
    H --> I{j <= n ?}
    I -- No --> J[i = i + 1]
    J --> F
    I -- Yes --> K[Compute: Dk i,j = min Dk-1 i,j, Dk-1 i,k + Dk-1 k,j]
    K --> L{New distance smaller?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Update Dk i,j and predecessor Pi i,j]
    L -- No --> N[Keep old value]
    M --> O[j = j + 1]
    N --> O
    O --> I
    Z --> P([Optional: Check diagonal of D for negative cycle detection])
    P --> Q([End])
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Routing Graph for a VLSI Net (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    pinA((Pin A)) -- 3 --> gcellB[Gcell B]
    pinA -- 7 --> gcellD[Gcell D]
    gcellB -- 4 --> gcellD
    gcellB -- inf --> gcellC[Gcell C]
    gcellD -- 2 --> gcellC
    gcellC -- 1 --> gcellB
    gcellC -- 5 --> pinA

    classDef pin fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gcell fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class pinA pin
    class gcellB,gcellC,gcellD gcell
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (VLSI CAD Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer [VLSI Design Input]
        A1[Netlist Specification]
        A2[Floorplan Gcell Graph]
    end

    subgraph FWCore [Floyd Warshall Engine]
        B1[Initialize Distance Matrix D0]
        B2[K-Iteration Loop: For k=1 to n]
        B3[Update Step: D k i,j = min]
        B4[Predecessor Update Step]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer [VLSI CAD Output]
        C1[All-Pairs Shortest Path Matrix]
        C2[Critical Path Delay Vector]
        C3[Global Routing Wirelength Estimate]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B2
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3

    classDef inputCls fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef fwCls fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputCls fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px
    class A1,A2 inputCls
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 fwCls
    class C1,C2,C3 outputCls
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The Floyd–Warshall engine consumes a *netlist* and a *floorplan graph*, then iteratively refines the distance matrix. The output directly feeds downstream VLSI stages: **timing analysis**, **routing**, and **wirelength optimization**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 1]**
*State the Floyd–Warshall recurrence relation. What is the meaning of $D^{(k)}[i][j]$?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Floyd–Warshall recurrence is

$$
D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min \big( D^{(k-1)}[i][j], \quad D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \big)
$$

Here, $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ denotes the **shortest path length** from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ using only the intermediate vertices from the set $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$. The first term represents paths that do *not* use vertex $k$, and the second represents paths that *do* use $k$ as an intermediate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, Module 1]**
*Compare the time and space complexity of Floyd–Warshall with Dijkstra's algorithm for solving APSP on a graph with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Floyd–Warshall:** Time = $\Theta(n^3)$, Space = $\Theta(n^2)$. Works with negative edge weights (but no negative cycles). Suitable for **dense** graphs.
- **Dijkstra's Algorithm (run from each source):** Time = $\Theta\big((n + m)\log n\big)$ per source, total = $\Theta\big(n(n+m)\log n\big)$. Space = $\Theta(n + m)$. Requires **non-negative** edges.
- **Johnson's Algorithm (sparse):** Time = $\Theta\big(n m \log n\big)$ — preferred when $m \ll n^2$.

For a dense VLSI routing graph with moderate $n$, Floyd–Warshall's simplicity is advantageous.

---

### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)**

> **ESE Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, ESE Pattern, Module 1]**

> **Q.A(a) [7 marks]:** Given the directed graph $G$ with the following weight matrix, compute the **initial distance matrix** $D^{(0)}$ and apply **Floyd–Warshall** for $k = 1$ and $k = 2$ only. Show all matrix updates explicitly.
>
> $$
> W = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 3 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\ \infty & 4 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}
> $$

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initialization (1 mark):**
$$
D^{(0)} = W = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 3 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\ \infty & 4 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — $k = 1$ (Vertex 1 as intermediate) (3 marks):**

Use $D^{(1)}[i][j] = \min\!\big(D^{(0)}[i][j],\; D^{(0)}[i][1] + D^{(0)}[1][j]\big)$.

- $D^{(1)}[2][1] = \min(\infty,\; \infty + 0) = \infty$ (no incoming to 1)
- $D^{(1)}[2][2] = \min(0,\; \infty + 5) = 0$ (no change)
- $D^{(1)}[3][1] = \min(3,\; 3 + 0) = 3$ (no change)
- $D^{(1)}[3][2] = \min(\infty,\; 3 + 5) = 8$ ← **updated**
- $D^{(1)}[4][2] = \min(4,\; \infty + 5) = 4$ (no change)
- $D^{(1)}[4][4] = \min(0,\; \infty + \infty) = 0$ (no change)

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 3 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ \infty & 4 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — $k = 2$ (Vertex 2 as intermediate) (3 marks):**

Use $D^{(2)}[i][j] = \min\!\big(D^{(1)}[i][j],\; D^{(1)}[i][2] + D^{(1)}[2][j]\big)$.

- $D^{(2)}[1][3] = \min(\infty,\; 5 + 2) = 7$ ← **updated**
- $D^{(2)}[1][1] = \min(0,\; 5 + \infty) = 0$ (no change)
- $D^{(2)}[3][1] = \min(3,\; 8 + \infty) = 3$ (no change)
- $D^{(2)}[3][3] = \min(0,\; 8 + 2) = 0$ (no change)
- $D^{(2)}[4][1] = \min(\infty,\; 4 + \infty) = \infty$ (no change)
- $D^{(2)}[4][3] = \min(\infty,\; 4 + 2) = 6$ ← **updated**

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & 7 & \infty \\ \infty & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 3 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ \infty & 4 & 6 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

> **Q.A(b) [7 marks]:** Explain how Floyd–Warshall is used in **VLSI global routing**. Discuss the role of the *transitive closure* variant in design-rule checking (DRC).

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Floyd–Warshall in Global Routing (4 marks):**
In a VLSI layout, the chip is divided into a grid of **global cells (gcells)**. A routing graph $G_r = (V, E)$ is constructed where $V$ represents gcells and $E$ represents inter-gcell capacities. For a multi-net design, finding the shortest path between **all pin pairs** of a net is an APSP problem. Floyd–Warshall computes the complete distance matrix in $O(n^3)$ time, which is then used by **rip-up-and-reroute** algorithms to:
1. Minimize total wirelength.
2. Avoid routing congestion on hot gcells.
3. Honor timing constraints by preferring low-capacitance paths.

**Transitive Closure for Design-Rule Checking (3 marks):**
The Boolean version of Floyd–Warshall computes reachability:

$$
T^{(k)}[i][j] = T^{(k-1)}[i][j] \lor \big(T^{(k-1)}[i][k] \land T^{(k-1)}[k][j]\big)
$$

In DRC, this is used to verify **connectivity rules**, e.g., ensuring all pins of a power net (VDD) are mutually reachable through metal layers, or detecting **antenna violations** via path-tracing across the routing graph. $T[i][j] = 1$ implies a valid electrical path exists.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE Pattern, Module 1]**

> **Q.B(a) [7 marks]:** Apply Floyd–Warshall to find the **all-pairs shortest path** for the graph given by:
>
> $$
> W = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & \infty & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & \infty & 0 & \infty \\ \infty & 2 & 9 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
> $$
>
> Show all four iterations ($k = 1, 2, 3, 4$).

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**$D^{(0)}$:**
$$
D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & \infty & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & \infty & 0 & \infty \\ \infty & 2 & 9 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 1$:** Check $D^{(0)}[i][1] + D^{(0)}[1][j]$ for all $i, j$. Only new value: $D^{(1)}[3][2] = 4 + 8 = 12$.

$$
D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & \infty & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & \infty \\ \infty & 2 & 9 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 2$:** New values: $D^{(2)}[4][3] = \min(9, 2+1) = 3$, $D^{(2)}[4][4] = 0$ (unchanged), $D^{(2)}[1][3] = \min(\infty, 8+1) = 9$, $D^{(2)}[3][4] = \min(\infty, 12+\infty) = \infty$.

$$
D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & 9 & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & \infty \\ \infty & 2 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 3$:** $D^{(3)}[2][4] = \min(\infty, 1+\infty) = \infty$, $D^{(3)}[4][4] = 0$, $D^{(3)}[1][3] = \min(9, 8+1) = 9$ (no change).

$$
D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 8 & 9 & 1 \\ \infty & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & \infty \\ \infty & 2 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**$k = 4$:** $D^{(4)}[2][3] = \min(1, \infty+\infty) = 1$ (no change), $D^{(4)}[3][1] = \min(4, \infty+1) = 4$ (no change), $D^{(4)}[1][2] = \min(8, 1+2) = 3$, $D^{(4)}[2][1] = \min(\infty, 1+1) = 2$, $D^{(4)}[3][2] = \min(12, \infty+2) = 12$ (no change).

$$
D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 9 & 1 \\ 2 & 0 & 1 & \infty \\ 4 & 12 & 0 & \infty \\ \infty & 2 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The entries that remain $\infty$ indicate no path exists (e.g., $2 \to 4$).

---

> **Q.B(b) [7 marks]:** How is the **negative-cycle detection** property of Floyd–Warshall exploited in VLSI **timing analysis**? Justify with an example.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Negative Cycles in Timing Graphs (4 marks):**
In static timing analysis (STA), the timing graph is a directed graph with edges weighted by **gate delays** (positive values). A "negative cycle" in the *slack graph* (delays negated) corresponds to a **combinational loop** or an **unstable timing path** in the circuit. Floyd–Warshall's diagonal check:

$$
\text{If } \exists\, i \text{ such that } D^{(n)}[i][i] < 0 \implies \text{negative cycle exists}
$$

allows the CAD tool to flag such loops as **design errors** before fabrication.

**Example (3 marks):** Consider a ring oscillator built from 3 inverters: each inverter has delay $1\,ns$. The slack graph has edges $1 \rightarrow 2, 2 \rightarrow 3, 3 \rightarrow 1$ with weights $-1$. After Floyd–Warshall, $D[1][1] = -3 < 0$, instantly detecting the illegal combinational loop. This is why the algorithm is integrated into commercial EDA tools like **Synopsys PrimeTime** and **Cadence Tempus**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update $D^{(0)}[i][i] = 0$** — partial mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing row/column indices in the recurrence** — students often write $D[i][k] + D[k][j]$ as $D[k][i] + D[j][k]$ (wrong directionality).
> 3. **Skipping the predecessor matrix** — KTU explicitly tests path reconstruction in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Forgetting to check the diagonal** when asked about negative cycles — full mark loss on that sub-part.
> 5. **Stating complexity as $O(n^2)$ instead of $O(n^3)$** — a frequent slip in 3-mark questions.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- [x] Floyd–Warshall is a **dynamic programming** algorithm for **All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP)**.
- [x] Master recurrence: $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\!\big(D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\big)$.
- [x] Time complexity: $\mathbf{O(n^3)}$; Space complexity: $\mathbf{O(n^2)}$.
- [x] Handles **negative edge weights** but **not negative cycles** (detected by $D[i][i] < 0$).
- [x] In VLSI CAD: used in **global routing**, **STA**, **TAM optimization**, and **FPGA routing**.
- [x] **Transitive closure** is the Boolean variant, used in **DRC** for connectivity checks.
- [x] **Predecessor matrix** $\Pi[i][j]$ is essential for **path reconstruction** in 14-mark problems.
- [x] Compared to Dijkstra (run $n$ times), Floyd–Warshall is **simpler to implement** and works on **dense** graphs efficiently.
- [x] Initialization: $D^{(0)}[i][j] = W[i][j]$, with $D^{(0)}[i][i] = 0$ and $D^{(0)}[i][j] = \infty$ for missing edges.
- [x] Final answer after $k = n$ iterations gives the **complete APSP matrix**.
- [x] KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes both **algorithmic steps** and **VLSI application context** — memorize at least 2 VLSI use-cases for 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Levels of Partitioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Levels of Partitioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: LEVELS OF PARTITIONING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Partitioning** in VLSI design is the process of dividing a complex circuit, system, or graph into a set of smaller, manageable sub-circuits (or subsets) such that the number of connections (cut-size) crossing the boundaries between partitions is minimized, while satisfying design constraints such as area, delay, power, and pin-count budgets.

In hierarchical design automation, **Levels of Partitioning** refers to the top-down recursive decomposition of a VLSI system into progressively smaller, self-contained physical and logical units, traversing from the system specification down to the leaf-level standard cells or transistors.

The canonical hierarchy recognized in the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEVT524) syllabus is:

| Level | Granularity | Typical Module Count | Partitioning Objective |
|-------|------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Level 1: System Level** | Algorithm / Functional Blocks | 1 – 10 | Architectural partitioning across ICs |
| **Level 2: Board / MCM Level** | ICs on a PCB / MCM | 5 – 50 | Component placement on substrate |
| **Level 3: Chip / Floor-Planning Level** | Macros / IP blocks | 10 – 500 | Floorplan block placement |
| **Level 4: Block / Sub-Chip Level** | Sub-modules / clusters | 50 – 5,000 | Logical block construction |
| **Level 5: Cell Level** | Standard cells / gates | 10³ – 10⁶ | Row/column placement |
| **Level 6: Transistor Level** | Devices / polygons | 10⁵ – 10⁹ | Mask-level geometry |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: "Designing a City"**
>
> Imagine you are an urban planner tasked with designing an entire metropolis. You wouldn't draw every brick of every house at once. Instead, you work **top-down**:
> - **System Level** → Decide that the city will have 5 distinct zones (residential, industrial, commercial, recreational, transport).
> - **Board Level** → Decide which major building (hospital, factory, school) goes into which zone.
> - **Chip Level** → Within the industrial zone, place the major factory buildings (assembly, warehouse, packaging).
> - **Block Level** → Inside the warehouse, allocate rooms (loading dock, storage racks, office).
> - **Cell Level** → Inside each room, place the standard shelves and desks.
> - **Transistor Level** → Finally, the geometry of every individual shelf, screw, and tile.
>
> **Partitioning** is the act of drawing the dashed lines ("cut-lines") between zones, buildings, and rooms — and the goal is always to **minimize traffic (wires) crossing these cut-lines**, while ensuring no room is over-crowded (area constraint) or has too many doors (pin constraint).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are universally accepted in the KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation for partitioning:

- **Cut-size (c)**: Number of hyperedges crossing the partition boundary.
- **Cut-set cost**: Total weighted cost of nets crossing the cut.
- **Balance factor (ρ)**: $\rho = \dfrac{|A|}{|V|}$, where $|A|$ is the size of partition A and $|V|$ is total vertex count.
- **Pins per partition (P_i)**: Total terminal count for the i-th partition; bounded by available routing resources.
- **Standard balance tolerance**: $\pm 10\%$ to $\pm 20\%$ of the average partition size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEEVT524 / Module 2):** The course explicitly categorizes partitioning into **System, Board, Chip, Block, and Cell levels**, each characterized by distinct objective functions, constraint sets, and algorithmic complexity classes.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition Logic

The VLSI design flow applies partitioning at every level with progressively finer granularity. Each level is governed by the **Y-chart** correspondence (Gajski-Kuhn): as we move down the partitioning hierarchy, the *behavioral domain* contracts, the *structural domain* expands, and the *physical domain* becomes more concrete.

#### Step 1 — System Level Partitioning (Top of the Hierarchy)

- **Granularity**: Algorithmic / functional specification.
- **Inputs**: RTL description, system constraints (throughput, latency, power budget).
- **Output**: Decision on **how many chips** are required; which algorithm runs on which chip; allocation of software (firmware) versus hardware (ASIC/FPGA).
- **Constraint Set**: Pin-out budget per package, die-area budget, I/O bandwidth.
- **Primary Cost Function**: Minimize inter-chip communication latency and inter-chip pin count.

#### Step 2 — Board / MCM Level Partitioning

- **Granularity**: Packaged ICs and discrete components on a Printed Circuit Board (PCB) or Multi-Chip Module (MCM).
- **Inputs**: Netlist of ICs, thermal map, signal integrity requirements.
- **Output**: Placement of ICs on the board; routing topology between packages.
- **Constraint Set**: Board area, thermal hot-spot limits ($\le 85^{\circ}\text{C}$ typical), EMI shielding zones.
- **Primary Cost Function**: Minimize wirelength between chips, balance thermal dissipation.

#### Step 3 — Chip / Floor-Plan Level Partitioning

- **Granularity**: Macros, hard IP blocks, large standard-cell clusters.
- **Inputs**: Hierarchical netlist, RTL synthesis results, I/O pad positions.
- **Output**: Floorplan (slicing tree, mosaic, or B*-tree representation).
- **Constraint Set**: Die area utilization ($\le 70\%-80\%$ for routing), aspect ratio, fixed-outline constraint.
- **Primary Cost Function**: Minimize total wirelength; minimize critical-path delay.

#### Step 4 — Block / Sub-Chip Level Partitioning

- **Granularity**: Hundreds to thousands of standard cells, grouped into functional blocks.
- **Inputs**: Flat netlist, timing constraints, area targets.
- **Output**: Logical blocks with internal cell clusters and external pin assignments.
- **Constraint Set**: Block area (multiples of standard cell row height), pin accessibility, routability.
- **Primary Cost Function**: Minimize inter-block cut-size, balance block areas.

#### Step 5 — Cell Level Partitioning

- **Granularity**: Standard cells (NAND, NOR, DFF, etc.) with uniform height.
- **Inputs**: Combinational + sequential netlist.
- **Output**: Rows of cells, each assigned to a placement site.
- **Constraint Set**: Row capacity, channel density, abutment rules.
- **Primary Cost Function**: Minimize total net wirelength (HPWL).

#### Step 6 — Transistor / Device Level Partitioning

- **Granularity**: Individual transistors, contacts, vias.
- **Inputs**: Transistor-level netlist (SPICE).
- **Output**: Geometric polygons on each mask layer.
- **Constraint Set**: Design rule checking (DRC) — minimum width, spacing, enclosure.
- **Primary Cost Function**: Minimize total silicon area, maximize yield.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Levels"?** The term "level" reflects the recursive nature of partitioning: at every level, a previously-aggregated module is decomposed into sub-modules. The same *min-cut* objective reappears, but the *graph type* evolves — from a coarse functional graph (System) to a fine hypergraph of cells (Cell) to a geometric polygon graph (Transistor).

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of the Generic Partitioning Problem

Let $G = (V, E)$ be a hypergraph with vertex set $V$ (representing modules) and hyperedge set $E$ (representing nets). A **k-way partition** is a mapping:

$$P : V \rightarrow \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$$

such that each vertex $v \in V$ is assigned to exactly one partition $V_i$, where $\bigcup_{i=1}^{k} V_i = V$ and $V_i \cap V_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$.

The **cut-size** $c(P)$ is defined as:

$$c(P) = \left| \left\{ e \in E \;\middle|\; \exists\, v_i, v_j \in e \text{ with } P(v_i) \neq P(v_j) \right\} \right|$$

That is, $c(P)$ counts the number of hyperedges that span at least two different partitions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Levels of Partitioning (PEEVT524 Module 2)**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Cut-size (graph) | $c = \vert E_{\text{cut}} \vert$ | count | Number of edges spanning partitions |
| 2 | Cut-size (hypergraph) | $c = \vert \{ e \in E \mid e \cap V_i \neq \emptyset \text{ and } e \cap V_j \neq \emptyset\} \vert$ | count | A net is "cut" if its pins lie in 2+ parts |
| 3 | Balance factor (2-way) | $\rho = \dfrac{\vert A \vert}{\vert V \vert}$ | unitless | Ideal: $\rho = 0.5$ |
| 4 | Balance ratio (k-way) | $r_i = \dfrac{\vert V_i \vert}{\vert V \vert / k}$ | unitless | $r_i \in [1-\epsilon,\ 1+\epsilon]$ |
| 5 | Min-cut objective | $\min\; c(P) = \min \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}$ | count | NP-hard for $k \ge 2$ |
| 6 | Ratio cut (RC) | $RC = \dfrac{c}{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}$ | $1/\text{count}$ | Favors balanced partitions |
| 7 | Normalized cut (Ncut) | $Ncut = \dfrac{c}{d_A} + \dfrac{c}{d_B}$ | $1/\text{degree}$ | Used in spectral methods |
| 8 | Scaled cost | $\Phi = c + \lambda \cdot (\rho - 0.5)^2$ | count | $\lambda$ = balance penalty weight |
| 9 | Kernighan–Lin gain | $\Delta g(v) = D_{\text{ext}}(v) - D_{\text{int}}(v)$ | count | Used in iterative improvement |
| 10 | FM gain (vertex move) | $g(v) = FS(v) - TE(v)$ | count | Fiduccia–Mattheyses bucket-based |
| 11 | Rent's Rule | $T = K \cdot B^{p-1}$ | count | $T$ = external pins, $B$ = block size, $p$ = Rent exponent |
| 12 | Wirelength bound | $L_{\max} \le R \cdot t_{pd}$ | $\mu m$ / $ns$ | $R$ = driver resistance, $t_{pd}$ = delay target |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Partitioning at every level directly drives the **Quality of Results (QoR)** of the final GDSII layout:

- **System-Level** partitioning decisions determine whether a design fits in 1 chip or 4 chips, dictating the Bill of Materials (BOM) cost and PCB complexity.
- **Chip-Level** partitioning directly influences the routability — a poorly partitioned chip will have 20–40% greater congestion.
- **Cell-Level** partitioning is the foundation of timing closure; an imbalanced partition creates long wires that exceed clock-period timing budgets.

In industry EDA flows (Synopsys ICC2, Cadence Innovus, Mentor Olympus), the partitioning engine is invoked **after synthesis** and **before placement**, making it a critical convergence point for area, timing, and power optimization.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Min-Cut Objective from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define the cut function for a single net.**

Let $e = \{v_1, v_2, v_3\}$ be a 3-pin net. If the partition $P$ assigns:
- $P(v_1) = 1,\ P(v_2) = 1,\ P(v_3) = 2$, then net $e$ is **cut** (it spans partitions 1 and 2).

Define the indicator:
$$x_e = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } e \text{ is cut} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all nets to get total cut-size.**

$$c(P) = \sum_{e \in E} x_e$$

**Step 3 — Express $x_e$ using the partition assignment vector.**

For each vertex $v \in V$, define a binary label $y_v \in \{0, 1\}$ (for 2-way partition). A net $e$ is cut if and only if there exist $u, v \in e$ with $y_u \neq y_v$. Equivalently:

$$x_e = 1 - \prod_{v \in e} \mathbb{I}[y_v = y_{v^*}]$$

where $v^*$ is an arbitrarily chosen reference vertex of $e$ and $\mathbb{I}$ is the indicator function.

**Step 4 — Linearize for tractability (Lagrangian relaxation in KL).**

Kernighan and Lin (1970) reformulated the cut-minimization as a **swap gain**:

$$\Delta g(a, b) = D_{\text{ext}}(a) + D_{\text{ext}}(b) - 2 \cdot c(a, b)$$

where:
- $D_{\text{ext}}(v)$ = number of external edges incident on $v$ (i.e., edges from $v$ to the *other* partition).
- $D_{\text{int}}(v)$ = number of internal edges incident on $v$ (i.e., edges from $v$ to the *same* partition).
- $c(a, b) = 1$ if $a$ and $b$ are connected, else $0$.

**Step 5 — Algorithmic gain computation.**

The move of swapping $a \in A$ and $b \in B$ changes the cut-size by exactly $\Delta g(a, b)$. We then iteratively pick the pair with maximum $\Delta g$, lock them, and update the gain values for the unlocked vertices. This is the foundation of the **Kernighan–Lin (KL) algorithm**, which runs in $O(n^3)$ time in its classical form.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM) Improvement

**Step 1 — Single-vertex move (vs. pair swap).**

FM generalizes KL to allow moving a single vertex from $A$ to $B$ (or vice versa) to handle the case where $\vert A \vert \neq \vert B \vert$. The gain of moving $v$ is:

$$g(v) = FS(v) - TE(v)$$

where:
- $FS(v)$ = **F**rom-**S**ide: number of nets that would become *internal* after moving $v$ (i.e., nets that have *all other* pins on the destination side).
- $TE(v)$ = **T**o-**E**xternal: number of nets that would become *cut* after moving $v$.

**Step 2 — Bucket-based data structure for $O(1)$ maximum-gain selection.**

Since $g(v) \in [-P_{\max}, +P_{\max}]$ where $P_{\max}$ is the max net size, we maintain $2P_{\max}+1$ doubly-linked lists (buckets). Insertion/deletion of a vertex into a bucket is $O(1)$, so the maximum-gain vertex can be retrieved in $O(1)$.

**Step 3 — Roll-back mechanism for the pass.**

All moves in one "pass" are tentative. After completing the pass (where $n$ moves have been made), the cumulative cut-sizes at each move step are computed, and the prefix with the **minimum** cumulative cut is retained; the remaining moves are rolled back.

**Step 4 — Total time complexity.**

$$T_{\text{FM}} = O(P_{\max} \cdot n) \cdot (\text{number of passes})$$

Typically, FM converges in 2–5 passes, making it linear in $n$ for practical circuits. This is why FM remains the workhorse of modern partitioners.

### 3.3 Derivation of Rent's Rule (a Key Tool in Partitioning)

**Step 1 — Empirical observation (Landman & Russo, 1971).**

For a wide range of ICs, the number of external pins $T$ of a logic block is empirically related to the number of gates $B$ inside the block via:

$$T = K \cdot B^{p-1}$$

where $K$ is a technology-dependent constant and $p$ is the **Rent exponent**, $0 \le p \le 1$.

**Step 2 — Interpretation.**

- $p$ close to 1 → high interconnection (the block needs many pins to communicate outward).
- $p$ close to 0 → low interconnection (the block is self-contained).

**Step 3 — Use in partitioning.**

The Rent exponent $p$ is used in **wirelength estimation** during hierarchical placement and in the **planning of pin count** for chip-level partitioning. The total wirelength of a placement can be estimated as:

$$L_{\text{total}} = K_{\text{tech}} \cdot \sum_{i} B_i^{p}$$

where the sum is taken over all blocks $B_i$. This Donath-style estimate is often used to drive the cost function of cell-level and block-level partitioners.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Partitioning Levels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD COMPARISON TABLE — Levels of Partitioning**

| Level | Module Granularity | Graph Type | Algo. Complexity | Tool / Language | Main Heuristic |
|-------|--------------------|------------|------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| System | Functional blocks | Coarsened DAG | NP-hard | SystemC, MATLAB | Hardware/Software split |
| Board | ICs on PCB | Bipartite | NP-hard | Cadence Allegro | Force-directed |
| Chip | Macros | Hypergraph | NP-hard | Synopsys ICC, Innovus | Min-cut + simulated annealing |
| Block | Sub-blocks | Hypergraph | NP-hard | Cadence Genus | FM, MLPart |
| Cell | Standard cells | Hypergraph | NP-hard | Capo, FengShui | MLPart, hMetis |
| Transistor | Devices | Geometric | Polynomial | Magic, Electric | Euler paths, compaction |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Partitioning Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    SYS["Level 1: System Partitioning"]:::lvl1
    BRD["Level 2: Board / MCM Partitioning"]:::lvl2
    CHP["Level 3: Chip / Floorplan Partitioning"]:::lvl3
    BLK["Level 4: Block Partitioning"]:::lvl4
    CEL["Level 5: Cell Partitioning"]:::lvl5
    TRN["Level 6: Transistor Partitioning"]:::lvl6

    SYS --> BRD
    BRD --> CHP
    CHP --> BLK
    BLK --> CEL
    CEL --> TRN

    SYS --> S1["Objective: Multi-chip allocation"]:::obj
    BRD --> B1["Objective: Component placement"]:::obj
    CHP --> C1["Objective: Macro floorplan"]:::obj
    BLK --> L1["Objective: Logical block synthesis"]:::obj
    CEL --> M1["Objective: Standard cell rows"]:::obj
    TRN --> T1["Objective: Mask polygons"]:::obj

    classDef lvl1 fill:#1a3d6b,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef lvl2 fill:#2e6da4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef lvl3 fill:#4a8fc7,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef lvl4 fill:#6fb1e0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lvl5 fill:#9ad0f0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lvl6 fill:#cfe8fb,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef obj fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Generic Partitioning Algorithm

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["Input: Netlist G = V, E"]:::stage
    A1["Read constraints: area, pin, balance"]:::stage
    A2["Choose k-way target"]:::stage
    A3["Initial constructive partition: random or connectivity-based"]:::stage
    A4["Compute cut-size c"]:::stage
    A5["Iterative improvement: KL or FM"]:::stage
    A6["Update gains via bucket data structure"]:::stage
    A7["Pass termination check: c_k = c_k-1?"]:::stage
    A8["Output: Partitioned netlist with min cut"]:::stage

    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7
    A7 -- "No, improve" --> A5
    A7 -- "Yes, converged" --> A8

    classDef stage fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — A 2-Way Cut on a Small Netlist

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1((v1)):::partA
    V2((v2)):::partA
    V3((v3)):::partA
    V4((v4)):::partB
    V5((v5)):::partB
    V6((v6)):::partB

    N1([Net N1]):::net
    N2([Net N2]):::net
    N3([Net N3]):::net
    N4([Net N4]):::net

    V1 --- N1
    V2 --- N1
    V4 --- N1

    V2 --- N2
    V3 --- N2
    V5 --- N2

    V3 --- N3
    V6 --- N3

    V4 --- N4
    V5 --- N4
    V6 --- N4

    CUT["Cut: N1, N2, N3, cut-size = 3"]:::cut

    classDef partA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef partB fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#aa8800,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef cut fill:#ffe680,stroke:#aa6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Vertices v1, v2, v3 belong to Partition A (red); v4, v5, v6 belong to Partition B (green). The four nets N1, N2, N3, N4 are drawn in yellow. Net N4 lies entirely inside Partition B (uncut), while N1, N2, N3 each have pins in both partitions and therefore contribute to the cut. The cut-size is **3**.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> *Define the levels of partitioning in VLSI design. List the six standard levels in hierarchical order with one-line objectives for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **levels of partitioning** describe the top-down hierarchical decomposition of a VLSI system into progressively smaller units. The six standard levels are:

1. **System Level** — Decides multi-chip allocation and hardware/software split.
2. **Board / MCM Level** — Decides placement of ICs on PCB/MCM.
3. **Chip / Floorplan Level** — Decides macro and IP block placement on the die.
4. **Block Level** — Clusters standard cells into logical blocks.
5. **Cell Level** — Places standard cells in rows.
6. **Transistor / Geometry Level** — Generates the mask polygons.

> **Marking Note:** Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the list with objectives.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> *Distinguish between constructive partitioning and iterative improvement partitioning. Give one example algorithm for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Constructive Partitioning | Iterative Improvement |
|--------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| **Strategy** | Builds an initial partition from scratch | Starts with an initial guess and refines it |
| **Speed** | Fast (linear/polynomial) | Slower but higher quality |
| **Output Quality** | Suboptimal | Near-optimal |
| **Example** | Random partitioning, greedy growth | Kernighan–Lin (KL), Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM) |

> **Marking Note:** 1 mark for each of the four cells + 1 mark for the examples.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Question Pattern**

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **six levels of partitioning** in VLSI design, indicating the granularity of modules and the primary objective at each level. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Formulate the **min-cut bipartition problem** as a combinatorial optimization. State the NP-hardness of the problem and outline the **Kernighan–Lin (KL) algorithm** with its gain computation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

[Refer to Section 2.1 of these notes and the Mermaid hierarchy in Section 4.1.]

The six levels in hierarchical order are:

| Level | Granularity | Primary Objective |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| System | Algorithm / RTL blocks | Multi-chip allocation, HW/SW split |
| Board | Packaged ICs | Component placement on substrate |
| Chip | Macros / IP blocks | Floorplan quality (wirelength, area) |
| Block | Sub-modules | Logical clustering of cells |
| Cell | Standard cells | Row-based placement |
| Transistor | Devices | Geometry & mask generation |

*(Full credit: 1 mark for the diagram + 1 mark per level with its objective = 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Problem Formulation (2 marks):**

Given a hypergraph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k = 2$, find a partition $P : V \to \{A, B\}$ such that:
- (Balance) $\big| \vert A \vert - \vert B \vert \big| \le 1$ (or within a tolerance $\epsilon$).
- (Objective) $c(P) = \sum_{e \in E_{\text{cut}}} w(e)$ is minimized.

This is the **Min-Cut Bipartition Problem** and is **NP-hard** (proven by reduction from Max-Cut / Minimum Bisection).

**Step 2 — Kernighan–Lin Algorithm (3 marks):**

1. Start with an arbitrary initial partition $(A, B)$.
2. Compute the **gain** $\Delta g(v) = D_{\text{ext}}(v) - D_{\text{int}}(v)$ for every $v \in A \cup B$.
3. Select the unlocked pair $(a, b)$ with $a \in A$, $b \in B$ that **maximizes** $\Delta g = D_{\text{ext}}(a) + D_{\text{ext}}(b) - 2 c(a, b)$.
4. Lock $(a, b)$; move $a \to B$ and $b \to A$; update the $\Delta g$ of unlocked neighbors.
5. After $n/2$ pairs are selected and tentatively moved, compute the cumulative cut-size after each step. Roll back all moves after the prefix of minimum cumulative cut.
6. Repeat until no improvement is obtained.

**Step 3 — Complexity (1 mark):**

Classical KL runs in $O(n^2 \log n)$ per pass; full convergence takes $O(n^3)$ worst-case.

**Step 4 — Modern Usage (1 mark):**

KL is rarely used directly today; it is the basis for **FM (Fiduccia–Mattheyses)**, which runs in $O(P_{\max} \cdot n)$ and is the workhorse in EDA tools (hMetis, MLPart, UPlace).

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (FM) algorithm** in detail. Discuss how it improves upon Kernighan–Lin, the role of the **bucket data structure**, and the **roll-back** mechanism. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **system-level, chip-level, and cell-level partitioning** with respect to objective, constraints, and algorithms used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Algorithmic Motivation (1 mark):**

KL swaps *pairs* and requires $|A| = |B|$. FM generalizes to **single-vertex moves**, allowing $|A| \neq |B|$ and supporting **balance constraints** naturally.

**Step 2 — Gain Definition (1 mark):**

$$g(v) = FS(v) - TE(v)$$

where $FS$ = From-Side (nets becoming internal), $TE$ = To-External (nets becoming cut).

**Step 3 — Bucket Data Structure (2 marks):**

Since $g(v) \in [-P_{\max}, +P_{\max}]$, FM maintains $2P_{\max} + 1$ doubly-linked lists (buckets) indexed by gain. Selecting the max-gain vertex is $O(1)$. After a move, the affected vertices are updated and re-bucketed in $O(1)$ amortized. This yields the **linear-time** complexity of FM.

**Step 4 — Roll-Back Mechanism (2 marks):**

All moves in one pass are tentative. After the pass, the algorithm records the cumulative cut-size after each move. The partition state is **rolled back** to the prefix with the minimum cumulative cut. This avoids being trapped in a local minimum that arises mid-pass.

**Step 5 — Convergence (1 mark):**

FM typically converges in 2–5 passes for circuits with $n \le 10^6$ cells. Total time: $O(P_{\max} \cdot n \cdot p)$ where $p$ = number of passes.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

| Criterion | System Level | Chip Level | Cell Level |
|-----------|-------------|------------|------------|
| **Granularity** | Algorithms / RTL blocks | Macros / IP blocks | Standard cells (10³ – 10⁶) |
| **Primary Objective** | Minimize inter-chip comm. cost, decide HW/SW | Minimize floorplan wirelength, balance die area | Minimize HPWL, balance row density |
| **Key Constraints** | I/O pin budget, package cost | Die area utilization, aspect ratio | Row capacity, channel density |
| **Graph Representation** | Functional DAG | Coarse hypergraph | Detailed hypergraph |
| **Typical Algorithm** | HW/SW partitioning, integer programming | Min-cut + simulated annealing, B*-tree | hMetis, MLPart, FM-based |
| **EDA Tool Examples** | SystemC, MATLAB partitioning | Synopsys ICC2, Cadence Innovus | Cadence Encounter, Capo |
| **Time Horizon in Flow** | Pre-RTL / pre-synthesis | Post-synthesis, pre-placement | Post-floorplan, placement stage |

*(Full credit: 1 mark per row × 7 rows = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "Levels of Partitioning"**
>
> 1. **Confusing "levels" with "stages of the design flow".** Students often write "specification → RTL → synthesis → layout" as levels. These are *stages*, not levels of partitioning. The correct terminology is *granularity levels* (System → Board → Chip → Block → Cell → Transistor).
>
> 2. **Omitting the constraint set at each level.** Many answers list levels but fail to mention what the *balance constraint* and *objective function* are at each level. The KTU rubric explicitly awards 1 mark per level for stating the objective.
>
> 3. **Confusing the Kernighan–Lin gain with the FM gain.** KL gain involves a *pair* $D_{\text{ext}}(a) + D_{\text{ext}}(b) - 2c(a,b)$; FM gain is a *single-vertex* $FS - TE$. Examiners allocate 1 full mark to this distinction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the roll-back mechanism in FM.** The "tentative moves + cumulative cut + roll-back to best prefix" is the most-tested sub-concept; missing it costs 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Writing "graph" instead of "hypergraph".** In real VLSI circuits, a net may have more than 2 pins. The correct object to partition is a **hypergraph**, not a simple graph. Examiners specifically test this awareness.
>
> 6. **Not stating NP-hardness.** Always state that min-cut partition is **NP-hard** and reference the use of heuristics (FM, KL, simulated annealing). Forgetting this is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Levels of Partitioning (PEEVT524 / Module 2)**

- ☐ **Six Levels**: System → Board → Chip → Block → Cell → Transistor (in hierarchical order).
- ☐ **Object Hierarchy**: HW/SW split → component placement → floorplan → logical clustering → row placement → mask geometry.
- ☐ **Hypergraph Formulation**: $G = (V, E)$, partition $P: V \to \{1,\ldots,k\}$, cut $c(P) = |\{e \in E : e \text{ spans 2+ parts}\}|$.
- ☐ **Min-Cut is NP-hard** for $k \ge 2$; heuristic algorithms are mandatory.
- ☐ **Kernighan–Lin (1970)**: Pair-swap, $O(n^3)$, foundation of modern iterative partitioners.
- ☐ **Fiduccia–Mattheyses (1982)**: Single-vertex move, $O(P_{\max} \cdot n)$, bucket data structure for max-gain selection, **roll-back** to best prefix.
- ☐ **Gain Definitions**: KL: $\Delta g = D_{\text{ext}}(a) + D_{\text{ext}}(b) - 2c(a,b)$. FM: $g(v) = FS(v) - TE(v)$.
- ☐ **Rent's Rule**: $T = K \cdot B^{p-1}$; used for wirelength estimation and pin planning.
- ☐ **Ratio Cut & Normalized Cut**: Bias the objective toward balanced partitions; used in spectral methods.
- ☐ **Balance Factor**: $\rho = |A| / |V|$; ideal $\rho = 0.5$; typical tolerance $\pm 10\%$.
- ☐ **EDA Tools**: Synopsys ICC2, Cadence Innovus, Mentor Olympus, Capo, hMetis, MLPart, UPlace.
- ☐ **Key Takeaway**: Every level uses the same min-cut principle but operates on a different graph type and constraint set — the algorithm scales from a coarse 10-node functional graph (System) to a 10⁶-node hypergraph (Cell).
- ☐ **Real-world impact**: A 1% improvement in cut-size at the chip level typically reduces total wirelength by 2–4% and improves timing closure by 5–10 ps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameters for Partitioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parameters for Partitioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Partitioning** is the fundamental pre-placement step in the VLSI physical design automation flow wherein a large, complex circuit (modeled as a hypergraph $\mathcal{H}(V, E)$) is recursively decomposed into a smaller number of disjoint, balanced sub-circuits or blocks such that the inter-block connectivity (routing requirement) is minimized while respecting hard constraints such as area, terminal count, and timing budgets.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme module structure, **Parameters for Partitioning** are the explicit mathematical variables and objective functions that define the *quality*, *feasibility*, and *optimality* of a partitioning solution. The standard input model used in the KTU reference literature is the **circuit graph** or **netlist hypergraph**, where:

- $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ represents the set of **cells** (standard cells, gates, macros).
- $E = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_m\}$ represents the set of **nets** (signal connections). A net $e_k$ may connect two or more cells (multi-terminal net).

The objective is to partition $V$ into $k$ disjoint subsets $\{V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_k\}$ such that the **partitioning cost** is minimized subject to a set of constraints.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Planning a Mega City"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Imagine designing a metropolitan city.** Before construction begins, the city planner divides the entire land into **zones** (residential, commercial, industrial). Each zone has constraints: a maximum population (area cap), a maximum number of highways connecting outward (terminal cap), and the goal is to place people who frequently interact (connected nets) inside the *same* zone so that long commutes (inter-block wires) are avoided.

**Mapping the analogy to VLSI partitioning:**

| City Planning Concept | VLSI Partitioning Parameter |
|---|---|
| Zone population cap | **Capacity constraint** (max cells per block) |
| Number of outgoing highways | **Terminal constraint** (IO pin limit per block) |
| Long-distance commuters | **Cut-size** (wires crossing partition boundary) |
| Number of zones | **Number of parts (k)** in $k$-way partitioning |

---

## 1.3 The Core Partitioning Objective

The canonical objective function for bipartitioning ($k = 2$) is:

$$
\min_{V_1, V_2} \quad \Phi(V_1, V_2) = \sum_{e \in E} c(e) \cdot \chi(e)
$$

where:
- $c(e)$ is the **cost/weight** of net $e$.
- $\chi(e)$ is the **cut-indicator function**, equal to **1** if net $e$ has terminals in both $V_1$ and $V_2$, and **0** otherwise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the evaluation weight for partitioning questions is typically tagged to **CO2** (Apply VLSI design automation algorithms to model circuit partitioning problems). Examiners reward students who explicitly state both the *objective function* and the *constraint set*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Decomposition of Partitioning Parameters

The parameters governing any partitioning instance can be classified into **three families**, each serving a distinct role in the optimization formulation.

### 2.1.1 Family 1 — Geometric / Cardinality Parameters

These define the **structural shape** of the partition.

1. **Number of Parts ($k$):** Specifies whether the problem is 2-way, 3-way, or $k$-way. In ESE questions, $k = 2$ (bipartitioning) and $k = 4$ (quad-partitioning) are the most common.
2. **Partition Sizes ($\vert V_i \vert$):** The number of cells assigned to block $i$. KTU examiners typically test the **balance ratio** $r$, defined as:

$$
r = \frac{\min_i \vert V_i \vert}{\max_i \vert V_i \vert}
$$

A perfect balance yields $r = 1$. A tolerance of $r \in [0.9, 1.0]$ is the industry standard.

---

### 2.1.2 Family 2 — Connectivity / Cost Parameters

These measure the **quality** of the cut.

3. **Cut-size ($\mathcal{C}$):** The total number of nets crossing the partition boundary. For bipartitioning:

$$
\mathcal{C}(V_1, V_2) = \sum_{e \in E \cap \text{cuts}} 1
$$

4. **Weighted Cut-size ($\mathcal{C}_w$):** Each net is weighted by its electrical/physical cost (e.g., driver strength, net criticality):

$$
\mathcal{C}_w(V_1, V_2) = \sum_{e \in \text{cuts}} c(e)
$$

5. **Maximum Cut-size per Net (for multi-terminal nets):** A net with $p$ terminals spanning $q$ different partitions contributes $\binom{q}{2}$ cut edges in a multi-terminal model. The **Sanchis multi-terminal cut model** gives:

$$
\chi_{\text{multi}}(e) = \binom{q(e)}{2} = \frac{q(e)\bigl(q(e) - 1\bigr)}{2}
$$

where $q(e)$ is the number of blocks containing at least one terminal of net $e$.

---

### 2.1.3 Family 3 — Feasibility / Constraint Parameters

These define **hard constraints** that any valid partition must satisfy.

6. **Capacity Constraint:** Each block $V_i$ must satisfy:

$$
\vert V_i \vert \leq C_{\max}
$$

where $C_{\max}$ is the maximum allowable cell count (set by area budget).

7. **Terminal Constraint:** Each block $V_i$ may connect to at most $T_{\max}$ external pins:

$$
T(V_i) = \bigl\vert \{v \in V_i \mid v \text{ is an IO cell}\} \bigr\vert + \bigl\vert \{e \in E \mid e \cap V_i \neq \emptyset \text{ and } e \cap \overline{V_i} \neq \emptyset\} \bigr\vert \leq T_{\max}
$$

8. **Maximum External Degree ($D_{\max}$):** Upper bound on the number of external connections for any single block.

9. **Timing / Path-length Constraint:** Critical paths must not be split across partitions, otherwise wire delay exceeds the clock period.

10. **Power-density Constraint:** Power dissipation per partition must stay below the cooling budget.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table** — every formula here has appeared (or will appear) in a KTU ESE paper under Module 2.

| # | Parameter | Mathematical Formulation | Constraint Type | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cut-size** | $\mathcal{C} = \sum_{e \in E} \chi(e)$ | Objective | Minimizes global wirelength |
| 2 | **Weighted Cut-size** | $\mathcal{C}_w = \sum_{e} c(e)\,\chi(e)$ | Objective | Prioritizes critical nets |
| 3 | **Multi-terminal cut** | $\chi_{\text{multi}} = \binom{q}{2}$ | Objective | Models hypergraph nets |
| 4 | **Balance ratio** | $r = \min \vert V_i \vert \,/\, \max \vert V_i \vert$ | Constraint | Ensures uniform block area |
| 5 | **Capacity** | $\vert V_i \vert \leq C_{\max}$ | Hard constraint | Prevents overflow |
| 6 | **Terminal cap** | $T(V_i) \leq T_{\max}$ | Hard constraint | Bounded IO per block |
| 7 | **External degree** | $D(V_i) = \sum_{v \in V_i} d_{\text{ext}}(v)$ | Constraint | Routing congestion control |
| 8 | **Absorption / absorption ratio** | $\alpha = \dfrac{\text{nets with all terminals inside } V_i}{\text{nets touching } V_i}$ | Quality metric | Measures block internalization |

> **Notation note:** In the table above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes cardinality of a set. In exam answers, write it explicitly as "cardinality of $V_i$" or use $\mid V_i \mid$ to avoid markdown pipe conflicts.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hierarchical Design:** Partitioning enables top-down VLSI design — large SoCs are split into IP blocks, allowing parallel design teams to work concurrently.
- **Multi-FPGA Prototyping:** A design exceeding a single FPGA capacity is partitioned across multiple FPGAs connected by a board-level interconnect.
- **Chip-Package-Board Co-Design:** Determines which gates go on-chip vs. in-package vs. on-board.
- **Yield Optimization:** Smaller, balanced blocks improve manufacturing yield by reducing defect probability.
- **Thermal Management:** Power-density partitioning prevents hotspots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From Set Partitioning to the Bipartitioning Objective

We start from the general problem of partitioning a hypergraph $\mathcal{H}(V, E)$ into $k$ subsets. For simplicity, we restrict ourselves to $k = 2$ (bipartitioning), which is the canonical KTU Module 2 formulation.

### Step 1 — Define the Universe

Let $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ be the set of all cells. We seek a partition $(V_1, V_2)$ such that:

$$
V_1 \cup V_2 = V \quad \text{and} \quad V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Cut-Indicator Function

For any net $e \in E$, define $\chi: E \to \{0, 1\}$ by:

$$
\chi(e) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } e \text{ has terminals in both } V_1 \text{ and } V_2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Logic:** A net is *cut* if and only if it spans both partitions — this is the defining property used in every KTU bipartitioning question.

### Step 3 — Compose the Objective Function

The cut-size $\mathcal{C}$ is the sum of all cut indicators:

$$
\mathcal{C}(V_1, V_2) = \sum_{e \in E} \chi(e)
$$

**Logic:** Each cut net contributes one unit to the total. To minimize wire crossings between blocks, we minimize $\mathcal{C}$.

### Step 4 — Incorporate the Balance Constraint

To prevent degenerate solutions (e.g., one empty partition), impose:

$$
\Bigl\lvert\,\vert V_1 \vert - \vert V_2 \vert\,\Bigr\rvert \leq \rho \cdot \vert V \vert
$$

where $\rho \in [0, 1]$ is the **allowed imbalance ratio** (commonly $\rho = 0.1$, i.e., 10% tolerance).

### Step 5 — The Final Constrained Optimization

$$
\boxed{\min_{V_1, V_2} \mathcal{C}(V_1, V_2) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \Bigl\lvert\,\vert V_1 \vert - \vert V_2 \vert\,\Bigr\rvert \leq \rho \cdot \vert V \vert}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Multi-Terminal Net Cut Contribution (Sanchis Model)

### Step 1 — Setup

Suppose a net $e$ has $p = 6$ terminals distributed across $q = 3$ different partitions ($V_1, V_2, V_3$). How many "cuts" does this net contribute?

### Step 2 — Pair Counting Argument

In the multi-terminal model, a net is considered *cut* whenever any two of its terminals lie in different partitions. The number of unordered terminal-pairs across two partitions is $\binom{p}{2}$, but the pair is cut only if the pair spans different partitions.

For $q$ partitions containing at least one terminal each, the net induces connections between every pair of partitions. The number of partition-pairs is:

$$
N_{\text{pairs}} = \binom{q}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Assign Cost

If each partition-pair is considered a cut, the contribution of net $e$ is:

$$
\chi_{\text{multi}}(e) = \binom{q}{2} = \frac{q(q-1)}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

Let $q = 4$ (a net spans four partitions).

$$
\chi_{\text{multi}} = \binom{4}{2} = \frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6
$$

**Logic verification:** $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ unordered pairs of partitions: $(1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (2,3), (2,4), (3,4)$. Each represents a possible cut route, so the net adds 6 to the total weighted cut-size. This is **exactly** the derivation KTU examiners expect for a multi-terminal net question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Trade-off Between Cut-size and Balance

The fundamental tension in partitioning is illustrated by this analytical matrix:

| Strategy | Cut-size $\mathcal{C}$ | Balance $r$ | Block Area Utilization | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Random split** | High ($\approx m/2$) | Poor ($r \approx 0$) | Unequal | ❌ Infeasible |
| **Naive greedy** | Medium | Medium | Moderate | ⚠️ Local minimum |
| **Kernighan-Lin (iterative swap)** | Low | High | Equal | ✅ Exam answer |
| **Optimal (exact)** | Minimum | Perfect ($r = 1$) | Uniform | ❌ NP-hard, intractable |
| **Skewed** | Very low | Deliberately bad | One block huge | ✅ Used in hierarchical design |

> **Take-away:** KTU exam answers must always pair an *objective* (cut-size) with a *constraint* (balance / capacity). A common valuation pitfall is mentioning only the objective and forgetting the constraint — this costs 2 to 3 marks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Kernighan-Lin Gain Function

The Kernighan-Lin algorithm uses a **gain** function $\Delta g$ for swapping two cells $v_a \in V_1$ and $v_b \in V_2$:

$$
\Delta g = \bigl[\text{external cost of } v_a \text{ to } V_2\bigr] + \bigl[\text{external cost of } v_b \text{ to } V_1\bigr] - 2 \cdot c(v_a, v_b)
$$

### Step-by-step derivation:

**Step 1:** Compute the external cost $E(v_a)$ = number of nets touching $v_a$ whose other terminals lie *outside* $V_1$ (i.e., in $V_2$). If we move $v_a$ from $V_1$ to $V_2$, those nets become internal — cut decreases by $E(v_a)$.

**Step 2:** Similarly, the internal cost $I(v_a)$ = number of nets that are fully inside $V_1$ via $v_a$. Moving $v_a$ to $V_2$ cuts these — cut increases by $I(v_a)$.

**Step 3:** Net change in cut-size from moving $v_a$ alone:

$$
\Delta \mathcal{C}(v_a) = I(v_a) - E(v_a)
$$

**Step 4:** Similarly for $v_b$:

$$
\Delta \mathcal{C}(v_b) = I(v_b) - E(v_b)
$$

**Step 5:** If $v_a$ and $v_b$ are connected by a net $e_{ab}$ of weight $c(v_a, v_b)$, and that net was previously cut, swapping them *uncuts* it, contributing an additional $2 \cdot c(v_a, v_b)$ benefit (the net was counted in $E(v_a)$ and $E(v_b)$, both are now wrong by 1, but reclassification gives double count).

**Step 6:** Total gain:

$$
\Delta g = \Delta \mathcal{C}(v_a) + \Delta \mathcal{C}(v_b) - 2 \cdot c(v_a, v_b)
$$

**Logic:** A positive $\Delta g$ means the swap reduces cut-size — keep it. A negative $\Delta g$ means it increases cut — but K-L still records it temporarily to escape local minima. The cumulative maximum $\Delta g_{\max}$ over the pass defines the swap sequence to commit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The K-L gain equation above is worth **up to 7 marks** in a 14-mark Part-B question if asked "explain the Kernighan-Lin algorithm." Students must derive $\Delta g$ explicitly — drawing the formula without derivation scores partial credit at best.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Partitioning Flow in VLSI Design Automation

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture** showing where partitioning sits in the full physical design flow and how its parameters interact with downstream stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RTL Netlist Input] --> B[Logic Synthesis]
    B --> C[Gate Level Netlist V and E]
    C --> D[Partitioning Stage]
    D --> D1[Parameter 1 Number of Parts k]
    D --> D2[Parameter 2 Capacity Constraint]
    D --> D3[Parameter 3 Cut-size Objective]
    D --> D4[Parameter 4 Terminal Constraint]
    D1 --> E[Block Level Sub netlists]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Floorplanning]
    F --> G[Placement]
    G --> H[Clock Tree Synthesis]
    H --> I[Global Routing]
    I --> J[Detailed Routing]
    J --> K[GDSII Layout Output]
    D3 --> L[Feedback for Iterative Refinement]
    L --> D
```

## 4.2 Partitioning Parameter Interaction Map

This diagram shows how the three parameter families (Geometric, Connectivity, Feasibility) interact to form a valid partition.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEOM[Geometric Parameters]
        G1[Number of Parts k]
        G2[Partition Sizes Vi]
    end
    subgraph CONN[Connectivity Parameters]
        C1[Cut size C]
        C2[Weighted Cut size Cw]
        C3[Multi terminal Cut]
    end
    subgraph FEAS[Feasibility Constraints]
        F1[Capacity Cmax]
        F2[Terminal Tmax]
        F3[Max External Degree Dmax]
        F4[Timing Path Length]
        F5[Power Density]
    end
    G1 --> PART[Partition Solution]
    G2 --> PART
    C1 --> PART
    C2 --> PART
    C3 --> PART
    F1 --> PART
    F2 --> PART
    F3 --> PART
    F4 --> PART
    F5 --> PART
    PART --> OUT[Minimized Cutsize with Satisfied Constraints]
```

## 4.3 Bipartitioning Input/Output Schematic

The following diagram illustrates a concrete bipartitioning instance. Input: a hypergraph with 6 cells and 5 nets. Output: two blocks ($V_1$, $V_2$) with the cut set highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Hypergraph H V E]
        N1[Cell A]
        N2[Cell B]
        N3[Cell C]
        N4[Cell D]
        N5[Cell E]
        N6[Cell F]
        NET1((Net 1 A B C))
        NET2((Net 2 A D))
        NET3((Net 3 B E))
        NET4((Net 4 C D F))
        NET5((Net 5 E F))
    end
    N1 --- NET1
    N2 --- NET1
    N3 --- NET1
    N1 --- NET2
    N4 --- NET2
    N2 --- NET3
    N5 --- NET3
    N3 --- NET4
    N4 --- NET4
    N6 --- NET4
    N5 --- NET5
    N6 --- NET5
    INPUT --> PROC[Partitioning Algorithm]
    PROC --> V1[Block V1 contains A B C]
    PROC --> V2[Block V2 contains D E F]
    V1 -.Cut Net 1.-> V2
    V1 -.Cut Net 2.-> V2
    V1 -.Cut Net 4.-> V2
    PROC -.Cut size 3.--> RESULT[Output Bipartition]
```

> **Reading guide:** In the diagram above, dashed red arrows represent **cut nets** (those crossing the partition boundary). The cut-size is **3** (nets 1, 2, and 4 are cut; nets 3 and 5 remain internal).

## 4.4 Cut-size vs Balance Trade-off Schematic

This block diagram contrasts a "min-cut, balanced" solution with a "min-cut, imbalanced" solution — the classic KTU conceptual trap.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SCEN[Partitioning Instance] --> Q{Trade off Decision}
    Q -->|Balanced| B1[k equals 2 and ratio r close to 1]
    Q -->|Imbalanced| B2[Unequal block sizes allowed]
    B1 --> P1[Minimize Cut size with Capacity Constraint]
    B2 --> P2[Aggressively minimize Cut size ignoring Balance]
    P1 --> R1[Cut size equals 8 and Balance equals 0.95]
    P2 --> R2[Cut size equals 4 and Balance equals 0.4]
    R1 --> USECASE1[Used for Multi FPGA and Chip Partitioning]
    R2 --> USECASE2[Used for Hierarchical Design and IO Grouping]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical note:** When the KTU examiner asks "explain the role of balance constraint," the answer must contrast **both** scenarios shown above. A unidirectional answer mentioning only the balanced case loses marks.
