> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Students often write "FM is O(n)" in exams. The correct answer is **O(P) per pass**, where P is the **number of pins**, not the number of cells. This distinction is worth full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the FM Algorithm

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the *control flow* and *data-structure interactions* of the FM partitioning kernel. This is the most-tested diagram type in KTU 14-mark FM questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Hypergraph H of V,N] --> A1[Parse Netlist and Build Cell-to-Net and Net-to-Cell Indexes]
    A1 --> A2[Initial Random or Constructive Bipartition into A and B]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Initial Gains g_v for All Free Cells]
    A3 --> A4[Distribute Free Cells into Bucket Array Indexed by Gain]
    A4 --> A5[Set Base Cell Pointer to Highest Non-Empty Bucket]
    A5 --> B1[Begin Pass Loop: Move Counter k = 0]
    B1 --> B2[Select Base Cell v from Highest Non-Empty Bucket]
    B2 --> B3{Balance Constraint Satisfied if v Moves?}
    B3 -- No --> B4[Skip v and Move to Next Highest Bucket]
    B4 --> B2
    B3 -- Yes --> B5[Lock v and Record Move Sequence Entry M_k]
    B5 --> B6[Update Cutsize: C = C - g_v]
    B6 --> B7[For Each Net n Incident to v, Recompute Criticality of n]
    B7 --> B8[For Each Unlocked Cell u in Net n, Update Gain g_u]
    A8[Re-bucket u into New Gain Bucket] --> A9[Continue until All Cells Locked or No Legal Move]
    B8 --> A8
    A9 --> C1[End of Pass: Move Sequence M_1 to M_n Complete]
    C1 --> C2[Compute Cumulative Delta-Cutsize G_k = Sum g_M_i for i = 1 to k]
    C2 --> C3[Find Best Prefix k-star = arg max G_k]
    C3 --> C4[Undo Moves M_k-star+1 to M_n by Reversing Partition Assignment]
    C4 --> D1{Cutsize Improved or Limit Reached?}
    D1 -- Yes --> A3
    D1 -- No --> D2[Output Final Bipartition A, B]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — The Pass / Rollback / Iterate Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialization
    Initialization --> BucketFill: Compute initial gains and balance
    BucketFill --> CellSelection: Pick highest-gain unlocked cell
    CellSelection --> BalanceCheck: Check balance constraint
    BalanceCheck --> CellSelection: Skip if imbalanced, return to bucket
    BalanceCheck --> CellMove: Lock and move cell
    CellMove --> NeighborUpdate: Update gains of all neighbor cells
    NeighborUpdate --> CellSelection: Continue if cells remain
    CellSelection --> Rollback: All cells moved OR no legal move
    Rollback --> IterCheck: Find best prefix and revert tail
    IterCheck --> Initialization: Re-run if improvement found
    IterCheck --> [*]: Stable or limit reached
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bucket Array Data Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BucketArray [Gain-Indexed Bucket Array]
        B_neg2[Bucket g = minus 2]
        B_neg1[Bucket g = minus 1]
        B_zero[Bucket g = 0]
        B_pos1[Bucket g = plus 1]
        B_pos2[Bucket g = plus 2]
        B_max[Bucket g = plus P max]
    end
    Selector[FM Move Selector Pointer] --> B_max
    B_max --> CellA[Cell A, g = plus P max]
    B_max --> CellB[Cell B, g = plus P max]
    B_pos2 --> CellC[Cell C, g = plus 2]
    B_pos1 --> CellD[Cell D, g = plus 1]
    Selector -- Picks head of highest non-empty bucket --> CellA
    CellA -. After move, neighbor Cell D re-bucketed .-> B_pos1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the bucket diagram:** The selector pointer always points to the rightmost (highest-gain) non-empty bucket. After Cell A is moved, all of its neighbors have their gains recomputed, and each is re-bucketed — possibly into a different bucket. This is the **O(1) selection + O(P) total re-bucketing** mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) bipartitioning algorithm. List any three of its advantages over the Kernighan-Lin (K-L) algorithm.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
The Fiduccia-Mattheyses algorithm is an iterative, move-based bipartitioning heuristic for hypergraphs that minimizes the cutsize between two partitions while respecting a balance constraint. Unlike K-L, it moves **one cell at a time** (not a pair), uses a **bucket-based priority queue** to select the highest-gain cell in O(1) time, and operates in **O(P) per pass** where P is the number of pins.
**Three advantages over K-L:**
1. **Hypergraph support** — handles nets with more than two pins natively.
2. **Single-cell move granularity** — finer control and better local minima escape.
3. **Tolerant of imbalance** — supports a balance tolerance $\beta$, unlike K-L's strict 50:50 split.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
**What are *free cells* and *critical (terminal) cells* in the FM algorithm? Why is the distinction important?**

**Model Answer (8 lines):**
A **free cell** is a cell that is currently *unlocked* and is a candidate to be moved in the current pass. A **critical cell** (or *terminal cell*) is a cell whose movement would cause one of its incident nets to change its cut-status (i.e., a net that is on the cut, with the cell as its only remaining pin on one side). The distinction matters because **only the gains of free cells are kept in the bucket structure**, and the gain update upon a move is restricted to the *unlocked* neighbors sharing the affected nets. This decoupling gives FM its O(P) efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **not** confuse the **gain of a cell** $g(v)$ with the **delta-cutsize of a move** $\Delta C$. They are the *same number* for the moved cell, but the **cumulative** $\Delta C_k$ is the *sum* of individual gains, and the **best prefix** $k^*$ is where the cumulative is maximized, **not** where the last individual gain is positive.
> - Do **not** claim FM is O(n). The correct bound is **O(P) per pass**.
> - When asked to *compare* FM and K-L, **always** mention that K-L swaps *pairs* while FM moves *single* cells.
> - The bucket is **doubly-linked** so that removal and re-insertion are O(1) — many students forget this.

---

### Question A. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Choice 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example hypergraph, **explain the Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) algorithm in detail**, clearly defining the **gain function**, the **bucket data structure**, and the **balance constraint**. Show one full pass on your example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** **Derive the time complexity** of the FM algorithm per pass, and **prove** that the cutsize monotonically non-increases across passes (i.e., the best-prefix rollback guarantees no worsening of the solution across iterations).

#### Model Solution for (a)

Consider a small hypergraph with **5 cells** $\{a, b, c, d, e\}$ and **4 nets** $n_1 = \{a, b, c\}$, $n_2 = \{a, d\}$, $n_3 = \{b, d, e\}$, $n_4 = \{c, e\}$. Initial balanced bipartition: $A = \{a, b, c\}$, $B = \{d, e\}$. The current cutsize is $C = 3$ (nets $n_2, n_3, n_4$ are on the cut; $n_1$ is internal to $A$).

**Step 1 — Compute initial gains for each cell:**

For cell $a$: nets $\{n_1, n_2\}$. $n_1$ is internal to $A$ ⇒ ignored. $n_2$ is on the cut, with $a$ as the only A-pin ⇒ $n_2 \in C(a)$, so $C(a) = 1, F(a) = 0$. Hence $g(a) = 0 - 1 = -1$.

For cell $b$: nets $\{n_1, n_3\}$. $n_1$ internal ⇒ ignored. $n_3$ is on the cut, with $b$ as the only A-pin (d, e in B) ⇒ $C(b) = 1, F(b) = 0$. $g(b) = -1$.

For cell $c$: nets $\{n_1, n_4\}$. $n_1$ internal ⇒ ignored. $n_4$ is on the cut, with $c$ as the only A-pin (e in B) ⇒ $g(c) = -1$.

For cell $d$: nets $\{n_2, n_3\}$. $n_2$ on cut, $d$ is the only B-pin (a in A) ⇒ $C(d) = 1, F(d) = 0$. $g(d) = -1$.

For cell $e$: nets $\{n_3, n_4\}$. Both on cut, $e$ is the only B-pin in each ⇒ $g(e) = -2$.

**Step 2 — Buckets:** All cells have negative gain. With perfect balance (3 vs. 2 is off by one, so we can move one cell from A to B), only one cell can move from A → B in the first move. The highest-gain A-cell is any with $g = -1$ (tie broken arbitrarily); suppose we pick $a$. Move $a$ to $B$. New $A = \{b, c\}$, $B = \{a, d, e\}$.

**Step 3 — Recompute cutsize:** $n_1 = \{a, b, c\}$ now has $a \in B$ and $b, c \in A$ ⇒ **on the cut (was internal)**. $n_2 = \{a, d\}$: both in $B$ ⇒ **internal (was on cut)**. $n_3 = \{b, d, e\}$: $b \in A$, $d, e \in B$ ⇒ **still on the cut**. $n_4 = \{c, e\}$: $c \in A$, $e \in B$ ⇒ **still on the cut**. New cutsize: $C = 3$ (nets $n_1, n_3, n_4$). No improvement; we continue. The pass terminates with a **best prefix** found at the move that gave the lowest cumulative $\Delta C$.

**Explanation of the gain function (formal):** $g(v) = F(v) - C(v)$, where $F(v)$ counts free nets that would become internal, and $C(v)$ counts critical nets (on the cut, $v$ being the only pin on its current side) that would, upon $v$'s movement, become cut-nets on the other side.

**Explanation of the bucket structure:** A doubly-linked list of $2p_{\max}+1$ buckets indexed by signed gain, allowing O(1) extraction of the maximum-gain cell.

**Explanation of the balance constraint:** At any prefix $k$ of the move sequence, the partition sizes must satisfy $\left\lvert \mid A_k \mid - \mid B_k \mid \right\rvert \le \beta \cdot \mid V \mid$.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Time complexity derivation:**
- Initial gain computation scans all pins: $O(P)$.
- Each move triggers a gain update of all neighbors, which collectively scans all pins of affected nets. Because each net is touched at most once per pass (its cells become locked), total update work is $O(P)$.
- Move selection is $O(1)$ per move via the bucket.
- Rollback to best prefix scans the move sequence once: $O(n) = O(P)$ in dense graphs.
- Total: $T_{\text{pass}} = O(P) + O(P) + O(n) = O(P)$.

**Proof of non-increasing cutsize across passes:**
Let $C^{(t)}$ be the cutsize at the end of pass $t$. The FM algorithm:
1. Records the cumulative $\Delta C_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g(M_i)$ for all $k = 1, \ldots, n$.
2. Selects $k^* = \arg\min_k C^{(t-1)} + \Delta C_k$ (equivalently, $\arg\max_k (-\Delta C_k)$).
3. Commits moves $M_1, \ldots, M_{k^*}$ and undoes $M_{k^*+1}, \ldots, M_n$.

The new cutsize is $C^{(t)} = C^{(t-1)} + \max_k \Delta C_k$. Since $\max_k \Delta C_k \ge 0$ (because $k=0$ gives $\Delta C_0 = 0$ which is always a valid prefix), we have $C^{(t)} \le C^{(t-1)}$. Thus $C^{(t)}$ is a non-increasing sequence bounded below by $0$, and therefore it converges in finite iterations. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Choice 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Kernighan-Lin** and **Fiduccia-Mattheyses** bipartitioning algorithms along **at least six** dimensions. Justify why FM is preferred for VLSI netlists.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the netlist below with cell sizes $s(v) = 1$ for all $v$ and a balance tolerance $\beta = 0.1$, perform **one pass** of the FM algorithm starting from the initial partition $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $B = \{4, 5, 6\}$. Show the bucket state after each move.

| Net | Cells |
| :---: | :--- |
| $n_1$ | 1, 2, 3 |
| $n_2$ | 1, 4 |
| $n_3$ | 2, 4, 5 |
| $n_4$ | 3, 5, 6 |
| $n_5$ | 2, 6 |

#### Model Solution for (a)

| Dimension | Kernighan-Lin (K-L, 1970) | Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM, 1982) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Graph type** | Undirected graph (2-pin nets only) | Hypergraph (multi-pin nets supported) |
| **Move primitive** | Pairwise swap of two vertices | Single-vertex move |
| **Balance** | Strict 50:50 | Tolerates imbalance $\beta$ |
| **Per-pass complexity** | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(P)$ per pass |
| **Cell sizes** | Uniform only | Individual cell weights allowed |
| **Selection data structure** | Gain arrays sorted periodically | Bucket-based priority queue, O(1) max |
| **Locking** | Both vertices in a swap are locked | Only the moved cell is locked |
| **Rollback** | Best swap prefix | Best move prefix |

**Why FM is preferred for VLSI:** VLSI nets routinely have 5–50 pins, not 2. K-L would require *clique expansion* or *star expansion*, blowing up the graph. FM handles hyperedges natively. VLSI partitions are also rarely perfectly 50:50 — area, IO, and pre-placed macros force imbalance, which K-L forbids. Finally, VLSI netlists have $P \gg n$, so FM's $O(P)$ scaling is the only practical choice for million-cell modern designs.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Initial state:** $A = \{1,2,3\}$, $B = \{4,5,6\}$, cutsize $C = 5$ (all nets are on the cut).

**Initial gain computation:**

| Cell | Nets | Net status (F=free, C=critical) | $g(v)$ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $n_1, n_2$ | $n_1$ on cut, 1 is one of three A-pins ⇒ F. $n_2$ on cut, 1 is the only A-pin ⇒ C. | $1 - 1 = 0$ |
| 2 | $n_1, n_3, n_5$ | $n_1$ F (2 is one of three A-pins). $n_3$ on cut, 2 only A-pin ⇒ C. $n_5$ on cut, 2 only A-pin ⇒ C. | $1 - 2 = -1$ |
| 3 | $n_1, n_4$ | $n_1$ F. $n_4$ on cut, 3 only A-pin ⇒ C. | $1 - 1 = 0$ |
| 4 | $n_2, n_3$ | $n_2$ C, $n_3$ C. | $0 - 2 = -2$ |
| 5 | $n_3, n_4$ | $n_3$ C, $n_4$ C. | $0 - 2 = -2$ |
| 6 | $n_4, n_5$ | $n_4$ C, $n_5$ C. | $0 - 2 = -2$ |

**Initial bucket state:** $\text{Bucket}(+1) = \emptyset$, $\text{Bucket}(0) = \{1, 3\}$, $\text{Bucket}(-1) = \{2\}$, $\text{Bucket}(-2) = \{4, 5, 6\}$.

**Move 1:** Pick cell 1 (highest-gain A-cell, $g=0$). Balance: A becomes $\{2,3\}$, B becomes $\{1,4,5,6\}$ — within $\beta = 0.1 \cdot 6 = 0.6$? No, imbalance = $|3-3| = 0$, fine. Lock 1.

- Recompute nets of 1: $n_1, n_2$.
  - $n_1$: was F (multi-pin in A), now has $a=1 \in B$ and $2,3 \in A$ ⇒ **still on cut, but now F-type for A-side**.
  - $n_2$: was C for 1 (only A-pin), now $\{1, 4\}$ both in B ⇒ **internal**.
- Recompute gains of unlocked neighbors of 1 (i.e., cells 2, 3 in $n_1$; and 4 in $n_2$, but 4 is in B and was $-2$):
  - Cell 2 in $n_1$: $n_1$ was F, remains F (still on cut) ⇒ no gain change for $g(2)$? But cell 2's $n_5$ status also unchanged. So $g(2)$ stays $-1$.
  - Cell 3 in $n_1$: same as 2, $g(3)$ stays $0$.
  - Cell 4 in $n_2$: $n_2$ went from cut to internal ⇒ $g(4)$ goes from $-2$ to $-1$.

**Cumulative $\Delta C$:** $G_1 = 0$.

**Move 2:** Highest-gain unlocked A-cell is 3 ($g=0$). Balance check: moving 3 to B gives A = $\{2\}$, B = $\{1, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$ — imbalance = $|1-5| = 4 \gg 0.6$ ⇒ **SKIP** due to balance violation. Try next: cell 2 ($g = -1$). Move 2 to B gives A = $\emptyset$, B = $\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$ — imbalance = 6 ≫ 0.6 ⇒ **SKIP**. No legal A→B move possible; try B→A: cells 4, 5, 6 have $g = -1$ (or whatever their updated gains are). Highest is 4 at $g = -1$. Move 4 to A: A = $\{1, 4\}$, B = $\{2, 3, 5, 6\}$. Imbalance = $|2-4| = 2 \gg 0.6$ ⇒ **SKIP**. With $\beta = 0.1$, no move is legal except cell 1. Pass terminates with best prefix $k^* = 1$.

**Result:** Best prefix undoes the move and we re-bucket. The algorithm reports $C^{(1)} = 5$ (no improvement). A second pass would be tried, etc.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often *forget to apply the balance constraint* and greedily move every cell. Always check $\mid \mid A \mid - \mid B \mid \mid \le \beta \cdot \mid V \mid$ **before** locking the move.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] FM is a **move-based** (not swap-based) **hypergraph bipartitioner** for VLSI netlists.
- [x] Move primitive = **single vertex**, locked after moving.
- [x] **Gain function:** $g(v) = F(v) - C(v)$, where $F$ = free nets, $C$ = critical nets.
- [x] **Bucket data structure** is a doubly-linked list of $2p_{\max}+1$ buckets; O(1) max extraction.
- [x] **Time complexity per pass = O(P)**, where P = total pins (NOT O(n)).
- [x] **Best-prefix rollback** at end of each pass ensures the cutsize is **non-increasing** across passes.
- [x] **Free cell** = unlocked candidate; **Critical/Terminal cell** = its move changes a net's cut-status.
- [x] **Balance constraint:** $\mid \mid A \mid - \mid B \mid \mid \le \beta \cdot \mid V \mid$, with $\beta \in [0, 0.5]$.
- [x] FM allows **unbalanced** partitions; K-L does not.
- [x] FM supports **individual cell sizes/weights**; K-L requires uniform weights.
- [x] **Cumulative delta-cutsize:** $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g(M_i)$; best prefix $k^* = \arg\max_k G_k$.
- [x] **Gain update** is restricted to **unlocked neighbors** of the moved cell, keeping the per-pass cost linear.
- [x] FM is the **kernel** of modern multi-level partitioners (hMetis, MLPart, KaHyPar).
- [x] Always re-bucket cells after updating their gain — most KTU full-mark answers mention this explicitly.
- [x] The algorithm terminates when the cutsize stabilizes or a pass limit is reached.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layout Layers and Design Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Layout Layers and Design Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Layout in VLSI Design — The Physical Blueprint

In VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration) physical design, the **layout** is the geometric representation (top-down mask-level view) of an integrated circuit. It is a set of **closed, non-overlapping polygons** drawn on different **mask layers** that, when projected onto a silicon wafer through photolithographic processes, fabricate the transistors, interconnections, and passive structures that realize the schematic netlist.

A layout is the **final, fabrication-ready artefact** that bridges the logical (gate-level) world and the physical (manufacturing) world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEEVT524 Module 3):**
> "Layout consists of coloured geometric patterns on different mask layers. Each colour corresponds to a specific fabrication step such as diffusion, polysilicon, contact cut, or metal interconnect. Design rules are the geometric constraints (width, spacing, enclosure) that ensure these patterns are manufacturable with acceptable yield."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Architectural Blueprint

Imagine a **multi-storey building**:
- The **architectural drawing (schematic)** shows *what* rooms exist and *how* they are connected (living room → kitchen → bedroom).
- The **structural blueprint (layout)** shows *exact dimensions* of every wall, beam, pillar, pipe, and wire, layer by layer (foundation layer, brick layer, plumbing layer, electrical layer).

Similarly:
- **Schematic** = connectivity / logic.
- **Layout** = physical geometry on layered masks.
- **Design Rules** = building codes (minimum wall thickness, minimum pipe spacing, beam-to-pillar overlap).

If you violate a building code (e.g., a wall thinner than 15 cm), the building *might* collapse — likewise, violating design rules produces chips that *might* fail.

### 1.3 Why Multiple Layers Are Required

A single layer cannot fabricate a CMOS transistor because transistor formation requires **multiple sequential process steps** — each photolithographic step corresponds to one mask layer. Hence the layout is drawn as a stack of differently-coloured polygons, each colour encoding a specific mask.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Set of Mask Layers in a Generic n-Well CMOS Process:**
> - **n-well** — defines the deep n-type tub where PMOS transistors are built.
> - **Active (Thinox / Diffusion)** — defines the thin-oxide regions where transistors are formed.
> - **n-select (n+ implant)** — heavy n-type doping inside active regions (NMOS source/drain & n+ contacts to active).
> - **p-select (p+ implant)** — heavy p-type doping inside active regions (PMOS source/drain & p+ contacts to active).
> - **Polysilicon (Poly)** — gate electrode material; also used for short interconnects.
> - **Contact (Cut)** — opens a hole in the inter-layer dielectric so Poly/Active can connect to Metal-1.
> - **Metal-1** — first level of aluminum interconnect.
> - **Via** — connects Metal-1 to Metal-2.
> - **Metal-2** — second level of aluminum interconnect.
> - **Overglass / Pad** — opens for bonding pads.

Each mask layer is essentially a **2-D polygon mask** drawn in the layout editor (Magic, Cadence Virtuoso, Electric, etc.) using a unique colour code.

### 1.4 Design Rules — The Geometric Contract Between Designer and Fab

**Design Rules** specify the **minimum allowed geometry** (widths, spacings, enclosures, extensions) of the polygons on each mask layer. They are the **interface specification** between the circuit designer and the fabrication foundry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Primary Design-Rule Philosophies:**
>
> 1. **Lambda (λ) Rules (Mead & Conway, 1979)** — All dimensions expressed as multiples of a single parameter **λ**. Rules are *scalable*: as process improves and λ shrinks, the entire rule set shrinks proportionally. Used for educational and quick designs.
>
> 2. **Micron (Absolute) Rules** — Dimensions expressed in absolute micrometers (µm). Captures finer, non-uniform process constraints (e.g., minimum poly width = 0.18 µm, metal spacing = 0.20 µm). Used in industry (e.g., MOSIS SCMOS, TSMC, Intel process design kits).

### 1.5 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Width** | Minimum dimension of a polygon on a given layer. |
| **Spacing** | Minimum distance between two polygons of the *same* layer. |
| **Enclosure** | Minimum overlap of one layer over a hole/contact in another layer. |
| **Extension** | Minimum protrusion of one layer beyond another layer. |
| **λ (Lambda)** | Half of the minimum feature size of the process; unit of scaling in λ-rules. |
| **DRC (Design Rule Check)** | EDA tool that verifies the layout obeys all geometric rules. |
| **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)** | EDA tool that verifies the layout's connectivity matches the schematic. |

### 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Process Metrics (Bold for Emphasis):**
> - **λ = (minimum feature size) / 2**. Example: for 0.5 µm process, **λ = 0.25 µm**.
> - **Channel length L** of minimum-size transistor ≈ **2λ**.
> - **Minimum n+/p+ spacing** (to prevent latch-up) = **4λ – 6λ** depending on process.
> - **Contact cut size** ≈ **2λ × 2λ**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomy of a CMOS Layout — The Inverter as a Worked Example

Consider a CMOS inverter with NMOS in the p-substrate and PMOS in an n-well. The layout consists of the following geometric elements:

1. **n-well** — a large rectangular tub drawn on the **n-well** layer, typically of minimum size such that all active regions of PMOS transistors fit inside with sufficient enclosure.
2. **Active regions** — two rectangular regions drawn on the **active** layer. One lies inside the n-well (forms PMOS), the other lies outside in p-substrate (forms NMOS).
3. **Polysilicon gate** — a poly rectangle crossing each active region. Where poly crosses active, a transistor is formed.
4. **Select implants** — **n-select** over the NMOS active + n+ contact regions; **p-select** over the PMOS active + p+ contact regions. Select layers typically overhang the active by 1λ to ensure complete doping.
5. **Contacts** — square cuts on the **contact** layer, landing partially on active and partially on poly (or fully on metal for via).
6. **Metal-1** — horizontal interconnect wires joining drains of NMOS and PMOS to form the output, and bringing GND/VDD to sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb (Mead & Conway):** Coloured regions of the *same* layer **may not overlap**; regions of *different* layers *may* overlap, and the overlap has a specific fabrication meaning (e.g., n+ over active → source/drain implant).

## 2.2 Stick Diagram — The Quick-Design Notation

A **stick diagram** is an abstract, un-scaled representation of a layout. It uses lines of different colours to denote different mask layers. It is faster to draw than a full layout and is excellent for capturing connectivity in early design.

**Stick diagram colour code (Mead & Conway convention):**

| Colour | Layer |
|--------|-------|
| 🟢 Green | n-diffusion (n-select over active) |
| 🟡 Yellow | p-diffusion (p-select over active) |
| 🔴 Red | Polysilicon (gate) |
| 🔵 Blue | Metal-1 |
| ⚫ Black | Contact cut |
| 🟣 Magenta (outline) | n-well border |
| 🟤 Brown | p-select |
| 🟢 Light Green | n-select |

> [!NOTE]
> Stick diagrams omit absolute dimensions — they only show topology and connectivity. They are converted to layouts by applying λ-rules and choosing minimum dimensions for each layer.

## 2.3 Lambda-Based Design Rules — The Mead & Conway Rule Set

The classic **λ-based design rules** (Mead & Conway, *"Introduction to VLSI Systems"*, 1980) abstract a wide variety of CMOS processes into a single scalable parameter **λ**.

### 2.3.1 Core Lambda Rules (SCMOS Subset)

The following table lists the canonical λ-rules used for SCMOS (Scalable CMOS) processes. All distances are in **λ units**.

| # | Rule | Symbol | Value |
|---|------|--------|-------|
| 1 | Minimum **active width** (n+ or p+) | Wₐ | **2λ** |
| 2 | Minimum **active-to-active spacing** (same type) | Sₐₐ | **3λ** |
| 3 | Minimum **active-to-active spacing** (different type, i.e., n+ to p+) | Sₐₐ_diff | **4λ** |
| 4 | Minimum **poly width** | Wₚ | **2λ** |
| 5 | Minimum **poly-to-poly spacing** | Sₚₚ | **2λ** |
| 6 | Minimum **gate extension of poly beyond active** | Eₚₐ | **2λ** |
| 7 | Minimum **active extension beyond poly (gate)** | Eₐₚ | **3λ** |
| 8 | Minimum **poly-to-active spacing** (outside transistor) | Sₚₐ | **1λ** |
| 9 | Minimum **metal width** | Wₘ | **3λ** |
| 10 | Minimum **metal-to-metal spacing** | Sₘₘ | **3λ** |
| 11 | Minimum **contact size** | C | **2λ × 2λ** |
| 12 | Minimum **active overlap of contact** | Eₐ_¢ | **1λ** |
| 13 | Minimum **poly overlap of contact** | Eₚ_¢ | **1λ** |
| 14 | Minimum **metal overlap of contact** | Eₘ_¢ | **1λ** |
| 15 | Minimum **contact-to-contact spacing** (same layer) | S_¢_¢ | **2λ** |
| 16 | Minimum **contact-to-poly spacing** (in active region) | S_¢_ₚ | **2λ** |
| 17 | Minimum **contact-to-gate spacing** (along active) | S_¢_g | **2λ** |
| 18 | Minimum **well overlap of active** | E_w_a | **3λ** |
| 19 | Minimum **n-select overhang of active** | E_n_a | **2λ** |
| 20 | Minimum **p-select overhang of active** | E_p_a | **2λ** |
| 21 | Minimum **n-select to p-select spacing** | S_np | **4λ** |

### 2.3.2 Why These Specific Numbers?

- **Active width = 2λ**: ensures the n+/p+ region fully covers the channel and source/drain regions even with mask misalignment up to λ/2.
- **n+ to p+ spacing = 4λ**: prevents the formation of a parasitic lateral p-n-p-n thyristor (latch-up) by ensuring a wide, lightly-doped substrate region between them.
- **Poly extension over active = 2λ**: ensures complete coverage of the thin-oxide region under the gate even with misalignment.
- **Active extension beyond poly = 3λ**: ensures the source/drain regions overlap the gate even with misalignment, forming good ohmic contacts.

## 2.4 Micron (Absolute) Design Rules

Industrial processes use **absolute micron rules** because each layer has its own lithographic constraints independent of others. Example — TSMC 0.18 µm CMOS process (representative subset):

| Rule | Value |
|------|-------|
| Min. poly width | 0.18 µm |
| Min. metal-1 width | 0.23 µm |
| Min. metal-1 spacing | 0.23 µm |
| Min. contact size | 0.22 × 0.22 µm |
| Min. active width | 0.30 µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Absolute rules are **non-uniform** — the scaling is not simply λ-based. They encode detailed process realities like separate metal-1 vs metal-2 lithography.

## 2.5 Euler's Rule for Layout Verification

A layout drawn on coloured layers can be represented as a **planar graph**. Each vertex is a junction where layers meet; each edge is a polygon side; each face is an enclosed region (or the outside).

> [!NOTE]
> **Euler's Formula for Connected Planar Graphs:**
>
> $$V - E + F = 2$$
>
> Where:
> - **V** = number of **vertices** (transistor terminals, contacts, layer intersections).
> - **E** = number of **edges** (graph edges between vertices — typically 3 per transistor in the stick-graph sense: source, gate, drain, with appropriate interconnects).
> - **F** = number of **faces** (regions enclosed by polygons), including the outer (infinite) face.

### 2.5.1 Application to VLSI Layouts — The 3-Edge Rule

For a properly-designed CMOS layout, the Euler relation is used in a simplified form:

$$F = E - V + 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Edge Rule (Baker & Terman):**
> Every transistor in the stick diagram must have **exactly 3 edges** incident on its terminals when the stick graph is properly formed. The complete stick graph (with one vertex per "stuck-at" terminal connection) must satisfy:
>
> - Each transistor has **3 edges**.
> - The graph is **planar** (no crossing wires — wires may not cross on the same metal layer).
>
> If a layout violates these rules, it indicates either an **open circuit** (graph disconnected) or a **short circuit** (graph has an unwanted cycle through a transistor).

### 2.5.2 Worked Example — CMOS Inverter Stick Graph

For a CMOS inverter, the stick graph contains:
- 2 transistors (NMOS + PMOS) → 6 edges total.
- 1 input (gate) vertex connected to both gates → shared.
- 1 output vertex connecting both drains → shared.
- 2 supply vertices (VDD, GND).

So **V = 4** (input, output, VDD, GND), **E = 6**, and **F = E − V + 2 = 4**. The 4 faces are: NMOS channel, PMOS channel, n+ source region, p+ source region.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Lambda definition | $\lambda = L_{min}/2$ | µm |
| Minimum channel length | $L = 2\lambda$ | µm |
| Minimum active width | $W_a = 2\lambda$ | µm |
| Minimum poly width | $W_p = 2\lambda$ | µm |
| Minimum metal width | $W_m = 3\lambda$ | µm |
| n+ to p+ spacing | $S_{np} = 4\lambda$ | µm — anti-latchup |
| Well overlap of active | $E_{wa} = 3\lambda$ | µm |
| Contact cut size | $C = 2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ | µm² |
| Active enclosure of contact | $E_{a\_c} = 1\lambda$ | µm |
| Poly enclosure of contact | $E_{p\_c} = 1\lambda$ | µm |
| Metal enclosure of contact | $E_{m\_c} = 1\lambda$ | µm |
| Euler relation | $V - E + F = 2$ | dimensionless |
| Faces from edges & vertices | $F = E - V + 2$ | dimensionless |
| Three-edge rule per transistor | $E_{transistor} = 3$ | count |
| Transistor aspect ratio (W/L) | $W/L = (n\lambda)/(2\lambda) = n/2$ | unitless |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering the SCMOS rule of thumb:**
> "**2** for active & poly, **3** for metal & wide, **4** for n+/p+."
> This covers most asked values in KTU exam short-questions.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DRC sign-off** in industry (Synopsys IC Validator, Mentor Calibre, Cadence Pegasus) uses absolute rule decks with thousands of rules.
- **Portability**: λ-rules let an academic design be migrated across generations (e.g., from 1.2 µm to 0.6 µm by halving λ).
- **Latch-up prevention**: the **4λ n+/p+ spacing** is the foundation of the foundry's anti-latchup design manual.
- **Yield maximisation**: tighter-than-minimum geometries yield poorly because of photolithographic variations. Design rules encode statistical yield considerations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From a Schematic to a λ-Scaled Layout (CMOS Inverter)

We shall **derive the minimum bounding-box dimensions** of a minimum-size CMOS inverter layout using the λ-rule table from Section 2.3.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify Active Regions

For minimum-size NMOS and PMOS (W/L = 2λ/2λ each, so n=1 multiplier), each transistor's active region is **W = 2λ** wide.

Both NMOS and PMOS are placed side by side. The active regions extend along the vertical direction (height of the diffusion).

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Compute Vertical Height (H)

Vertical dimensions needed:

1. Contact region for source (top): requires **1λ** for active enclosure of contact above + **2λ** contact height + **1λ** below = **4λ** total vertical span at the source end.

2. Channel region: minimum poly-extension = **2λ** above the channel + active-extension-beyond-poly = **3λ** below the channel = **2λ + 2λ + 3λ = 7λ** (gate crosses 2λ-wide active, poly sticks out 2λ above and below).

3. Contact region for drain (middle, shared output): another **4λ** vertical span (top 1λ enclosure + 2λ contact + 1λ enclosure).

Adding:
$$
H = 4\lambda \;(\text{source contact}) + 7\lambda \;(\text{gate region}) + 4\lambda \;(\text{drain contact})
$$
$$
H = 15\lambda
$$

> So the **minimum vertical extent** of the active regions is **15λ**.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute Horizontal Width (W_total)

Horizontal layout (left to right): NMOS active (2λ wide) + spacing to PMOS active.

- NMOS active width: **2λ**.
- Spacing between n+ active and p+ active (different type) inside the same layout, considering the **n+/p+ spacing** S_np = **4λ** (between the closest n+ and p+ doping edges — but the active regions themselves must satisfy the geometry).
- A more realistic accounting for a side-by-side active pair with a separating substrate contact gives a horizontal pitch ≈ **12λ – 14λ** in a typical inverter.

A standard textbook result is **W_total ≈ 12λ** for the width when both transistors are aligned horizontally.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute n-Well Enclosure

The PMOS active sits inside an n-well. The well must enclose the PMOS active by **3λ** on all sides.

$$
\text{Well width} = W_{PMOS} + 2 \cdot 3\lambda = 2\lambda + 6\lambda = 8\lambda
$$
$$
\text{Well height} = H_{active} + 2 \cdot 3\lambda = 15\lambda + 6\lambda = 21\lambda
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Compute Final Bounding Box

Including well enclosure on both sides horizontally (the well only covers PMOS side; NMOS is in p-substrate without an explicit well for a single-well process):

$$
\text{Total layout width} \approx W_{NMOS} + S_{np} + W_{PMOS} + 2 \cdot E_{wa} = 2\lambda + 4\lambda + 2\lambda + 6\lambda = 14\lambda
$$
$$
\text{Total layout height} = 21\lambda
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Euler's Rule Application to a NAND Gate

Consider a 2-input NAND gate: 2 NMOS in series (pull-down), 2 PMOS in parallel (pull-up). Let us count V, E, F in its stick graph.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Identify Vertices

Vertices are electrical nodes:
- V1 = input A, V2 = input B, Vout = output node, VDD, GND = supply rails, and internal series node between the two NMOS transistors.

Total: **V = 6** vertices.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Identify Edges

Each transistor contributes **3 edges** (S, G, D). We have 4 transistors → **4 × 3 = 12 edges**, but shared terminals reduce the count:
- Both NMOS share gate with PMOS inputs → already accounted as vertices, not new edges.
- Series NMOS share one diffusion node → that node is already a vertex; only its connections count.

After consolidation, **E = 12** edges in the stick graph.

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Compute Faces

$$
F = E - V + 2 = 12 - 6 + 2 = 8
$$

The 8 faces correspond to: 4 transistor channels + 4 supply/diffusion inner faces. This confirms the stick graph is consistent — no open or short circuits.

## 3.3 Derivation: Why n+/p+ Spacing Must Be ≥ 4λ — A Latch-Up Argument

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Identify the Parasitic Structure

Adjacent n+ in p-sub and p+ in n-well form a **parasitic p-n-p-n thyristor**: p+ (source of lateral pnp) → n-well → p-sub → n+ (source of lateral npn). If this thyristor turns on, VDD shorts to GND through low impedance — destroying the chip (latch-up).

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Current-Gain Condition for Latch-Up

The thyristor latches when $\beta_{pnp} \cdot \beta_{npn} \geq 1$. Both betas depend on the **base width** of the lateral transistors, which equals the spacing between n+ and p+.

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Minimum Spacing to Ensure $\beta < 1$

The lateral base width $W_B$ must be sufficient that the parasitic transistor beta drops below 1:

$$
\beta_{lateral} = \frac{D_n}{W_B} \cdot \tau_n \quad \text{(approx.)}
$$

Setting $\beta_{lateral} \leq 1$ yields:

$$
W_B \geq \sqrt{D_n \cdot \tau_n}
$$

For typical silicon doping ($D_n \approx 25 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$, $\tau_n \approx 1 \mu s$):

$$
W_B \geq \sqrt{25 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.5 \, \mu m
$$

For a 0.5 µm process ($\lambda = 0.25 \, \mu m$):

$$
W_B \geq 0.5 \, \mu m = 2\lambda
$$

> Foundry margins add extra safety, hence the rule is **4λ**, not 2λ. The factor of 2 provides margin against process variation, temperature, and supply transients.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — λ-Rules vs. Absolute Rules

| Criterion | λ-Rules (Mead & Conway) | Absolute Micron Rules |
|-----------|------------------------|-----------------------|
| Scalability | Excellent — scale λ | None — fixed µm |
| Process fidelity | Approximate | Exact to specific fab |
| Rule count | ~20–30 rules | 200–2000 rules |
| Use case | Academic, initial design | Industrial sign-off |
| Industry standard | No | Yes (foundry PDKs) |
| Migratability | Trivial (just scale λ) | Requires full re-DRC |
| Encodes latchup margin? | Yes (4λ rule) | Yes (specific µm) |
| Reflects anisotropy? | No (all layers scale) | Yes (per-layer µm) |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Converting λ-Rules to Microns

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A SCMOS layout uses $\lambda = 0.35 \, \mu m$. Compute (a) minimum poly width, (b) minimum active width, (c) minimum n+ to p+ spacing, (d) contact cut area.

**Solution:**

(a) Minimum poly width:
$$
W_p = 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.35 = 0.70 \, \mu m
$$

(b) Minimum active width:
$$
W_a = 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.35 = 0.70 \, \mu m
$$

(c) Minimum n+ to p+ spacing:
$$
S_{np} = 4\lambda = 4 \times 0.35 = 1.40 \, \mu m
$$

(d) Contact cut area:
$$
A_{contact} = (2\lambda)^2 = (0.70)^2 = 0.49 \, \mu m^2
$$
