# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DAG Under Analysis

The following Mermaid diagram represents the 6-node DAG analyzed in Section 3.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["A  PI  d=2ns"]:::piNode
    nodeB["B  PI  d=3ns"]:::piNode
    nodeC["C  d=1ns"]
    nodeD["D  d=2ns"]
    nodeE["E  d=4ns"]:::critNode
    nodeF["F  PO  d=1ns"]:::poNode

    nodeA -->|wire| nodeC
    nodeA -->|wire| nodeD
    nodeB -->|wire| nodeC
    nodeB -->|wire| nodeD
    nodeC -->|wire| nodeE
    nodeD -->|wire| nodeE
    nodeE -->|wire| nodeF

    classDef piNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef poNode fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#b3320a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef critNode fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#b37700,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The node $E$ is highlighted in amber because it lies on the critical path. Nodes $A, B$ are primary inputs (blue), node $F$ is the primary output (red), and the critical path $B \to D \to E \to F$ carries the **maximum delay of 10 ns**.

## 4.2 Algorithm Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the **two-stage processing flow** of the Longest Path Algorithm on a DAG.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["START: Input DAG G(V,E) and delay function d(v)"]) --> topo["STAGE 1: Topological Sort"]
    topo --> topoOut["Output: Linear order sigma v1, v2, ..., vn"]
    topoOut --> init["STAGE 2: Initialize L array"]
    init --> initPI["For v in PI: L(v) = d(v)"]
    init --> initNon["For v not in PI: L(v) = - infinity"]
    initPI --> loop
    initNon --> loop
    loop["FOR each v in topological order sigma DO"]
    loop --> relax["Relaxation:  L(v) = max over u in fanin v of L(u) + d(u,v)"]
    relax --> check{"All nodes processed?"}
    check -->|No| loop
    check -->|Yes| extract["Extract critical path by backtracking through max-predecessor pointers"]
    extract --> crit["T_crit = max over w in PO of L(w)"]
    crit --> end(["END: Return T_crit and critical path list"])

    classDef stage1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef stage2 fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#b37700,color:#000

    class topo,topoOut stage1
    class init,initPI,initNon,loop,relax,extract,crit stage2
    class check decision
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Critical-Path Visualization

The following Mermaid graph shows how the DP table is built layer-by-layer. Each level corresponds to a topological rank.

```mermaid
graph LR
    rank0["Rank 0: Primary Inputs"] --- rank1["Rank 1: First-level gates"] --- rank2["Rank 2: Second-level gates"] --- rank3["Rank 3: Primary Outputs"]

    rank0N["L(a)=2, L(b)=3"]:::lvl0
    rank1N["L(c)=4, L(d)=5"]:::lvl1
    rank2N["L(e)=9"]:::lvl2
    rank3N["L(f)=10  CRITICAL"]:::lvl3

    rank0 --- rank0N
    rank1 --- rank1N
    rank2 --- rank2N
    rank3 --- rank3N

    classDef lvl0 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef lvl1 fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000
    classDef lvl2 fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#b37700,color:#000
    classDef lvl3 fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#b3320a,color:#000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). Why is the combinational logic of a digital circuit always represented as a DAG in VLSI timing analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ that contains **no directed cycles** — i.e., there is no path that starts and ends at the same vertex. In VLSI timing analysis, a combinational logic circuit is represented as a DAG because combinational logic, by definition, has **no feedback loops**; every signal flows from primary inputs through logic gates to primary outputs in a strictly forward direction. This acyclic property guarantees that a valid **topological ordering** exists, which is the prerequisite for the Longest Path Algorithm to compute the critical path delay in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of the "critical path" in a combinational circuit? How does it determine the minimum clock period of a synchronous design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **critical path** of a combinational circuit is the path from a primary input to a primary output that carries the **maximum cumulative propagation delay**. Its significance is that it determines the **worst-case time** required for a signal change at any input to settle at the output. In a synchronous design, the **minimum clock period** $T_{clk}$ is bounded below by:

$$T_{clk} \geq T_{crit} + T_{setup} + T_{ck2q}$$

where $T_{crit}$ is the critical path delay, $T_{setup}$ is the flip-flop setup time, and $T_{ck2q}$ is the clock-to-Q delay. If the clock period is shorter than this bound, the next-stage flip-flop may latch incorrect data, causing **setup-time violations** and functional failure. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse longest path with shortest path.** The Longest Path problem on a DAG is polynomial-time solvable; on a *general* graph it is NP-hard. State the precondition explicitly.
> 2. **Always mention the topological sort first.** Many students skip this prerequisite and directly write the DP recurrence, losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Always state complexity.** Writing $O(n)$ instead of $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ is incomplete; you must include the edge term.
> 4. **Show all relaxation steps in a table** — partial marks are awarded for each correctly evaluated node.
> 5. **Highlight the critical path** explicitly at the end with a clear backtrack; do not leave the answer ambiguous.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**For the DAG shown below, compute the longest path delay from any primary input to the primary output using the Longest Path Algorithm for DAGs. Identify the critical path and the minimum clock period.**

The DAG is the same as in Section 3, restated here:

- **PIs:** $a, b$ with $d(a) = 2\ \text{ns}$, $d(b) = 3\ \text{ns}$.
- **Internal nodes:** $c, d, e$ with $d(c) = 1\ \text{ns}$, $d(d) = 2\ \text{ns}$, $d(e) = 4\ \text{ns}$.
- **PO:** $f$ with $d(f) = 1\ \text{ns}$.
- **Edges:** $a \to c,\ a \to d,\ b \to c,\ b \to d,\ c \to e,\ d \to e,\ e \to f$.

#### (a) **Apply the Longest Path Algorithm. Compute $L(v)$ for every node, and present your work in a tabular form. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

**Step 1: Topological Sort.** A valid topological order is $\sigma = (a, b, c, d, e, f)$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Initialization.**

$$
L(a) = 2\ \text{ns},\ L(b) = 3\ \text{ns},\ L(c) = L(d) = L(e) = L(f) = -\infty
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3: Apply recurrence** $L(v) = \max\limits_{u \in \text{fanin}(v)} L(u) + d(v)$ in topological order.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(c) &= \max[L(a), L(b)] + d(c) = \max[2, 3] + 1 = 4\ \text{ns} \\
L(d) &= \max[L(a), L(b)] + d(d) = \max[2, 3] + 2 = 5\ \text{ns} \\
L(e) &= \max[L(c), L(d)] + d(e) = \max[4, 5] + 4 = 9\ \text{ns} \\
L(f) &= L(e) + d(f) = 9 + 1 = 10\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(4 marks)** for the relaxation computations.

**Step 4: Tabulation (1 mark).**

| Node | $\max L(u)$ | $d(v)$ (ns) | $L(v)$ (ns) | Critical predecessor |
|:----:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:--------------------:|
| $a$ | — | 2 | 2 | — |
| $b$ | — | 3 | 3 | — |
| $c$ | 3 | 1 | 4 | $b$ |
| $d$ | 3 | 2 | 5 | $b$ |
| $e$ | 5 | 4 | 9 | $d$ |
| $f$ | 9 | 1 | 10 | $e$ |

#### (b) **Identify the critical path. State the minimum clock period assuming $T_{setup} = 0.5\ \text{ns}$ and $T_{ck2q} = 0.3\ \text{ns}$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Critical Path Backtracking** from $f$: **(3 marks)**

- $f \leftarrow e$ (since $L(f) = L(e) + d(f)$)
- $e \leftarrow d$ (since $L(e) = L(d) + d(e)$ achieved the max over $L(c), L(d)$)
- $d \leftarrow b$ (since $L(d) = L(b) + d(d)$ achieved the max over $L(a), L(b)$)

**Critical Path:** $b \to d \to e \to f$ with total delay:

$$
T_{crit} = 3 + 2 + 4 + 1 = 10\ \text{ns}
$$

**(2 marks)** for delay verification.

**Minimum Clock Period Computation:** **(2 marks)**

$$
T_{clk} \geq T_{crit} + T_{setup} + T_{ck2q} = 10 + 0.5 + 0.3 = 10.8\ \text{ns}
$$

**Answer:** The minimum clock period is $\boxed{10.8\ \text{ns}}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Consider a combinational circuit modeled as a DAG with the following nodes and gate delays:**

- PIs: $A, B, C$ with $d(A) = 1\ \text{ns}$, $d(B) = 2\ \text{ns}$, $d(C) = 3\ \text{ns}$.
- Internal nodes: $D, E, F$ with $d(D) = 2\ \text{ns}$, $d(E) = 1\ \text{ns}$, $d(F) = 3\ \text{ns}$.
- PO: $G$ with $d(G) = 1\ \text{ns}$.
- **Edges:** $A \to D$, $B \to D$, $B \to E$, $C \to E$, $C \to F$, $D \to F$, $E \to G$, $F \to G$.

#### (a) **Draw the DAG and compute the longest path delay from any PI to every node using the Longest Path Algorithm. Show all steps in a table. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

**Step 1: Topological Sort.** A valid topological order is $\sigma = (A, B, C, D, E, F, G)$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Initialization.** $L(A) = 1$, $L(B) = 2$, $L(C) = 3$; $L(D) = L(E) = L(F) = L(G) = -\infty$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3: Recurrence application.** **(4 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(D) &= \max[L(A), L(B)] + d(D) = \max[1, 2] + 2 = 4\ \text{ns} \\
L(E) &= \max[L(B), L(C)] + d(E) = \max[2, 3] + 1 = 4\ \text{ns} \\
L(F) &= \max[L(C), L(D)] + d(F) = \max[3, 4] + 3 = 7\ \text{ns} \\
L(G) &= \max[L(E), L(F)] + d(G) = \max[4, 7] + 1 = 8\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Tabulation (1 mark).**

| Node | $\max L(u)$ | $d(v)$ (ns) | $L(v)$ (ns) | Critical predecessor |
|:----:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:--------------------:|
| $A$ | — | 1 | 1 | — |
| $B$ | — | 2 | 2 | — |
| $C$ | — | 3 | 3 | — |
| $D$ | 2 | 2 | 4 | $B$ |
| $E$ | 3 | 1 | 4 | $C$ |
| $F$ | 4 | 3 | 7 | $D$ |
| $G$ | 7 | 1 | 8 | $F$ |

#### (b) **Identify the critical path. Suppose the design team wants to reduce the critical path by 2 ns. Suggest two specific circuit-level transformations and quantify their impact. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Critical Path Backtracking** from $G$: **(3 marks)**

- $G \leftarrow F$ (since $L(G) = L(F) + d(G) = 7 + 1$)
- $F \leftarrow D$ (since $L(F) = L(D) + d(F)$, and $L(D) = 4 > L(C) = 3$)
- $D \leftarrow B$ (since $L(D) = L(B) + d(D)$, and $L(B) = 2 > L(A) = 1$)

**Critical Path:** $B \to D \to F \to G$ with total delay:

$$
T_{crit} = 2 + 2 + 3 + 1 = 8\ \text{ns}
$$

**(1 mark)** for verification.

**Circuit-Level Transformations to reduce $T_{crit}$ by 2 ns:** **(2 marks for suggestions + 1 mark for quantification)**

1. **Gate Sizing on $F$:** Replace gate $F$ with a higher-drive-strength variant that has $d'(F) = 1\ \text{ns}$ (a 2 ns reduction). The new critical path becomes $B \to D \to F \to G = 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 6\ \text{ns}$. *Trade-off:* Increased area and dynamic power.

2. **Path Balancing — Buffer Insertion between $D$ and $F$:** Instead of routing $D \to F$ directly through a slow gate, insert a fast buffer with $d_{buf} = 0.5\ \text{ns}$ and reduce $F$'s effective load. New path delay $\approx 2 + 2 + 0.5 + 1 = 5.5\ \text{ns}$. *Trade-off:* Extra area, but reduced load on $F$.

3. **Logic Restructuring:** Re-factor the Boolean expression driving $F$ to reduce its gate-depth from 1 to 0 (e.g., collapse $D$ and $C$ into a single complex gate), reducing path depth by 1 gate-level delay.

**Conclusion:** All three transformations can achieve the target reduction of $\geq 2\ \text{ns}$ with different area/power trade-offs. **(1 mark for final synthesis)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Mandatory for KTU Exam Day):**

- ✅ A **DAG** models combinational logic; cycles in combinational logic imply latches/feedback (sequential elements).
- ✅ The **Longest Path Algorithm** has **two stages**: **Topological Sort** + **DP Relaxation**.
- ✅ **Time complexity is $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$** — not just $O(n)$.
- ✅ **Boundary condition:** $L(v) = d(v)$ for primary inputs; $L(v) = -\infty$ for all others initially.
- ✅ **Recurrence:** $L(v) = \max\limits_{u \in \text{fanin}(v)} [L(u) + d(u, v)]$.
- ✅ **Critical path delay:** $T_{crit} = \max\limits_{w \in PO} L(w)$.
- ✅ **Minimum clock period:** $T_{clk} \geq T_{crit} + T_{setup} + T_{ck2q}$.
- ✅ **On a general (cyclic) graph, the Longest Path problem is NP-hard**; on a DAG it is polynomial — this is the *key* exam point.
- ✅ **Critical-path backtracking** is performed by following the *max-predecessor* pointers from the PO to a PI.
- ✅ **The longest path may not be unique** — multiple critical paths can have the same maximum delay.
- ✅ **Slack = Required Time $-$ Arrival Time**; positive slack = safe, negative slack = timing violation.
- ✅ **Optimization techniques** to reduce $T_{crit}$: gate sizing, buffering, logic restructuring, retiming, pipelining.
- ✅ The algorithm assumes **fixed delays**; for statistical timing, **probabilistic SSTA** extends this to delay distributions.
- ✅ **KTU often asks** for both the **delay value** and the **critical path**; missing either loses 50% of the marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When the question says "compute the longest path", always present your answer in **three parts** — (1) the topological order, (2) the DP table, (3) the backtracked critical path. Examiners award step-wise marks; a single final number without the table fetches only 2 out of 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liao-Wong Algorithm (1983):** A **time-driven, iterative-improvement, multi-way circuit partitioning algorithm** that simultaneously minimizes the **cut-size (number of inter-partition nets)** while satisfying the **timing (performance) constraints** of a VLSI circuit. It extends the classical **Kernighan-Lin (KL) bipartitioning** framework to handle *critical paths* and *strongly connected components (SCCs)*, ensuring that cells along timing-critical signal paths are placed in the same partition to avoid introducing unnecessary logic-level delays.

The algorithm operates on a **hypergraph representation** $H = (V, E)$ of a circuit, where:
- $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ → set of cells (logic gates, standard cells, or macro blocks)
- $E = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_m\}$ → set of hyperedges (signal nets connecting multiple cells)

The objective is to partition $V$ into $k$ balanced blocks $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_k$ such that:
$$
\min \sum_{i<j} c(P_i, P_j) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \forall \, i : |P_i| \leq (1+\beta) \cdot \frac{|V|}{k}
$$
where $c(P_i, P_j)$ is the number of nets crossing between $P_i$ and $P_j$, and $\beta$ is the **balance tolerance** (typically **0.05 to 0.1**).

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Express Delivery Sorting Warehouse"**
>
> Imagine a massive Amazon-style warehouse where thousands of packages (**cells**) need to be sorted into delivery vans (**partitions**) destined for different regions. Two critical rules apply:
>
> 1. **Minimize van-to-van transfers** (analogous to minimizing **cut nets** — every cross-region transfer costs time and money).
> 2. **Time-critical packages** (e.g., birthday gifts, medical supplies — analogous to **critical path cells**) MUST travel together in the *same* van. Splitting them across vans would create unacceptable delays.
>
> The **Kernighan-Lin algorithm** is like a basic sorter that only cares about rule 1. The **Liao-Wong algorithm** is the *smarter* sorter that **also enforces rule 2** — it identifies urgent, time-sensitive package chains (critical paths) and keeps them bundled together. It repeatedly swaps packages between vans when doing so reduces total transfer cost **AND** does not break up a critical chain.

---

## 🏛️ Historical Context & Motivation

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Proposed By** | **Y. Liao and C. K. Wong** (1983) |
| **Published In** | *IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems* |
| **Predecessor** | **Kernighan-Lin (KL) Algorithm** (1970) |
| **Key Innovation** | First algorithm to integrate **timing (delay) constraints** into the partitioning cost function |
| **Standard Net Model** | **Unit-weight hyperedges** (each net contributes 1 to cut-size if split) |
| **Block Capacity** | **Balanced** with tolerance factor **β ∈ [0.05, 0.10]** |
| **Performance Metric** | **Cut-size minimization** + **Longest path delay minimization** |

---

## 🎯 Why Liao-Wong? — Motivation Over Pure KL

Standard **Kernighan-Lin** only minimizes cut-size, ignoring timing. In deep-submicron VLSI, **interconnect delay dominates gate delay**, so splitting a critical-path net across two partitions introduces:

- **Extra wire delay** (long global routing between partitions)
- **Buffer insertion overhead**
- **Potential timing violation** (setup-time failure, hold-time violation)

Liao-Wong solves this by **adding a "timing gain" term** to the KL gain function, making moves that keep critical-path cells together *more attractive* than purely cut-minimizing moves.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ⚙️ Algorithmic Foundations

The Liao-Wong algorithm is built upon **three pillars**:

### Pillar 1: The Kernighan-Lin (KL) Bipartitioning Framework

KL uses an **iterative pairwise swap** strategy:

1. Start with a random balanced bipartition $(A, B)$.
2. Compute the **gain** $g(v)$ for every unlocked cell $v$:
$$
g(v) = D(v) - I(v)
$$
where:
- $D(v)$ = **External degree** = number of nets that would be *cut* if $v$ is moved to the other side
- $I(v)$ = **Internal degree** = number of nets that would be *unsplit* (remain internal) if $v$ is moved

3. In each pass, lock the pair $(a_i, b_i)$ with **maximum gain $g_i$** in iteration $i$, and record cumulative gain $G_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$.
4. Find $k^*$ that maximizes $G_k$. If $G_{k^*} > 0$, perform the swaps and repeat; else stop.

### Pillar 2: Critical Path Identification (The Liao-Wong Differentiator)

Liao-Wong performs **two preprocessing steps** on the circuit hypergraph:

1. **Longest Path Computation (LPC):** On the **DAG of the circuit**, find the **longest combinational path** from any primary input (PI) to any primary output (PO). This becomes the **critical path** $\mathcal{P}_{crit}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path Definition:** A path $v_1 \to v_2 \to \ldots \to v_L$ where the **accumulated delay** equals:
>
> $$T_{crit} = \max_{p \in \text{Paths}} \sum_{i \in p} d_i$$
>
> The path achieving this maximum determines the **clock period** $T_{clk} \geq T_{crit}$.

2. **Strongly Connected Components (SCCs) Detection:** Apply **Tarjan's Algorithm** (linear time, $O(|V|+|E|)$) to find SCCs. All cells within a single SCC form a **"super-node"** that MUST be placed in the same partition — splitting an SCC breaks a feedback loop and is **strictly prohibited**.

### Pillar 3: Modified Gain Function with Timing

For each candidate cell $v$, Liao-Wong redefines the gain as:
$$
g_{LW}(v) = \underbrace{[D(v) - I(v)]}_{\text{KL cut gain}} + \underbrace{\Delta T(v)}_{\text{Timing delta}}
$$

where $\Delta T(v)$ is the **change in longest path delay** if $v$ is moved across the cut.

- If $v$ is on the **critical path** and lies in a partition with its critical-path neighbors → moving it **increases** $T_{crit}$ → $\Delta T(v) < 0$ (penalty).
- If $v$ is on the critical path but isolated from its critical-path neighbors → moving it **toward them** decreases $T_{crit}$ → $\Delta T(v) > 0$ (reward).

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g(v) = D(v) - I(v)$ | **KL gain** of cell $v$ | Integer (net count) |
| 2 | $g_{LW}(v) = g(v) + \Delta T(v)$ | **Liao-Wong gain** with timing | Integer + Delay |
| 3 | $D(v) = \vert \{n : v \in n, n \cap B = \emptyset\} \vert$ | External nets cut by moving $v$ to $B$ | Count |
| 4 | $I(v) = \vert \{n : v \in n, n \subseteq A \setminus \{v\}\} \vert$ | Internal nets unsplit if $v$ moves | Count |
| 5 | $T_{crit} = \max_{p} \sum_{i \in p} d_i$ | **Longest path delay** = clock lower bound | Nanoseconds (ns) |
| 6 | $T_{clk} \geq T_{crit}$ | **Timing feasibility constraint** | ns |
| 7 | $C(P) = \sum_{i<j} \vert \text{cuts}(P_i, P_j) \vert$ | **Cut cost** of partition $P$ | Net count |
| 8 | $\vert P_i \vert \leq (1+\beta) \cdot \vert V \vert / k$ | **Balance constraint** | Cells |
| 9 | $\beta$ | **Balance tolerance** | **0.05–0.10** (dimensionless) |
| 10 | $\text{SCC}(G)$ | Strongly Connected Components via Tarjan | Set of subsets of $V$ |
| 11 | $O(\vert V \vert^2 \log \vert V \vert)$ | **Time complexity** of one KL pass | — |
| 12 | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Tarjan's SCC time complexity | Linear |
| 13 | $\alpha(v)$ | Pin-count (number of pins on net containing $v$) | $\geq 1$ |
| 14 | $\Delta T(v) = T_{crit}^{\text{after}} - T_{crit}^{\text{before}}$ | Timing delta from moving $v$ | ns (can be negative) |
| 15 | $G_{k^*} = \max_k \sum_{i=1}^{k} g_i$ | Best cumulative gain in a pass | Integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Throughout this table, absolute-value/cardinality notation uses the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ (e.g., $\vert V \vert$, $\vert P_i \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The vertical pipe inside math context is *not* treated as a table separator.

---

## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Multi-FPGA Prototyping:** Partitioning large ASICs (millions of gates) into 3–8 FPGAs where *inter-FPGA delay* is 5–10× intra-FPGA delay.
- **Timing-Driven Floorplanning:** Modern commercial EDA tools (e.g., Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC) inherit Liao-Wong-style timing-aware partitioning in their hierarchical design flows.
- **Standard-Cell Placement Pre-pass:** Coarse partitioning to reduce placement runtime on million-cell designs.
- **Hardware-Software Co-Design Partitioning:** Separating RTL blocks for hardware implementation vs. software execution, respecting data-flow criticality.

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📐 Derivation 1: From KL Gain to Liao-Wong Gain

### Step 1 — Recall the KL External Degree

For a cell $v \in A$ in bipartition $(A, B)$:
$$
D(v) = \big\vert \{n \in N : v \in n \text{ and } (n \cap B) \neq \emptyset\} \big\vert
$$
This counts nets that, after $v$ leaves $A$, will have all their remaining pins **only in $B$** (hence *externally cut* after the move).

### Step 2 — Recall the KL Internal Degree

$$
I(v) = \big\vert \{n \in N : v \in n \text{ and } (n \setminus \{v\}) \subseteq A\} \big\vert
$$
These are nets that are **entirely inside $A$** but for which $v$ is the *only* cell connected to them within $A$. After $v$ moves, these nets *become* cut nets.

### Step 3 — The KL Net-Cut Delta

If $v$ is moved from $A$ to $B$, the change in total cut-size is precisely:
$$
\Delta C(v) = I(v) - D(v) = -g_{KL}(v)
$$
A *positive* KL gain $g_{KL}(v) > 0$ means cut-size **decreases** by $g_{KL}(v)$ after the move.

### Step 4 — Add the Timing Component

Liao-Wong augments this with the timing delta $\Delta T(v)$. The **composite objective** becomes:
$$
g_{LW}(v) = w_1 \cdot g_{KL}(v) + w_2 \cdot \Delta T(v)
$$
where $w_1, w_2$ are **weighting coefficients** typically chosen such that $w_1 + w_2 = 1$, with $w_1$ dominating in cut-driven mode and $w_2$ dominating in timing-driven mode.

### Step 5 — Normalized Timing Delta

Since KL gains are integer counts and timing is in nanoseconds, normalization is required:
$$
\Delta T_{\text{norm}}(v) = \frac{\Delta T(v)}{T_{clk} - T_{crit}^{\text{slack}}}
$$
where $T_{crit}^{\text{slack}}$ is the current slack on the critical path.

### Final Liao-Wong Gain Equation

$$
\boxed{\,g_{LW}(v) = \big[ D(v) - I(v) \big] + \lambda \cdot \frac{\Delta T(v)}{T_{clk} - T_{crit}}\,}
$$

where $\lambda$ is a **tuning parameter** (typically **0.5 to 2.0**) controlling the trade-off.

---

## 📐 Derivation 2: Critical Path Delay Computation via Topological Sort

Given a DAG with $n$ cells and per-cell delay $d_i$:

### Step 1 — Topological Ordering

Compute a topological order $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n$ in $O(|V|+|E|)$.

### Step 2 — Forward DP (Earliest Arrival Time)

$$
T_{\text{earliest}}(v_j) = d_j + \max_{v_i \in \text{pred}(v_j)} T_{\text{earliest}}(v_i)
$$
with base case $T_{\text{earliest}}(\text{PI}) = 0$.

### Step 3 — Required Arrival Time (Backward DP)

$$
T_{\text{latest}}(v_j) = \min_{v_k \in \text{succ}(v_j)} \big[ T_{\text{latest}}(v_k) - d_k \big]
$$
with base case $T_{\text{latest}}(\text{PO}) = T_{clk}$.

### Step 4 — Slack Computation

$$
\text{Slack}(v_j) = T_{\text{latest}}(v_j) - T_{\text{earliest}}(v_j)
$$

### Step 5 — Critical Path Extraction

All cells with $\text{Slack}(v_j) = 0$ lie on **at least one** critical path. The critical path $\mathcal{P}_{crit}$ is reconstructed by backtracing from a PO with zero slack to a PI.

---

## 🧮 Worked Example: Computing Gains on a 4-Cell Hypergraph

Consider circuit with cells $\{a, b, c, d\}$ and nets $n_1 = \{a, b\}$, $n_2 = \{b, c, d\}$, $n_3 = \{a, d\}$.

Initial bipartition: $A = \{a, c\}$, $B = \{b, d\}$.

**Compute for $a \in A$:**
- $D(a) = $ nets in $a$ that touch $B$ → $n_1 = \{a, b\}$ touches $b \in B$ ✓, $n_3 = \{a, d\}$ touches $d \in B$ ✓ → $D(a) = 2$
- $I(a) = $ nets that, after removing $a$, are entirely within $A$ → none → $I(a) = 0$
- $g_{KL}(a) = 2 - 0 = 2$

**Compute for $c \in A$:**
- $D(c) = $ nets in $c$ that touch $B$ → $n_2 = \{b, c, d\}$ touches $B$ ✓ → $D(c) = 1$
- $I(c) = $ nets remaining in $A$ after $c$ moves → none → $I(c) = 0$
- $g_{KL}(c) = 1 - 0 = 1$

**Compute for $b \in B$:**
- $D(b) = $ nets in $b$ that touch $A$ → $n_1$ touches $a \in A$ ✓, $n_2$ touches $c \in A$ ✓ → $D(b) = 2$
- $I(b) = 0$
- $g_{KL}(b) = 2 - 0 = 2$

**Compute for $d \in B$:**
- $D(d) = $ nets in $d$ that touch $A$ → $n_2$ touches $c \in A$ ✓, $n_3$ touches $a \in A$ ✓ → $D(d) = 2$
- $I(d) = 0$
- $g_{KL}(d) = 2 - 0 = 2$

**Best move:** Pick the highest-gain pair. Suppose $a \leftrightarrow b$ gives $g_1 = g(a) + g(b) = 2 + 2 = 4$ (largest). Lock $(a, b)$ and record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from Example:** When $I(v) = 0$ for all cells, every move *strictly decreases* the cut-size. This is why KL/Liao-Wong works efficiently in *sparse* hypergraphs where most nets have small pin counts.

---

## 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix: KL vs. Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) vs. Liao-Wong

| Criterion | Kernighin-Lin (KL) | Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) | **Liao-Wong (LW)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year** | 1970 | 1982 | **1983** |
| **Partitioning** | Bipartition (k=2) | Bipartition (k=2) | **Multi-way (k≥2)** |
| **Move Granularity** | Pair-wise swap | Single-cell move | **Pair-wise with timing** |
| **Time Complexity / Pass** | $O(n^2 \log n)$ | $O(n)$ (bucket-list) | $O(n^2 \log n)$ |
| **Timing Awareness** | ❌ None | ❌ None | ✅ **Yes — critical paths** |
| **SCC Handling** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ **Yes (Tarjan)** |
| **Cut-Weight Model** | Uniform | Uniform | **Uniform + timing** |
| **Balance Constraint** | Strict | Area-balanced | **Tolerance β** |
| **Multi-way Extension** | Recursive bisection | Recursive bisection | **Native k-way** |
| **Net Model** | Edges | Hyperedges (gain bucket) | **Hyperedges** |
| **Best Suited For** | Small circuits | Large VLSI | **Timing-critical large VLSI** |

# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Liao-Wong Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Circuit Netlist V, E] --> B[Build Hypergraph H]
    B --> C[Compute Longest Path via Topological Sort]
    C --> D[Extract Critical Path Pcrit]
    B --> E[Run Tarjan SCC Detection]
    E --> F[Collapse SCCs into Super-Nodes]
    F --> G[Initial k-way Random Partition]
    D --> G
    G --> H{Iterative Improvement Loop}
    H --> I[For Each Cell: Compute KL Gain D minus I]
    I --> J[Compute Timing Delta DeltaT for v on Pcrit]
    J --> K[Composite LW Gain gLW = gKL + lambda times DeltaT]
    K --> L[Select Best Gain Pair a in Pi, b in Pj]
    L --> M{SCC Constraint Satisfied?}
    M -- No --> N[Skip This Pair Mark as Illegal]
    N --> L
    M -- Yes --> O[Lock Pair Record Cumulative Gain Gk]
    O --> P{All Cells Locked?}
    P -- No --> L
    P -- Yes --> Q[Find k* Maximizing Gk]
    Q --> R{Gk positive?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Commit All Swaps Up to k*]
    S --> H
    R -- No --> T[STOP Partition is Locally Optimal]
    T --> U[Output Final k-way Partition]
```

---

## 📊 Partitioning Objective: Cut-size vs. Timing Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OBJECTIVE_SPACE[Optimization Objective Space]
        A1[Pure Cut-Size Minimization] --> A2[KL / FM Domain]
        A2 --> A3[Low Wire Congestion]
        A3 --> A4[No Timing Guarantee]
    end
    subgraph LW_DOMAIN[Liao-Wong Domain]
        B1[Cut-Size + Critical Path Delay] --> B2[Weighted Sum Objective]
        B2 --> B3[Balanced Cut with Timing]
        B3 --> B4[Feasible Clock Period]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A4 -. Trade-off .-> B4
```

---

## 📊 Gain Computation Flow Inside One Pass

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cellA[Cell v in Partition Pi] --> D1[Scan All Nets Containing v]
    D1 --> D2{Net Has Pins Outside Pi?}
    D2 -- Yes --> D3[Increment D of v]
    D2 -- No --> D4{Net Will Be Internal to Pi minus v?}
    D4 -- Yes --> D5[Increment I of v]
    D3 --> timingCheck{v on Critical Path?}
    D4 -- No --> timingCheck
    D5 --> timingCheck
    timingCheck -- Yes --> deltaT[Compute DeltaT via slack recomputation]
    timingCheck -- No --> gkl[Record gKL equals D minus I]
    deltaT --> composite[Composite Gain gLW equals gKL plus lambda times DeltaT]
    gkl --> composite
    composite --> bucket[Insert into Gain Bucket List]
```

---

## 📊 Comparison of Three Algorithms' Search Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph KL[KL Strategy 1970]
        K1[Random Bisection] --> K2[Compute Pair-wise Gains]
        K2 --> K3[Lock Pairs Sequentially]
        K3 --> K4[Find Best Prefix and Commit]
    end
    subgraph FM[FM Strategy 1982]
        F1[Random Bisection] --> F2[Single Cell Buckets]
        F2 --> F3[Move Highest Gain Cell]
        F3 --> F4[Update Incremental Gains]
    end
    subgraph LW[Liao-Wong Strategy 1983]
        L1[Random k-way Partition] --> L2[Identify Critical Path]
        L2 --> L3[Collapse SCCs]
        L3 --> L4[Compute Composite Gain gLW]
        L4 --> L5[Lock Pairs with SCC Constraint]
    end
```

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**
**Differentiate between the Kernighan-Lin (KL) algorithm and the Liao-Wong algorithm for circuit partitioning. Highlight the key feature that Liao-Wong adds over KL.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | Kernighan-Lin (1970) | Liao-Wong (1983) |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Pure cut-size minimization | Cut-size + **timing delay** minimization |
| **Critical-Path Awareness** | ❌ Ignores timing | ✅ **Considers longest path delay** |
| **Multi-way Support** | Inherently bipartite; needs recursive bisection | **Native k-way partitioning** |
| **SCC Handling** | No special handling | **Detects & preserves SCCs as super-nodes** |
| **Gain Function** | $g(v) = D(v) - I(v)$ | $g_{LW}(v) = g(v) + \lambda \cdot \Delta T(v)$ |

**Key Differentiator (1 mark):** Liao-Wong adds a **timing-delta term $\Delta T(v)$** to the KL gain, making the algorithm **timing-driven** — it keeps cells on the critical path together to avoid increasing $T_{crit}$. *(3 marks total)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
**Explain the role of Strongly Connected Components (SCCs) in the Liao-Wong partitioning algorithm. Why is it mandatory to keep all cells of an SCC in the same partition?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

- **Definition (1 mark):** An SCC is a maximal subset of cells in which every cell is reachable from every other cell via directed paths, indicating the presence of **combinational or sequential feedback loops**.
- **Detection (1 mark):** Liao-Wong uses **Tarjan's algorithm** ($O(|V|+|E|)$) to find all SCCs in the circuit hypergraph. Each SCC is collapsed into a **single super-node** before partitioning.
- **Mandatory Co-location (1 mark):** Splitting an SCC across two partitions would **break feedback loops**, requiring extra off-chip/global routing paths and causing **severe timing violations and unstable sequential behavior**. Hence, the Liao-Wong gain function **forbids any swap that would separate two cells belonging to the same SCC**. *(3 marks total)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Full-Weight Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q. (a)** Describe the **Liao-Wong time-driven partitioning algorithm** in detail with its **flowchart, objective function, and gain computation**. Discuss how critical-path analysis and SCC detection are integrated into the algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A circuit has the following netlist:

| Net | Connected Cells |
|---|---|
| $n_1$ | $\{v_1, v_2\}$ |
| $n_2$ | $\{v_2, v_3, v_4\}$ |
| $n_3$ | $\{v_1, v_4\}$ |
| $n_4$ | $\{v_3, v_5\}$ |
| $n_5$ | $\{v_4, v_5\}$ |

Initial bipartition: $A = \{v_1, v_3\}$, $B = \{v_2, v_4, v_5\}$.

Compute the **KL gain** $g(v)$ for every cell, and identify the **best swap pair** in the first iteration. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Solution to Q. (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Algorithm Overview (2 marks):**
Liao-Wong is a **time-driven, iterative-improvement, multi-way partitioning** algorithm that:
- Takes a circuit hypergraph $H = (V, E)$.
- Partitions $V$ into $k$ blocks subject to a **balance constraint** $\beta$.
- Optimizes a **composite objective**: cut-size + timing penalty.
- Uses **SCC collapsing** to enforce feedback-loop integrity.
- Iteratively **swaps pairs of cells** between blocks to maximize composite gain.

**2. Objective Function (2 marks):**
$$
\min_{P} \left[ w_1 \cdot C(P) + w_2 \cdot T_{crit}(P) \right]
$$
where $C(P)$ is the cut cost, $T_{crit}(P)$ is the longest path delay under partition $P$, and $w_1, w_2 \geq 0$ are weights.

**3. Gain Computation (1 mark):**
$$
g_{LW}(v) = [D(v) - I(v)] + \lambda \cdot \Delta T(v)
$$

**4. Flowchart (textual, 1 mark):** Input → Build H → Topological sort → Extract $P_{crit}$ → Tarjan SCC → Collapse → Initial k-way partition → **Iterative loop:** compute gains → select max-gain pair → check SCC constraint → lock pair → record cumulative gain → find $k^*$ → commit if $G_{k^*} > 0$.

**5. Integration of Critical Path + SCC (1 mark):**
- **Critical path** $\mathcal{P}_{crit}$ is computed by longest-path DP on the DAG; cells on $\mathcal{P}_{crit}$ get extra timing-delta weight in their gain.
- **SCCs** are collapsed to super-nodes; any swap separating an SCC is rejected. This guarantees that feedback loops remain intact and that timing-critical cells migrate together.

---

#### ✅ Solution to Q. (b) — 7 Marks

**Setup:** $A = \{v_1, v_3\}$, $B = \{v_2, v_4, v_5\}$.

**Nets with cross-partition pins:**
- $n_1 = \{v_1, v_2\}$ → $A$ and $B$ → **CUT**
- $n_2 = \{v_2, v_3, v_4\}$ → $A$ and $B$ → **CUT**
- $n_3 = \{v_1, v_4\}$ → $A$ and $B$ → **CUT**
- $n_4 = \{v_3, v_5\}$ → $A$ and $B$ → **CUT**
- $n_5 = \{v_4, v_5\}$ → $B$ and $B$ → **INTERNAL**

**Compute $D(v)$ and $I(v)$ for each cell:**

**For $v_1 \in A$:**
- Nets containing $v_1$: $n_1 = \{v_1, v_2\}$, $n_3 = \{v_1, v_4\}$.
- Both $n_1$ and $n_3$ have pins in $B$ ($v_2, v_4$). After $v_1$ moves to $B$, these nets are *entirely in $B$* → no longer cut.
- $D(v_1) = 2$ (external nets that will be cut/uncut).
- After $v_1$ leaves, check if any net becomes entirely in $A$: $n_1$ has $v_2 \in B$, $n_3$ has $v_4 \in B$ → none become fully internal to $A$ → $I(v_1) = 0$.
- $g(v_1) = 2 - 0 = \mathbf{2}$.

**For $v_3 \in A$:**
- Nets containing $v_3$: $n_2 = \{v_2, v_3, v_4\}$, $n_4 = \{v_3, v_5\}$.
- Both touch $B$ → after $v_3$ moves to $B$, $n_2$ will be entirely in $B$ (so $n_2$ becomes internal to $B$, not $A$), $n_4$ likewise.
- $D(v_3) = 2$ (these nets will be *uncut* after the move — they become internal to $B$).
- $I(v_3) = 0$.
- $g(v_3) = 2 - 0 = \mathbf{2}$.

**For $v_2 \in B$:**
- Nets containing $v_2$: $n_1 = \{v_1, v_2\}$, $n_2 = \{v_2, v_3, v_4\}$.
- $n_1$ has $v_1 \in A$ → after $v_2$ moves to $A$, $n_1$ will be entirely in $A$ → uncuts.
- $n_2$ has $v_3 \in A, v_4 \in B$ → still cut after move.
- $D(v_2) = $ number of nets whose external count changes → careful count: $D(v_2) = 2$ (both $n_1, n_2$ have external pins in $A$ after move they become internal).
- $I(v_2) = 0$ (no net becomes internal to $B$ after $v_2$ leaves, since both have pins in $A$).
- $g(v_2) = 2 - 0 = \mathbf{2}$.

**For $v_4 \in B$:**
- Nets containing $v_4$: $n_2 = \{v_2, v_3, v_4\}$, $n_3 = \{v_1, v_4\}$, $n_5 = \{v_4, v_5\}$.
- $n_2$ has $v_3 \in A$ → after move, $n_2$ still cut.
- $n_3$ has $v_1 \in A$ → after move, $n_3$ still cut.
- $n_5$ has $v_5 \in B$ → after $v_4$ moves to $A$, $n_5$ becomes entirely in $B$ → uncuts.
- $D(v_4) = 3$ (three nets have external pins).
- $I(v_4) = 0$.
- $g(v_4) = 3 - 0 = \mathbf{3}$.

**For $v_5 \in B$:**
- Nets containing $v_5$: $n_4 = \{v_3, v_5\}$, $n_5 = \{v_4, v_5\}$.
- $n_4$ has $v_3 \in A$ → after move still cut.
- $n_5$ has $v_4 \in B$ → after $v_5$ moves to $A$, $n_5$ becomes entirely in $B$ → uncuts.
- $D(v_5) = 2$, $I(v_5) = 0$.
- $g(v_5) = 2 - 0 = \mathbf{2}$.

**Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Cell | $D(v)$ | $I(v)$ | $g(v)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v_1$ | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| $v_3$ | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| $v_2$ | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| $v_4$ | 3 | 0 | **3** |
| $v_5$ | 2 | 0 | 2 |

**Best Swap Pair (2 marks):**
- Maximum individual gain is $v_4 \in B$ with $g = 3$.
- Pair $(v_4, v_3)$ yields $g_1 = 3 + 2 = 5$ → **best pair** in first iteration.
- Alternative: $(v_4, v_1)$ also gives $3 + 2 = 5$ (tied). Either is acceptable as "best" pair.

**Result after first swap:** New partition becomes $A' = \{v_1, v_3, v_4\}$, $B' = \{v_2, v_5\}$. Cumulative gain $G_1 = 5 > 0$, so commit and continue.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With a **neat flowchart**, explain the **Liao-Wong time-driven multi-way partitioning algorithm**. Compare it with the **Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) algorithm** on the basis of: move granularity, time complexity per pass, timing awareness, and SCC handling. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** For the circuit hypergraph below, identify all the **Strongly Connected Components (SCCs)** using Tarjan's algorithm and explain the resulting **super-node structure**:

| Net | Connected Cells |
|---|---|
| $n_1$ | $\{a, b\}$ |
| $n_2$ | $\{b, c\}$ |
| $n_3$ | $\{c, a\}$ |
| $n_4$ | $\{c, d\}$ |
| $n_5$ | $\{d, e\}$ |
| $n_6$ | $\{e, c\}$ |

Comment on how these SCCs influence the Liao-Wong partitioning decisions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Solution to Q. (a) — 7 Marks

**Flowchart Description (3 marks):**

```
[Input: Netlist V, E, k, β, T_clk]
       ↓
[Build Hypergraph H = (V, E)]
       ↓
[Topological Sort + Longest Path → P_crit]
       ↓
[Tarjan's Algorithm → SCCs]
       ↓
[Collapse SCCs into super-nodes]
       ↓
[Initial k-way Partition (random, balanced)]
       ↓
┌──→ ITERATION LOOP:
│    [For each cell v: compute D(v), I(v)]
│    [For v on P_crit: compute ΔT(v)]
│    [g_LW(v) = (D−I) + λ·ΔT(v)]
│    [Pick max-gain pair (a ∈ Pi, b ∈ Pj)]
│    [Check: same SCC? → Skip if violated]
│    [Lock (a,b), record cumulative G_k]
│    [Repeat until all locked]
│    [Find k* argmax G_k]
│    [If G_k* > 0 → commit swaps; else STOP]
└──── (loop back)
       ↓
[Output: Final k-way Partition]
```

**Comparison Table with FM (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Fiduccia-Mattheyses (1982) | Liao-Wong (1983) |
|---|---|---|
| **Move Granularity** | Single-cell move (one at a time) | **Pair-wise swap** (one from each of two partitions) |
| **Time Complexity / Pass** | $O(n)$ via bucket-list data structure | $O(n^2 \log n)$ due to pair-wise evaluation |
| **Timing Awareness** | ❌ None | ✅ **Explicit critical-path integration** |
| **SCC Handling** | ❌ Not addressed | ✅ **Tarjan's SCC + super-node collapse** |
| **Multi-way** | Recursive bisection only | **Native k-way** |
| **Net Model** | Hyperedges with vertex weights | Hyperedges + **path-based timing weights** |

---

#### ✅ Solution to Q. (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Construct the Graph (1 mark)**

Edges: $a \to b$, $b \to c$, $c \to a$ (cycle!), $c \to d$, $d \to e$, $e \to c$ (cycle!).

**Step 2: Identify Cycles (2 marks):**

- **Cycle 1:** $a \to b \to c \to a$ → cells $\{a, b, c\}$ form one cycle.
- **Cycle 2:** $c \to d \to e \to c$ → cells $\{c, d, e\}$ form another cycle.
- Note that $c$ is **shared** between both cycles.

**Step 3: Tarjan's Algorithm Trace (2 marks):**

Using DFS with low-link values:
- Start DFS at $a$: $\text{disc}(a)=1, \text{low}(a)=1$.
- Visit $b$: $\text{disc}(b)=2, \text{low}(b)=2$. Visit $c$: $\text{disc}(c)=3, \text{low}(c)=3$. Edge $c \to a$ with $a$ on stack → $\text{low}(c) = \min(3, 1) = 1$. Backtrack: $\text{low}(b) = \min(2, 1) = 1$, $\text{low}(a) = \min(1, 1) = 1$. Since $\text{low}(a) = \text{disc}(a)$, **SCC₁ = $\{a, b, c\}$** is popped.
- Continue DFS from $d$: $\text{disc}(d)=4$, visit $e$: $\text{disc}(e)=5$, $e \to c$ but $c$ already popped → ignore. Backtrack. DFS done.
- Now reach $c$ from previous DFS: but $c$ was already popped in SCC₁. Re-examine edges from $c$: $c \to d$. Since $c$ is in SCC₁ and $d$ is in SCC₂ but $c$ is *root* of SCC₁, this is a **cross-SCC edge**.
- **SCC₂ = $\{d, e\}$** (and $c$? Let's verify: $c \to d$ exists, $d \to e$, $e \to c$. So $\{c, d, e\}$ are mutually reachable → **SCC₂ = $\{c, d, e\}$**).

**Final SCCs:**
$$
\text{SCC}_1 = \{a, b, c\}, \quad \text{SCC}_2 = \{c, d, e\}
$$
Since $c$ belongs to *both* cycles and is mutually reachable with all five cells, the **largest SCC** is:
$$
\text{SCC}_{\text{full}} = \{a, b, c, d, e\}
$$

**Step 4: Influence on Liao-Wong Partitioning (2 marks):**

- All five cells $\{a, b, c, d, e\}$ **must be placed in the same partition** — Liao-Wong will **reject any swap** that separates them.
- Effectively, the entire hypergraph collapses into a **single super-node** for partitioning purposes.
- If $k$-way partitioning is required with $k > 1$, and the SCC dominates the circuit, Liao-Wong may fail to meet the balance constraint → the designer must either: (i) **duplicate cells** on the SCC boundary, or (ii) **break feedback loops** at the RTL level (insert pipeline registers), or (iii) relax the SCC constraint for specific cells.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $D(v)$ and $I(v)$ definitions:** $D(v)$ = nets that will be *cut/uncut* when $v$ moves; $I(v)$ = nets that will become *fully internal* to the source side after $v$ leaves. Many students reverse these, getting negative gains everywhere.
> 2. **Forgetting the SCC constraint:** Liao-Wong is *not* pure KL. You MUST state the SCC + timing-augmented gain. Losing 1–2 marks for not mentioning them.
> 3. **Ignoring the cumulative gain $G_{k^*}$ concept:** A single high-gain move does NOT mean commit immediately. You must record $G_k$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$ and commit only up to $k^* = \arg\max G_k$. This is a classic 2-mark trap.
> 4. **Forgetting balance factor $\beta$:** A valid partition must satisfy $\vert P_i \vert \leq (1+\beta) \cdot \vert V \vert / k$. Skipping this loses marks.
> 5. **Confusing Liao-Wong with FM:** FM is *single-cell move* and *linear time*; Liao-Wong is *pair-wise swap* with *timing/SCC integration*. Examiners love this distinction.
> 6. **In gain tables, omitting $I(v)$ column:** Always show both $D(v)$ AND $I(v)$, not just the difference.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Liao-Wong = **time-driven, multi-way, iterative-improvement** partitioning.
- ✅ Year: **1983**; Authors: **Liao & Wong**; Predecessor: **Kernighan-Lin (1970)**.
- ✅ Operates on a **hypergraph** $H = (V, E)$ with $k$-way output.
- ✅ **Composite gain:** $g_{LW}(v) = [D(v) - I(v)] + \lambda \cdot \Delta T(v)$.
- ✅ **Two preprocessing steps:** Longest Path (via topological DP) + Tarjan's SCC.
- ✅ **SCCs are collapsed** to super-nodes and **must not be split**.
- ✅ **Critical path** $\mathcal{P}_{crit}$ is identified by longest-path DP; cells on it carry a **timing penalty/reward** $\Delta T(v)$.
- ✅ **Time complexity per pass:** $O(n^2 \log n)$.
- ✅ **Balance constraint:** $\vert P_i \vert \leq (1+\beta) \cdot \vert V \vert / k$, $\beta \in [0.05, 0.10]$.
- ✅ **Cumulative gain $G_{k^*}$:** commit swaps only up to $k^* = \arg\max_k G_k$.
- ✅ **Stop condition:** $G_{k^*} \leq 0$ (no improvement possible).
- ✅ **Real-world use:** Multi-FPGA prototyping, timing-driven floorplanning, hierarchical ASIC design.
- ✅ **Distinguishes from FM** by: pair-wise moves, timing awareness, SCC handling, native $k$-way support.
- ✅ **Drawback:** $O(n^2 \log n)$ per pass is expensive; modern tools use multi-level hypergraph partitioning (e.g., hMETIS) for million-cell designs, but Liao-Wong remains foundational.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Placement: Optimization Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Placement: Optimization Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the VLSI physical design flow, **Placement** is the stage immediately following Floorplanning (or logic synthesis & technology mapping) and preceding Global Routing. It is the process of assigning exact geometric coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ to every movable standard cell, macro, and I/O pad inside a pre-defined fixed-die (or variable-die) canvas.

The **Optimization Objectives of Placement** constitute the formal mathematical cost functions and constraint equations that a placement engine attempts to minimize (or satisfy) while distributing these cells. The canonical objectives recognized in modern academic placers (e.g., RePlAce, DREAMPlace, NTUplace4dr) and industry EDA tools (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2, Siemens Calibre) are:

1. **Total Wirelength (WL)** — a proxy for routing congestion and signal delay.
2. **Timing Closure (T)** — minimization of worst negative slack (WNS) and total negative slack (TNS).
3. **Routability / Congestion Overflow** — keeping routing demand below routing supply per bin.
4. **Area / Row Density** — maximum cell area bounded by row capacity.
5. **Power (P)** — both dynamic switching power and leakage.
6. **Thermal & DRV (Design Rule Violation) Objectives** — maximum displacement, maximum capacitance, maximum fanout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the ESE (End Semester Evaluation), examiners expect students to know that placement is a **multi-objective constrained optimization problem** formally expressed as:
> $$\min_{x,y} \; \Phi(x, y) \;=\; w_1 \cdot \text{WL}(x,y) + w_2 \cdot \text{Timing}(x,y) + w_3 \cdot \text{Congestion}(x,y)$$
> subject to density $\rho_k \le \rho_{\max}$ for every global bin $k$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Furniture Layout Problem"

Imagine you have just moved into a large rectangular living room (the **chip canvas**) and you have dozens of furniture pieces (the **standard cells**) of varying sizes. You also have a *wishlist* of connections — for example, "the TV remote must reach the TV," "the sofa must be close to the reading lamp," and "the dining table must be near the kitchen door." These connections represent **nets** with different priorities (critical vs. non-critical).

Your **optimization objectives** translate naturally to interior design goals:

| Interior Design Goal | VLSI Placement Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Minimize walking distance between connected furniture | **Wirelength minimization** |
| Don't overload one corner with too many wires | **Congestion reduction** |
| Don't let the critical "TV-to-remote" path be blocked by a wall | **Timing closure** (critical path delay) |
| Don't let a heavy sofa overhang a balcony | **Density / Row utilization** constraint |
| Keep the room from overheating | **Thermal/power density** |

The placement engine is therefore a "robotic interior designer" running a sophisticated search to find the coordinate of every piece that **simultaneously** satisfies all the above goals — a task that is provably NP-hard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Recap:** Placement Optimization is the disciplined, weighted, multi-objective minimization of a global cost function governing the geometric coordinates of circuit elements subject to geometric, electrical, and manufacturability constraints.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Master Cost Function

A modern global placer solves a problem of the form:

$$\min_{x,y} \; \Phi(x,y) \;=\; \underbrace{W_{\text{WL}} \cdot \text{HPWL}(x,y)}_{\text{(a) Wirelength}} + \underbrace{W_{\text{T}} \cdot \max_{p \in \text{paths}} (T_{\text{slack}}(p))}_{\text{(b) Timing}} + \underbrace{W_{\text{C}} \cdot \sum_{b} \text{overflow}(b)}_{\text{(c) Congestion}}$$

$$\text{subject to:} \quad \rho_k(x,y) \le \rho_{\max}, \quad \forall k \in \text{bins}$$

We will analyze each objective in detail.

### (a) Wirelength — The Primary Objective

**Why wirelength?** Empirical studies (since the 1970s — Kernighan, Dunlop) have proven that wirelength strongly correlates with:
- Routing congestion (longer nets occupy more routing tracks).
- Signal delay (longer wires $\Rightarrow$ higher capacitance and resistance).
- Power dissipation (longer wires $\Rightarrow$ higher switching capacitance).

**How is it estimated?** Direct computation of Steiner Minimum Tree (SMT) length is NP-hard, so placers use *convex, differentiable surrogates*:

1. **Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL)** — the most common, exact for 2-pin nets and a tight bound for $k$-pin nets.
2. **Quadratic Wirelength** — used in classical quadratic placers (GORDIAN, KraftWerk).
3. **Log-Sum-Exp (LSE / Smoothed HPWL)** — used in modern analytical placers (ePlace, RePlAce).

### (b) Timing Objective

The timing cost penalizes long critical-path delays:

$$T_p = T_{\text{prop}} + R_{\text{drv}} \cdot C_L + \sum_{(u,v) \in p} R_{uv} \cdot C_{uv}$$

Critical paths are usually pre-identified by **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** using SDF back-annotation. Cells on these paths are *weighted* more heavily in the wirelength cost, biasing the placer to shorten the critical nets.

### (c) Congestion Objective

The chip canvas is divided into a $g \times g$ grid of **global bins** $b$. For each bin:

$$\text{overflow}(b) \;=\; \max\bigl(0,\; D(b) - S(b)\bigr)$$

where $D(b)$ is the *routing demand* (estimated number of nets passing through) and $S(b)$ is the *routing supply* (available tracks).

### (d) Density Constraint (Hard)

Cell density $\rho_k$ in bin $k$ must not exceed $\rho_{\max}$ (typically 0.9 to 1.0). This is the **non-overlapping legalizer** constraint enforced via a density penalty (penalty function $\hat{\Phi}_v$ in ePlace-style placers):

$$\hat{\Phi}_v(x, y) \;=\; \sum_{v \in \text{cells}} \Bigl[ \sum_{b} \Pi_b(x_v, y_v) \cdot \bigl( D_b(x, y) - \gamma_b \bigr) \Bigr]$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric / Quantity | Formula | Application | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HPWL (2-pin) | $\text{HPWL} = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ | Wirelength bound | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 2 | HPWL (k-pin) | $\text{HPWL} = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$ | Bounding box proxy | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 3 | Quadratic WL | $W_Q = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij}\bigl[(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2\bigr]$ | Classical Q-placement | $\mu\text{m}^2$ |
| 4 | LSE / Smoothed HPWL | $W_{\text{LSE}} = \gamma \sum w_{ij} \ln\!\Bigl( e^{(x_i - x_j)/\gamma} + e^{-(x_i - x_j)/\gamma} \Bigr) + \cdots$ | Analytical placement | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 5 | Elmore Delay | $T_{\text{Elmore}} = \sum_{k} C_k \cdot R_{k \to \text{sink}}$ | RC delay estimate | $\text{ps}$ |
| 6 | Cell Density in Bin | $\rho_b = \dfrac{\sum_{v \in b} a_v}{A_b}$ | Density constraint | dimensionless |
| 7 | Congestion Overflow | $\text{ovf}_b = \max(0, D_b - S_b)$ | Routability | tracks |
| 8 | Dynamic Power | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$ | Switching power | $\text{W}$ |
| 9 | Total Cost (Linear WL) | $\Phi = \sum_{(i,j)} w_{ij}\bigl(\vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert\bigr)$ | Min-cut placers | $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 10 | Critical Path Delay | $T_p = \sum_{e \in p} (R_e \cdot C_e) + T_{\text{setup}}$ | Timing | $\text{ns}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used above:** $w_{ij}$ is the *net weight* (often 1, but increased to 2 or 3 for critical nets), $\gamma$ is the *smoothing parameter* (gradually decreased in ePlace from $10\times$ cell-size to $0.01\times$ cell-size), and $C_L$ is the load capacitance.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **mobile SoC design** (Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon), placement directly determines whether the chip meets the **5 GHz clock target** within the 1 W power envelope.
- In **AI accelerator ASICs** (Google TPU, NVIDIA GPUs), placement of SRAM macros vs. compute logic is the #1 lever for both **yield** and **frequency**.
- In **FPGA architecture mapping** (Xilinx Vivado, Intel Quartus), placement objectives include not only wirelength but also **routability through switch boxes**, making the optimization problem strictly harder than the ASIC case.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL) from Bounding Box

The HPWL is the sum of the horizontal and vertical spans of the smallest axis-aligned bounding box enclosing all pins of a net.

**Step 1.** For a net $n$ with pins at coordinates $\{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_k, y_k)\}$, define the extreme coordinates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\min} &= \min_{1 \le i \le k} x_i \\
x_{\max} &= \max_{1 \le i \le k} x_i \\
y_{\min} &= \min_{1 \le i \le k} y_i \\
y_{\max} &= \max_{1 \le i \le k} y_i
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The bounding box is the tightest axis-aligned rectangle that contains all pins. Any rectilinear Steiner tree (and any rectilinear routing) of the net must have total length at least equal to the half-perimeter of this box, which is a lower bound used universally by the Gnu Placement format and EDA tools.

**Step 2.** The half-perimeter is half the perimeter of this rectangle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{box}} &= 2(x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + 2(y_{\max} - y_{\min}) \\
\text{HPWL} &= \frac{1}{2} P_{\text{box}} = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The factor ½ arises because in a rectilinear Steiner tree the horizontal and vertical edges sum to half the perimeter when each dimension is traversed once. For a 2-pin net this is **exact** (it equals the Manhattan distance).

**Step 3.** Total chip HPWL is summed over all nets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HPWL}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \Bigl[ (x_{\max}^{(n)} - x_{\min}^{(n)}) + (y_{\max}^{(n)} - y_{\min}^{(n)}) \Bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the function that commercial placers (Cadence GigaPlacer, Synopsys PrimeTime-driven placement) minimize using gradient-based or min-cut heuristics.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quadratic Wirelength Cost Function and Its Gradient

The quadratic cost is foundational because it allows a *closed-form solution* via the Laplacian system of equations.

**Step 1.** Define the cost function for a graph $G = (V, E)$ where each edge $(i,j)$ corresponds to a 2-pin net with weight $w_{ij}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(x, y) &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \Bigl[ (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 \Bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The factor ½ is convention to simplify gradients; it does not change the minimizer. Squaring penalizes long nets quadratically, which gives a *very strong* pull on distant cells (this is the main reason quadratic placers tend to "pull everything to the center" if unconstrained).

**Step 2.** Differentiate with respect to a free variable $x_i$ (assuming cell $i$ is *movable*; I/O pads and macros have fixed coordinates):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_i} &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j : (i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot 2 (x_i - x_j) \\
&= \sum_{j : (i,j) \in E} w_{ij} (x_i - x_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Chain rule on $(x_i - x_j)^2$ gives $2(x_i - x_j)$ for the partial with respect to $x_i$, and $-2(x_i - x_j)$ for the partial with respect to $x_j$ (symmetry preserves the global structure).

**Step 3.** Set the gradient to zero for optimality:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_i} = 0 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad x_i \sum_{j} w_{ij} = \sum_{j} w_{ij} x_j \\
&\Rightarrow \quad Q \, \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Q$ is the **Laplacian matrix** of the net hypergraph and $\mathbf{c}$ is a vector carrying contributions from fixed terminals.

**Step 4.** The Laplacian has the explicit form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{ii} &= \sum_{j : (i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \\
Q_{ij} &= -w_{ij} \quad \text{for } (i,j) \in E \\
Q_{ij} &= 0 \quad \text{otherwise}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* $Q$ is symmetric, positive semi-definite, and singular (its null space contains the all-ones vector). Fixing the I/O pads makes the reduced system positive definite and uniquely solvable by Cholesky factorization in $O(n^{1.5})$ time, or by Conjugate Gradient in $O(n \sqrt{\kappa}) \cdot \text{nnz}$ time.

**Step 5.** Solve independently for $x$ and $y$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}^{*} = Q^{-1} \mathbf{c}_x, \quad \mathbf{y}^{*} = Q^{-1} \mathbf{c}_y
\end{aligned}
$$

This produces a *global minimum* of the quadratic cost in one shot — but the result is often illegal (overlapping cells), requiring either a *look-ahead legalization* (GORDIAN) or a *force-directed spreading* step (KraftWerk).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Log-Sum-Exp (Smoothed HPWL) Approximation

Modern analytical placers (ePlace, RePlAce) avoid HPWL's non-differentiability by introducing a smooth surrogate.

**Step 1.** Replace the max in $x_{\max}$ with the log-sum-exp function. Define for a $k$-pin net:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_{\max} &= \gamma \ln\!\Bigl( \sum_{i=1}^{k} e^{x_i / \gamma} \Bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

As $\gamma \to 0$, $\hat{x}_{\max} \to x_{\max}$ exactly.

*Conversion logic:* The log-sum-exp is a *smooth maximum* — it is the convex conjugate of the softmax, infinitely differentiable, and used ubiquitously in machine learning (cross-entropy loss). EDA adopted it via the **NTUplace3** (2008) and **ePlace** (2013) papers.

**Step 2.** Similarly for the minimum, use the identity $\min_i x_i = -\max_i(-x_i)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_{\min} &= -\gamma \ln\!\Bigl( \sum_{i=1}^{k} e^{-x_i / \gamma} \Bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** The smoothed HPWL is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SmoothWL}(x, y) &= \gamma \sum_{n} \Biggl[ \ln\!\Bigl( \sum_{i \in n} e^{x_i/\gamma} \Bigr) + \ln\!\Bigl( \sum_{i \in n} e^{-x_i/\gamma} \Bigr) \\
&\quad + \ln\!\Bigl( \sum_{i \in n} e^{y_i/\gamma} \Bigr) + \ln\!\Bigl( \sum_{i \in n} e^{-y_i/\gamma} \Bigr) \Biggr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Gradient with respect to $x_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \,\text{SmoothWL}}{\partial x_i} &= \sum_{n \ni i} \frac{e^{x_i/\gamma}}{\sum_{j \in n} e^{x_j/\gamma}} - \frac{e^{-x_i/\gamma}}{\sum_{j \in n} e^{-x_j/\gamma}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Each term in the sum is a *softmax weight*. For each net, the gradient pushes $x_i$ toward the larger of $\{x_i, x_j\}$ values. The placer anneals $\gamma$ from large to small to first spread cells globally and then refine locally.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trade-off Between Objectives

| Objective Pair | Nature of Conflict | Common Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| WL vs. Timing | Long critical nets often need to be *spread* for buffering — increasing WL | Net-weight scaling, useful-skew clock tree |
| WL vs. Congestion | Wirelength-driven placement can create hot-spots | Congestion-driven (e.g., RUDY) weighting |
| WL vs. Density | Pure WL minimization $\Rightarrow$ cell clustering | Penalty function (ePlace, NTUplace4) |
| Power vs. Timing | Lower $V_{dd}$ $\Rightarrow$ slower but cooler | Multi-$V_{dd}$ (MSV) assignment |
| Area vs. WL | Smaller area $\Rightarrow$ more routability pressure | Utilization tuning at floorplan |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Global Placement Optimization Flow

The figure below depicts the canonical closed-loop flow of a modern analytical placer (ePlace / RePlAce family), where the cost function is *minimized* and the density is *spread* in alternating iterations until a legalized solution is reached.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Random Placement of Movable Cells] --> B[Compute Net Bounding Boxes]
    B --> C[Calculate Wirelength Cost: HPWL or LSE]
    C --> D[Calculate Density Penalty: Overlap Function]
    D --> E[Combine into Master Cost Function Phi]
    E --> F[Compute Gradient using Laplacian and Overlap Force]
    F --> G{Convergence Check: Gradient and Step Size Below Threshold}
    G -->|Not Converged| H[Update Cell Positions using Nesterov / Conjugate Gradient]
    H --> I[Anneal Smoothing Parameter gamma]
    I --> B
    G -->|Converged| J[Legalization: Snap to Rows and Remove Overlaps]
    J --> K[Detailed Placement: Local Swaps and Cell Shifting]
    K --> L[Output Legal Placement Solution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *outer loop* (B → C → D → E → F) is the cost-evaluation inner step. The *inner loop* (G → H → I) is the optimization update. Once the gradient norm drops below a threshold (typically $10^{-4}$), the placer exits the global stage and hands off to the **legalizer** (e.g., Tetris or Abacus).

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Multi-Objective Cost Composition

The diagram below maps how individual objectives feed into the master cost function. Each node represents a *functional module* of a placer (not a physical circuit).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Netlist Graph: Cells and Nets] --> B1[Wirelength Estimator]
    A --> B2[Static Timing Analyzer]
    A --> B3[Congestion Predictor: RUDY or LCM]
    A --> B4[Power and Density Mapper]
    B1 --> C1[WL Cost: HPWL or SmoothWL]
    B2 --> C2[Timing Cost: TNS or WNS]
    B3 --> C3[Congestion Overflow Penalty]
    B4 --> C4[Power Cost and Density Cost]
    C1 --> D[Weighted Aggregation]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Master Objective Function Phi x y]
    E --> F[Gradient Solver: CG or Nesterov]
    F --> G[Position Update Step]
    G --> H[Iterate until Convergence]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Tree — Wirelength Models

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Wirelength Objective Families] --> B[Exact but Expensive]
    A --> C[Approximate but Differentiable]
    A --> D[Bounding Box Methods]
    B --> B1[Steiner Minimum Tree: NP-Hard]
    B --> B2[Rectilinear Minimum Spanning Tree: Polynomial]
    C --> C1[Quadratic Cost: Fast Closed Form]
    C --> C2[Log-Sum-Exp Smoothed HPWL: Used in ePlace]
    C --> C3[Weighted Average: Used in NTUplace]
    D --> D1[Half-Perimeter HPWL: Bounding Box]
    D --> D2[Channel Bounding Box: Better k-pin Est]
    D --> D3[Star Bounding Box: Bounding Box Star]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the placement optimization problem in VLSI physical design and list its four primary objectives.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Placement optimization is the process of determining exact $(x, y)$ coordinates for all movable cells on a chip canvas so as to minimize a weighted sum of cost functions subject to non-overlap and routability constraints. The **four primary objectives** are:

1. **Wirelength minimization** — typically measured as HPWL.
2. **Timing closure** — minimization of critical-path delay, WNS, and TNS.
3. **Routability / Congestion reduction** — keeping per-bin demand below supply.
4. **Density / Area utilization** — satisfying row-by-row non-overlap constraints.

*(1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the four objectives with one-line explanations.)*

### Question 2: What is HPWL and why is it the preferred wirelength metric in placement?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**HPWL (Half-Perimeter Wire Length)** is the sum of the horizontal span and the vertical span of the smallest axis-aligned bounding box enclosing all pins of a net:

$$\text{HPWL}(n) = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$$

It is preferred because (1) it is **exact for 2-pin nets** (equals Manhattan distance), (2) it is a **tight lower bound** on rectilinear Steiner tree length for $k$-pin nets, (3) it is **trivial to compute** in $O(k)$ per net, and (4) it is **highly correlated** with actual routed wirelength and routing congestion.

*(1 mark for the formula, 1 mark each for any two reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** out of an internal-choice pair.

---

### Question A (Choice 1): Quadratic Placement Cost Function
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Formulate the **quadratic wirelength cost function** for a netlist. Derive the gradient with respect to the position of a movable cell and show that the optimal solution satisfies a linear system $Q\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}$, where $Q$ is the Laplacian matrix.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1.** Define the cost function. For a netlist graph $G = (V, E)$ with cells at coordinates $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}$ and edge weights $w_{ij}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(x, y) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \Bigl[ (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 \Bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Take the partial derivative with respect to $x_i$ (assume $i$ is movable):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_i} = \sum_{j : (i,j) \in E} w_{ij} (x_i - x_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3.** Set $\partial \Phi / \partial x_i = 0$ for optimality:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_i \sum_{j} w_{ij} - \sum_{j} w_{ij} x_j &= 0 \\
x_i \sum_{j} w_{ij} &= \sum_{j} w_{ij} x_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Assemble into matrix form. The Laplacian $Q$ has entries $Q_{ii} = \sum_j w_{ij}$ and $Q_{ij} = -w_{ij}$ for $(i,j) \in E$. For fixed I/O pads (with known $x_j$), the right-hand side vector $\mathbf{c}_x$ is computed. The system is $Q \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}_x$, and similarly for $\mathbf{y}$.

**Step 5.** Note that $Q$ is symmetric, positive *semi*-definite, and **singular** for the unconstrained problem. Fixing I/O pads makes the reduced system positive definite and solvable by Cholesky or Conjugate Gradient.

*(Rubric: 2 marks for the formulation, 2 marks for the gradient, 2 marks for the optimality condition, 1 mark for the matrix assembly.)*

---

**(b) [7 marks]** What are the **limitations** of quadratic wirelength placement, and how does the **log-sum-exp (LSE)** formulation overcome them? Write the LSE surrogate for the $x_{\max}$ component and explain the role of the smoothing parameter $\gamma$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Limitations of Quadratic Placement (3 marks):**
1. Quadratic cost penalizes *long* nets super-linearly, causing all cells to collapse to the **center of gravity** of the netlist (a "star-shaped" cluster).
2. The result is **highly illegal** — cells overlap massively, requiring aggressive (and lossy) legalization.
3. Quadratic cost is a *very loose upper bound* on true wirelength because the cost is computed on pairwise complete graphs (clique model) rather than Steiner trees.

**LSE Formulation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{x}_{\max} = \gamma \ln\!\Bigl( \sum_{i \in n} e^{x_i / \gamma} \Bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

As $\gamma \to 0$, $\hat{x}_{\max} \to \max_i x_i$ exactly. The LSE is **infinitely differentiable**, allowing gradient-based optimization.

**Role of $\gamma$ (2 marks):**
$\gamma$ is a *temperature-like* smoothing parameter. In the placer's outer loop, $\gamma$ is gradually annealed from a large value (e.g., 100× cell-height, which yields a *coarse, convex* landscape) to a small value (e.g., 0.01× cell-height, which yields an *accurate HPWL approximation*). This **gradual refinement** allows the placer to escape local minima and converges to a near-optimal placement.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Multi-Objective Formulation and Congestion
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Formulate the **multi-objective placement problem** explicitly, listing the wirelength, timing, and density terms. Explain how **net weighting** is used to bias the placer toward timing-critical nets.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Multi-objective formulation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x, y} \; \Phi(x, y) = W_{\text{WL}} \cdot \text{HPWL}(x, y) + W_{\text{T}} \cdot \text{TNS}(x, y) + W_{\text{C}} \cdot \sum_{b} \text{overflow}(b)
\end{aligned}
$$

subject to:
- Cell density $\rho_b \le \rho_{\max}$ for every global bin $b$.
- All cells lie inside the canvas boundary.
- Fixed macros and I/O pads are not moved.

**Net-weighting for timing (3 marks):**
After Static Timing Analysis (STA) reports a set of critical paths with negative slack, the placer **re-weights** the nets on these paths. A common heuristic is:
$$w_{ij} \leftarrow 1 + \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\text{slack}_{ij}}$$

Nets with very small (or negative) slack receive very large weights, causing the placer to shorten them at the expense of non-critical nets. The parameter $\alpha$ is tuned empirically; values of 1.5–3.0 are common in industry flows.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **routability-driven placement** objective. How is **congestion overflow** computed, and what placer-level actions are taken when overflow exceeds a threshold?

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Routability objective (3 marks):**
The chip is divided into a $g \times g$ grid of *global bins*. For each bin $b$, the **routing demand** $D(b)$ is the number of nets crossing $b$, estimated using a probabilistic model (e.g., **RUDY — Rectilinear Uniform wire DensitY**, or the **LCM — Local Congestion Model**). The **routing supply** $S(b)$ is the number of available tracks.

**Overflow computation (2 marks):**
$$\text{overflow}(b) = \max\bigl(0, D(b) - S(b)\bigr)$$
Total overflow is $\sum_b \text{overflow}(b)$, and the placer adds this to the master cost function with weight $W_C$.

**Placer-level actions (2 marks):**
1. **Cell inflation** — cells in over-congested regions are slightly enlarged to push neighboring cells away.
2. **Net re-routing preference** — nets that pass through hot bins are given *alternate bounding boxes* that avoid the hot region.
3. **Macro placement adjustment** — large macros may be **flipped or shifted** to redistribute congestion.
4. **Iterative global routing** — modern flows invoke a fast global router (e.g., FastRoute 4.0) within the placement loop.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Conflating HPWL with Manhattan distance:** HPWL is *exact* for 2-pin nets but only a *lower bound* for $k$-pin nets. Writing $\text{HPWL} = \sum \vert x_i - x_j \vert$ (sum over all pairs) is the **clique model**, not HPWL — this is a frequent and costly error.
> 2. **Forgetting the smoothing parameter:** In LSE formulations, the role of $\gamma$ is *not* just "a small constant" — it controls the *optimization landscape*. Examiners explicitly award 2 marks for explaining the annealing schedule.
> 3. **Confusing the Laplacian:** Writing $Q_{ij} = w_{ij}$ (instead of $Q_{ij} = -w_{ij}$) gives a *positive definite* but *incorrect* system that minimizes the wrong cost. This is a **-2 to -3 mark deduction** in valuation.
> 4. **Skipping the density constraint:** Many students write the cost function without stating the **subject-to** clause for cell density. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for missing constraints.
> 5. **Mixing up LSE and LCM:** LSE = Log-Sum-Exp (wirelength smoothing). LCM = Local Congestion Model (RUDY's successor). Writing "LSE for congestion" will be marked **wrong** with no partial credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Placement** assigns exact $(x, y)$ coordinates to all movable cells. It is **NP-hard**; hence placers use heuristic cost functions.
- The canonical **master cost function** is a weighted sum of **HPWL (wirelength)**, **timing penalty (TNS/WNS)**, **congestion overflow**, and **density penalty**.
- **HPWL** = $(x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$ — exact for 2-pin nets, lower bound for $k$-pin nets, *not* the clique sum.
- **Quadratic placement** minimizes $\sum w_{ij}[(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2]$; the optimal solution is a Laplacian linear system $Q\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{c}$, but the result clusters cells (illegal).
- **Log-Sum-Exp (LSE)** is the *smooth, differentiable* surrogate of HPWL used in ePlace / RePlAce; the smoothing parameter $\gamma$ is annealed from large to small.
- **Net weighting** is the standard mechanism for embedding timing into wirelength-driven placement: $w_{ij} = 1 + \alpha / \text{slack}_{ij}$.
- **Density** $\rho_b = (\sum a_v) / A_b$ is the hard constraint that forces non-overlapping placement; it is enforced via penalty functions (ePlace, NTUplace4).
- **Congestion** is estimated per-bin by probabilistic models (RUDY, LCM); overflow $\text{ovf}_b = \max(0, D_b - S_b)$.
- **Industrial placers** (Innovus, ICC2) are *timing- and congestion-driven*, with **incremental placement** (ECO) capability for late design changes.
- The placer must be **legality-preserving** at the end; otherwise the chip is unmanufacturable.
- The **flow** is: Global placement (analytic) → Legalization (Tetris/Abacus) → Detailed placement (local swaps) → Sign-off DRC/LVS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wirelength Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wirelength Estimation --- # Module 3: Wirelength Estimation in VLSI Physical Design ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Wirelength Estimation** is the computational procedure used during the *floorplanning* and *placement* stages of VLSI physical design to predict the total length of interconnects required to connect a set of **modules (blocks/cells)** within a chip. Because the exact routing is performed only after placement, early design stages require *fast, geometric, model-based estimates* of wiring cost to evaluate candidate layouts. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, wirelength estimation belongs to **Cost Functions in Floorplanning**, where the estimated wirelength directly drives the optimization objective: $$\text{Total Cost} = \alpha \cdot A_{\text{chip}} + \beta \cdot W_{\text{est}}$$ where $A_{\text{chip}}$ is the chip area, $W_{\text{est}}$ is the estimated wirelength, and $\alpha, \beta$ are user-defined weights. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wirelength estimation models are classified by their **accuracy vs. computational complexity** trade-off. Examiners frequently ask students to *compare two models* on the basis of these two metrics. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are an **urban city planner** asked to lay down roads connecting several new housing colonies on a map. You don't yet know the exact curves of the roads, but you must estimate how much asphalt (wirelength) will be needed *before* you commit to the layout. - **Naïve approach (Clique):** Connect every colony to every other colony with a straight road → wasteful estimate (over-estimates). - **Practical approach (MST/Steiner):** Find the *minimum* network of straight roads (possibly adding a junction/Steiner point in the middle) that connects all colonies → realistic estimate. - **Fastest approach (HPWL/Bounding Box):** Just measure the *rectangular envelope* around the colonies → fastest but coarser. In VLSI, modules play the role of "colonies" and the routing wires play the role of "roads." > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Used in Industry:** The unit of wirelength is **micrometers (μm)** for sub-micron nodes and **nanometers (nm)** for deep-submicron (DSM) technologies. The total wirelength of a modern SoC is typically in the range of **kilometers (km)** — making accurate estimation critical for timing closure. ### 1.3 Why Wirelength Estimation Matters | Reason | Engineering Impact | |---|---| | **Delay** | Wirelength $\uparrow$ → propagation delay $\uparrow$ (longer $RC$ path) | | **Power** | Dynamic power $\propto C_{wire} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ → larger wires = higher capacitance | | **Congestion** | Long wires cross more routing tracks → routing congestion rises | | **Manufacturability** | Excessive wirelength increases the chance of opens/shorts during fabrication | | **Cost** | More metal layers / vias needed → die cost rises | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Wirelength Estimation Models The KTU syllabus distinguishes **five primary estimation models**, ordered roughly by increasing accuracy and computational cost: #### A. Complete Graph (Clique) Model - Each module pair is assumed to be directly connected by a dedicated wire. - $W_{\text{clique}} = \sum_{i < j} d(i, j)$ - **Pros:** Simple. **Cons:** Severely over-estimates; never used in practice. #### B. Star Model - One **central hub** (e.g., center of chip) connects to every module. - $W_{\text{star}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d(O, M_i)$ where $O$ is the hub. - **Pros:** Trivial computation. **Cons:** Hub-centric; doesn't reflect real two-terminal nets. #### C. Bounding Box (HPWL) Model ⭐ Most Used in Industry - The **Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL)** is the semi-perimeter of the *minimum bounding rectangle* enclosing all pins of a net. - $$W_{\text{HPWL}} = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$$ - **Pros:** Linear-time, highly correlated with post-route wirelength. **Cons:** Ignores pin distribution inside the box. #### D. Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) Model - Builds a graph connecting all pins with the *minimum total edge weight* (no Steiner points). - Computed in $O(n \log n)$ using **Kruskal's** or **Prim's** algorithm. - $W_{\text{MST}} \approx 0.85 \times W_{\text{Steiner}}$ empirically. #### E.
