Rectilinear Minimum Spanning Tree (RMST) - MST where distance is measured using **Manhattan (rectilinear / $L_1$) distance**: - $$d_{L_1}(P_i, P_j) = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$$ - This is the *true* Manhattan metric; standard in VLSI because routing is constrained to horizontal/vertical tracks. #### F. Rectilinear Minimum Steiner Tree (RMST / RST) — Most Accurate - Allows **Steiner points** (junctions not on any pin) added at $L_1$ geometry to reduce total length. - Computed via **Hanan Grid** (1966 Hanan's theorem): Steiner points are restricted to the grid formed by drawing horizontal/vertical lines through every pin. - NP-hard in general — solved using heuristics (e.g., **1-Steiner heuristic** by Kahng & Robins). > [!NOTE] > **Hanan's Theorem (1966):** For a set of $n$ points, there exists an MRST whose Steiner points lie at the intersections of the *Hanan grid* — at most $\binom{n}{2}^2$ intersection points. This reduces the search space dramatically. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Model | Formula | Complexity | Accuracy | KTU Weight | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Complete Graph (Clique)** | $\sum_{i [!IMPORTANT] > **Unit Convention:** All distances are in the same length unit as the coordinate system. When coordinates are in *placement grid units*, wirelength is reported as grid units; convert to μm using the manufacturing pitch. ### 2.3 The Why and How Behind Each Step **Why HPWL is the industrial default:** - The HPWL of a net **lower-bounds** the MRST length (Hwang's theorem): $W_{\text{HPWL}} \le W_{\text{MRST}}$. - It is *additive* across nets: $\sum_{\text{nets}} \text{HPWL}_k = \text{HPWL}_{\text{total}}$, which makes cost-driven optimization mathematically tractable. - Computable in **one pass** over pin coordinates — crucial when evaluating millions of nets during simulated annealing in floorplanning. **Why Rectilinear Distance and Not Euclidean?** - VLSI routing layers are *rectilinear* — wires run horizontally in M1, vertically in M2, etc. - Two diagonal points $(0,0)$ and $(1,1)$ have Euclidean distance $\sqrt{2}$ but require **2 unit-length rectilinear segments** (one H + one V). - Hence the $L_1$ (Manhattan) metric is the *correct* engineering distance. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: HPWL Lower Bound for a Single Net **Claim:** For any single net connecting $k$ pins at positions $(x_i, y_i)$, the HPWL provides a lower bound on the rectilinear Steiner tree length. **Step 1 — Define the bounding box.** Let the bounding box $B$ have corners: $$x_{\min} = \min_i x_i, \quad x_{\max} = \max_i x_i$$ $$y_{\min} = \min_i y_i, \quad y_{\max} = \max_i y_i$$ **Step 2 — Write the HPWL expression.** $$\text{HPWL} = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$$ **Step 3 — Reason about the spanning tree.** Any tree connecting all $k$ pins must, at minimum, span the *horizontal extent* and the *vertical extent* of the bounding box. A path between the leftmost and rightmost pin contributes $\ge (x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ horizontal length. Similarly for vertical. **Step 4 — Formally prove the lower bound.** In any rectilinear spanning tree $T$ of the pins, the projection of $T$ onto the x-axis forms a connected subgraph of $\mathbb{R}$ containing every $x_i$. Such a subgraph must contain an interval covering $[x_{\min}, x_{\max}]$, contributing at least $(x_{\max} - x_{\min})$ horizontal length. The same applies to the y-projection. Summing: $$W_{\text{MRST}} \ge (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min}) = \text{HPWL}$$ **Step 5 — Conclude.** $$\boxed{\; \text{HPWL} \;\le\; W_{\text{MRST}}\;}$$ This is a strict bound used in *every modern placer* (e.g., GDSII tools like Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2). --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — All Models on the Same Net > **KTU-style 14-mark style question:** *Compute the wirelength of the net connecting the 4 pins given below using (a) HPWL and (b) Rectilinear MST.* **Given pins:** $A(0, 0),\ B(4, 0),\ C(4, 3),\ D(0, 3)$ #### (a) HPWL Calculation $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\min} &= 0, \quad x_{\max} = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta x = 4 \\ y_{\min} &= 0, \quad y_{\max} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta y = 3 \\ \text{HPWL} &= (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min}) \\ &= 4 + 3 \\ &= 7 \text{ units} \end{aligned} $$ #### (b) Rectilinear MST Calculation First, compute all pairwise $L_1$ distances: $$ \begin{aligned} d(A, B) &= \vert 0-4\vert + \vert 0-0\vert = 4 \\ d(A, C) &= \vert 0-4\vert + \vert 0-3\vert = 7 \\ d(A, D) &= \vert 0-0\vert + \vert 0-3\vert = 3 \\ d(B, C) &= \vert 4-4\vert + \vert 0-3\vert = 3 \\ d(B, D) &= \vert 4-0\vert + \vert 0-3\vert = 7 \\ d(C, D) &= \vert 4-0\vert + \vert 3-3\vert = 4 \end{aligned} $$ Apply **Kruskal's algorithm** (sort edges by weight, add if no cycle): | Step | Edge | Length | Action | Cumulative Cost | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | A–D | 3 | Add ✓ | 3 | | 2 | B–C | 3 | Add ✓ | 6 | | 3 | A–B | 4 | Skip (creates cycle A-D-C-B-A) | 6 | | 4 | C–D | 4 | Skip (creates cycle) | 6 | | 5 | A–C | 7 | Add ✓ (connects two components) | **13** | | 6 | B–D | 7 | Skip (cycle) | 13 | $$\boxed{\;W_{\text{MST}} = 3 + 3 + 7 = 13 \text{ units}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** For this symmetric 4-pin rectangle, MST = 13 > HPWL = 7, confirming the inequality. The HPWL underestimates by 6 units (≈46% error) because it ignores the *interior pin-to-pin* routing cost. This is exactly why modern placers refine HPWL with **place-and-route iteration**. #### (c) Rectilinear Steiner Tree (RST) Calculation For this rectangular configuration, the optimal RST simply uses the bounding box edges: $$W_{\text{RST}} = 2 \cdot (4 + 3) = 14 \text{ units (if routed around)}$$ OR using Steiner point at center $(2, 1.5)$: $$W_{\text{RST}} = 4 \cdot (\vert 2 \vert + \vert 1.5 \vert) = 4 \cdot 3.5 = 14 \text{ units (worse)}$$ Actually for this simple rectangle, the MST = RST = 13. The HPWL is 7 because routing is *one horizontal line + one vertical line* = 4 + 3 = 7 along the bounding box perimeter half. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students confuse HPWL with the *full perimeter* of the bounding box. HPWL is **half** the perimeter, not the full perimeter. The "H" in HPWL stands for **Half**-Perimeter. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite) | Property | HPWL | MST | Steiner Tree | |---|---|---|---| | Time Complexity | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | NP-hard | | Quality | Lower bound | Within ~85% of optimum | Optimum (heuristic) | | Implementation | Trivial | Standard graph algo | Heuristic (1-Steiner, BGA) | | Industrial Use | **Default** | Validation | Final route | | Add Steiner Points? | No | No | Yes | | Symmetric Net Behavior | Always exact | Variable | Variable | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Wirelength Estimation Pipeline in a Floorplanner ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: Netlist + Module Positions] --> B[Pin Coordinate Extraction] B --> C{Bounding Box Computation} C --> D[Compute x_max, x_min, y_max, y_min] D --> E[HPWL = Delta x + Delta y] B --> F{Pairwise Distance Matrix} F --> G[Kruskal or Prim Algorithm] G --> H[Rectilinear MST Cost] B --> I{Hanan Grid Construction} I --> J[Steiner Point Search] J --> K[Heuristic 1-Steiner / BGA] K --> L[Rectilinear Steiner Tree Cost] E --> M[Cost Aggregator] H --> M L --> M M --> N[Total Estimated Wirelength W_est] N --> O[Cost Function: alpha A + beta W] O --> P{Floorplan Optimization Loop} P --> Q[Accept / Reject New Layout] Q --> P ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The flow shows three parallel estimation paths (HPWL, MST, RST) that feed a *Cost Aggregator*. This is exactly how commercial floorplanners (e.g., **Blizzard**, **Capo**) compute cost during simulated annealing. --- ### 4.2 Bounding Box Visualization (HPWL Concept) ```mermaid graph LR P1((Pin A
0,0)) --- P2((Pin B
4,0)) P2 --- P4((Pin C
4,3)) P4 --- P3((Pin D
0,3)) P3 --- P1 style P1 fill:#FFD700 style P2 fill:#FFD700 style P3 fill:#FFD700 style P4 fill:#FFD700 ``` The four pins form a rectangle. HPWL is the sum of horizontal extent + vertical extent = 4 + 3 = **7 units**. --- ### 4.3 MST vs Steiner Tree Comparison (Architecture-Level) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph M1[HPWL Approach] H1[Pin 0,0] --- H2[Pin 6,0] H2 --- H3[Pin 6,4] H3 --- H4[Pin 0,4] H4 --- H1 end subgraph M2[MST Approach] A1[Pin 0,0] --- A2[Pin 6,0] A2 --- A3[Pin 6,4] A3 --- A4[Pin 0,4] end subgraph M3[Steiner Tree with Junction] S1[Pin 0,0] --- Sx((Steiner Point)) Sx --- S2[Pin 6,0] Sx --- S3[Pin 6,4] Sx --- S4[Pin 0,4] end ``` > **Interpretation:** The Steiner tree introduces an *extra junction* (Steiner point) that is not on any pin — this often reduces total length when pins form an L-shape or T-shape. --- ### 4.4 Decision Tree: Which Model Should I Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need Wirelength Estimate?] --> Q1{Stage of Design?} Q1 -->|Floorplanning| HPWL[Use HPWL] Q1 -->|Placement Refinement| MST[Use RMST] Q1 -->|Post-Route Validation| ST[Use RST] HPWL --> R1[Fast, Linear] MST --> R2[Accurate, n log n] ST --> R3[Best Quality, Expensive] R1 --> Choose[Choose based on time budget] R2 --> Choose R3 --> Choose ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. Define Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL). Why is it used as the default wirelength estimator in floorplanning?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Half-Perimeter Wire Length of a net is the semi-perimeter of the minimum bounding rectangle enclosing all pins of the net. It is given by: $$\text{HPWL} = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$$ **Why it is the default (1.5 marks):** 1. **Computational efficiency:** Computable in $O(n)$ time per net by simple min/max scans. 2. **Theoretical foundation:** It is a *provable lower bound* on the rectilinear minimum Steiner tree length (Hwang's theorem). 3. **Additivity:** Total wirelength across nets is simply the sum of per-net HPWLs — this makes it usable as a cost function for simulated annealing. 4. **High empirical correlation:** In real designs, HPWL correlates >90% with post-route wirelength. --- **Q2. State Hanan's theorem and explain its significance in Rectilinear Steiner Tree construction.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement (1 mark):** Hanan's Theorem (1966): *For a set of $n$ points in the Manhattan plane, there exists a Rectilinear Minimum Steiner Tree (MRST) whose Steiner points are all located at intersections of the Hanan grid — the grid formed by drawing vertical lines through every $x$-coordinate and horizontal lines through every $y$-coordinate of the pins.* **Significance (2 marks):** 1. The Hanan grid contains at most $n^2$ line intersections — it **reduces an infinite search space** to a finite, polynomial-sized candidate set. 2. It transforms the NP-hard MRST problem into a **search over candidate Steiner points**, enabling practical heuristic algorithms (e.g., 1-Steiner heuristic, BGA). 3. Without Hanan's theorem, no practical optimal/heuristic Steiner tree construction would be feasible. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ## Question A (14 Marks) — HPWL + MST Numerical `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** A net has 4 pins located at $A(1, 1),\ B(7, 1),\ C(7, 5),\ D(1, 5)$. Calculate the wirelength of this net using: (i) Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL) model. **(3 marks)** (ii) Rectilinear Minimum Spanning Tree (RMST) model. **(4 marks)** **(b)** Compare HPWL and RMST models with respect to accuracy, time complexity, and practical applicability in floorplanning. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution #### (a)(i) HPWL Calculation (3 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\min} &= 1, \quad x_{\max} = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta x = 6 \\ y_{\min} &= 1, \quad y_{\max} = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta y = 4 \\ \text{HPWL} &= (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min}) \\ &= 6 + 4 \\ &= \mathbf{10 \text{ units}} \end{aligned} $$ #### (a)(ii) RMST Calculation (4 marks) Pairwise $L_1$ distances: - $d(A,B) = 6+0 = 6$ - $d(A,C) = 6+4 = 10$ - $d(A,D) = 0+4 = 4$ - $d(B,C) = 0+4 = 4$ - $d(B,D) = 6+4 = 10$ - $d(C,D) = 6+0 = 6$ **Kruskal's MST construction:** | Step | Edge | Length | Action | Total | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | A–D | 4 | Add ✓ | 4 | | 2 | B–C | 4 | Add ✓ | 8 | | 3 | A–B | 6 | Add ✓ (connects components) | **14** | $$\boxed{\;W_{\text{RMST}} = 14 \text{ units}\;}$$ The MST edges form an L-shape: A→D (vertical, 4 units) + A→B (horizontal, 6 units) + B→C (vertical, 4 units) = 14 units. #### (b) HPWL vs RMST Comparison (7 marks) | Aspect | HPWL | RMST | |---|---|---| | **Formula** | $(x_{\max}-x_{\min})+(y_{\max}-y_{\min})$ | $\sum_{e \in \text{MST}} d_{L_1}(e)$ | | **Time Complexity** | $O(n)$ per net | $O(n \log n)$ per net (Kruskal/Prim) | | **Accuracy** | Lower bound; underestimates for spread-out pins | Tighter; within 5–15% of MRST | | **Implementation** | Trivial min/max scans | Requires graph construction + sorting | | **Use Case** | Floorplanning cost function | Placement refinement, validation | | **Scales to Millions of Nets** | Yes | Expensive; used in post-floorplan | | **Theoretical Bound** | $W_{\text{HPWL}} \le W_{\text{MRST}}$ (Hwang) | $W_{\text{MST}} \approx 0.85 W_{\text{MRST}}$ (Chung-Graham) | | **Pin Distribution** | Ignored | Considered | | **Net Topology** | Rectangle approximation | Real connection structure | **Conclusion:** HPWL is preferred during **iterative optimization** (simulated annealing, where millions of layouts are evaluated), while RMST is preferred during **final placement validation** when accuracy outweighs cost. --- ## Question B (14 Marks) — Steiner Tree Theory + Cost Function `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define a Rectilinear Minimum Steiner Tree (RMST). Explain the concept of a Steiner point and the construction of a Hanan grid with an example of 3 pins at $P_1(0, 0),\ P_2(4, 0),\ P_3(2, 3)$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A floorplan has 4 blocks with total area $A = 100 \text{ mm}^2$ and total estimated wirelength $W = 50 \text{ mm}$. If the cost weights are $\alpha = 2$ and $\beta = 0.5$, compute the total cost. Comment on how varying $\beta$ affects the optimization. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution #### (a) RMST + Hanan Grid (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** A *Rectilinear Minimum Steiner Tree (RMST)* is a tree, made of horizontal and vertical line segments, that connects a given set of pins using the *minimum total length*, where additional junction points (**Steiner points**) — not coincident with any pin — are allowed to reduce the total wirelength. **Steiner Point Concept (2 marks):** A Steiner point is a junction where three or more edges meet at right angles. Unlike an MST (which only uses pins as nodes), an RST may introduce such points when doing so strictly *reduces* total wirelength. **Hanan Grid Construction (3 marks):** For 3 pins $P_1(0,0),\ P_2(4,0),\ P_3(2,3)$: - Vertical lines: $x = 0,\ x = 2,\ x = 4$ - Horizontal lines: $y = 0,\ y = 3$ - Hanan grid intersection points (excluding pin points): $(0, 3),\ (2, 3),\ (4, 3),\ (2, 0)$ For this L-shaped pin set, the MRST introduces a Steiner point at $(2, 0)$: $$\text{RST edges: } P_1 \to (2,0) \to P_2 \text{ (horizontal)}, \quad (2,0) \to P_3 \text{ (vertical)}$$ $$W_{\text{RST}} = 2 + 2 + 3 = 7 \text{ units} < W_{\text{MST}} = 8 \text{ units (without Steiner)}$$ #### (b) Cost Function (7 marks) **Computation (3 marks):** $$\Phi = \alpha \cdot A + \beta \cdot W$$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Phi &= 2 \cdot 100 + 0.5 \cdot 50 \\ &= 200 + 25 \\ &= \mathbf{225 \text{ cost units}} \end{aligned} $$ **Effect of Varying $\beta$ (4 marks):** | $\beta$ Value | Effect on Optimization | |---|---| | $\beta = 0$ | Pure area minimization; wirelength is ignored. May produce poorly connected layouts with long wires. | | $\beta$ small (e.g., 0.1) | Area-dominated optimization; minor wirelength reduction. | | $\beta = 0.5$ (given) | Balanced trade-off — used in most industrial floorplanners. | | $\beta$ large (e.g., 5) | Wirelength-dominated; layout may become overly compact with poor area utilization. | | $\beta \to \infty$ | Pure wirelength minimization; area constraints become secondary. | **Conclusion:** $\beta$ controls the **wirelength-vs-area trade-off**. Designers tune $\beta$ based on whether the chip is *timing-critical* (high $\beta$) or *area-cost-critical* (low $\beta$). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing HPWL with full perimeter** — HPWL is HALF the perimeter of the bounding box. A student writing $W = 2(\Delta x + \Delta y)$ loses 2 marks immediately. > > 2. **Using Euclidean distance instead of Manhattan** — In VLSI, routing is rectilinear. Using $\sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ is incorrect and loses 1–2 marks per sub-question. > > 3. **Forgetting to sort edges in Kruskal's algorithm** — A non-sorted edge addition gives wrong MST length. Always show the sorted order in the table. > > 4. **Cycle detection in MST** — Students add all shortest edges without checking cycles. Always verify "no cycle forms" before adding. > > 5. **Wrong units in cost function** — Mixing mm² with mm, or forgetting units entirely, costs the *unit* mark. > > 6. **Mishandling Steiner points** — A Steiner point reduces wirelength only if placed correctly. Random addition increases cost. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Wirelength Estimation:** > > - **HPWL formula:** $W = (x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$. Always HALF perimeter, not full. > - **Manhattan (L1) distance:** $d(P_i, P_j) = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$. Use this in VLSI, NOT Euclidean. > - **HPWL ≤ RMST ≤ RST** — strict inequality chain in general. > - **HPWL time complexity:** $O(n)$ per net → industrial default. > - **MST time complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ per net via Kruskal/Prim. > - **MRST is NP-hard** — solved using Hanan grid + heuristics (1-Steiner, BGA). > - **Hanan's theorem:** Optimal Steiner points lie on the grid formed by pin x/y lines. > - **Cost function:** $\Phi = \alpha A + \beta W$. $\alpha$ weights area, $\beta$ weights wirelength. > - **Chung-Graham bound:** $W_{\text{MST}} \le 0.866 \cdot W_{\text{RST}}$ (approximate). > - **Industry tools using HPWL:** Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2, Mentor Olympus. > - **Key personalities:** Hanan (1966), Hwang (1976), Kahng & Robins (1-Steiner, 1992). > - **Unit conversion:** 1 placement grid unit = manufacturing pitch (e.g., 90 nm, 65 nm, 7 nm). > - **Modern trend:** As feature size shrinks, wire delay dominates gate delay → wirelength estimation is MORE important than ever (Dennard scaling has ended). ---

topics.title: Weighted Wirelength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Weighted Wirelength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **VLSI Physical Design Automation**, **Weighted Wirelength (WWL)** is an enhanced formulation of the classical wirelength objective function used during the **Placement** stage of physical design. While the conventional **Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL)** treats every net identically, *weighted wirelength* assigns a **net weight (w_k)** to each net *k* to bias the placer toward shrinking critical or high-priority interconnects.

Formally, for a netlist consisting of *N* nets, the **Total Weighted Wirelength** objective is defined as:

$$
W \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} w_k \cdot \text{HPWL}(N_k)
$$

where:
- $w_k$ is the **scalar weight** assigned to the $k^{\text{th}}$ net (dimensionless, $w_k \geq 0$)
- $\text{HPWL}(N_k)$ is the bounding-box perimeter divided by two for the $k^{\text{th}}$ net

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Placement):** Weighted wirelength is a prerequisite concept for advanced topics such as **Simulated Annealing**, **Quadratic Placement**, and **Force-Directed Placement**, all of which appear in the latter half of Module 3 of PEEVT524. Mastery of WWL is essential for solving numerical problems in Part B of the University Examination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Expressway Tolls" Intuition

Imagine you are a **city traffic engineer** planning a road map. Every road (net) carries vehicles (signals), but a few roads serve the *hospital* — they must remain **short and fast**. You cannot widen every road (silicon area is finite), so you place **toll booths (weights)** strategically: heavy tolls on non-critical roads deter traffic from getting long, and zero tolls on the hospital road encourage it to be drawn as short as possible.

In silicon terms:
- **Net weight $w_k$ = the toll** you charge the algorithm for allowing that net to grow.
- **Total weighted wirelength $W$ = the total toll revenue**, which the placer tries to **minimize**.
- **Critical nets (clock, reset, scan)** are assigned **higher $w_k$** so the placer pays a *bigger penalty* if they become long.

This is exactly how commercial placers (e.g., Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2) bias their cost functions toward timing closure.

## 1.3 Why Pure HPWL is Insufficient

The classical HPWL estimator is **isotropic** — it treats every net as equally important. However, in a real chip:
- A **clock net** violation of 10 µm can break timing by 50 ps.
- A **data net** of 50 µm may be perfectly fine.

Hence, **weighted HPWL** introduces *selective importance*, allowing the placer to perform **multi-objective optimization** (wirelength + timing + congestion) simultaneously within a single scalar objective.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Net Weight Unit:** dimensionless (typical range $1$–$10$, may scale to $100$ for critical nets)
> - **Wirelength Unit:** $\mu m$ or standard cell grid units ($\lambda$)
> - **Total Weighted Wirelength Unit:** $\mu m$ (since weight is dimensionless)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Building Blocks

A digital netlist $\mathcal{N} = \{N_1, N_2, \ldots, N_N\}$ contains $N$ nets. Each net $N_k$ connects a set of pins $P_k = \{p_{k,1}, p_{k,2}, \ldots, p_{k,m_k}\}$ where $m_k$ is the *terminal count* of net $k$.

### 2.1.1 Unweighted HPWL (Review)

For net $N_k$ with pins at coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$:

$$
\text{HPWL}(N_k) \;=\; \max_{p \in P_k} x_p \;-\; \min_{p \in P_k} x_p \;+\; \max_{p \in P_k} y_p \;-\; \min_{p \in P_k} y_p
$$

### 2.1.2 Weighted HPWL (Direct Form)

The simplest extension multiplies HPWL by a scalar weight:

$$
\text{WHPWL} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} w_k \cdot \Big[ \max_{p \in P_k} x_p \;-\; \min_{p \in P_k} x_p \;+\; \max_{p \in P_k} y_p \;-\; \min_{p \in P_k} y_p \Big]
$$

### 2.1.3 Star Model with Weighted Pins (Advanced)

In the **clique / star model**, each multi-terminal net $N_k$ is represented by a *virtual star center* $s_k = (x_{s_k}, y_{s_k})$ connected to every pin via 2-pin wires. The wirelength contribution of net $N_k$ becomes:

$$
L_k \;=\; \sum_{p \in P_k} \alpha_{k,p} \cdot \Big( \vert x_{s_k} - x_p \vert + \vert y_{s_k} - y_p \vert \Big)
$$

where $\alpha_{k,p}$ is the *pin weight* (typically $\alpha_{k,p} = 1/m_k$ or a custom value from timing analysis).

### 2.1.4 Weighted Center of Gravity (Star Anchor Point)

To find the optimal star center that minimizes $L_k$, set $\partial L_k / \partial x_{s_k} = 0$ (separately for x and y):

$$
x_{s_k}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{p \in P_k} \alpha_{k,p} \cdot x_p}{\displaystyle\sum_{p \in P_k} \alpha_{k,p}}
\qquad
y_{s_k}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{p \in P_k} \alpha_{k,p} \cdot y_p}{\displaystyle\sum_{p \in P_k} \alpha_{k,p}}
$$

This is the **weighted centroid** — the *anchor* that minimizes weighted L1 distance to all pins.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Reasoning Trace

1. **Why weights?** Real chips have heterogeneous criticality. Timing analysis (STA) flags specific nets as critical; the placer must respect that.
2. **How are weights generated?** From **timing-driven placement** flows — the *net criticality* $\beta_k$ is computed from slack, then $w_k = 1 + \lambda \cdot \beta_k$ where $\lambda$ is a user-tunable scaling factor.
3. **How is the gradient used?** During force-directed or quadratic placement, weights become **edge coefficients** in a sparse linear system $A \vec{x} = \vec{b}$, where each off-diagonal entry $A_{ij} = -w_k$ if cells $i, j$ share net $k$.
4. **Why does it converge?** Because the cost function remains convex (or quasi-convex) when $w_k > 0$, gradient-based placers remain numerically stable.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this table** — these formulas appear in every KTU Part B question on Module 3 Placement.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Weighted Wirelength** | $W = \sum_{k=1}^{N} w_k \cdot \text{HPWL}(N_k)$ | $w_k$: dimensionless, $\text{HPWL}$: $\mu m$ |
| 2 | **Weighted HPWL (expanded)** | $\sum_k w_k \big[ \max x_p - \min x_p + \max y_p - \min y_p \big]$ | per-net bounding box |
| 3 | **Weighted Star Center (x)** | $x_{s_k}^{*} = \frac{\sum_p \alpha_{k,p} x_p}{\sum_p \alpha_{k,p}}$ | $\alpha_{k,p}$: pin weight |
| 4 | **Weighted Star Center (y)** | $y_{s_k}^{*} = \frac{\sum_p \alpha_{k,p} y_p}{\sum_p \alpha_{k,p}}$ | analogous |
| 5 | **Star-Model Net Length** | $L_k = \sum_p \alpha_{k,p} (\vert x_{s_k} - x_p \vert + \vert y_{s_k} - y_p \vert)$ | L1 metric |
| 6 | **Quadratic Energy (B^{*}) | $W = \sum_k w_k \sum_{i,j \in N_k} (x_i - x_j)^2$ | quadratic form |
| 7 | **Criticality-Based Weight** | $w_k = 1 + \lambda \cdot \beta_k$ | $\beta_k$: net criticality $\in [0,1]$ |
| 8 | **Slack-to-Criticality** | $\beta_k = \max\big(0, \frac{T_{\text{slack}} - s_k}{T_{\text{slack}}}\big)$ | $s_k$: slack, $T_{\text{slack}}$: threshold |
| 9 | **Weighted Average Wirelength** | $\bar{W} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_k w_k \cdot \text{HPWL}(N_k)$ | normalization |
| 10 | **Net Length Bound (Star vs. Steiner)** | $L_{\text{star}} \leq 2 \cdot L_{\text{Steiner}}$ for 2D | geometric bound |

> **Critical Substitution Rule:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values (e.g., $\vert x_i - x_j \vert$), **never** the bare pipe `|`, because it breaks markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern sub-7nm ASIC and CPU design, **weighted wirelength** drives **timing-driven placement** flows used by every major EDA vendor. Without WWL:
- Hold-time violations proliferate on clock trees.
- Setup slack degrades on long data paths.
- IR-drop worsens on power grids (power nets weighted heavily).

In **FPGA placement**, WWL is used to favor **DSP-to-BRAM adjacency**, minimizing critical multiplier-accumulator paths.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Weighted Star Center that Minimizes L1 Wirelength

**Setup.** Consider a 2-pin net $N_k$ connecting cells $A = (x_A, y_A)$ and $B = (x_B, y_B)$ with pin weights $\alpha_A$ and $\alpha_B$. Introduce a star center $S = (x_S, y_S)$.

**Step 1 — Express the weighted Manhattan distance.**

The weighted L1 distance from $S$ to both pins is:

$$
L_k(x_S, y_S) \;=\; \alpha_A \cdot \big( \vert x_S - x_A \vert + \vert y_S - y_A \vert \big) \;+\; \alpha_B \cdot \big( \vert x_S - x_B \vert + \vert y_S - y_B \vert \big)
$$

**Step 2 — Exploit separability of x and y.**

Since $x_S$ and $y_S$ are independent in L1 metric, minimize $L_k$ separately:

$$
L_k^x(x_S) \;=\; \alpha_A \cdot \vert x_S - x_A \vert \;+\; \alpha_B \cdot \vert x_S - x_B \vert
$$

$$
L_k^y(y_S) \;=\; \alpha_A \cdot \vert y_S - y_A \vert \;+\; \alpha_B \cdot \vert y_S - y_B \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the subgradient optimality condition.**

For a convex function $\vert \cdot \vert$, the subgradient of $L_k^x$ is:

$$
\partial L_k^x / \partial x_S \;=\; \alpha_A \cdot \text{sgn}(x_S - x_A) \;+\; \alpha_B \cdot \text{sgn}(x_S - x_B)
$$

Setting the subgradient to contain zero gives the minimizer range. If $x_A < x_B$, the unique minimizer is:

$$
x_S^{*} \;=\; \frac{\alpha_A \cdot x_A \;+\; \alpha_B \cdot x_B}{\alpha_A \;+\; \alpha_B}
$$

**Step 4 — Symmetric derivation for y.**

$$
y_S^{*} \;=\; \frac{\alpha_A \cdot y_A \;+\; \alpha_B \cdot y_B}{\alpha_A \;+\; \alpha_B}
$$

This matches the general formula in Row 3 of the cheat sheet. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalization to $m$-pin nets:** Sum over all pins — the weighted centroid is always the unique minimizer of L1 distance with positive weights.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified):**
> A 3-pin net has pins at coordinates $P_1 = (2, 4)$, $P_2 = (6, 1)$, $P_3 = (4, 7)$ with pin weights $\alpha_1 = 1$, $\alpha_2 = 2$, $\alpha_3 = 1$. Compute (a) the weighted star center, and (b) the weighted star-model net length.

### Part (a): Weighted Star Center

**Step 1 — Compute the weighted x-coordinate of centroid.**

$$
x_S^{*} \;=\; \frac{(1)(2) + (2)(6) + (1)(4)}{(1) + (2) + (1)} \;=\; \frac{2 + 12 + 4}{4} \;=\; \frac{18}{4} \;=\; 4.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the weighted y-coordinate of centroid.**

$$
y_S^{*} \;=\; \frac{(1)(4) + (2)(1) + (1)(7)}{(1) + (2) + (1)} \;=\; \frac{4 + 2 + 7}{4} \;=\; \frac{13}{4} \;=\; 3.25
$$

**Result:** Star center $S^* = (4.5,\; 3.25)$.

### Part (b): Weighted Star-Model Net Length

**Step 1 — Compute weighted Manhattan distance from $S^*$ to each pin.**

$$
d(S^*, P_1) \;=\; \alpha_1 \cdot (\vert 4.5 - 2 \vert + \vert 3.25 - 4 \vert) \;=\; 1 \cdot (2.5 + 0.75) \;=\; 3.25
$$

$$
d(S^*, P_2) \;=\; \alpha_2 \cdot (\vert 4.5 - 6 \vert + \vert 3.25 - 1 \vert) \;=\; 2 \cdot (1.5 + 2.25) \;=\; 7.50
$$

$$
d(S^*, P_3) \;=\; \alpha_3 \cdot (\vert 4.5 - 4 \vert + \vert 3.25 - 7 \vert) \;=\; 1 \cdot (0.5 + 3.75) \;=\; 4.25
$$

**Step 2 — Sum to obtain net length.**

$$
L_k \;=\; 3.25 + 7.50 + 4.25 \;=\; 15.00 \; \mu m
$$

**Verification:** Compare with unweighted star (centered at centroid $(4, 4)$, length = 11.5). The weighted version is **larger** because $P_2$ (with double weight) pulls the star center closer to it, increasing distance to $P_3$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Wirelength Models

| Model | Optimizer Speed | Accuracy vs. Steiner | Supports Weights? | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HPWL** | Very Fast | Bounded by $2\times$ Steiner | No (uniform) | All classical placers |
| **Weighted HPWL** | Very Fast | Same bound | Yes | Timing-driven placement |
| **Weighted Star / Clique** | Fast | Approximate | Yes (pin-level) | Quadratic placement (e.g., GORDIAN) |
| **Steiner Tree (RSMT)** | Slow | Exact (lower bound) | Partial (net-level) | Global routing prep |
| **Weighted Bounded-Slack** | Moderate | Approximate | Yes | Modern timing-driven flows |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exams:** Most questions on Module 3 use **Weighted HPWL** or **Weighted Star Model**. RSMT (Rectilinear Steiner Minimum Tree) appears in routing modules, not placement.

## 3.4 Derivation: Weighted Quadratic Placement Energy

**Setup.** Replace L1 with L2 metric and write wirelength as sum over all edges in star model:

$$
W \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} w_k \cdot \sum_{i,j \in N_k} (x_i - x_j)^2
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the inner sum for a single net with $m_k$ pins.**

For a 4-pin net with equal pin weights, the clique expansion gives 6 edges. In general:

$$
\sum_{i,j \in N_k} (x_i - x_j)^2 \;=\; m_k \sum_{i \in N_k} x_i^2 \;-\; \Big(\sum_{i \in N_k} x_i\Big)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite the total energy in matrix form.**

$$
W \;=\; \vec{x}^T \, Q \, \vec{x}
$$

where $Q$ is the **Laplacian** of the weighted hypergraph with $Q_{ii} = w_k m_k$ (sum of incident weights) and $Q_{ij} = -w_k$ if $i, j$ share net $k$.

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system.**

Differentiating $W$ with respect to $\vec{x}$:

$$
\frac{\partial W}{\partial \vec{x}} \;=\; 2 Q \vec{x} \;=\; \vec{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q \vec{x} \;=\; \vec{0}
$$

Adding anchor constraints (one cell fixed to break degeneracy) yields a sparse symmetric positive-definite system solved via **Conjugate Gradient** in $O(n \log n)$ time.

This is the heart of **Kraftwerk**, **FastPlace**, and similar quadratic placers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Weighted Wirelength Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Read Netlist] --> readA[Parse Netlist n1 n2 ... nN]
    readA --> weightA{Load Net Weights}
    weightA -->|w1 w2 ... wN| bboxA[Compute Bounding Box per Net]
    bboxA --> hpwlA[Calculate HPWL per Net]
    hpwlA --> multiplyA[Multiply by Net Weight]
    multiplyA --> sumA[Sum All Weighted Contributions]
    sumA --> outputA[Output Total Weighted Wirelength W]
    outputA --> stopA[End]
```

**Reading Guide:**
- `startA`, `bboxA`, etc., are purely alphanumeric IDs (Node Identifier Alpha Rule).
- Each arrow represents a sequential step in the WWL evaluation engine.
- The decision diamond `weightA` represents weight loading — *the only branching point* in the algorithm.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Timing-Driven Weighted Placement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph stage1A[Stage 1 Global Placement]
        gA[Initial Random Placement]
        gB[Compute Weighted WWL]
        gC[Gradient Descent Move]
        gD[Convergence Check]
        gA --> gB --> gC --> gD
        gD -->|Not Converged| gA
        gD -->|Converged| nextA
    end
    subgraph stage2A[Stage 2 Timing Update]
        tA[Static Timing Analysis STA]
        tB[Compute Net Criticality beta k]
        tC[Update Net Weights w k = 1 + lambda times beta k]
        tA --> tB --> tC
        tC --> nextB
    end
    nextA --> tA
    nextB --> stage3A
    subgraph stage3A[Stage 3 Legalization]
        lA[Cell Snap to Rows]
        lB[Overlap Removal]
        lC[Final WWL Report]
        lA --> lB --> lC
    end
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram shows how **net weights are dynamically updated** during placement based on timing analysis — a feedback loop central to all commercial timing-driven placers.

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree — Multi-Terminal Net Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    netA[Net N k with m k pins] --> starA[Insert Star Center S k]
    starA --> e1A[Edge w1 to Pin P1]
    starA --> e2A[Edge w2 to Pin P2]
    starA --> e3A[Edge w3 to Pin P3]
    starA --> eNA[Edge wn to Pin Pn]
    e1A --> costA[Sum Weighted Manhattan]
    e2A --> costA
    e3A --> costA
    eNA --> costA
```

**Purpose:** Visually demonstrates how a multi-terminal net is decomposed into multiple 2-pin edges through a virtual star center, each carrying an independent weight. This is the canonical transformation used in quadratic placement.

## 4.4 Schematic — Weight Assignment Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA[Net k Enters Timing Analysis] --> qB{Slack s k Less Than Threshold T}
    qB -->|Yes| qC[Mark Net as Critical beta k High]
    qB -->|No| qD[Mark Net as Non-Critical beta k Low]
    qC --> qE[Assign w k = w high]
    qD --> qF[Assign w k = 1]
    qE --> qG[Placer Penalizes Long Wires]
    qF --> qG
```

**Use Case:** This is the *weight generation policy* used in most EDA tools — students should reproduce a version of this in KTU descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define *Weighted Wirelength* in the context of VLSI placement. Why is it preferred over standard HPWL in modern timing-driven flows?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Weighted wirelength is the sum over all nets of (net weight $w_k$) $\times$ (HPWL of that net), i.e., $W = \sum_k w_k \cdot \text{HPWL}(N_k)$. It is preferred over standard HPWL because **HPWL treats all nets uniformly**, while WWL allows the placer to **assign higher weights to timing-critical nets** (e.g., clock, reset, scan), thereby **biasing the placement to keep critical interconnects short** and improving timing closure. Modern chips have heterogeneous criticality, making WWL essential for high-performance ASIC design.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the *weighted star center* of a multi-terminal net? Write the formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The weighted star center is the virtual anchor point of a multi-terminal net in the star-model representation that **minimizes the weighted Manhattan distance** to all pins. For a net with pins $(x_p, y_p)$ and pin weights $\alpha_p$, the weighted star center is:

$$
x_{s_k}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\sum_p \alpha_p \cdot x_p}{\sum_p \alpha_p} \qquad y_{s_k}^{*} \;=\; \frac{\sum_p \alpha_p \cdot y_p}{\sum_p \alpha_p}
$$

This point is the unique minimizer of $\sum_p \alpha_p (\vert x_{s_k} - x_p \vert + \vert y_{s_k} - y_p \vert)$ and forms the basis of the **clique / star model** used in quadratic placement.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Weighted Wirelength** in placement. Derive the formula for the **weighted star center** of an $m$-pin net with arbitrary pin weights. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Consider the following 4-pin net with the given data:

| Pin | x (μm) | y (μm) | Pin Weight α |
|---|---|---|---|
| P₁ | 1 | 2 | 1 |
| P₂ | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| P₃ | 3 | 6 | 2 |
| P₄ | 7 | 1 | 1 |

Compute (i) the weighted star center, (ii) the weighted star-model net length, and (iii) the unweighted HPWL of the net. Compare (ii) and (iii) and comment. **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the star-model representation of an $m$-pin net $N_k$ with pins $P = \{p_1, \ldots, p_m\}$ and pin weights $\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_m$. A virtual star center $S = (x_S, y_S)$ is introduced.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the weighted L1 length function:

$$
L_k \;=\; \sum_{p=1}^{m} \alpha_p \cdot \big( \vert x_S - x_p \vert + \vert y_S - y_p \vert \big)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Exploit separability. Minimize $x$-part first:

$$
L_k^x(x_S) \;=\; \sum_{p=1}^{m} \alpha_p \cdot \vert x_S - x_p \vert
$$

Take the subgradient and set to zero:

$$
\partial L_k^x / \partial x_S \;=\; \sum_{p=1}^{m} \alpha_p \cdot \text{sgn}(x_S - x_p) \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Solve using the property of weighted medians / centroids. For positive weights the minimizer is the weighted mean:

$$
x_S^{*} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{p=1}^{m} \alpha_p \cdot x_p}{\sum_{p=1}^{m} \alpha_p}
\qquad
y_S^{*} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{p=1}^{m} \alpha_p \cdot y_p}{\sum_{p=1}^{m} \alpha_p}
$$

The same argument applies to the $y$ component. **Conclude** that the star center is the unique minimizer. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the weighted star center.

$$
x_S^{*} \;=\; \frac{(1)(1) + (3)(5) + (2)(3) + (1)(7)}{1 + 3 + 2 + 1} \;=\; \frac{1 + 15 + 6 + 7}{7} \;=\; \frac{29}{7} \approx 4.143
$$

$$
y_S^{*} \;=\; \frac{(1)(2) + (3)(4) + (2)(6) + (1)(1)}{1 + 3 + 2 + 1} \;=\; \frac{2 + 12 + 12 + 1}{7} \;=\; \frac{27}{7} \approx 3.857
$$

Star center: $S^* \approx (4.143,\; 3.857)$.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Compute weighted star-model net length.

$$
d(S^*, P_1) \;=\; 1 \cdot (\vert 4.143 - 1 \vert + \vert 3.857 - 2 \vert) \;=\; 1 \cdot (3.143 + 1.857) \;=\; 5.000
$$

$$
d(S^*, P_2) \;=\; 3 \cdot (\vert 4.143 - 5 \vert + \vert 3.857 - 4 \vert) \;=\; 3 \cdot (0.857 + 0.143) \;=\; 3.000
$$

$$
d(S^*, P_3) \;=\; 2 \cdot (\vert 4.143 - 3 \vert + \vert 3.857 - 6 \vert) \;=\; 2 \cdot (1.143 + 2.143) \;=\; 6.572
$$

$$
d(S^*, P_4) \;=\; 1 \cdot (\vert 4.143 - 7 \vert + \vert 3.857 - 1 \vert) \;=\; 1 \cdot (2.857 + 2.857) \;=\; 5.714
$$

Total weighted star length:

$$
L_k \;=\; 5.000 + 3.000 + 6.572 + 5.714 \;=\; 20.286 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute unweighted HPWL.

Bounding box: $x_{\min} = 1$, $x_{\max} = 7$, $y_{\min} = 1$, $y_{\max} = 6$.

$$
\text{HPWL} \;=\; (7 - 1) + (6 - 1) \;=\; 6 + 5 \;=\; 11 \; \mu m
$$

**Comparison (2 marks worth embedded in Step 2):** Weighted star length (20.286 μm) is **larger** than HPWL (11 μm) because the star model adds redundant routing through the virtual center. However, the weighted version is **continuous and differentiable**, making it suitable for gradient-based placement, whereas HPWL is non-smooth.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **Weighted Wirelength Computation Pipeline**. Discuss how net weights are derived from **timing criticality** in a static-timing-driven placement flow. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A circuit has 4 nets with the following bounding boxes and weights:

| Net k | x_min (μm) | x_max (μm) | y_min (μm) | y_max (μm) | Weight w_k |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 8 | 1 | 5 | 1 |
| 2 | 0 | 6 | 3 | 9 | 3 |
| 3 | 4 | 10 | 2 | 6 | 2 |
| 4 | 1 | 5 | 4 | 7 | 4 |

Compute the **Total Weighted HPWL** of the circuit. **[7 marks]**

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Conceptual **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 (3 marks):** Draw and explain the flowchart:
1. Read netlist.
2. Load net weights $w_k$ from timing analyzer.
3. For each net, compute bounding box.
4. Compute HPWL = $(x_{\max} - x_{\min}) + (y_{\max} - y_{\min})$.
5. Multiply by $w_k$.
6. Sum across all nets.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Discuss timing criticality derivation:
- Run **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** to obtain slack $s_k$ for each net.
- Compute criticality: $\beta_k = \max(0, (T_{\text{slack}} - s_k) / T_{\text{slack}})$.
- Derive weight: $w_k = 1 + \lambda \beta_k$, where $\lambda$ is a tunable scaling factor (typical $\lambda \in [5, 50]$).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Explain feedback loop:
After each placement iteration, STA is re-run, $\beta_k$ and $w_k$ are updated, and the placer minimizes the new WWL. This *iterative weight refinement* is the core of modern timing-driven placement.

#### Part (b) — Numerical **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 (4 marks):** Compute HPWL for each net.

$$
\text{HPWL}(N_1) \;=\; (8-2) + (5-1) \;=\; 6 + 4 \;=\; 10 \; \mu m
$$

$$
\text{HPWL}(N_2) \;=\; (6-0) + (9-3) \;=\; 6 + 6 \;=\; 12 \; \mu m
$$

$$
\text{HPWL}(N_3) \;=\; (10-4) + (6-2) \;=\; 6 + 4 \;=\; 10 \; \mu m
$$

$$
\text{HPWL}(N_4) \;=\; (5-1) + (7-4) \;=\; 4 + 3 \;=\; 7 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Multiply by weights.

$$
w_1 \cdot \text{HPWL}_1 \;=\; 1 \times 10 \;=\; 10
$$

$$
w_2 \cdot \text{HPWL}_2 \;=\; 3 \times 12 \;=\; 36
$$

$$
w_3 \cdot \text{HPWL}_3 \;=\; 2 \times 10 \;=\; 20
$$

$$
w_4 \cdot \text{HPWL}_4 \;=\; 4 \times 7 \;=\; 28
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Sum to obtain Total Weighted HPWL.

$$
W \;=\; 10 + 36 + 20 + 28 \;=\; 94 \; \mu m
$$

**Final Answer:** $W = 94 \; \mu m$. The dominant contribution comes from Net 2 (weight 3) and Net 4 (weight 4, despite smaller HPWL), illustrating that **high weight × small HPWL can outweigh low weight × large HPWL**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PEEVT524:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize pin weights** when computing the weighted centroid. Many students compute $\sum \alpha_p x_p$ but forget the denominator $\sum \alpha_p$. This silently shifts the answer by a factor. **Always show the denominator.**
> 2. **Using L2 (Euclidean) instead of L1 (Manhattan) distance** in the star model. KTU expects Manhattan distance for VLSI placement because routing follows the Manhattan grid.
> 3. **Confusing *net weights* with *pin weights*.** Net weights scale the entire net's contribution; pin weights scale individual pin distances within a single net. They are not interchangeable.
> 4. **In numerical answers, dropping the unit (μm).** KTU board examiners *require* units on numerical wirelength answers.
> 5. **Not specifying separability** in the derivation. The derivation must mention that x and y can be minimized independently in the L1 metric.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Weighted Wirelength (Module 3):**
> - **Definition:** $W = \sum_k w_k \cdot \text{HPWL}(N_k)$ — the weighted sum of bounding-box half-perimeters.
> - **Why weights:** Heterogeneous net criticality (clock, reset, scan, timing-critical data paths).
> - **Star model:** Multi-terminal nets decomposed via a virtual star center; weighted Manhattan distance is used.
> - **Weighted star center (centroid) formula:** $x_{s_k}^{*} = \frac{\sum_p \alpha_p x_p}{\sum_p \alpha_p}$, same for $y$.
> - **Pin weights ≠ net weights.** Pin weights scale distances within a single net; net weights scale the whole net's contribution.
> - **Quadratic placement:** Energy $W = \vec{x}^T Q \vec{x}$ where $Q$ is the weighted hypergraph Laplacian; solved via sparse linear system.
> - **Slack-to-weight mapping:** $w_k = 1 + \lambda \beta_k$ with $\beta_k$ derived from timing slack.
> - **HPWL is non-smooth** (uses max/min), but the **weighted star model is smooth** — preferred for gradient-based placement.
> - **Unit convention:** Weights are dimensionless; wirelength is in μm; total weighted wirelength inherits the wirelength unit (μm).
> - **Pipeline order:** Parse → Load weights → Bounding box → HPWL → Multiply → Sum → Report.
> - **Examiners' favorites:** Weighted centroid derivation (7 marks), Total weighted HPWL numerical (7 marks), timing-criticality weight generation (3 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Cut Size
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maximum Cut Size

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the VLSI physical design automation context, a **Cut** is a partition of the netlist hypergraph $G = (V, E)$ into two or more disjoint subsets, and the **Cut Size** $C(P)$ is the cardinality of the set of hyperedges (nets) that have endpoints residing in *at least two different partitions*. The **Maximum Cut Size** is the upper bound of cut size encountered over all admissible partition configurations $P$ of the netlist, typically obtained via recursive bipartitioning, where each sub-region of the chip area is recursively bisected.

Mathematically, for a bipartition $P = (P_1, P_2)$:

$$C(P) \;=\; \bigl\vert \{e \in E : e \cap P_1 \neq \emptyset \;\land\; e \cap P_2 \neq \emptyset\} \bigr\vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Maximum Cut Size is a fundamental metric in the **min-cut (Kernighan-Lin / Fiduccia-Mattheyses) placement framework**, and it directly influences the final wirelength and routing congestion of the chip. It is also the cornerstone of the classical analytical placement formulations such as **Kang's quadratic placement**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Building" Intuition

Imagine a **large corporate office building** with hundreds of employees (representing *cells/standard cells* of a chip) who constantly send memos (representing *nets/signals*) to one another. The building manager (the **CAD tool**) wants to split the building into two wings — **Wing A and Wing B** — by drawing a *red line down the middle corridor* (the **cut line**).

Now, any memo that needs to travel between an employee in Wing A and an employee in Wing B must **cross the red line**. The number of such memos is the **Cut Size**.

- If the manager groups friends into the same wing, the cut size is **small** (good — fewer inter-wing memos, less corridor traffic).
- If the manager places enemies into the same wing, the cut size is **large** (bad — corridor becomes a bottleneck).

The **Maximum Cut Size** refers to the **worst-case** number of crossing memos over *all possible ways* to draw the red line. The CAD tool's job is to make this number as *small* as possible (a **min-cut problem**), but it must always be aware of the maximum theoretical cut size to plan routing resources (the *corridor width*) in advance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In VLSI, a *small* cut size ⇒ *less* routing congestion, *shorter* global wires, and *better* timing closure. A *large* cut size ⇒ *more* congestion, *longer* wires, and *higher* power dissipation. The Maximum Cut Size is, therefore, the **pessimistic upper bound** used to reserve routing resources early in the design flow.

### Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

| Metric | Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Cut Size | $C(P)$ | Number of nets crossing the partition |
| Number of Cells | $N$ | Total number of cells in the netlist |
| Number of Nets | $E$ | Total number of hyperedges (nets) |
| Rent's Exponent | $p$ | Empirical constant (typically $0.5 \le p \le 0.8$) |
| Rent's Coefficient | $t$ | Average number of pins per block |
| Block Count | $B$ | Number of logic blocks in a region |
| Bisection Width | $B_w$ | Cut size at exact half-area partition |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Cut Size Problem — Structured Logical Decomposition

A VLSI netlist is modelled as a **hypergraph** $G = (V, E)$ where:

- $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_N\}$ is the set of *vertices* (cells/macros).
- $E = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_M\}$ is the set of *hyperedges* (nets). Each net $e_k$ connects a subset of vertices: $e_k \subseteq V$.

For a given **bipartition** $P = (P_1, P_2)$ with $P_1 \cup P_2 = V$ and $P_1 \cap P_2 = \emptyset$:

- **Step 1 — Classify every net** into one of three categories:
    - *Internal net of $P_1$:* all pins of the net lie in $P_1$.
    - *Internal net of $P_2$:* all pins of the net lie in $P_2$.
    - **Crossing net (cut net):** at least one pin in $P_1$ and at least one pin in $P_2$.
- **Step 2 — Count crossing nets.** The number of crossing nets *is* the cut size.
- **Step 3 — Define the cut set** $E_{\text{cut}} \subseteq E$ — this is the set of nets that must be routed *across* the partition boundary.
- **Step 4 — Compute the maximum cut size** by exploring the partition space. In the *worst case* (a random partition), the expected cut size is:

$$E[C_{\text{random}}] = \sum_{k=1}^{M} \bigl[ 1 - x_k^{N_1} - (1 - x_k)^{N_1} \bigr]$$

where $x_k = N_1/N$ is the balance ratio and $N_1 = \vert P_1 \vert$.

### 2.2 Why Cut Size Matters in VLSI

- **Routing Congestion:** Each cut net consumes channels at the partition boundary. The maximum number of channels reserved equals the maximum cut size.
- **Timing Closure:** Long wires crossing multiple cuts contribute to inter-block delay. Smaller cut size ⇒ shorter critical paths.
- **Power Dissipation:** Capacitive loading on global interconnects scales with wire length. Maximum cut size is a *proxy* for global wirelength.
- **Area Estimation:** Routing area is proportional to cut size in standard-cell and FPGA architectures.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the LaTeX `\vert` notation in place of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C(P) = \vert \{e \in E : e \cap P_1 \neq \emptyset \;\land\; e \cap P_2 \neq \emptyset\}\vert$ | Bipartition cut size | Nets (integer) |
| 2 | $C_{\max} = \max_{P \in \mathcal{P}} C(P)$ | Maximum cut size over all partitions | Nets (integer) |
| 3 | $B_w = C(P^*)$ where $\vert P_1 \vert = \vert V \vert / 2$ | Bisection width | Nets (integer) |
| 4 | $T = t \cdot B^{p}$ | **Rent's Rule** (external pin count) | Pins |
| 5 | $C(B) = t \cdot B^{p} - B$ | Cut size estimate (excluding self-pins) | Nets |
| 6 | $W_{\text{est}} = \alpha \cdot C_{\max}$ | Estimated global wirelength | $\mu m$ |
| 7 | $\Delta C = C(P') - C(P)$ | Cut delta after a single cell move | Integer |
| 8 | $\text{gain}(v) = \sum_{e \ni v} \delta(e)$ | FM algorithm gain function | Integer |
| 9 | $L_{\max} \le 2 \cdot \log_2(N) \cdot C_{\max}$ | Recursive bisection depth bound | Levels |
| 10 | $\rho_c = C(P) / C_{\max}$ | Normalized cut ratio | $[0, 1]$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Cut Size Analysis |
|---|---|
| **FPGA Placement** | Determines routing channel widths in island-style FPGAs |
| **Standard-Cell Placement** | Min-cut placement via FM algorithm — the industry workhorse |
| **Multi-Chip Modules (MCM)** | Inter-chip pin count optimization using Rent's rule |
| **Network-on-Chip (NoC)** | Tile partitioning for IP-block mapping |
| **3D-IC Stacking** | Through-Silicon-Via (TSV) minimization by tier partitioning |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Cut Size from a Hypergraph

**Problem Setup:** Let the netlist hypergraph $G = (V, E)$ have $N$ cells and $M$ nets. Let the bipartition be $P = (P_1, P_2)$ with $\vert P_1 \vert = a$ and $\vert P_2 \vert = N - a$.

**Step 1 — Probability a net $e_k$ of size $s_k$ is internal to $P_1$.**

A net is internal to $P_1$ if *all* $s_k$ of its pins fall inside $P_1$. Assuming uniform random assignment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr(e_k \subseteq P_1) &= \frac{\binom{a}{s_k}}{\binom{N}{s_k}} = \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{a - i}{N - i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability a net is internal to $P_2$.**

By symmetry (replacing $a$ with $N - a$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr(e_k \subseteq P_2) &= \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{N - a - i}{N - i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability a net is a *cut* net.**

Since these two events are *disjoint* (a net cannot be simultaneously internal to both partitions in a bipartition), we use the complement rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr(e_k \text{ is cut}) &= 1 - \Pr(e_k \subseteq P_1) - \Pr(e_k \subseteq P_2) \\
&= 1 - \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{a - i}{N - i} - \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{N - a - i}{N - i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Expected cut size under random partition.**

By linearity of expectation across all $M$ nets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[C_{\text{random}}] &= \sum_{k=1}^{M} \Pr(e_k \text{ is cut}) \\
&= \sum_{k=1}^{M} \left[ 1 - \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{a - i}{N - i} - \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{N - a - i}{N - i} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Special case: balanced bisection with 2-pin nets.**

For $a = N/2$ and $s_k = 2$ for all $k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[C_{\text{bisect}}] &= M \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{(N/2)(N/2 - 1)}{N(N-1)} - \frac{(N/2)(N/2 - 1)}{N(N-1)} \right] \\
&= M \cdot \left[ 1 - \frac{N - 2}{2(N-1)} \right] \\
&= M \cdot \frac{N}{2(N-1)} \;\approx\; \frac{M}{2} \quad \text{for large } N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A random bipartition cuts, on average, *half* the nets. This is the *baseline* that good partitioning algorithms must beat by a large margin.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Rent's Rule and Cut Size

**Empirical Foundation (Rent, 1971):** Observations of pin counts in real ICs follow the power law:

$$T = t \cdot B^{p}$$

where:
- $T$ = average number of *external* pins (signal terminals).
- $B$ = number of logic blocks (gates/cells).
- $t$ = Rent's coefficient (average pins per block).
- $p$ = Rent's exponent ($0 \le p \le 1$); typical: $0.5$ for random logic, $0.7$ for memory.

**Step 1 — Cut size from Rent's rule.**

If we isolate a region of $B$ blocks from the rest of the chip, the *external* connections of that region are precisely the nets that cross the cut. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(B) &= T - (\text{internal connections}) \\
&\approx t \cdot B^{p} - B \quad \text{(subtracting one internal pin per block)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum cut size via recursive bisection.**

After $\log_2(N)$ levels of recursive bisection, the original $N$-cell region has been divided into unit-sized cells. The maximum cut size is bounded by the sum of cut sizes at every level:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\max} &\le \sum_{j=0}^{\log_2(N) - 1} N_j^{p} \\
&\le t \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{\log_2(N) - 1} \left(\frac{N}{2^{j}}\right)^{p} \\
&= t \cdot N^{p} \sum_{j=0}^{\log_2(N) - 1} 2^{-jp} \\
&= t \cdot N^{p} \cdot \frac{1 - 2^{-p \log_2(N)}}{1 - 2^{-p}} \\
&= t \cdot N^{p} \cdot \frac{1 - N^{-p}}{1 - 2^{-p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Asymptotic simplification.**

For $N \to \infty$ and $p > 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\max} \;\sim\; \frac{t}{1 - 2^{-p}} \cdot N^{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Conclusion:** The maximum cut size scales as $O(N^{p})$ with $p < 1$, confirming that well-designed circuits have **sub-linear** cut growth — this is the theoretical justification for hierarchical (divide-and-conquer) design.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A circuit has $N = 100$ cells and $M = 80$ nets, with each net being 3-pin. A random bipartition is created with $a = 40$ cells in $P_1$. Compute the expected cut size.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitute into the cut probability formula** for $s_k = 3$, $N = 100$, $a = 40$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr(e_k \subseteq P_1) &= \frac{40 \cdot 39 \cdot 38}{100 \cdot 99 \cdot 98} = \frac{59280}{970200} \approx 0.06109
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Same for $P_2$** with $N - a = 60$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr(e_k \subseteq P_2) &= \frac{60 \cdot 59 \cdot 58}{100 \cdot 99 \cdot 98} = \frac{205320}{970200} \approx 0.21164
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cut probability per net:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr(e_k \text{ cut}) &= 1 - 0.06109 - 0.21164 = 0.72727
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Expected cut size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[C] &= 80 \times 0.72727 \approx 58.18 \;\approx\; 58 \text{ nets}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** A well-tuned FM/ML partitioning algorithm should reduce this from ~58 to **20–25 nets** in practice — a 60% reduction, demonstrating the value of min-cut heuristics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

---

> **Reading guide:** At each successive level, the cut size scales as $C_j \approx t \cdot (N / 2^{j})^{p}$. The total maximum cut size is the sum of all per-level cuts.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the term "cut size" in the context of VLSI netlist partitioning. How does a balanced bisection differ from a general bipartition?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Cut size (1 mark):** Cut size $C(P)$ of a bipartition $P = (P_1, P_2)$ of a netlist hypergraph $G = (V, E)$ is defined as the number of hyperedges (nets) having at least one endpoint in each of the two partitions, i.e.,

$$C(P) = \vert \{e \in E : e \cap P_1 \neq \emptyset \;\land\; e \cap P_2 \neq \emptyset\}\vert.$$

- **General bipartition (1 mark):** Any arbitrary split of the cell set $V$ into two non-empty subsets $P_1$ and $P_2$ — the sizes $\vert P_1 \vert$ and $\vert P_2 \vert$ need not be equal.
- **Balanced bisection (1 mark):** A specific bipartition where the two subsets are *equal in size*, i.e., $\vert P_1 \vert = \vert P_2 \vert = N/2$. The cut size of such a partition is called the **bisection width** $B_w$, a key measure of the *interconnect complexity* of the netlist.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**State and explain Rent's Rule. What is the significance of the Rent's exponent $p$ in determining the maximum cut size?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Rent's Rule statement (1 mark):** $T = t \cdot B^{p}$, where $T$ is the average number of external pins/connections of a region containing $B$ logic blocks, $t$ is the average number of pins per block, and $p$ is Rent's exponent.
- **Physical meaning of $p$ (1 mark):** The exponent $p$ captures the *complexity of interconnection*. Lower $p$ (e.g., $0.5$) indicates regular, locality-preserving designs like memory arrays; higher $p$ (e.g., $0.7$) indicates dense random logic with high global interconnect.
- **Significance for cut size (1 mark):** The maximum cut size grows as $O(N^{p})$ where $p < 1$, ensuring **sub-linear scaling** that justifies hierarchical (recursive bisection) design. Designs with $p \to 1$ are notoriously difficult to partition and route.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **expected cut size** of a random bipartition of a hypergraph with $N$ cells and $M$ nets of sizes $s_1, s_2, \dots, s_M$, where $P_1$ contains $a$ cells.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A circuit has $N = 64$ cells, $M = 100$ nets, and all nets are 2-pin. The netlist is bipartitioned with $a = 32$ cells in $P_1$. Compute:
  (i) The expected cut size of a *random* partition.
  (ii) The estimated maximum cut size using Rent's rule with $t = 4$ and $p = 0.6$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Full Derivation [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Model the bipartition as a random assignment. The probability that all $s_k$ pins of net $e_k$ fall into $P_1$ is the hypergeometric probability:

$$
\Pr(e_k \subseteq P_1) = \frac{\binom{a}{s_k}}{\binom{N}{s_k}} = \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{a - i}{N - i}
$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Similarly, the probability of internal-to-$P_2$ placement:

$$
\Pr(e_k \subseteq P_2) = \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{N - a - i}{N - i}
$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* Since the two events are *mutually exclusive*, the cut probability by the complement rule is:

$$
\Pr(e_k \text{ is cut}) = 1 - \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{a - i}{N - i} - \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{N - a - i}{N - i}
$$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* Apply linearity of expectation over the $M$ independent nets:

$$
E[C] = \sum_{k=1}^{M} \Pr(e_k \text{ is cut}) = \sum_{k=1}^{M} \left[ 1 - \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{a - i}{N - i} - \prod_{i=0}^{s_k - 1} \frac{N - a - i}{N - i} \right]
$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* For 2-pin nets ($s_k = 2$), simplify the binomial coefficients:

$$
\Pr(e_k \subseteq P_1) = \frac{a(a-1)}{N(N-1)}, \quad \Pr(e_k \subseteq P_2) = \frac{(N-a)(N-a-1)}{N(N-1)}
$$

$$
E[C] = M \left[ 1 - \frac{a(a-1) + (N-a)(N-a-1)}{N(N-1)} \right]
$$

*Step 6 (1 mark):* The cut probability is *maximized* at balanced bisection $a = N/2$:

$$
E[C_{\text{bisect}}] = M \cdot \frac{N}{2(N-1)} \;\xrightarrow{N \to \infty}\; \frac{M}{2}
$$

*Step 7 (1 mark):* **Engineering interpretation:** A random bipartition cuts, on average, half the nets. Min-cut algorithms (Kernighan-Lin, FM) can reduce this by 40–60%, making them indispensable in industrial EDA flows.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Identify the parameters: $N = 64$, $M = 100$, $s_k = 2$, $a = 32$.

*Step 2 (2 marks):* For 2-pin nets at $a = N/2 = 32$:

$$
\Pr(e_k \subseteq P_1) = \frac{32 \cdot 31}{64 \cdot 63} = \frac{992}{4032} \approx 0.2460
$$

$$
\Pr(e_k \subseteq P_2) = \frac{32 \cdot 31}{64 \cdot 63} \approx 0.2460
$$

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Per-net cut probability and expected cut size:

$$
\Pr(e_k \text{ cut}) = 1 - 0.2460 - 0.2460 = 0.5079
$$

$$
E[C] = 100 \times 0.5079 \approx 50.79 \;\approx\; 51 \text{ nets}
$$

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Rent's rule calculation with $t = 4$, $p = 0.6$, $B = 64$:

$$
C_{\max} \approx \frac{t \cdot B^{p}}{1 - 2^{-p}} = \frac{4 \cdot 64^{0.6}}{1 - 2^{-0.6}}
$$

$$
64^{0.6} = (2^{6})^{0.6} = 2^{3.6} \approx 12.13
$$

$$
2^{-0.6} = 1/2^{0.6} = 1/1.516 \approx 0.6598
$$

$$
C_{\max} \approx \frac{4 \times 12.13}{1 - 0.6598} = \frac{48.52}{0.3402} \approx 142.6 \text{ nets}
$$

*Final Answers:*
- **(i)** $E[C_{\text{random}}] \approx 51$ nets.
- **(ii)** $C_{\max} \approx 143$ nets (upper bound; the actual maximum of any specific partition is $\le 100$, the total net count).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Kernighan-Lin (KL) algorithm** for bipartitioning. Show how the **gain function** is computed for a single cell move and describe the role of the *lock array*.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply the **Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM) algorithm** to the following 5-cell, 5-net circuit. The initial partition is $P_1 = \{C_1, C_2\}$ and $P_2 = \{C_3, C_4, C_5\}$. Compute two passes and show the gain updates. Cell-to-net incidence:

| Net | Cells |
|---|---|
| $n_1$ | $C_1, C_2$ |
| $n_2$ | $C_1, C_3$ |
| $n_3$ | $C_2, C_4, C_5$ |
| $n_4$ | $C_3, C_4$ |
| $n_5$ | $C_4, C_5$ |

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Kernighan-Lin Algorithm [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 — Initialization (1 mark):* Start from a *random* initial bipartition $P = (P_1, P_2)$ of the netlist. The goal is to find a partition $P^*$ that *minimizes* $C(P)$.

*Step 2 — Gain function definition (2 marks):* For a cell $v \in P_1$, define:
- $D(v) = $ number of nets incident to $v$ that become *internal* to $P_1$ if $v$ moves to $P_2$ (decrease).
- $I(v) = $ number of nets incident to $v$ that become *cut* if $v$ moves to $P_2$ (increase).

The **gain** of moving $v$ is:

$$
g(v) = D(v) - I(v)
$$

*Step 3 — Pairwise swap (1 mark):* KL performs *pairwise swaps*: at each step, the algorithm finds the pair $(u \in P_1, v \in P_2)$ whose swap *maximally reduces* the cut. The *cumulative* gain is tracked across multiple swaps within a single pass.

*Step 4 — Lock array (1 mark):* Once a cell has been moved, it is **locked** (marked in a Boolean array) to prevent it from being moved again within the same pass. This guarantees that the algorithm makes $O(N)$ unique moves per pass.

*Step 5 — Pass termination (1 mark):* After $N/2$ swaps, the algorithm inspects the sequence of cumulative gains and selects the partition corresponding to the *maximum cumulative gain*. If positive, this is accepted; otherwise, the algorithm has converged.

*Step 6 — Complexity and limitations (1 mark):* KL runs in $O(N^{2} \log N)$ per pass. Its primary limitation is *cell pair balancing* — it can only maintain balance in pairs. The **Fiduccia-Mattheyses** variant lifts this restriction by allowing *single-cell moves* with $O(N)$ per pass using a *bucket-list* data structure.

---

**Part (b) — FM Algorithm Application [7 marks]:**

*Step 1 — Initial state (1 mark):* $P_1 = \{C_1, C_2\}$, $P_2 = \{C_3, C_4, C_5\}$.

Compute initial cut nets:
- $n_1 = \{C_1, C_2\}$ → both in $P_1$ → **internal** to $P_1$.
- $n_2 = \{C_1, C_3\}$ → $C_1 \in P_1$, $C_3 \in P_2$ → **cut** ✓
- $n_3 = \{C_2, C_4, C_5\}$ → $C_2 \in P_1$, $C_4, C_5 \in P_2$ → **cut** ✓
- $n_4 = \{C_3, C_4\}$ → both in $P_2$ → **internal** to $P_2$.
- $n_5 = \{C_4, C_5\}$ → both in $P_2$ → **internal** to $P_2$.

**Initial cut size = 2.** (Nets $n_2$, $n_3$.)

*Step 2 — Compute gains for all cells (2 marks):*

| Cell | $D$ (becomes internal) | $I$ (becomes cut) | Gain $g = D - I$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ (move to $P_2$): $n_1$ becomes cut, $n_2$ becomes internal | 1 | 1 | **0** |
| $C_2$ (move to $P_2$): $n_1$ becomes cut, $n_3$ becomes internal | 1 | 1 | **0** |
| $C_3$ (move to $P_1$): $n_2$ becomes internal, $n_4$ becomes cut | 1 | 1 | **0** |
| $C_4$ (move to $P_1$): $n_3$ becomes internal, $n_4$ becomes internal, $n_5$ becomes cut | 2 | 1 | **+1** |
| $C_5$ (move to $P_1$): $n_3$ becomes internal, $n_5$ becomes cut | 1 | 1 | **0** |

*Step 3 — Pass 1, Move 1 (1 mark):* Maximum gain is $C_4$ with $g = +1$. Move $C_4$ to $P_1$. Lock $C_4$.

New state: $P_1 = \{C_1, C_2, C_4\}$, $P_2 = \{C_3, C_5\}$.
- $n_3$ is now internal to $P_1$ (both $C_2$ and $C_4$ there). $C_5$ alone in $P_2$ → $n_3$ now **internal** (saving 1 cut).
- $n_4 = \{C_3, C_4\}$ → $C_4 \in P_1$, $C_3 \in P_2$ → **cut** (new cut introduced).
- $n_2 = \{C_1, C_3\}$ → still **cut**.
- $n_5 = \{C_4, C_5\}$ → $C_4 \in P_1$, $C_5 \in P_2$ → **cut** (new).

**New cut size = 3** (nets $n_2, n_4, n_5$). Cumulative gain = +1.

*Step 4 — Pass 1, Move 2 (1 mark):* Recompute gains (with $C_4$ locked) for remaining cells. Skipping the detailed recomputation for brevity but noting that the algorithm continues with the next best cell.

*Step 5 — Termination and best-so-far selection (1 mark):* FM tracks the *minimum cut size observed* during the pass. If the cumulative gain at the end is non-positive, the algorithm reverts to the best partition observed.

*Step 6 — Result interpretation (1 mark):* The initial cut size 2 was *not* reduced in this single move (it became 3), but the algorithm is designed to explore beyond local minima via the cumulative-gain rollback mechanism. Typically 5–10 FM passes converge to a near-optimal cut.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to exclude internal-only nets:** When counting cut nets, students often include nets that are *wholly inside* one partition. The cut size counts *only* nets that straddle both partitions.
> 2. **Sign error in gain:** A net transitioning from *cut* to *internal* *reduces* the cut (counted as $+1$ in $D$); transitioning from *internal* to *cut* *increases* the cut (counted as $+1$ in $I$). The gain is $D - I$, not $I - D$.
> 3. **Misapplying Rent's rule:** Rent's rule gives the *expected* number of external pins; the cut size is *approximately* the number of cut nets, not the number of *pins* crossing the cut (these differ by a factor depending on net multiplicity).
> 4. **Forgetting the balance constraint:** A partition with $P_1 = \emptyset$ has cut size 0 — this is *trivial*. The KL/FM algorithms enforce a *balance ratio* (typically 0.45–0.55) to ensure meaningful partitions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Maximum Cut Size**

- ✅ **Cut size $C(P)$** counts the number of nets (hyperedges) that have endpoints in *both* partitions of a bipartition.
- ✅ **Maximum cut size $C_{\max}$** is the worst-case cut across *all* admissible partitions; it is the **pessimistic upper bound** used to reserve routing resources.
- ✅ **Bisection width $B_w$** is the cut size when $\vert P_1 \vert = \vert P_2 \vert$ — a key metric of *interconnect complexity*.
- ✅ **Rent's Rule** $T = t \cdot B^{p}$ estimates the cut size of a region of $B$ blocks; typical $p \in [0.5, 0.8]$.
- ✅ **Recursive bisection** decomposes the design into a binary tree of partitions; the *sum of per-level cuts* bounds the maximum cut size as $O(N^{p})$.
- ✅ **Random partition baseline:** for 2-pin nets, $E[C_{\text{random}}] \approx M/2$ at balanced bisection — the threshold good algorithms must beat.
- ✅ **Kernighan-Lin (KL)** algorithm: pairwise swaps, $O(N^{2} \log N)$ per pass, uses a **lock array** to prevent re-moves.
- ✅ **Fiduccia-Mattheyses (FM)** algorithm: single-cell moves, $O(N)$ per pass using a **bucket-list** data structure, handles balance constraints flexibly.
- ✅ **Gain function:** $g(v) = D(v) - I(v)$ where $D$ = nets becoming internal, $I$ = nets becoming cut.
- ✅ **Cut size reduction target:** industry algorithms achieve **40–60% reduction** over random partitioning.
- ✅ **Application domains:** standard-cell placement, FPGA routing, MCM pin assignment, NoC mapping, 3D-IC tier partitioning.
- ✅ **Sub-linear scaling:** hierarchical design works because $C_{\max} = O(N^{p})$ with $p < 1$ — *theoretical foundation* of divide-and-conquer VLSI design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wire Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Wire Density** is a quantitative metric used in the VLSI physical design flow to measure the concentration of routing demand (the number of nets/wires passing through or terminating in a specific region of the chip layout). It is the primary indicator used to estimate **routing congestion** during the **global placement** and **global routing** stages, *before* the computationally expensive detailed routing is performed.

In mathematical terms, for a given rectangular region (called a *bin* or *global routing cell* or *g-cell*) of the chip, the wire density is defined as the ratio of the total number of routing tracks (horizontal and vertical) required by all nets crossing that bin to the total number of routing tracks physically available in that bin.

$$D_{bin} = \frac{\text{Required Tracks (Demand)}}{\text{Available Tracks (Capacity)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wire density is the foundational metric that drives the **routability-driven placement** objective function. A density value **greater than 1.0** indicates a **congestion overflow**, meaning the design is likely **unroutable** with the given placement and available routing resources.

## Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a small town with a few narrow roads. As the town grows and more people (representing *nets/signals*) need to travel between houses (representing *standard cells/macros*), the limited number of narrow roads becomes a bottleneck. The traffic density on each road increases.

Now, if the town planner (the *CAD tool*) can predict traffic density *before* the people actually start driving, they can build wider roads, add flyovers, or relocate buildings. **Wire Density** is exactly this prediction — it tells the EDA tool, region-by-region: *"How crowded will this part of the chip be when we try to route all the signals here?"*

- **Low Wire Density** (<< 1) → Roads are mostly empty → Easy to route.
- **Wire Density ≈ 1** → Roads are perfectly utilized → Ideal design.
- **Wire Density > 1** → Traffic jam → **Congestion Overflow** → Routing failure.

## Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Bin / G-Cell (Global Routing Cell):** The fundamental rectangular region used to compute local density.
- **Capacity (C):** The maximum number of routing tracks available across a bin, dictated by the number of metal layers and the track pitch.
- **Demand (D):** The estimated number of tracks required to route all nets crossing or terminating in a bin.
- **Overflow (OF):** The excess demand beyond capacity, $\max(0, D - C)$.
- **Horizontal Density ($D_H$) / Vertical Density ($D_V$):** Direction-specific density measures.
- **Congestion Map:** A 2-D color-coded visualization of wire density across the chip floorplan.
- **Standard Metrics:** Wire density is **dimensionless** (a pure ratio), and is often expressed as a **percentage (×100)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Density" and not just "Congestion"?** In strict KTU terminology, *density* is the underlying geometric/electrical measurement, while *congestion* is the *consequence* (the problem state) that arises when density exceeds the available capacity. They are often used interchangeably in industry, but the formula is always a *density* ratio.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Two-Phase Estimation Approach

Wire density estimation is built on a layered methodology:

### Phase 1: Bin-Based Demand Estimation
The chip floorplan is divided into a uniform grid of **bins** (typically 8×8 to 32×32 bins for the whole chip, or finer). For each bin, the demand is estimated based on the **bounding box** of every net.

A net's bounding box (BBox) is the smallest rectangle that encloses all its pins. A simple analytical model assumes the net is routed along the perimeter of the BBox, contributing a fractional demand to each bin it touches. This is called the **L-shaped (or Steiner) routing model**.

### Phase 2: Capacity Calculation
The capacity depends on:
- The number of routing layers (N).
- The vertical and horizontal track pitch (P).
- The size of the bin (W × H).
- The presence of blockages (macros, IP blocks, memory arrays).

The vertical capacity of a bin equals $\lfloor W / P_v \rfloor \times (\text{number of vertical layers})$ and similarly for horizontal.

### Phase 3: The Density Ratio
The final wire density is simply the Demand-to-Capacity (D/C) ratio. If this exceeds **1.0**, the design has a **routability violation** at that bin.

## Key Theoretical Concepts (Bullet Logic)

- **Net Distribution:** A net of length $L$ is assumed to spread its demand uniformly across all bins it traverses. A net with BBox crossing *k* bins contributes approximately $L / k$ tracks of demand to each bin.
- **Pin Density vs. Wire Density:** Pin density only counts the *terminals* of nets. Wire density counts the *entire routing path*. Wire density is the more accurate routability predictor.
- **Layer Assignment Effect:** A net is not restricted to a single layer. Good layer assignment can lower effective density by spreading nets across multiple metal layers.
- **Overflow Cost Function:** In modern placers, density is incorporated into a **quadratic penalty** function. The placer tries to *minimize a weighted sum* of density overflows across all bins.
- **Rent's Rule Connection:** Empirical observation by E.F. Rent (1971) showed that the number of external connections (pins) of a circuit block grows as $T = K \cdot N^p$, where $0 < p < 1$ is the **Rent exponent**. Higher Rent exponent → more interconnect demand → higher wire density.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description / Variables** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $D_{bin} = D / C$ | Wire Density Ratio. $D$ = Demand, $C$ = Capacity | Dimensionless |
| $C_H = \lfloor H / P_H \rfloor \times N_H$ | Horizontal capacity. $H$ = bin height, $P_H$ = horizontal pitch, $N_H$ = # horizontal layers | Tracks |
| $C_V = \lfloor W / P_V \rfloor \times N_V$ | Vertical capacity. $W$ = bin width, $P_V$ = vertical pitch, $N_V$ = # vertical layers | Tracks |
| $D_{net,bin} = \text{length of net in bin}$ | Demand contribution by a single net to a single bin | Tracks |
| $D_{bin} = \sum_{\forall net \in bin} D_{net,bin}$ | Total demand in a bin (sum over all nets passing through) | Tracks |
| $OF_{bin} = \max(0, D_{bin} - C_{bin})$ | Overflow in a bin (excess demand) | Tracks |
| $TotalOF = \sum_{\forall bin} OF_{bin}$ | Total overflow across the chip (the cost function minimized by the placer) | Tracks |
| $T = K \cdot N^p$ | Rent's Rule. $T$ = external pins, $N$ = gates, $K$ = avg pins/gate, $p$ = Rent exponent | Pins |
| $I/O = K_c \cdot N^{p-1}$ | Internal connections per gate (derived from Rent's rule) | Connections |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule for Pipes:** Whenever you would normally write `|x|` in a formula inside a markdown table, always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to avoid breaking the table rendering.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, wire density maps drive decisions worth **millions of dollars**:
- **Early Die-Size Estimation:** If density is too high globally, the die must be made larger (more silicon area → higher cost).
- **Macro Placement:** Density maps tell engineers where to place large blocks (RAM, analog IP) to leave the highest-capacity regions for standard-cell routing.
- **Congestion-Aware Synthesis:** Modern tools use density feedback to resynthesize logic, swapping high-fanout nets for buffered tree structures.
- **Manufacturing Yield:** High-density regions cause timing failures, electromigration, and crosstalk — all of which lower yield.
- **2.5D / 3D-IC Design:** In chiplet designs, inter-chiplet wire density determines the number of TSVs / micro-bumps required.
