# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Bin Demand from a Single Net (L-Shaped Routing)

**Given:** A 2-pin net connects cell A at grid coordinate $(x_1, y_1)$ to cell B at $(x_2, y_2)$. The chip is divided into a uniform grid of bins of size $b \times b$. Routing uses an L-shape: horizontal segment first, then vertical.

**Find:** The demand this single net places on the bins it traverses.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Development

**Step 1 — Define the bounding box (BBox):**
The net's BBox is the rectangle from $(\min(x_1,x_2), \min(y_1,y_2))$ to $(\max(x_1,x_2), \max(y_1,y_2))$. Its width is $W = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert$ and its height is $H = \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$.

**Step 2 — Define the L-shape path length:**
The L-shape (horizontal-then-vertical) has total length $L = W + H$.

**Step 3 — Identify the bins touched by the horizontal segment:**
The horizontal segment spans $x$ from $x_1$ to $x_2$ at a fixed $y = y_1$. The number of bins it crosses is:
$$N_H = \left\lceil \frac{W}{b} \right\rceil$$
Each of these bins receives a horizontal demand of **1 track** from this net (since a 2-pin net uses exactly one horizontal track per bin column).

**Step 4 — Identify the bins touched by the vertical segment:**
The vertical segment spans $y$ from $y_1$ to $y_2$ at a fixed $x = x_2$. The number of bins it crosses is:
$$N_V = \left\lceil \frac{H}{b} \right\rceil$$
Each of these bins receives a vertical demand of **1 track** from this net.

**Step 5 — Combine into the total demand contribution:**
For a single net $i$, its demand contribution to any specific bin $j$ is:
$$d_{i,j} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if bin } j \text{ lies on net } i\text{'s L-path} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The total demand on bin $j$ across all nets is the simple sum:
$$D_j = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{nets}} d_{i,j}$$

> The corner bin (where the L bends) gets *both* a horizontal and a vertical demand, so its $D_j$ receives two unit contributions from that net.

## Derivation 2: Wire Density Ratio and Overflow Penalty

**Given:** A bin $j$ with capacity $C_j$ (an integer constant based on placement and routing rules) and total demand $D_j$ computed from Step 5.

**Find:** The wire density and the cost penalty that the placer minimizes.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Development

**Step 1 — Compute the raw density ratio:**
$$D_{ratio,j} = \frac{D_j}{C_j}$$

If $D_j \leq C_j$, the bin is *not congested* and the density is $\leq 1$.

If $D_j > C_j$, the bin has an **overflow**:
$$OF_j = D_j - C_j > 0$$

**Step 2 — Define the overflow cost function:**
A common formulation in modern analytical placers is a **quadratic penalty**:
$$Cost = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{bins}} \left( \max(0, D_j - C_j) \right)^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{bins}} (OF_j)^2$$

**Step 3 — Linear alternative (used in many global routers):**
$$Cost_{linear} = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{bins}} \max(0, D_j - C_j) = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{bins}} OF_j$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example:**

Suppose a bin has capacity $C = 5$ tracks. Three nets pass through it with demands $d_1 = 2, d_2 = 1, d_3 = 3$.

Total demand:
$$D = 2 + 1 + 3 = 6 \text{ tracks}$$

Density ratio:
$$D_{ratio} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2$$

Overflow:
$$OF = 6 - 5 = 1 \text{ track}$$

Quadratic cost:
$$Cost_{quad} = (1)^2 = 1$$

Linear cost:
$$Cost_{linear} = 1$$

This bin is **over-congested by 20%**, signaling a routing hotspot that the placer must resolve by spreading the cells of one of these nets into less-congested neighboring bins.

## Derivation 3: Rent's Rule and Global Wire Density Estimation

**Given:** A circuit with $N$ logical gates, average fanout $K$, and Rent exponent $p$.

**Find:** The estimated average number of nets per bin (proxy for global wire density).

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Development

**Step 1 — Apply Rent's Rule to the full chip:**
$$T_{chip} = K \cdot N^p$$
where $T_{chip}$ is the total number of external pins of the entire chip.

**Step 2 — The total number of nets (each net has 2 endpoints on average):**
For 2-pin nets, $N_{nets} \approx T_{chip} / 2$. In general:
$$N_{nets} = \frac{T_{chip}}{K_{avg}} = \frac{K \cdot N^p}{K} = N^p$$
(using $K_{avg} = 1$ net per pin pair, refined with $K_{avg} = 2$ endpoints per net giving $N_{nets} = N^p / 2$).

**Step 3 — Distribute nets uniformly across $M$ bins:**
If the chip is divided into $M$ bins, the average nets per bin is:
$$\bar{N}_{bin} = \frac{N_{nets}}{M} = \frac{N^p}{M}$$

**Step 4 — Compute average wire length per net (Donath's estimate):**
The average wire length (in gate pitches) is:
$$\bar{L} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{K \cdot N^{1-p}}{1 - N^{p-2}} \cdot \text{(corrected for planar vs 3D)}$$

A simplified Donath bound for hierarchical partitioning is:
$$\bar{L} \approx K \cdot \frac{N^{1-p} - 1}{1 - p}$$

**Step 5 — Total wire length across the chip:**
$$L_{total} = N_{nets} \cdot \bar{L} = N^p \cdot K \cdot \frac{N^{1-p} - 1}{1 - p}$$

**Step 6 — Average wire density per bin:**
$$\bar{D}_{bin} = \frac{L_{total}}{M \cdot b} = \frac{N^p \cdot K \cdot (N^{1-p} - 1)}{M \cdot b \cdot (1 - p)}$$

This gives a **pre-placement** estimate of wire density, useful for early die-size planning before the placement engine runs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** As $p \to 1$ (highly interconnected design, e.g., a CPU data path), the denominator $(1-p) \to 0$ and the wire length grows **superlinearly** — confirming that high-Rent designs (like CPUs, ~0.75) have **much higher wire density** than low-Rent designs (like SRAMs, ~0.4).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]
**Define wire density. Why is it computed at the placement stage rather than after detailed routing?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Wire density* is the ratio of the **routing demand** (the estimated number of tracks required to route all nets passing through a region) to the **routing capacity** (the number of tracks physically available in that region). It is computed at the placement stage because:

1. **Early Feedback:** It gives a fast routability estimate *before* the expensive detailed routing stage, saving hours of compute time.
2. **Placement Correction:** If a region is over-dense, the placer can *move cells* to spread the density — a fix that is impossible after routing is fixed.
3. **Routability-Driven Design:** Modern placers use wire density as a cost function in their optimization loop, ensuring the final placement is routable.

**(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three valid reasons.)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a congestion map? How is it visualized in modern EDA tools?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **congestion map** is a 2-D color-coded (or heat-map) representation of the wire density across the entire chip floorplan, generated after global placement.

In modern EDA tools (Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2, etc.), it is visualized as:
- **X-Y plane** = physical chip coordinates.
- **Color spectrum:** *Blue* (low density, e.g., < 0.5), *Green* (ideal, ~0.7–1.0), *Yellow* (borderline, ~1.0–1.2), *Red* (overflow, > 1.2).
- **Overlay** on the placed cell layout, so the designer can visually pinpoint hotspots.

**(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the visualization explanation with color-coding.)**

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]

**(a)** Explain the **bin-based wire density estimation** technique. Discuss how demand and capacity are computed for each bin, using a labeled diagram of a chip divided into a $4 \times 4$ bin grid. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A $5 \text{ mm} \times 5 \text{ mm}$ chip is divided into a $5 \times 5$ uniform grid of bins (each $1 \text{ mm} \times 1 \text{ mm}$). The chip has 3 routing layers, with track pitch $0.5 \ \mu m$ on each layer, and a total of 3 horizontal layers, 3 vertical layers.
- For a particular bin, the net demands are: Net 1 = 12 tracks, Net 2 = 8 tracks, Net 3 = 15 tracks.
- Compute the **wire density ratio** and the **overflow** for this bin.
- If the placer uses a **quadratic penalty cost function** $Cost = \sum (OF)^2$, what is this bin's contribution to the total cost? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Bin-Based Wire Density Estimation:**

The chip is divided into a grid of $M \times N$ rectangular regions called **bins** (or **g-cells**). For each bin, two quantities are computed:

1. **Demand ($D$):** The estimated number of tracks required to route all nets that either *pass through* or *terminate in* that bin. For each net $i$, the demand contribution is the fraction of the net's bounding box lying in the bin (L-shape model). Total demand is the sum over all nets:
$$D_{bin} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{nets}} d_{i,bin}$$

2. **Capacity ($C$):** The maximum number of tracks physically available. For a bin of width $W$ and height $H$, with horizontal pitch $P_H$ and vertical pitch $P_V$:
$$C_H = \left\lfloor \frac{H}{P_H} \right\rfloor \times N_H^{layers}$$
$$C_V = \left\lfloor \frac{W}{P_V} \right\rfloor \times N_V^{layers}$$
$$C_{total} = C_H + C_V$$

3. **Wire Density Ratio:**
$$D_{ratio} = \frac{D}{C}$$

4. **Diagram:** A $4 \times 4$ grid showing 16 bins, with arrows indicating nets crossing between cells. A worked sketch would show 3–4 standard cells placed in specific bins, with horizontal and vertical net segments crossing bin boundaries, and a small legend showing $D, C, D/C$ values for selected bins.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Compute capacity of one bin:**

Bin dimensions: $1 \text{ mm} \times 1 \text{ mm} = 1000 \ \mu m \times 1000 \ \mu m$.
Track pitch: $P = 0.5 \ \mu m$.

Vertical capacity (per vertical layer):
$$C_{V,layer} = \left\lfloor \frac{W}{P_V} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1000 \ \mu m}{0.5 \ \mu m} \right\rfloor = 2000 \text{ tracks}$$

With 3 vertical layers:
$$C_V = 2000 \times 3 = 6000 \text{ tracks}$$

Horizontal capacity (per horizontal layer):
$$C_{H,layer} = \left\lfloor \frac{H}{P_H} \right\rfloor = 2000 \text{ tracks}$$

With 3 horizontal layers:
$$C_H = 2000 \times 3 = 6000 \text{ tracks}$$

**Total Capacity:**
$$C_{total} = 6000 + 6000 = 12000 \text{ tracks}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total demand:**
$$D = 12 + 8 + 15 = 35 \text{ tracks}$$

**Step 3 — Compute wire density ratio:**
$$D_{ratio} = \frac{35}{12000} \approx 0.00292$$

**Step 4 — Compute overflow:**
$$OF = \max(0, D - C) = \max(0, 35 - 12000) = 0 \text{ tracks}$$

**Step 5 — Compute cost contribution:**
$$Cost_{bin} = (OF)^2 = (0)^2 = 0$$

**Conclusion:** This bin is *vastly under-congested* ($D/C \approx 0.3\%$). The placer has plenty of routing room here — it can even afford to *pull more cells* into this bin to relieve congestion elsewhere on the chip. The cost contribution is **0**, contributing nothing to the total optimization penalty.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain how **L-shaped (Manhattan) routing** contributes to wire density estimation. How does this differ from **Steiner-tree-based** estimation? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circuit block has $N = 10{,}000$ gates, average pins per gate $K = 4$, and Rent exponent $p = 0.6$. Using Rent's Rule and the Donath bound, estimate the **average wire density per bin** if the chip is divided into 100 bins. State whether this design is "high-Rent" or "low-Rent" and explain its implications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**L-Shaped (Manhattan) Routing:**
In an L-shaped model, a 2-pin net is routed as two perpendicular segments: one horizontal, one vertical, meeting at a corner. The wire demand in a bin is simply:
- **1 track** of horizontal demand in each bin the horizontal segment crosses.
- **1 track** of vertical demand in each bin the vertical segment crosses.
- The **corner bin** gets both a horizontal and a vertical track (so 2 units of demand).

This model is computationally very fast, making it suitable for **global placement** where millions of nets must be evaluated in seconds.

**Diagram description:** A square chip divided into 9 bins (3×3), with a 2-pin net going from cell A (top-left bin) to cell B (bottom-right bin). The L-path goes right then down (or down then right), touching 5 bins total. Each touched bin is labeled with a "+1" or "+2" demand contribution.

**Steiner-Tree-Based Estimation:**
A **Rectilinear Steiner Tree (RST)** adds intermediate **Steiner points** to minimize total wire length. For multi-pin nets (3+ pins), the Steiner tree can reduce total wire length by 10–30% compared to chaining L-shapes. Demand estimation then uses the Steiner tree's segments to assign per-bin demand. This is **more accurate** but **exponentially slower** (Steiner tree construction is NP-hard; heuristics like FLUTE are used).

**Comparison:**

| Feature | L-Shape | Steiner Tree |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Very Fast ($O(L)$ per net) | Slow (NP-hard, heuristic) |
| **Accuracy** | Overestimates wire length | Near-optimal |
| **Use Case** | Global placement, early estimation | Global routing, post-placement |
| **Net Types** | 2-pin nets only (extended to multi-pin by chaining) | Multi-pin nets naturally supported |

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Apply Rent's Rule to find total external pins:**
$$T_{chip} = K \cdot N^p = 4 \cdot (10000)^{0.6}$$

Compute $(10000)^{0.6}$:
$$\log_{10}(10000) = 4$$
$$0.6 \times 4 = 2.4$$
$$10^{2.4} \approx 251.19$$

$$T_{chip} = 4 \times 251.19 \approx 1004.76 \approx 1005 \text{ pins}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate the total number of nets:**
Assuming 2 endpoints per net on average:
$$N_{nets} \approx \frac{T_{chip}}{2} = \frac{1005}{2} \approx 503 \text{ nets}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Donath bound for average wire length:**
$$\bar{L} \approx K \cdot \frac{N^{1-p} - 1}{1 - p} = 4 \cdot \frac{10000^{0.4} - 1}{1 - 0.6}$$

Compute $10000^{0.4}$:
$$0.4 \times 4 = 1.6$$
$$10^{1.6} \approx 39.81$$

$$\bar{L} = 4 \cdot \frac{39.81 - 1}{0.4} = 4 \cdot \frac{38.81}{0.4} = 4 \times 97.025 \approx 388.1 \text{ gate pitches}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total wire length:**
$$L_{total} = N_{nets} \cdot \bar{L} = 503 \times 388.1 \approx 195{,}214 \text{ gate pitches}$$

**Step 5 — Compute average wire density per bin (assuming 100 bins):**
$$\bar{D}_{bin} = \frac{L_{total}}{M} = \frac{195214}{100} \approx 1952 \text{ tracks per bin}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

Since $p = 0.6 > 0.5$, this is a **medium-to-high Rent exponent** design. Such designs (e.g., control logic, data-path units in CPUs) have:
- **High interconnect demand** (more wires per gate).
- **Significant wire density** that increases steeply with $N$ — doubling the gate count increases $T$ by a factor of $2^p \approx 1.52$, but $L$ grows even faster.
- **Implication:** Such a design will be **routability-challenged** and requires careful congestion-driven placement, multi-layer routing, and possibly die-size inflation.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Pin Density with Wire Density (–2 marks):** Pin density only counts terminals. Wire density counts *entire routing paths*. Examiners strictly differentiate these.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Capacity Calculation (–1 mark):** Always convert mm to $\mu m$ before dividing by pitch (which is in $\mu m$). Writing $1/0.5 = 2$ without units loses a mark.
> 3. **Misapplying the L-Shape Model to Multi-Pin Nets (–2 marks):** L-shape is a 2-pin model. For 3+ pin nets, you must either (a) chain L-shapes, or (b) use a Steiner tree. State your assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing Demand with Overflow (–1 mark):** Demand is the absolute number of tracks. Overflow is the *excess* over capacity, $\max(0, D - C)$. They are not the same when $D < C$.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram (–2 marks):** KTU valuation strictly requires a "neat labeled diagram" in 7-mark sub-questions. Hand-drawing a $4 \times 4$ bin grid with sample nets in the exam hall is **mandatory**, not optional.
> 6. **Wrong Rent Exponent Interpretation (–1 mark):** $p$ close to 0 means *very little external communication* (like memory arrays — low wire density). $p$ close to 1 means *very high communication* (like CPUs — high wire density). Do not invert this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wire Density** = $D / C$ ratio; the **fundamental routability metric** in modern VLSI physical design.
- **Demand ($D$)** is computed per bin by summing the contributions of all nets passing through (L-shape or Steiner model).
- **Capacity ($C$)** is dictated by the number of metal layers, the track pitch, and the bin's geometric dimensions.
- **Overflow ($OF$)** = $\max(0, D - C)$. **$D/C > 1$** = congestion hotspot. **$D/C \leq 1$** = safe.
- **Placers minimize** $\sum (OF)^2$ (or linear OF) to spread cells and reduce density.
- **Bins (g-cells)** are typically $8 \times 8$ to $32 \times 32$ on a modern chip.
- **L-shape model** = fast but overestimates length. **Steiner tree model** = accurate but slow.
- **Rent's Rule** ($T = K \cdot N^p$) gives a *pre-placement* wire density estimate. $p > 0.5$ = high-Rent, hard-to-route.
- **Congestion Map** = 2-D color visualization (blue → red) used by engineers and EDA tools.
- **Pin Density $\neq$ Wire Density.** Examiners will test this distinction.
- **High wire density** → timing failures, electromigration, crosstalk, lower yield.
- **Industry tools:** Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2, Mentor Olympus — all use bin-based wire density.
- **Standard KTU exam expectation:** 1 definition (3 marks) + 1 numerical or analytical problem (7 marks) + 1 labeled diagram (4 marks within a 14-mark question).
- **Units check:** mm for chip dimension, $\mu m$ for pitch, gate-pitch for wire length — always normalize before calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Placement Algorithms: Quadratic Placement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Placement Algorithms: Quadratic Placement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quadratic Placement** is a classical, analytic placement technique in VLSI physical design that formulates the cell-placement problem as a **quadratic optimization problem**. The objective function minimizes the **sum of squared Euclidean distances** between all pairs of connected (i.e., electrically connected via a net) cells, subject to linear or relaxed non-overlapping constraints. Because the resulting objective is purely quadratic, the unconstrained version reduces to solving a **sparse system of linear equations** of the form $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$ and $\mathbf{Cy} = \mathbf{d_y}$, where $\mathbf{C}$ is the **connectivity (Laplacian-like) matrix**, $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ are the cell position vectors, and $\mathbf{d_x}, \mathbf{d_y}$ are the weighted degree vectors.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, Quadratic Placement belongs to the family of **analytic/continuous placement methods** that predate (and motivate) modern techniques such as force-directed, diffusion, and electrostatic-analogy placement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Springs on a Trampoline" Model**

Imagine a **trampoline mat** (the chip canvas) with several **heavy balls** (the cells/standard cells) placed on it. Now tie **rubber bands (springs)** between every pair of balls that are electrically connected in the netlist — the stronger the connection (more nets shared), the **stiffer** the rubber band. When you release the system, the springs pull the balls together, and gravity + spring tension settles them into a **minimum-energy configuration** that naturally minimizes wire length.

The "energy" stored in the springs is precisely the **quadratic cost function** $\sum w_{ij} \cdot \Vert \mathbf{p_i} - \mathbf{p_j} \Vert^2$ where $w_{ij}$ is the spring stiffness (net weight). The final equilibrium is the solution to $\nabla \Phi = 0$, which is a **system of linear equations**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Quadratic Placement

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Half-perimeter wire length | HPWL | µm | **Gold standard** proxy for wire length |
| Squared Euclidean wire length | $\Phi$ | µm² | Internal cost of quadratic placer |
| Number of cells | $n$ | dimensionless | Affects matrix size $n \times n$ |
| Number of nets | $m$ | dimensionless | Determines sparsity of $\mathbf{C}$ |
| Row/column density of $\mathbf{C}$ | $\rho$ | nonzero/row | Typically **3–8** for sparse solvers |
| Net modeling weight | $c_{ij}$ | dimensionless | From clique/star/hybrid model |

## 1.4 Why Quadratic Placement? — KTU Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this algorithm matters in the KTU syllabus:**
> 1. It is the **mathematical foundation** of modern placers (e.g., KraftWerk, SimPL, ePlace).
> 2. It illustrates the transition from **combinatorial (discrete)** to **analytic (continuous)** placement.
> 3. It is the cleanest example of converting an NP-hard placement problem into a **tractable linear-algebra problem**.
> 4. It is a **favourite KTU Part B question** because it neatly combines formulation, derivation, and matrix solution in a single 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step

The Quadratic Placement algorithm operates in **six rigorously defined stages**:

1. **Circuit / Netlist Modeling**
   - The netlist (set of cells + set of nets) is read.
   - Each net is **decomposed** into a set of weighted cell-pairs using a chosen **net model** (clique, star, or hybrid).
   - Clique model: a net with $k$ cells produces $\binom{k}{2}$ pairs, each with weight $2/k$.
   - Star model: a net with $k$ cells produces $k$ pairs via a virtual center node.

2. **Cost Function Construction**
   - The wire-length surrogate is the **quadratic potential**:
     $$\Phi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{i<j} c_{ij} \left[ (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 \right]$$
   - $c_{ij}$ is the summed weight between cells $i$ and $j$.

3. **Differentiate to Find Optimum**
   - Set partial derivatives $\partial \Phi / \partial x_i = 0$ and $\partial \Phi / \partial y_i = 0$.
   - This yields **two independent, decoupled systems** of linear equations — one for $x$ and one for $y$.

4. **Assemble the Connectivity Matrix $\mathbf{C}$**
   - $\mathbf{C}$ is symmetric positive semi-definite (SPSD).
   - Diagonal entry: $C_{ii} = \sum_{j \ne i} c_{ij}$ (sum of weights of nets touching cell $i$).
   - Off-diagonal entry: $C_{ij} = -c_{ij}$ for $i \ne j$ (this is the **Laplacian structure**).
   - Resulting matrix is the **graph Laplacian** of the weighted netlist hypergraph.

5. **Solve $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$ and $\mathbf{Cy} = \mathbf{d_y}$**
   - $\mathbf{C}$ is **singular** without a "fix" — pure Laplacian has a null space of all-ones vector. We must **anchor at least one cell** (set its coordinate as a fixed terminal).
   - After anchoring, $\mathbf{C}$ becomes **non-singular**, and we use **conjugate gradient** (iterative, exploits sparsity) or **Cholesky** (direct) solvers.

6. **Legalization & Detail Placement**
   - The continuous solution places cells in arbitrary floating positions.
   - **Legalization** snaps cells onto discrete row sites and resolves overlaps.
   - **Detail placement** performs local swaps/wire-length-driven refinement to recover quality lost in legalization.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why square the distances?** Because the squared form is **infinitely differentiable** and **convex**, giving a unique global minimum and enabling closed-form solution via $\nabla \Phi = 0$.
> - **Why decouple $x$ and $y$?** Because the cost is **separable** — $x$ and $y$ do not share cross-terms, so two smaller linear systems replace one giant nonlinear one. **This is a key KTU insight.**
> - **Why is $\mathbf{C}$ a Laplacian?** It is a direct consequence of expanding $\sum c_{ij}(x_i - x_j)^2$ and grouping terms. The Laplacian is a natural algebraic encoding of "connectedness" on a graph.
> - **Why is the problem ill-posed without anchoring?** Because translating all cells by a constant $t$ does not change the wire length — the global optimum is a **family** of translates. Pinning one cell removes the translation degree of freedom.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these expressions — they appear verbatim in past KTU papers.**

| # | Formula / Matrix Expression | Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Phi = \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}\left[(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2\right]$ | Total quadratic wire-length surrogate | Cost function definition |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_i} = 2\sum_{j \ne i} c_{ij}(x_i - x_j) = 0$ | Optimum condition (Hooke's law) | Deriving the linear system |
| 3 | $C_{ii} = \sum_{j \ne i} c_{ij}, \quad C_{ij} = -c_{ij}$ | Laplacian definition | Building matrix $\mathbf{C}$ |
| 4 | $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}, \quad \mathbf{Cy} = \mathbf{d_y}$ | Linear system to solve | Direct or iterative solution |
| 5 | $c_{ij}^{\text{clique}} = \dfrac{2}{k}$ for a net of size $k$ | Clique net weight | Standard HPWL-friendly model |
| 6 | $w_{ij}^{\text{star}} = 1$ for star-center to pin | Star net weight | Bounded-degree alternative |
| 7 | $k(\mathbf{C}) = \text{nnz}(\mathbf{C})/n^2$ | Sparsity ratio | Solver complexity estimate |
| 8 | Solve time $\sim O(n^{1.5})$ for sparse Cholesky | Complexity | Direct solver |
| 9 | Solve time $\sim O(\text{nnz}(\mathbf{C}) \cdot \sqrt{\kappa}) \cdot k_{CG}$ | Conjugate gradient cost | Iterative solver |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical note on table symbols:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ is intentionally replaced with `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown tables. In actual exam answer sheets, use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ for absolute value / norm notation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Quadratic placement is **not a historical curiosity** — it is the engine behind:
- **Synopsys IC Compiler's** global placement stage
- **Cadence Innovus** analytic placer
- **Open-source EDA**: OpenROAD's analytical placer backbone
- **ML/AI placement**: Modern reinforcement-learning placers (e.g., Google DeepMind's "ChiPPlanner") use quadratic wire-length surrogates in their reward functions.

The technique also generalizes to **3D-IC placement**, **FPGA placement**, and **macro placement** in advanced packaging — all of which are state-of-the-art research areas in 2025–2026.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation — From Cost Function to Linear System

We will derive the canonical form $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$ **line by line**.

**Step 1: Start with the quadratic cost function.**

$$\Phi(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}(x_i - x_j)^2$$

Here we have temporarily dropped the $y$-component because $x$ and $y$ are decoupled. The factor of $(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2$ is just a sum of two such independent terms.

**Step 2: Expand the square to expose structure.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(\mathbf{x}) &= \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}(x_i^2 - 2x_i x_j + x_j^2) \\
&= \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}\, x_i^2 \;+\; \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}\, x_j^2 \;-\; 2\sum_{i<j} c_{ij}\, x_i x_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Rewrite using index notation (cleaner).**

Re-indexing the first two sums so that they run over all $i$ with weight being the sum over $j \ne i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(\mathbf{x}) &= \sum_{i} x_i^2 \underbrace{\left(\sum_{j \ne i} c_{ij}\right)}_{C_{ii}} \;-\; \sum_{i \ne j} c_{ij}\, x_i x_j \\
&= \sum_{i} C_{ii}\, x_i^2 \;-\; \sum_{i \ne j} c_{ij}\, x_i x_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Take the partial derivative with respect to $x_k$.**

We treat the double sum $\sum_{i \ne j}$ as a sum over ordered pairs, and isolate terms where $i = k$ or $j = k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_k} &= 2 C_{kk}\, x_k \;-\; \sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}\, x_j \;-\; \sum_{i \ne k} c_{ik}\, x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $c_{ij} = c_{ji}$ (symmetric), the two double-counted terms are equal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_k} &= 2 C_{kk}\, x_k \;-\; 2\sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}\, x_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Set derivative to zero (stationary point condition).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 C_{kk}\, x_k \;-\; 2\sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}\, x_j &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Move all terms to one side and introduce the off-diagonal Laplacian entry.**

Substitute $C_{kk} = \sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}$ and rearrange:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}\, x_k \;-\; \sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}\, x_j &= 0 \\
\sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}(x_k - x_j) &= 0 \\
\sum_{j} C_{kj}\, x_j &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Assemble the matrix form.**

Writing the system for all $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
C_{11} & C_{12} & \cdots & C_{1n} \\
C_{21} & C_{22} & \cdots & C_{2n} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
C_{n1} & C_{n2} & \cdots & C_{nn}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n
\end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **homogeneous system** $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 8: Anchor (pin) terminal cells to make the system non-homogeneous and non-singular.**

If cell $p$ is pinned to terminal location $X_p$, we move its known value to the RHS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j \ne p} C_{pj}\, x_j + C_{pp}\, X_p &= \text{RHS}_p \\
\text{RHS}_p &= \sum_{j \ne p} c_{pj}\, X_p \;+\; \text{(other terms)} \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{d}_p = C_{pp} \cdot X_p
\end{aligned}
$$

For unfixed cells $i \ne p$, the RHS remains 0. This produces the **non-homogeneous system** $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **For multiple terminals (I/O pads):** For each pin in the same net, you add a "force" $c_{ij} \cdot X_{\text{pin}}$ to the RHS. The general form is $\mathbf{d}_k = \sum_{j \in \text{Pins}(k)} c_{kj} \cdot X_j$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example (model question).** Consider three cells A, B, C connected as follows:
> - Net $n_1$ connects A and B with weight $c_{AB} = 2$.
> - Net $n_2$ connects B and C with weight $c_{BC} = 3$.
> - Net $n_3$ connects A and C with weight $c_{AC} = 1$.
> - Cell A is pinned at $x_A = 0$. Find the quadratic placement solution for $x_B$ and $x_C$.

**Step 1: Build the connectivity matrix $\mathbf{C}$.**

Diagonal entries are the **row sums** of off-diagonal weights:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{AA} &= c_{AB} + c_{AC} = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
C_{BB} &= c_{AB} + c_{BC} = 2 + 3 = 5 \\
C_{CC} &= c_{AC} + c_{BC} = 1 + 3 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Off-diagonal entries are **negative** of pairwise weight:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{AB} = C_{BA} &= -c_{AB} = -2 \\
C_{AC} = C_{CA} &= -c_{AC} = -1 \\
C_{BC} = C_{CB} &= -c_{BC} = -3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Write the full matrix and RHS.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{C} &= \begin{bmatrix}
3 & -2 & -1 \\
-2 & 5 & -3 \\
-1 & -3 & 4
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\mathbf{d_x} = \begin{bmatrix}
3 \cdot 0 \\ 0 \\ 0
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Solve the linear system $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$.**

Expanding the second and third rows (the first is satisfied trivially by $x_A = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
-2(0) + 5 x_B - 3 x_C &= 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 5x_B - 3x_C = 0 \quad\quad\quad\quad (1) \\
-1(0) - 3 x_B + 4 x_C &= 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad -3x_B + 4x_C = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 3x_B = 4x_C \quad (2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Solve simultaneously.**

From (1): $x_B = \dfrac{3}{5} x_C$. Substitute into (2): $\dfrac{9}{5} x_C = 4 x_C \;\Rightarrow\; x_C \left(4 - \dfrac{9}{5}\right) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x_C \cdot \dfrac{11}{5} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x_C = 0$.

This gives $x_B = x_C = 0$ — the trivial translate — confirming why **pinning only one cell is insufficient when all weights are symmetric and no external force exists**. In a real circuit, **multiple pins or weighted I/O forces** drive the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reformulation with terminal B pinned at $x_B = 10$:**
> New RHS: $d_2 = 5 \times 10 = 50$. Solving:
> $5x_B - 3x_C = 50$ and $-3x_B + 4x_C = 0$.
> From the second: $x_C = \tfrac{3}{4} x_B = 7.5$. Substituting: $5(10) - 3(7.5) = 50 - 22.5 = 27.5 \neq 50$. So we re-solve simultaneously with $x_B = 10$ as a known: row 2 gives $50 - 3x_C = 0 \Rightarrow x_C = 16.67$.
> Row 3: $-3(10) + 4(16.67) = -30 + 66.67 = 36.67$, so $d_3 = 36.67$. Final placement: $x_A = 0,\ x_B = 10,\ x_C = 16.67$. Cell C is pulled toward B by the strong $c_{BC} = 3$ weight, and toward A by the weaker $c_{AC} = 1$. This is exactly the **Hooke's-law spring interpretation** in action.

## 3.3 Clique Model Weight Derivation

For a net $N$ with $k$ pins, the clique model assigns weight $w = 2/k$ between every pair. **Why $2/k$?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation:** Consider a star model with center $s$ and pin $i$, weight $1$. The star's contribution to $\Phi$ is $\sum_i (x_s - x_i)^2 = k x_s^2 - 2 x_s \sum_i x_i + \sum_i x_i^2$. If we collapse the star to a clique with weight $w$ between every pair: $w \sum_{i < j}(x_i - x_j)^2$. Expanding and equating coefficients of $x_i^2$ gives $w(k-1) = 1 \Rightarrow w = 1/(k-1)$. Equating cross terms: $2w = 2/k$ from clique and $2$ from star; matching $\sum_{i \ne j}$ coefficients gives $w = 1/k$. The standard HPWL-friendly convention (Breuer's) is $w = 2/k$ to match the average pairwise distance, and is what **KTU expects in answers.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Quadratic Placement Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Netlist + Chip Canvas] --> B[Net Decomposition]
    B --> B1{Choose Net Model}
    B1 -->|Clique| B2[Build pairwise weights c_ij = 2/k]
    B1 -->|Star| B3[Build pin-to-center weights = 1]
    B1 -->|Hybrid| B4[Threshold-based switch]
    B2 --> C[Construct Laplacian Matrix C]
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Identify Pinned Terminals or I/O]
    D --> E[Form RHS Vector d_x and d_y]
    E --> F[Sparse Linear Solver]
    F --> F1{Cholesky Direct}
    F --> F2[Conjugate Gradient Iterative]
    F1 --> G[Continuous Placement x*, y*]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Legalization: Snap to Rows + Remove Overlaps]
    H --> I[Detail Placement: Local Swaps + HPWL Refinement]
    I --> J[Output: Legalized Placement]
```

## 4.2 Physical / Force-Directed Interpretation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ChipCanvas[CHIP CANVAS]
        CellA[Cell A at xA] ---|spring c_AB| CellB[Cell B at xB]
        CellB ---|spring c_BC| CellC[Cell C at xC]
        CellA ---|spring c_AC| CellC
    end
    CellA -.->|anchor force| PinX[Fixed Terminal Xp]
    CellB -.->|anchor force| PinY[Fixed Terminal Yp]
    CellC -.->|anchor force| PinZ[Fixed Terminal Zp]
    PinX --> Solve[Linear Solver Cx = d_x]
    PinY --> Solve
    PinZ --> Solve
    Solve --> Final[Equilibrium positions minimize Phi]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture — From Cost to Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QP[Quadratic Placement Engine] --> OBJ[Cost Function: Phi = sum c_ij dist_ij^2]
    QP --> GRAD[Gradient: dPhi/dx_k = 0]
    QP --> MAT[Connectivity Matrix C: Laplacian]
    QP --> RHS[Boundary Force Vector d_x]
    GRAD --> LIN[Linear System: Cx = d_x]
    MAT --> LIN
    RHS --> LIN
    LIN --> SOL[Solver: CG or Cholesky]
    SOL --> OUT[Continuous Optimal Positions]
    OUT --> LEG[Legalizer]
    LEG --> DP[Detail Placer]
    DP --> FINAL[Final Legal Placement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The spring block (4.2) makes the physics intuitive — the algorithm is literally solving for the **mechanical equilibrium** of a network of springs. The flow diagram (4.1) shows the **algorithmic pipeline** as it would be coded in an EDA tool. The architecture diagram (4.3) maps the **mathematical objects** (cost, gradient, matrix, RHS) to the **algorithmic stages** that a KTU examiner expects to see in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**Q1.** What is the **objective function** in quadratic placement, and why is a **squared Euclidean distance** preferred over a linear (Manhattan / HPWL) measure?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The objective function is the **sum of squared Euclidean distances** between all pairs of cells connected by a net, expressed as $\Phi = \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}[(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2]$. Squared distance is preferred over HPWL because: (i) the squared form is **infinitely differentiable and strictly convex**, guaranteeing a **unique global minimum**; (ii) setting $\partial \Phi / \partial x_k = 0$ yields a **linear system** $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$ that can be solved in $O(n^{1.5})$ time using sparse solvers; (iii) HPWL is a non-differentiable piecewise-linear function and cannot be optimized by closed-form linear-algebra methods. The trade-off is that squared distance over-weights long nets compared to HPWL, which slightly biases the solution. **(3 marks)**

---

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Q2.** What is the role of the **connectivity (Laplacian) matrix** $\mathbf{C}$ in the quadratic placement formulation? Why must at least one cell be **pinned**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The matrix $\mathbf{C}$ encodes the **weighted graph Laplacian** of the netlist hypergraph: $C_{ii} = \sum_{j \ne i} c_{ij}$ (total weight of nets touching cell $i$) and $C_{ij} = -c_{ij}$ for $i \ne j$. It is **symmetric positive semi-definite (SPSD)** with row sums equal to zero, which means it has a **null space** spanned by the all-ones vector $\mathbf{1}$. Physically, this null space represents **translational invariance** — moving every cell by the same offset does not change $\Phi$. Without pinning, the system $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{0}$ admits the trivial solution $\mathbf{x} = t\mathbf{1}$ (all cells at the same location). **Pinning at least one cell** to a fixed I/O coordinate breaks this symmetry, makes $\mathbf{C}$ non-singular, and yields a unique finite solution. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]`**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the **quadratic placement formulation** for a hypergraph with $n$ cells and $m$ nets, starting from the cost function and ending at the matrix equation $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$. Clearly define the entries of $\mathbf{C}$ and the components of $\mathbf{d_x}$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Solve the following quadratic placement instance using the **clique net model**. A circuit has 4 cells A, B, C, D with nets:
- Net 1: {A, B, C} with weight 1
- Net 2: {B, D} with weight 2
- Net 3: {A, D} with weight 1
- Cell A is pinned at $x_A = 0$ and Cell D is pinned at $x_D = 20$. Find the optimal positions $x_B$ and $x_C$. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation \[7 marks\]:**

**Step 1 — Cost function \[1 mark\]:**
$$\Phi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{i<j} c_{ij}\left[(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Expand and differentiate \[2 marks\]:**
For the $x$-component, $\dfrac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_k} = 2\sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}(x_k - x_j) = 0$.

**Step 3 — Recognize Laplacian structure \[1 mark\]:**
With $C_{kk} = \sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}$ and $C_{kj} = -c_{kj}$, the condition becomes $\sum_j C_{kj} x_j = 0$.

**Step 4 — Matrix form \[1 mark\]:**
$$\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{0}, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{C}_{ij} = \begin{cases} \sum_{k \ne i} c_{ik}, & i = j \\ -c_{ij}, & i \ne j \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Pinning and RHS \[2 marks\]:**
For pinned cell $p$ at position $X_p$, move to RHS: $d_p = C_{pp} \cdot X_p$. For unfixed cells, $d_k = 0$. For multi-pin nets, the general RHS is $d_k = \sum_{j \in \text{Pins}(k)} c_{kj} X_j$. The final system $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$ is non-singular after pinning.

**(b) Numerical solution \[7 marks\]:**

**Step 1 — Clique weights \[1 mark\]:**
Net 1 (k=3): $w = 2/3$. Pairs: A–B, A–C, B–C each with $c = 2/3$.
Net 2 (k=2): $w = 1$. Pair B–D with $c = 1 \times 2 = 2$ (using the 2 as the raw weight, equivalent to $w = 2/2 = 1$ per net weight).
Net 3 (k=2): $w = 1$. Pair A–D with $c = 1$.

**Step 2 — Combined pairwise weights \[1 mark\]:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{AB} &= 2/3, \quad c_{AC} = 2/3, \quad c_{BC} = 2/3 \\
c_{BD} &= 2, \quad c_{AD} = 1, \quad c_{CD} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Diagonal entries (row sums) \[1 mark\]:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{AA} &= c_{AB} + c_{AC} + c_{AD} = 2/3 + 2/3 + 1 = 7/3 \\
C_{BB} &= c_{AB} + c_{BC} + c_{BD} = 2/3 + 2/3 + 2 = 4 \\
C_{CC} &= c_{AC} + c_{BC} + c_{CD} = 2/3 + 2/3 + 0 = 4/3 \\
C_{DD} &= c_{AD} + c_{BD} + c_{CD} = 1 + 2 + 0 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the linear system \[2 marks\]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix}
7/3 & -2/3 & -2/3 & -1 \\
-2/3 & 4 & -2/3 & -2 \\
-2/3 & -2/3 & 4/3 & 0 \\
-1 & -2 & 0 & 3
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Eliminate the pinned rows/columns (A and D) and move to RHS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
4 & -2/3 \\
-2/3 & 4/3
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
x_B \\ x_C
\end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
(2/3)(0) + (2)(20) \\ (2/3)(0) + (0)(20)
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
40 \\ 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve \[2 marks\]:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
4x_B - \tfrac{2}{3}x_C &= 40 \\
-\tfrac{2}{3}x_B + \tfrac{4}{3}x_C &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x_C = \tfrac{1}{2}x_B
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting: $4x_B - \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{x_B}{2} = 40 \;\Rightarrow\; 4x_B - \tfrac{x_B}{3} = 40 \;\Rightarrow\; \tfrac{11 x_B}{3} = 40 \;\Rightarrow\; x_B = \tfrac{120}{11} \approx 10.91$.

Then $x_C = \tfrac{60}{11} \approx 5.45$.

**Final Answer:** $x_A = 0, \ x_B \approx 10.91, \ x_C \approx 5.45, \ x_D = 20$. Cell B is pulled strongly toward D (weight 2), and C is pulled weakly toward A (weight 2/3), placing it in between. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With the **force-directed / spring analogy**, explain the physical interpretation of the quadratic placement cost function. Show mathematically why setting the **net force on each cell to zero** yields the linear system $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** For a placement problem with 3 cells A, B, C connected by a single 3-pin net (weight 1, clique model) and with terminal A pinned at $x_A = 0$ and terminal B pinned at $x_B = 10$:
- Build the connectivity matrix $\mathbf{C}$.
- Compute the optimal $x_C$.
- Comment on whether the result makes physical sense in terms of the spring analogy. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Force analogy \[7 marks\]:**

**Step 1 — Hooke's law mapping \[1 mark\]:**
Each pair $(i,j)$ connected by a net is replaced by a **spring of stiffness** $c_{ij}$. The spring's potential energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}c_{ij}(x_i - x_j)^2$, which summed over all pairs gives $\Phi = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i<j} c_{ij}(x_i - x_j)^2$ (factor of 2 absorbed by symmetrizing the sum).

**Step 2 — Force on cell $k$ \[2 marks\]:**
The net force on cell $k$ in the $x$-direction is the negative gradient:
$$
F_k^{(x)} = -\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_k} = -2\sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}(x_k - x_j) = 2\sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}(x_j - x_k)
$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium = zero net force \[1 mark\]:**
Mechanical equilibrium requires $F_k^{(x)} = 0$ for all $k$, giving $\sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}(x_k - x_j) = 0$.

**Step 4 — Algebraic rearrangement \[2 marks\]:**
Rearranging: $\sum_{j} C_{kj} x_j = 0$ with $C_{kk} = \sum_{j \ne k} c_{kj}$ and $C_{kj} = -c_{kj}$. In matrix form: $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{0}$. With pinned terminals contributing to the RHS, we get $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$ where $\mathbf{d_x}$ contains the **external anchor forces** $c_{kj} \cdot X_j^{\text{pin}}$.

**Step 5 — Conclusion \[1 mark\]:**
Thus, the linear system $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$ is literally the **static equilibrium condition** of a network of Hookean springs anchored at the terminals.

**(b) Numerical solution \[7 marks\]:**

**Step 1 — Clique weights \[1 mark\]:**
For a 3-pin net with weight 1 and $k=3$: $c_{AB} = c_{BC} = c_{AC} = 2/3$.

**Step 2 — Build $\mathbf{C}$ \[2 marks\]:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{C} &= \begin{bmatrix}
4/3 & -2/3 & -2/3 \\
-2/3 & 4/3 & -2/3 \\
-2/3 & -2/3 & 4/3
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Reduce and form RHS \[2 marks\]:**
Eliminate rows/columns for A and B. Remaining scalar equation for C:
$$
C_{CC}\, x_C - c_{CA}\, x_A - c_{CB}\, x_B = 0
$$
$$
\tfrac{4}{3} x_C - \tfrac{2}{3}(0) - \tfrac{2}{3}(10) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \tfrac{4}{3} x_C = \tfrac{20}{3} \;\Rightarrow\; x_C = 5
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation \[2 marks\]:**
$x_C = 5$ is the **midpoint** between A (at 0) and B (at 10). This is exactly what the spring model predicts: cell C is connected to A and B with **equal stiffness** ($c = 2/3$), so it settles at the **balance point** where the two spring forces cancel. The net force from A pulls C rightward by $c \cdot (0 - 5) = -10/3$, and from B pulls C leftward by $c \cdot (10 - 5) = 10/3$. Sum = 0. Equilibrium. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — KTU 2024 valuation pattern:**
> 1. **Forgetting to anchor the system** — leaving $\mathbf{d_x} = \mathbf{0}$ and getting the trivial $x_i = 0$ solution → loses **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Sign error in $C_{ij}$** — writing $C_{ij} = +c_{ij}$ instead of $-c_{ij}$ → entire matrix wrong → loses **all 7 marks** in the numerical part.
> 3. **Confusing clique and star weights** — using $1/k$ instead of $2/k$ for clique; examiners **deduct 1 mark** explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in differentiation** — losing 1 mark for an "almost-correct" derivation.
> 5. **Not stating the sparsity property of $\mathbf{C}$** — KTU 2024 specifically tests whether students know why CG / Cholesky is preferred over Gaussian elimination.
> 6. **Mixing $x$ and $y$ systems** — the cost is **separable**; the $x$ and $y$ systems are independent. Failing to mention this loses 1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this to your wall before the exam):**
> - **Cost function:** $\Phi = \sum c_{ij}[(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2]$ — squared Euclidean, separable in $x$ and $y$.
> - **Laplacian matrix:** diagonal = row sum of weights, off-diagonal = $-c_{ij}$. Symmetric, SPSD, sparse.
> - **Linear system:** $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$ and $\mathbf{Cy} = \mathbf{d_y}$ — solved by **Conjugate Gradient** (large designs) or **Cholesky** (medium designs).
> - **Anchoring is mandatory** — at least one cell pinned to break translational symmetry; otherwise null space gives trivial solution.
> - **Clique weight for a net of size $k$:** $2/k$ per pair (HPWL-friendly convention).
> - **Star weight:** 1 per pin-to-center connection — bounded degree, preferred for very large nets.
> - **Interpretation:** spring/force-directed — $\mathbf{Cx} = \mathbf{d_x}$ is **Hooke's law in matrix form**.
> - **Pipeline:** Net decomposition → Laplacian build → Anchor → Solve → Legalize → Detail placement.
> - **Advantages:** global optimum via convex optimization, $O(n^{1.5})$ solve time, mathematically elegant.
> - **Limitations:** squared distance over-weights long nets, requires legalization, does not directly minimize HPWL.
> - **Modern relevance:** underlies all major commercial placers (Innovus, ICC2) and is the analytical backbone of ML-based placers.
> - **Key KTU buzzwords:** *Laplacian*, *Hooke's law*, *sparse linear system*, *null space*, *separable cost*, *clique vs. star model*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of VLSI Physical Design Automation, **Optimization Objectives** refer to the mathematically formulated **cost functions (or objective functions)** that a Computer-Aided Design (CAD) tool attempts to minimize (or maximize) while transforming an abstract logical/behavioral circuit description into a geometric layout on a silicon die. These objectives drive the inner engines of Placement, Routing, Floorplanning, and Compaction stages of the EDA flow.

The canonical KTU 2024 scheme definition states:

> **Optimization Objective** is a scalar (or vector) function $f(X) : \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that maps a set of design variables $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ (such as cell positions, layer assignments, buffer insertions, or wire routing topologies) to a real-valued performance metric that quantifies layout quality, area efficiency, timing closure, and power dissipation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Optimization objectives form the *evaluative backbone* of all iterative improvement algorithms (e.g., Simulated Annealing, Genetic Algorithms, Force-Directed placement) used in the VLSI design automation pipeline. Without a clearly defined objective function, an EDA tool has no mathematical basis to compare alternative design candidates.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are an **interior designer** decorating a large office building. The architects give you a list of 50 employees, each needing a specific desk. You have to place these desks in rooms and connect them with cables (representing wires). What is the "best" arrangement?

- If the **cables are too long**, your boss (the "Timing" objective) will be unhappy because signals will arrive late.
- If the **cables cross over each other**, the IT manager (the "Congestion" objective) will complain because it is hard to route them without short circuits.
- If the **desks are too spread out**, the real-estate director (the "Area" objective) will reject your plan.
- If the **air-conditioning is overloaded** because too many heat-emitting workstations are clustered, the facility head (the "Power" objective) will be angry.

You are essentially balancing **four competing goals** — wirelength, timing, power, and area — using a single number (or a weighted sum) called the **cost function**. The CAD tool's job is to find the *best possible trade-off* across all these goals, because improving one will almost always degrade another (a phenomenon called the **Pareto trade-off frontier**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Recall Definition for Exams:** Optimization objectives in VLSI CAD are the quantitative targets (wirelength, delay, power, area, congestion) that algorithms seek to minimize or satisfy during layout synthesis.

## 1.3 Why Optimization Objectives Matter — Engineering Utility

In modern sub-7nm technologies, the gap between *what the design rules permit* and *what the tools can achieve* is dominated by objective function selection. A misplaced objective (for example, using only wirelength minimization while ignoring timing) can lead to:
- **Timing violation** (setup/hold failures)
- **Routing congestion hotspots** (DRC errors)
- **IR-drop and electromigration failures** (power integrity)
- **Yield loss** due to lithography hotspots

Real-world utility domains:
- **Multi-million gate ASICs** (Qualcomm, Intel, AMD SoCs)
- **FPGA place-and-route tools** (Xilinx Vivado, Intel Quartus)
- **Standard-cell library characterization** (Liberty files)

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Unit | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wirelength | $L$ or $WL$ | **µm** (micrometers) | Most universal objective |
| Delay | $\tau$ or $T_{delay}$ | **ps** (picoseconds) | Timing closure |
| Power | $P$ | **µW** or **mW** | Battery/thermal limit |
| Area | $A$ | **µm²** | Die cost |
| Congestion | $\rho$ | **% overflow** | Routability |
| Capacitance | $C$ | **fF** (femtofarads) | RC delay & power |
| Voltage | $V_{dd}$ | **V** (Volts) | Power scaling |
| Frequency | $f$ | **GHz** | Throughput |

> [!NOTE]
> These seven metrics form the "KTU Big-Seven" — every optimization objective in VLSI CAD reduces to a weighted combination of one or more of these.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Optimization Objectives

The KTU 2024 syllabus organizes optimization objectives into **five primary categories**. Each category contains multiple sub-objectives, all of which are candidates for inclusion in the global cost function.

### 2.1.1 Wirelength-Based Objectives (Most Frequently Tested)

**(a) Total Wirelength (TW)** — Sum of all individual net lengths:
$$L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i$$

**(b) Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL)** — The most widely used approximation:
$$L_{HPWL} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( (x_{max,i} - x_{min,i}) + (y_{max,i} - y_{min,i}) \right)$$

**(c) Bounding Box Wirelength** — Equivalent to $2 \times HPWL$ for Manhattan routing.

**(d) Rectilinear Minimum Spanning Tree (RMST)** — A graph-based approximation:
$$L_{RMST} = \min_{T \in \text{Spanning Trees}} \sum_{(u,v) \in T} d_M(u,v)$$

where $d_M(u,v)$ is the Manhattan distance.

**(e) Rectilinear Steiner Tree (RST)** — The theoretical optimum, but **NP-hard**:
$$L_{RST} = \min_{S} \sum_{e \in \text{Steiner Tree}} d_M(e)$$

**(f) Star (Clique) Wirelength** — Center at centroid $(x_c, y_c)$:
$$L_{star} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( \vert x_i - x_c \vert + \vert y_i - y_c \vert \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** HPWL is the *de facto industry standard* because it is **additive** (sum of independent net contributions) and is also **convex**, which means it has a single global minimum — this is essential for gradient-based optimizers.

### 2.1.2 Timing-Based Objectives

**(a) Critical Path Delay** — Longest combinational path:
$$T_{crit} = \max_{p \in \text{Paths}} \sum_{g \in p} (d_{g,int} + d_{g,wire})$$

**(b) Worst Negative Slack (WNS)**:
$$WNS = \min_{i} (T_{required,i} - T_{arrival,i})$$

**(c) Total Negative Slack (TNS)**:
$$TNS = \sum_{i : slack_i < 0} slack_i$$

where $slack_i = T_{required,i} - T_{arrival,i}$.

### 2.1.3 Power-Based Objectives

**(a) Dynamic Power** (switching power):
$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{load} \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$

**(b) Static (Leakage) Power**:
$$P_{static} = V_{dd} \cdot I_{leak}$$

**(c) Total Power**:
$$P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{static} + P_{short}$$

### 2.1.4 Area-Based Objectives

**(a) Bounding Box Area**:
$$A_{BB} = W_{die} \times H_{die}$$

**(b) Cell Area**:
$$A_{cell} = \sum_{i} w_i \times h_i$$

**(c) Utilization Factor**:
$$\eta = \frac{A_{used}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.5 Congestion / Routability Objectives

**(a) Routing Congestion per Bin**:
$$\rho_b = \frac{U_b}{C_b}$$

where $U_b$ is used tracks and $C_b$ is the capacity in bin $b$.

**(b) Total Overflow**:
$$OF = \sum_{b} \max(0, U_b - C_b)$$

## 2.2 The Unified Cost Function (Weighted-Sum Form)

In practice, the EDA tool combines all objectives into a single scalar cost function:

$$\Phi(X) = \lambda_1 \cdot \frac{L(X)}{L_{ref}} + \lambda_2 \cdot \frac{T(X)}{T_{ref}} + \lambda_3 \cdot \frac{P(X)}{P_{ref}} + \lambda_4 \cdot \frac{A(X)}{A_{ref}} + \lambda_5 \cdot \frac{\rho(X)}{\rho_{ref}}$$

where the $\lambda_i$ are non-negative scalar **weights** chosen by the designer/algorithm, and the denominators are **normalization constants** to keep each term dimensionally consistent.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **exam-ready reference** for any numerical problem on optimization objectives. Memorize the conditions, not just the formulas.

| Objective Type | Formula | Key Property | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| HPWL | $L_{HPWL} = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min})$ | Additive, Convex, **O(n)** | Global placement |
| MST Wirelength | $L_{MST} = \sum_{edges} d_M(u,v)$ | Lower bound on RST, **O(n log n)** | Quick estimation |
| RST Wirelength | Optimal Steiner tree | **NP-hard**, exact | Final routing |
| Star Wirelength | $L_{star} = \sum \vert x_i - x_c \vert + \vert y_i - y_c \vert$ | Simple, optimistic | Lower bound check |
| Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$ | Quadratic in $V_{dd}$ | Low-power design |
| Static Power | $P_{static} = V_{dd} I_{leak}$ | Linear in $V_{dd}$ | Sleep-mode analysis |
| Critical Delay | $T_{crit} = \sum (d_{int} + d_{wire})$ | Path-based, not additive | Timing closure |
| Slack | $slack = T_{req} - T_{arr}$ | Negative ⇒ violation | Static timing analysis |
| Utilization | $\eta = A_{used} / A_{total}$ | Must be **< 100%** | Floorplanning |
| Congestion | $\rho = U / C$ | $\rho > 1$ ⇒ overflow | Global routing |

## 2.4 Why These Formulas? The Engineering Logic

- **HPWL is additive** because each net's bounding box is independent of other nets → allows decomposition across millions of nets.
- **Power scales quadratically with $V_{dd}$** because charging a capacitor $C$ to voltage $V$ requires energy $\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ per transition, and there are $2 \alpha f$ transitions per second.
- **RST is NP-hard** because the number of possible Steiner point locations grows combinatorially with net size.
- **Slack is a constraint**, not an objective — you *enforce* $slack \geq 0$ but only *minimize* violations when violated.
- **Utilization < 100%** is mandatory because we need free channels for global routing and to absorb white space for buffering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** In Synopsys ICC2 and Cadence Innovus, the placement engine's cost function is a **weighted sum of HPWL + congestion penalty + density penalty + timing penalty**. The weights are adaptively tuned by the tool's internal regression engine to converge on a multi-objective Pareto front.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: HPWL from Bounding Box Geometry

### Problem Statement
A net $N$ connects **four pins** at coordinates $P_1 = (2, 5)$, $P_2 = (8, 1)$, $P_3 = (5, 9)$, $P_4 = (6, 3)$. Compute the **HPWL** of this net.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the bounding box — find the extremal x and y coordinates.

$$x_{min} = \min(2, 8, 5, 6) = 2$$
$$x_{max} = \max(2, 8, 5, 6) = 8$$
$$y_{min} = \min(5, 1, 9, 3) = 1$$
$$y_{max} = \max(5, 1, 9, 3) = 9$$

**Step 2:** Compute the half-perimeter of the bounding box.

$$L_{HPWL} = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min})$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{HPWL} &= (8 - 2) + (9 - 1) \\
&= 6 + 8 \\
&= 14
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result. The HPWL of this net is **14 units** (e.g., 14 µm if coordinates are in µm). This means the minimum rectangular outline that encloses all four pins has a perimeter of 28 units, and HPWL gives half of that perimeter (14 units) as the estimated routing length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "half" perimeter?** In Manhattan routing, a multi-terminal net routed as a Steiner tree has total wirelength **less than or equal to the half-perimeter** of its bounding box. HPWL is thus an *upper bound* on Steiner tree wirelength for that net.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Star vs. MST vs. RST Wirelength Comparison

### Problem Statement
A 3-pin net has pin locations: $A = (0, 0)$, $B = (6, 0)$, $C = (6, 4)$. Compare the wirelength using **Star**, **MST**, and **HPWL** methods.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: HPWL Calculation**

$$x_{min} = 0, \quad x_{max} = 6, \quad y_{min} = 0, \quad y_{max} = 4$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{HPWL} &= (6 - 0) + (4 - 0) \\
&= 6 + 4 = 10
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Star (Centroid) Wirelength Calculation**

The centroid is at $(x_c, y_c) = \left( \frac{0+6+6}{3}, \frac{0+0+4}{3} \right) = (4, 1.33)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
d(A, C) &= \vert 0 - 4 \vert + \vert 0 - 1.33 \vert = 4 + 1.33 = 5.33 \\
d(B, C) &= \vert 6 - 4 \vert + \vert 0 - 1.33 \vert = 2 + 1.33 = 3.33 \\
d(C\_star, C) &= \vert 6 - 4 \vert + \vert 4 - 1.33 \vert = 2 + 2.67 = 4.67
\end{aligned}$$

$$L_{star} = 5.33 + 3.33 + 4.67 = 13.33$$

**Step 3: MST (Minimum Spanning Tree) Calculation**

Compute all pairwise Manhattan distances:
- $d_M(A, B) = \vert 0-6 \vert + \vert 0-0 \vert = 6$
- $d_M(A, C) = \vert 0-6 \vert + \vert 0-4 \vert = 10$
- $d_M(B, C) = \vert 6-6 \vert + \vert 0-4 \vert = 4$

Sort edges: $B-C$ (4), $A-B$ (6), $A-C$ (10).
Kruskal's algorithm selects $B-C$ (4) first, then $A-B$ (6). Both form a spanning tree, and we stop.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{MST} &= d_M(B, C) + d_M(A, B) = 4 + 6 = 10
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: RST (Rectilinear Steiner Tree) Calculation**

For a 3-pin net in this geometry, the optimal Steiner tree equals the MST (no Steiner point needed because the three points form a right-angled configuration, and any Steiner point would lie on the existing path).

$$L_{RST} = L_{MST} = 10$$

**Step 5: Comparative Analysis**

| Method | Wirelength | % Overhead vs. RST |
|---|---|---|
| HPWL | 10 | **0%** (matches optimal) |
| MST | 10 | **0%** (matches optimal) |
| RST (optimal) | 10 | **0%** (baseline) |
| Star | 13.33 | **+33.3%** (overestimate) |

**Step 6: General Inequality (Important Result)**

For any multi-terminal net, the following inequality chain always holds:

$$L_{RST} \leq L_{MST} \leq L_{Star} \quad \text{and} \quad L_{HPWL} \geq L_{RST}$$

This is a classic KTU exam question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** HPWL is an *upper bound* on Steiner wirelength; MST and Star are *approximations* that over-estimate or equal the true optimal. The ordering $L_{RST} \leq L_{MST} \leq L_{HPWL}$ (in most cases) is the key takeaway.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Budget Calculation

### Problem Statement
A circuit has a switching activity $\alpha = 0.15$, total load capacitance $C = 250\text{ fF}$, supply voltage $V_{dd} = 1.2\text{ V}$, and clock frequency $f = 2.5\text{ GHz}$. Compute the dynamic power.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify given values.

$$\alpha = 0.15, \quad C = 250 \times 10^{-15}\text{ F}, \quad V_{dd} = 1.2\text{ V}, \quad f = 2.5 \times 10^{9}\text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the power equation $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{dyn} &= 0.15 \times (250 \times 10^{-15}) \times (1.2)^2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{9}) \\
&= 0.15 \times 250 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 \times 2.5 \times 10^{9} \\
&= 0.15 \times 250 \times 1.44 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-15+9} \\
&= 0.15 \times 250 \times 1.44 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 0.15 \times 900 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 135 \times 10^{-6}\text{ W} \\
&= 135\ \mu W
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation. The circuit consumes **135 µW** of dynamic power. Reducing $V_{dd}$ to 1.0 V would drop power to:

$$P_{dyn}^{(new)} = 135 \times \left( \frac{1.0}{1.2} \right)^{2} = 135 \times 0.694 = 93.75\ \mu W$$

> This demonstrates the **quadratic payoff of voltage scaling** in low-power VLSI design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Objective

| Design Stage | Primary Objective | Secondary Objective | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| System-level / Pre-layout | Power, Throughput | Area | Architecture decisions dominate |
| Floorplanning | Area, Aspect Ratio | Wirelength, Thermal | Pre-allocates resources |
| Global Placement | HPWL (wirelength) | Congestion, Density | Addable, convex, fast gradient |
| Detailed Placement | Timing (slack) | Wirelength | Local refinement |
| Global Routing | Congestion overflow | Wirelength | Avoid routing failures |
| Detailed Routing | DRV (Design Rule Violations), Crosstalk | Length, Via count | Manufacturability focus |
| CTS (Clock Tree) | Skew, Latency | Wirelength, Power | Clock distribution is critical |
| Optimization/Sizing | Timing, Power, Area | Hold, Noise | Transistor-level tuning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **directly aligned with the KTU 2024 PEEVT524 Module-4 syllabus mapping** and is a common exam question ("Compare the optimization objectives at different design stages").

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram: The VLSI Optimization Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Netlist + Constraints] --> B[Cost Function Aggregator]
    B --> C1[Wirelength Module]
    B --> C2[Timing Module]
    B --> C3[Power Module]
    B --> C4[Area Module]
    B --> C5[Congestion Module]
    C1 --> D1[HPWL Estimator]
    C1 --> D2[RST Builder]
    C1 --> D3[MST Builder]
    C2 --> D4[Static Timing Engine]
    C2 --> D5[Slack Calculator]
    C3 --> D6[Dynamic Power Calc]
    C3 --> D7[Static Power Calc]
    C4 --> D8[Utilization Tracker]
    C5 --> D9[Routing Density Map]
    D1 --> E[Weighted Sum Phi X]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
    D8 --> E
    D9 --> E
    E --> F[Optimization Kernel SA / GA / Gradient]
    F --> G{Convergence Check}
    G -->|Not Converged| H[Update Layout X]
    H --> B
    G -->|Converged| I[Final Layout Output]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Flow: Wirelength Estimation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Multi-pin Net Coordinates] --> B{Bounding Box Method}
    A --> C{Steiner Tree Method}
    A --> D{Spanning Tree Method}
    A --> E{Star Method}
    B --> B1[Compute xmin xmax ymin ymax]
    B1 --> B2[HPWL equals xmax minus xmin plus ymax minus ymin]
    C --> C1[Insert Steiner Points NP-hard]
    C1 --> C2[RST Length Optimal but Slow]
    D --> D1[Kruskal or Prim Algorithm]
    D1 --> D2[MST Length O of n log n]
    E --> E1[Compute Centroid]
    E1 --> E2[Sum Manhattan to Centroid]
    B2 --> F[Cost Function Phi]
    C2 --> F
    D2 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Optimizer Decision]
```

## 4.3 Multi-Objective Pareto Front Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Pareto_Front["Multi-Objective Trade-off Space"]
        P1[Solution A: Min Wirelength, Max Power] --> P2[Solution B: Balanced]
        P2 --> P3[Solution C: Min Power, Max Wirelength]
        P3 --> P4[Solution D: Min Area, High Wirelength]
        P4 --> P1
    end
    P2 --> Decision[Designer selects based on application priority]
    Decision --> Output[Final Optimized Layout]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Iterative Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Initial Random Placement] --> Eval[Compute Phi X using objectives]
    Eval --> Accept{Neighbor is better}
    Accept -->|Yes| Move[Accept Move update X]
    Accept -->|No| Prob[Compute acceptance probability]
    Prob --> Stochastic{Random less than Prob}
    Stochastic -->|Yes| Move
    Stochastic -->|No| Stay[Keep current X]
    Move --> Loop{Iteration less than Max}
    Stay --> Loop
    Loop -->|Yes| Eval
    Loop -->|No| Finish[Output best X found]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> Define **Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL)**. Why is it preferred over the Rectilinear Steiner Tree (RST) wirelength for global placement in modern EDA tools?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

HPWL is defined as the sum of the horizontal span and vertical span of the bounding box of a net. For a net with pins at coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$, the HPWL is:

$$L_{HPWL} = (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min})$$

HPWL is preferred over RST in global placement for three reasons:

1. **Computational Simplicity:** HPWL is computed in $O(n)$ time per net, while RST is NP-hard and requires exponential-time solvers.
2. **Additivity:** HPWL is a sum of independent net contributions, which allows the global cost to be decomposed and gradient-optimized.
3. **Convexity:** HPWL is a convex function in cell positions, guaranteeing a unique global minimum and making gradient-based optimizers convergent.

**(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three reasons.)**

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> List and briefly explain **any three optimization objectives** used during the global placement stage of VLSI design.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Total Wirelength (HPWL-based):** The most fundamental objective — minimizes the total estimated routing length, which directly correlates with timing, congestion, and dynamic power.

2. **Cell Density Penalty:** Penalizes placements where the local cell density exceeds a target utilization, preventing placement congestion that would later cause routing overflow.

3. **Timing-Driven Objective:** Uses a weighted HPWL where critical nets (those on the worst-slack path) are multiplied by a factor $> 1$, forcing the placer to reduce their length first.

**(1 mark per objective with explanation.)**

---

## Part B: Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — CHOSEN: Wirelength Estimation Comparison

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
> A 4-pin net has the following pin coordinates: $P_1 = (0, 0)$, $P_2 = (10, 0)$, $P_3 = (10, 8)$, $P_4 = (0, 8)$.
>
> **(a)** Compute the **HPWL** of this net. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Compute the **Rectilinear Minimum Spanning Tree (RMST)** length of the same net using Kruskal's algorithm. Show all pairwise Manhattan distances. Compare your result with the HPWL value. (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — HPWL Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the bounding box extrema.

$$x_{min} = \min(0, 10, 10, 0) = 0$$
$$x_{max} = \max(0, 10, 10, 0) = 10$$
$$y_{min} = \min(0, 0, 8, 8) = 0$$
$$y_{max} = \max(0, 0, 8, 8) = 8$$

**Step 2:** Apply the HPWL formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{HPWL} &= (x_{max} - x_{min}) + (y_{max} - y_{min}) \\
&= (10 - 0) + (8 - 0) \\
&= 10 + 8 \\
&= 18\ \text{units}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation. The HPWL is **18 units**, meaning half the perimeter of the bounding rectangle (which is a 10×8 square with full perimeter 36).

**(Distribution: 2 marks for extrema, 3 marks for formula and arithmetic, 2 marks for interpretation.)**

---

#### Part (b) — RMST Calculation & Comparison (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** List all six pairwise Manhattan distances.

| Edge | Calculation | Distance |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1 \leftrightarrow P_2$ | $\vert 0-10 \vert + \vert 0-0 \vert$ | 10 |
| $P_1 \leftrightarrow P_3$ | $\vert 0-10 \vert + \vert 0-8 \vert$ | 18 |
| $P_1 \leftrightarrow P_4$ | $\vert 0-0 \vert + \vert 0-8 \vert$ | 8 |
| $P_2 \leftrightarrow P_3$ | $\vert 10-10 \vert + \vert 0-8 \vert$ | 8 |
| $P_2 \leftrightarrow P_4$ | $\vert 10-0 \vert + \vert 0-8 \vert$ | 18 |
| $P_3 \leftrightarrow P_4$ | $\vert 10-0 \vert + \vert 8-8 \vert$ | 10 |

**Step 2:** Sort edges in ascending order.

| Rank | Edge | Length |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_1 \leftrightarrow P_4$ | 8 |
| 2 | $P_2 \leftrightarrow P_3$ | 8 |
| 3 | $P_1 \leftrightarrow P_2$ | 10 |
| 4 | $P_3 \leftrightarrow P_4$ | 10 |
| 5 | $P_1 \leftrightarrow P_3$ | 18 |
| 6 | $P_2 \leftrightarrow P_4$ | 18 |

**Step 3:** Apply Kruskal's algorithm.

- **Pick $P_1$-$P_4$ (length 8):** No cycle, accept. Connected: $\{P_1, P_4\}$.
- **Pick $P_2$-$P_3$ (length 8):** No cycle, accept. Connected: $\{P_1, P_4\}$, $\{P_2, P_3\}$.
- **Pick $P_1$-$P_2$ (length 10):** No cycle (merges the two components), accept. Connected: $\{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\}$. Tree is complete — stop.

**Step 4:** Sum the selected edges.

$$L_{RMST} = 8 + 8 + 10 = 26\ \text{units}$$

**Step 5:** Comparison with HPWL.

$$L_{HPWL} = 18, \quad L_{RMST} = 26, \quad L_{RST} \leq 18 \text{ (with Steiner point at } (10, 8) \text{ corner)}$$

Wait — the **optimal Steiner tree** for this square net is actually the perimeter of the square, which equals $4 \times 10 = 40$ if routed around, OR a tree through one corner with total $10 + 8 + 10 = 28$. The MST picks $P_1$-$P_4$ + $P_2$-$P_3$ + $P_1$-$P_2$ = 26, which is less than the Steiner alternative.

In this case, $L_{HPWL} < L_{RMST}$, confirming the general inequality.

**(Distribution: 2 marks for distance table, 2 marks for Kruskal's selection, 1 mark for sum, 2 marks for comparison and inequality.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE: Power & Timing Optimization

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
> A VLSI module operates at $V_{dd} = 1.2\text{ V}$ with a clock of $f = 1\text{ GHz}$. The total switching capacitance is $C = 1\text{ pF}$ and the average switching activity is $\alpha = 0.2$.
>
> **(a)** Compute the **dynamic power consumption**. If the supply voltage is scaled to $0.9\text{ V}$ (assuming constant frequency), what is the percentage reduction in dynamic power? (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Define **slack** in static timing analysis. A signal must arrive at a flip-flop input by $T_{required} = 500\text{ ps}$. It currently arrives at $T_{arrival} = 580\text{ ps}$. Is there a timing violation? What is the type of violation (setup or hold)? If the gate delay can be reduced by $30\ \text{ps}$ via buffer insertion, will the violation be resolved? (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Dynamic Power Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Substitute into the power equation.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{dyn} &= \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f \\
&= 0.2 \times (1 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1.2)^{2} \times (1 \times 10^{9}) \\
&= 0.2 \times 1 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.44 \times 10^{9} \\
&= 0.288 \times 10^{-3}\text{ W} \\
&= 288\ \mu W
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Recompute power at scaled $V_{dd} = 0.9\text{ V}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{dyn}^{(new)} &= 0.2 \times 1 \times 10^{-12} \times (0.9)^{2} \times 1 \times 10^{9} \\
&= 0.2 \times 0.81 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 162\ \mu W
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Percentage reduction.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction \%} &= \frac{P_{dyn} - P_{dyn}^{(new)}}{P_{dyn}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{288 - 162}{288} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{126}{288} \times 100 \\
&= 43.75\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Verify using the squared ratio.

$$\text{Reduction \%} = 1 - \left( \frac{0.9}{1.2} \right)^{2} = 1 - (0.75)^{2} = 1 - 0.5625 = 0.4375 = 43.75\%$$

**(Distribution: 3 marks for original power, 2 marks for new power, 2 marks for percentage reduction with justification.)**

---

#### Part (b) — Slack Analysis (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Define slack.

Slack is defined as the difference between the required arrival time and the actual arrival time of a signal at a timing endpoint:

$$slack = T_{required} - T_{arrival}$$

A **negative slack** indicates a **timing violation**.

**Step 2:** Compute the slack.

$$slack = 500 - 580 = -80\ \text{ps}$$

Since $slack < 0$, there is a **timing violation**. The signal arrives **80 ps too late** at the flip-flop input, which means the next clock edge will capture stale data. This is a **setup time violation** (also called a max-delay violation or long-path violation), because the data path is slower than the clock period permits.

> A **hold violation** would occur if the signal arrived *too early* — but here it is late, confirming it is a **setup violation**.

**Step 3:** After buffer insertion, recompute the slack.

$$T_{arrival}^{(new)} = 580 - 30 = 550\ \text{ps}$$
$$slack^{(new)} = 500 - 550 = -50\ \text{ps}$$

The slack is still **negative (-50 ps)**. The violation is **not resolved**. We would need a further delay reduction of at least 50 ps — equivalent to inserting two more buffers of 30 ps each, or a single buffer of 50 ps (assumed available).

**Step 4:** Minimum delay reduction required to fix the violation.

$$\Delta T_{min} = T_{arrival} - T_{required} = 580 - 500 = 80\ \text{ps}$$

So the delay must be reduced by at least **80 ps**, not just 30 ps.

**(Distribution: 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for slack computation and violation type, 3 marks for buffer analysis and conclusion.)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to label units.** A wirelength of "10" without "µm" or "units" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing HPWL with full bounding-box perimeter.** HPWL is *half* the perimeter — full perimeter is **2× HPWL**. Many students report 20 instead of 10 for the example above.
> 3. **Not sorting edges in Kruskal's algorithm.** Order matters; the algorithm is greedy and you must explicitly list sorted edges.
> 4. **Slack sign confusion:** Negative slack = violation. Positive slack = OK. Memorize this.
> 5. **Setup vs. Hold:** Late arrival → **setup violation**. Early arrival → **hold violation**. Students often swap these.
> 6. **Missing normalization in the cost function.** The $\lambda$ weights in $\Phi(X)$ are dimensionless only if each term is normalized by a reference value. Forgetting the $L_{ref}$ term is a common deduction.
> 7. **Power formula units:** If capacitance is in fF, you must convert to F (multiply by $10^{-15}$) before computing.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Day of Exam:**

- **HPWL** = (xmax − xmin) + (ymax − ymin) → *additive, convex, O(n) per net*. Default global placement objective.
- **RMST** ≤ **HPWL** generally, but RMST ≥ **RST** (Steiner is always optimal). The chain is: **RST ≤ MST ≤ HPWL** (typical case).
- **Star wirelength** is always an *overestimate* relative to the centroid being outside the bounding box. It is a useful **lower bound check**, not a real metric.
- **Dynamic power** scales **quadratically with $V_{dd}$**: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{dd}^{2} f$.
- **Static power** scales **linearly with $V_{dd}$**: $P_{static} = V_{dd} I_{leak}$.
- **Slack** = $T_{required} - T_{arrival}$. Negative slack ⇒ violation.
  - *Setup violation* = data arrives too late (max-delay).
  - *Hold violation* = data arrives too early (min-delay).
- **Critical path** = the longest combinational path; $T_{crit} = \max_{\text{paths}} \sum \text{gate delays}$.
- **Cost function** is a **weighted sum**: $\Phi = \sum \lambda_i \cdot \text{objective}_i / \text{reference}_i$.
- **Utilization** $\eta$ must be **less than 1.0** (typically 0.7–0.85) to leave room for routing.
- **Congestion** $\rho = U/C > 1$ ⇒ overflow, which causes routing failure.
- **HPWL is preferred** in placement because it is **additive** (decomposable), **convex** (single minimum), and **differentiable** (gradient-friendly).
- **RST is NP-hard** — use approximations like FLUTE, PDII, or Prim-based heuristics.
- The **Pareto trade-off frontier** governs multi-objective design: improving one objective worsens another, and the designer picks a knee-point solution.
- **Memorize the seven core metrics**: WL, Delay, Power, Area, Congestion, Capacitance, Slack.

> **Final Tip:** When asked "Why HPWL?" in an exam, always answer with the *three keywords*: **additive, convex, differentiable**. When asked "Why RST is hard?" answer **NP-hard / Steiner-point search combinatorial**. This is the KTU 2024 evaluation key pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slicing Floorplan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slicing Floorplan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slicing Floorplan (KTU 2024 Definition):**
