> A slicing floorplan is a rectangular dissection of a chip into a set of $n$ rectangular modules (blocks), such that the dissection can be obtained by recursively bisecting the chip rectangle using **horizontal** (H-cut) and **vertical** (V-cut) lines that traverse the entire rectangle from one side to the other. Every internal cut must propagate completely from one edge of the parent rectangle to the opposite edge.

Formally, a floorplan is **slicing** if and only if there exists a slicing tree (a rooted, full binary tree with $n$ leaves and $n-1$ internal nodes) whose internal nodes are labeled with operators $H$ or $V$, and whose leaves correspond one-to-one with the $n$ rectangular blocks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Cutting a Layered Cake

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine a rectangular layered cake that you want to serve to guests:
> 1. You first make a straight vertical cut (V-cut) that splits the entire cake into a **left half** and a **right half**.
> 2. You then take the left half and make a horizontal cut (H-cut) across it, splitting it into a **top piece** and a **bottom piece**.
> 3. You keep cutting each piece further, but **every knife stroke must travel fully from one edge to the opposite edge** of the piece being cut.
>
> The sequence of cuts can be drawn as an upside-down tree — this is your **slicing tree**, and the final arrangement of cake pieces on the tray is your **slicing floorplan**.

This is in contrast to a **non-slicing floorplan** (e.g., a "guillotine-defying" brick-wall pattern), where a single straight line cannot cleanly partition all modules.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

| Term | Standard Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Module / Block | $M_i$ | A rectangular functional unit with width $w_i$ and height $h_i$ |
| Horizontal cut | $H$ | A straight line cutting left-to-right, separating top from bottom |
| Vertical cut | $V$ | A straight line cutting top-to-bottom, separating left from right |
| Slicing Tree | $T$ | A full binary tree whose leaves are blocks and internal nodes are $H/V$ operators |
| Polish Expression | $P$ | A postfix (Reverse Polish) representation of the slicing tree |
| Skewed tree | — | A slicing tree where all internal nodes have the same operator ($H$ or $V$) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties of a Slicing Floorplan

A slicing floorplan must satisfy the following structural constraints:

- **Recursive Bisectability:** The chip rectangle can be split into exactly two sub-rectangles by either a horizontal or vertical line, and this property must hold recursively for every sub-rectangle.
- **Tree Structure:** The hierarchy of cuts is captured by a **rooted full binary tree** with $n$ leaves and $n-1$ internal nodes.
- **Polish Expression:** The postfix traversal of the slicing tree yields a valid Polish expression — a sequence of $n$ operands and $n-1$ binary operators with no two consecutive operands.
- **No Overlap:** All blocks are non-overlapping and cover the bounding rectangle completely (no dead-space holes in the slicing decomposition).

### 2.2 Slicing Tree Representation

> [!NOTE]
> **Construction Rule:**
> Each **leaf** of the slicing tree represents one module. Each **internal node** represents a cutting line — labeled $H$ (horizontal) or $V$ (vertical). The order of children in a $V$-node is conventionally **left-then-right**, and in an $H$-node is **top-then-bottom**.

For an $n$-block floorplan, the slicing tree has exactly:
- $n$ leaves (one per block)
- $n-1$ internal nodes (one per cut)
- Total nodes: $2n-1$

### 2.3 Polish Expression — Postfix Encoding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Polish expression** is a string over the alphabet $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\} \cup \{H, V\}$ where:
> - Exactly $n$ block labels appear.
> - Exactly $n-1$ operators ($H$ or $V$) appear.
> - When scanning left-to-right, the number of operators never exceeds the number of operands (ballot/stack property).
> - No two operands are adjacent operators without a separating operator.

**Example:** The expression `1 2 H 3 4 H V 5 H V` represents a 5-block slicing tree.

### 2.4 Count of Valid Slicing Floorplans

The number of distinct slicing floorplans for $n$ blocks is the product of:
1. Number of full binary trees with $n$ leaves (the **Catalan number** $C_{n-1}$)
2. Number of operator choices ($H$ or $V$) for each of the $n-1$ internal nodes ($2^{n-1}$)

$$N_{\text{slicing}}(n) = C_{n-1} \cdot 2^{n-1} = \frac{(2n-2)!}{(n-1)! \cdot n!} \cdot 2^{n-1}$$

**Reference values:** $N(2)=2$, $N(3)=8$, $N(4)=40$, $N(5)=224$.

### 2.5 Sizing a Slicing Floorplan (Bottom-Up Area Computation)

Given the dimensions $(w_i, h_i)$ of every block, the dimensions of the bounding rectangle at each internal node are computed bottom-up as follows:

> [!NOTE]
> **Sizing Rule (Recursive):**
> - For a leaf node $i$: $\text{Width}(i) = w_i$, $\text{Height}(i) = h_i$.
> - For an **H-operator** with left child $L$ and right child $R$:
> $$\text{Width}(H) = \max(W_L, W_R), \quad \text{Height}(H) = H_L + H_R$$
> - For a **V-operator** with left child $L$ and right child $R$:
> $$\text{Width}(V) = W_L + W_R, \quad \text{Height}(V) = \max(H_L, H_R)$$

### 2.6 Stockmeyer's Minimum-Area Algorithm

For a fixed slicing tree, the **minimum-area sizing** uses a Pareto-optimal set of $(w, h)$ pairs at each node to allow flexible combinations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A pair $(w_1, h_1)$ **dominates** $(w_2, h_2)$ if $w_1 \leq w_2$ AND $h_1 \leq h_2$. A non-dominated pair is called **Pareto-optimal**.

**Bottom-up update rules for the Pareto set $\mathcal{S}$:**
- **H-node:** $\mathcal{S}_H = \{( \max(w_L, w_R),\, h_L + h_R ) \mid (w_L, h_L) \in \mathcal{S}_L,\, (w_R, h_R) \in \mathcal{S}_R\}$, then prune dominated pairs.
- **V-node:** $\mathcal{S}_V = \{( w_L + w_R,\, \max(h_L, h_R) ) \mid (w_L, h_L) \in \mathcal{S}_L,\, (w_R, h_R) \in \mathcal{S}_R\}$, then prune dominated pairs.
- The **minimum area** of the root is $\min\{ w \cdot h \mid (w, h) \in \mathcal{S}_{\text{root}} \}$.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Variables | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{\text{tree}}(n) = \dfrac{(2n-2)!}{(n-1)!\,n!}$ | $n$ = number of blocks | Counting slicing trees |
| 2 | $N_{\text{floorplan}}(n) = C_{n-1} \cdot 2^{n-1}$ | $n$ = blocks | Counting slicing floorplans |
| 3 | $W(H) = \max(W_L, W_R);\; H(H) = H_L + H_R$ | $L,R$ = children | Sizing H-node |
| 4 | $W(V) = W_L + W_R;\; H(V) = \max(H_L, H_R)$ | $L,R$ = children | Sizing V-node |
| 5 | $\text{Area} = W \cdot H$ | bounding rect | Total chip area |
| 6 | $\text{Dominance: } (w_1,h_1) \succ (w_2,h_2)$ iff $w_1 \leq w_2 \land h_1 \leq h_2$ | Pareto-prune | Min-area sizing |
| 7 | $A_{\min} = \min\{w \cdot h \mid (w,h) \in \mathcal{S}_{\text{root}}\}$ | root Pareto set | Final min area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never use the vertical bar `|` symbol inside markdown tables.** For absolute value use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX form.

### 2.8 Real-World VLSI Engineering Utility

Slicing floorplans are foundational in early physical synthesis because:
- They are **algorithmically tractable** — global sizing and optimization can be done in polynomial time.
- They map naturally to **standard-cell row-based layouts** where rows are sliced horizontally and channel routing is sliced vertically.
- They form the basis of early **rectangular dualization** methods used in hierarchical design of memory arrays, datapaths, and analog blocks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sizing a Fixed Slicing Tree

**Problem Statement:**
Given 4 blocks with the following dimensions, compute the bounding rectangle of the slicing tree with Polish expression `1 2 H 3 4 H V`.

| Block | Width | Height |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 3 |
| 2 | 3 | 2 |
| 3 | 2 | 4 |
| 4 | 3 | 3 |

**Step 1 — Decode the Polish expression `1 2 H 3 4 H V`:**

Reading postfix (left-to-right, push operands, pop & apply on operator):
- `1` → push 1
- `2` → push 2
- `H` → pop 2, 1; build node $N_1 = H(1, 2)$; push $N_1$
- `3` → push 3
- `4` → push 4
- `H` → pop 4, 3; build node $N_2 = H(3, 4)$; push $N_2$
- `V` → pop $N_2$, $N_1$; build root = $V(N_1, N_2)$

**Step 2 — Bottom-up sizing for leaf node 1:**

$$\text{Width}(1) = 2, \quad \text{Height}(1) = 3$$

**Step 3 — Bottom-up sizing for leaf node 2:**

$$\text{Width}(2) = 3, \quad \text{Height}(2) = 2$$

**Step 4 — Size internal H-node $N_1 = H(1, 2)$:**

$$W(N_1) = \max(W_1, W_2) = \max(2, 3) = 3$$

$$H(N_1) = H_1 + H_2 = 3 + 2 = 5$$

Therefore, sub-floorplan $N_1$ occupies a rectangle of size $3 \times 5$.

**Step 5 — Size leaf node 3:**

$$\text{Width}(3) = 2, \quad \text{Height}(3) = 4$$

**Step 6 — Size leaf node 4:**

$$\text{Width}(4) = 3, \quad \text{Height}(4) = 3$$

**Step 7 — Size internal H-node $N_2 = H(3, 4)$:**

$$W(N_2) = \max(W_3, W_4) = \max(2, 3) = 3$$

$$H(N_2) = H_3 + H_4 = 4 + 3 = 7$$

Therefore, sub-floorplan $N_2$ occupies a rectangle of size $3 \times 7$.

**Step 8 — Size the root V-node:**

$$W(\text{root}) = W(N_1) + W(N_2) = 3 + 3 = 6$$

$$H(\text{root}) = \max(H(N_1), H(N_2)) = \max(5, 7) = 7$$

**Step 9 — Final bounding rectangle and area:**

$$W_{\text{chip}} = 6, \quad H_{\text{chip}} = 7, \quad A_{\text{chip}} = 6 \times 7 = 42 \text{ sq. units}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Stockmeyer Minimum-Area Sizing

**Problem Statement:**
Three blocks — $A(2,3), B(3,2), C(1,4)$ — must be combined using slicing tree `A B H C V`. Compute the **Pareto-optimal set** at every node and find the **minimum bounding-rectangle area**.

**Step 1 — Initialize leaf Pareto sets:**

$$\mathcal{S}_A = \{(2, 3)\}, \quad \mathcal{S}_B = \{(3, 2)\}, \quad \mathcal{S}_C = \{(1, 4)\}$$

**Step 2 — H-node combining $A$ and $B$:**

Single combination: $(\max(2,3),\, 3+2) = (3, 5)$.

$$\mathcal{S}_{AB} = \{(3, 5)\}$$

**Step 3 — V-node combining $AB$ and $C$:**

Single combination: $(3+1,\, \max(5, 4)) = (4, 5)$.

$$\mathcal{S}_{\text{root}} = \{(4, 5)\}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum area:**

$$A_{\min} = 4 \times 5 = 20 \text{ sq. units}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The chip is a $4 \times 5$ rectangle.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Block with Rotation (Pareto Set Grows)

**Problem Statement:**
Block $X$ can be placed in **original orientation** $(2, 5)$ or **rotated** $(5, 2)$. Block $Y$ can be $(3, 4)$ or $(4, 3)$. Combine them with H-cut. Find all Pareto-optimal $(W, H)$ pairs.

**Step 1 — Build leaf Pareto sets including rotations:**

$$\mathcal{S}_X = \{(2, 5),\, (5, 2)\}, \quad \mathcal{S}_Y = \{(3, 4),\, (4, 3)\}$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate all four H-cut combinations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Combo 1:} \quad & (2, 5) \oplus_H (3, 4) = (\max(2,3),\, 5+4) = (3, 9) \\
\text{Combo 2:} \quad & (2, 5) \oplus_H (4, 3) = (\max(2,4),\, 5+3) = (4, 8) \\
\text{Combo 3:} \quad & (5, 2) \oplus_H (3, 4) = (\max(5,3),\, 2+4) = (5, 6) \\
\text{Combo 4:} \quad & (5, 2) \oplus_H (4, 3) = (\max(5,4),\, 2+3) = (5, 5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Prune dominated pairs (keep only Pareto-optimal):**

Raw set: $\{(3,9),\, (4,8),\, (5,6),\, (5,5)\}$.

- $(3, 9)$ vs $(4, 8)$: neither dominates the other (3 < 4 but 9 > 8). Keep both.
- $(4, 8)$ vs $(5, 6)$: neither dominates. Keep.
- $(4, 8)$ vs $(5, 5)$: 4 < 5 but 8 > 5. Keep.
- $(5, 6)$ vs $(5, 5)$: same width, 5 < 6 ⇒ $(5, 5)$ dominates $(5, 6)$. **Drop $(5, 6)$.**
- $(3, 9)$ vs $(4, 8)$: 3 < 4 and 9 > 8. Neither dominates. **Keep both.**
- $(3, 9)$ vs $(5, 5)$: 3 < 5 and 9 > 5. Keep both.

**Step 4 — Final Pareto set:**

$$\mathcal{S}_{\text{H-root}} = \{(3, 9),\, (4, 8),\, (5, 5)\}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum area:**

$$\min(3 \cdot 9,\ 4 \cdot 8,\ 5 \cdot 5) = \min(27, 32, 25) = 25 \text{ sq. units}$$

The optimal solution is the $5 \times 5$ square obtained by rotating block $X$ and choosing the rotated orientation of $Y$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use H vs V Cut

| Design Goal | Preferred Cut Operator | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Stacking memory banks with equal width | $H$ (horizontal) | Allows widths to align via $\max$, stack heights add up |
| Placing control logic beside datapath | $V$ (vertical) | Widths add, heights align via $\max$ |
| Wire-intensive horizontal buses | $H$ | Long horizontal channels fit naturally |
| Wire-intensive vertical buses | $V$ | Long vertical channels fit naturally |
| Minimizing total area with square blocks | Mixed $H$ and $V$ | Need both $\max$/sum rules for trade-off |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Slicing Tree for `1 2 H 3 4 H V` (Worked Example 1)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootNode["V root"]:::vnode
    nodeH1["H node N1"]:::hnode
    nodeH2["H node N2"]:::hnode
    leaf1["Block 1 w=2 h=3"]:::leaf
    leaf2["Block 2 w=3 h=2"]:::leaf
    leaf3["Block 3 w=2 h=4"]:::leaf
    leaf4["Block 4 w=3 h=3"]:::leaf

    rootNode --> nodeH1
    rootNode --> nodeH2
    nodeH1 --> leaf1
    nodeH1 --> leaf2
    nodeH2 --> leaf3
    nodeH2 --> leaf4

    classDef vnode fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#FF6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef hnode fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#0033CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef leaf fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#339966,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bottom-Up Sizing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stage1["Stage 1: Leaf Initialization"]:::stage --> stage2["Stage 2: Combine via H or V Operator"]:::stage
    stage2 --> stage3["Stage 3: Compute candidate W and H pairs"]:::stage
    stage3 --> stage4["Stage 4: Pareto-Prune dominated pairs"]:::stage
    stage4 --> stage5{"Stage 5: Root reached?"}:::decision
    stage5 -- "No" --> stage2
    stage5 -- "Yes" --> stage6["Stage 6: Select min w*h from Pareto set"]:::stage
    stage6 --> stage7["Stage 7: Output min area and dims"]:::output

    classDef stage fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#6A329F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef decision fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef output fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

### 4.3 Skewed vs Balanced Slicing Tree (Illustration)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Balanced_Tree
        b_root["V root"]:::vnode
        b_left["H node"]:::hnode
        b_right["H node"]:::hnode
        b_l1["Block 1"]:::leaf
        b_l2["Block 2"]:::leaf
        b_r1["Block 3"]:::leaf
        b_r2["Block 4"]:::leaf
        b_root --> b_left
        b_root --> b_right
        b_left --> b_l1
        b_left --> b_l2
        b_right --> b_r1
        b_right --> b_r2
    end

    subgraph Skewed_Tree
        s_root["V root"]:::vnode
        s_mid["V node"]:::vnode
        s_mid2["V node"]:::vnode
        s_l1["Block 1"]:::leaf
        s_l2["Block 2"]:::leaf
        s_l3["Block 3"]:::leaf
        s_l4["Block 4"]:::leaf
        s_root --> s_mid
        s_root --> s_l4
        s_mid --> s_mid2
        s_mid --> s_l3
        s_mid2 --> s_l1
        s_mid2 --> s_l2
    end

    classDef vnode fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#FF6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef hnode fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#0033CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef leaf fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#339966,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

### 4.4 Geometric Layout of the Slicing Floorplan (Worked Example 1)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Chip["Chip Boundary 6 x 7"]
        direction TB
        subgraph LeftCol["Left Half 3 x 7 N1=H node"]
            direction TB
            subgraph TopPiece["Top 3 x 3 Block 1"]
                TopPiece
            end
            subgraph BotPiece["Bottom 3 x 2 Block 2"]
                BotPiece
            end
        end
        subgraph RightCol["Right Half 3 x 7 N2=H node"]
            direction TB
            subgraph TopPiece2["Top 3 x 4 Block 3"]
                TopPiece2
            end
            subgraph BotPiece2["Bottom 3 x 3 Block 4"]
                BotPiece2
            end
        end
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid geometry above is a logical block-layout map. The actual 2-D placement is described by the V-cut at the root: left sub-floorplan (N1: Block 1 on top of Block 2) and right sub-floorplan (N2: Block 3 on top of Block 4).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Define a slicing floorplan. State two key conditions that distinguish it from a non-slicing floorplan.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A slicing floorplan is a rectangular dissection of a chip into $n$ blocks such that the chip can be recursively bisected using horizontal (H) and vertical (V) cut lines that traverse the parent rectangle completely. **(1 mark)**
**Distinguishing conditions:** **(2 marks)**
1. Every internal cut must propagate from one edge of the parent rectangle to the opposite edge (guillotine-cut property).
2. The hierarchical structure of cuts can be represented as a **slicing tree** — a full binary tree with $n$ leaves and $n-1$ internal nodes labeled H or V.

A non-slicing floorplan, by contrast, contains a "wheel" or "cross" configuration in which no single straight line can cleanly separate the blocks into two non-empty rectangular sub-groups.

---

**Q.A2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`
**What is a Polish expression in the context of slicing floorplans? Write a valid Polish expression for a 3-block slicing tree.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Polish expression is a **postfix (Reverse Polish) representation** of a slicing tree, where each leaf block is denoted by an integer label and each internal cut by an operator $H$ or $V$. **(2 marks)**
**Validity rule:** During left-to-right scan, the count of operators must never exceed the count of operands (stack-balance property), and no two operators are adjacent.

**Example for 3 blocks** (where block 1 and 2 are combined by H, then combined with block 3 by V):

`1 2 H 3 V` **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, End Semester]`

**Q.B1.A.** **(a)** Explain the construction of a slicing tree from a Polish expression with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe Stockmeyer's algorithm for minimum-area sizing of a slicing floorplan. Apply it to compute the minimum bounding rectangle for blocks $A(2,3)$, $B(3,2)$, $C(1,4)$ combined with slicing tree `A B H C V`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Slicing Tree Construction from a Polish Expression (7 marks):**

A slicing tree is decoded from a Polish expression using a **stack-based postfix evaluator**.

**Algorithm:** **(3 marks)**
1. Initialize an empty stack.
2. Scan the Polish expression from left to right.
3. If the token is an **operand (block label)**, create a leaf node and push it onto the stack.
4. If the token is an **operator (H or V)**, pop the top two sub-trees $T_R$ (top) and $T_L$ (next), create a new internal node labeled with the operator, attach $T_L$ as the left/top child and $T_R$ as the right/bottom child, then push the new node.
5. At the end, the stack contains exactly one element — the root of the slicing tree.

**Worked Example:** Consider `1 2 H 3 H 4 V` for 4 blocks. **(4 marks)**
- `1` → push leaf 1
- `2` → push leaf 2
- `H` → pop 2, 1; create $N_1 = H(1, 2)$; push $N_1$
- `3` → push leaf 3
- `H` → pop 3, $N_1$; create $N_2 = H(N_1, 3)$; push $N_2$
- `4` → push leaf 4
- `V` → pop 4, $N_2$; create **root = $V(N_2, 4)$**

The resulting tree has the V-cut at the root separating the left $H$-subtree (blocks 1, 2, 3) from the right block 4.

**(b) Stockmeyer's Algorithm and Application (7 marks):**

**Algorithm Description:** **(3 marks)**
Stockmeyer's algorithm computes, for each sub-floorplan, the **Pareto-optimal set** $\mathcal{S}$ of (width, height) pairs. A pair $(w_1, h_1)$ **dominates** $(w_2, h_2)$ if $w_1 \leq w_2$ AND $h_1 \leq h_2$.

- For a leaf $i$: $\mathcal{S}_i = \{(w_i, h_i)\}$.
- For an **H-node** with children having sets $\mathcal{S}_L$ and $\mathcal{S}_R$:
  $$\mathcal{S}_H = \text{prune}\{(\max(w_L, w_R),\, h_L + h_R) \mid (w_L, h_L) \in \mathcal{S}_L, (w_R, h_R) \in \mathcal{S}_R\}$$
- For a **V-node**:
  $$\mathcal{S}_V = \text{prune}\{(w_L + w_R,\, \max(h_L, h_R)) \mid (w_L, h_L) \in \mathcal{S}_L, (w_R, h_R) \in \mathcal{S}_R\}$$
- Minimum area $= \min\{w \cdot h \mid (w, h) \in \mathcal{S}_{\text{root}}\}$.

**Application to `A B H C V`:** **(4 marks)**

Leaf sets:
$$\mathcal{S}_A = \{(2,3)\}, \quad \mathcal{S}_B = \{(3,2)\}, \quad \mathcal{S}_C = \{(1,4)\}$$

**H-node (A, B):**
$$\mathcal{S}_{AB} = \{(\max(2,3),\, 3+2)\} = \{(3, 5)\}$$

**V-node (AB, C):**
$$\mathcal{S}_{\text{root}} = \{(3+1,\, \max(5, 4))\} = \{(4, 5)\}$$

**Minimum bounding rectangle area:**
$$A_{\min} = 4 \times 5 = 20 \text{ sq. units}$$

**Final chip dimensions: 4 × 5.**

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Q.B1.B.** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the representation of a slicing floorplan using a slicing tree. State the formula for the number of slicing floorplans for $n$ blocks. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Consider 4 blocks with widths and heights: $1(2,4), 2(3,3), 3(4,2), 4(3,4)$. Compute the bounding-rectangle dimensions and area for the Polish expression `1 2 H 3 4 H V` using bottom-up sizing rules. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Slicing Tree Representation (7 marks):**

A slicing tree is a **rooted full binary tree** where: **(2 marks)**
- **Leaves** correspond to the $n$ rectangular blocks (modules) of the floorplan.
- **Internal nodes** represent cutting lines, labeled $H$ (horizontal cut) or $V$ (vertical cut).
- The tree has $n$ leaves and $n-1$ internal nodes (full binary tree property).

**Diagrammatic illustration:** **(3 marks)** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the equivalent Mermaid slicing tree.)

For the expression `1 2 H 3 H 4 V`, the root V-cut separates block 4 from the left sub-tree, while the left sub-tree itself is a horizontal stack of (block 1 on top of block 2) and block 3, with another H-cut.

**Formula for number of slicing floorplans:** **(2 marks)**

$$N(n) = C_{n-1} \cdot 2^{n-1} = \frac{(2n-2)!}{(n-1)! \cdot n!} \cdot 2^{n-1}$$

where $C_{n-1}$ is the $(n-1)$-th Catalan number (count of full binary trees with $n$ leaves) and $2^{n-1}$ counts the choice of H or V at each internal node.

**(b) Bottom-Up Sizing Computation (7 marks):**

**Given blocks:** $1(2,4), 2(3,3), 3(4,2), 4(3,4)$.

**Polish expression:** `1 2 H 3 4 H V`.

**Decoding:** The expression builds the tree:
- $N_1 = H(1, 2)$ — stack block 1 on top of block 2
- $N_2 = H(3, 4)$ — stack block 3 on top of block 4
- **Root = $V(N_1, N_2)$** — place $N_1$ to the left of $N_2$

**Sizing at $N_1 = H(1, 2)$:** **(2 marks)**
$$W(N_1) = \max(W_1, W_2) = \max(2, 3) = 3$$
$$H(N_1) = H_1 + H_2 = 4 + 3 = 7$$

**Sizing at $N_2 = H(3, 4)$:** **(2 marks)**
$$W(N_2) = \max(W_3, W_4) = \max(4, 3) = 4$$
$$H(N_2) = H_3 + H_4 = 2 + 4 = 6$$

**Sizing at the root V-node:** **(2 marks)**
$$W_{\text{root}} = W(N_1) + W(N_2) = 3 + 4 = 7$$
$$H_{\text{root}} = \max(H(N_1), H(N_2)) = \max(7, 6) = 7$$

**Final bounding rectangle: 7 × 7, Total Area = 49 sq. units.** **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Operand–Operator Order in Postfix:** When evaluating `A B H`, the **second pop is the LEFT child** and the **first pop is the RIGHT child**. Reversing this swaps the positions of the two sub-blocks.
> 2. **Confusing H/V semantics:** H means the children are stacked **vertically** (heights add), V means they are placed **side-by-side** (widths add). Many students invert this and lose 2–3 marks per sub-part.
> 3. **Forgetting Pareto-pruning:** Without pruning dominated pairs in Stockmeyer's algorithm, the candidate set can explode combinatorially. Always prune after each combination step.
> 4. **Skipping the Catalan formula:** A common 2-mark question asks for the count $C_{n-1} \cdot 2^{n-1}$. Students who write only $C_{n-1}$ lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Stating area without units:** Always specify the area in **square units** (or $\mu m^2$ in real designs). Examiners deduct marks for vague numerical answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Slicing Floorplan**

- ✅ A slicing floorplan admits a **guillotine-cut decomposition** where every cut traverses the parent rectangle fully.
- ✅ Slicing tree: **full binary tree** with $n$ leaves and $n-1$ internal nodes (H/V labels).
- ✅ Polish expression = **postfix** traversal of the slicing tree. **Stack-validity rule** must hold during evaluation.
- ✅ Number of slicing floorplans for $n$ blocks: $\dfrac{(2n-2)!}{(n-1)! \cdot n!} \cdot 2^{n-1}$.
- ✅ H-node sizing: $W = \max(W_L, W_R)$, $H = H_L + H_R$.
- ✅ V-node sizing: $W = W_L + W_R$, $H = \max(H_L, H_R)$.
- ✅ **Stockmeyer's algorithm** uses Pareto-optimal $(w, h)$ sets; prune dominated pairs after every combination.
- ✅ Block rotation (allowing both $(w, h)$ and $(h, w)$ at the leaf) **enlarges the Pareto set** and may yield a smaller minimum area.
- ✅ Skewed slicing tree = all internal nodes have the **same operator** (all H or all V).
- ✅ Slicing floorplans are a strict **subset** of all floorplans; **non-slicing** floorplans (e.g., wheel configurations) cannot be represented by a slicing tree.
- ✅ Practical VLSI use: **datapath placement, memory array layout, mixed-signal block stacking** where clean hierarchical cuts are feasible.
- ✅ Most KTU 2024 questions on this topic test: definition, Polish expression encoding/decoding, tree-to-floorplan sizing, and Stockmeyer's Pareto-pruning logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Slicing Floorplan
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non-Slicing Floorplan

# Module 4 — Non-Slicing Floorplan

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Non-Slicing Floorplan?

A **Floorplan** is the problem of placing a set of rectangular modules (hard or soft macros) on a chip such that no two modules overlap, while optimizing objectives such as area, wirelength, and timing. The output is a set of $(x_i, y_i, w_i, h_i)$ coordinates for every module.

A **Slicing Floorplan** is one that can be recursively divided into two parts by a straight vertical (V) or horizontal (H) cut line that runs the **entire width/height** of the current rectangle. This recursive structure can be captured by a **slicing tree** and represented compactly by a **Polish expression**.

A **Non-Slicing Floorplan** (sometimes called a *mosaic* or *general* floorplan) is one in which **no such recursive guillotine cut exists**. The arrangement of modules is more general — for instance, a central block surrounded by other blocks on multiple sides — and cannot be decomposed into two sub-floorplans via a straight cut. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes non-slicing structures because modern VLSI designs (ASICs, SoCs, GPUs) routinely contain such topologies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> A floorplan $F$ is said to be **non-slicing** if there exists **no** recursive bipartition of $F$ using straight vertical/horizontal cut-lines. Equivalently, $F$ cannot be encoded as a binary slicing tree or a single Polish expression.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tetris Puzzle"

Imagine fitting irregularly shaped rectangular tiles onto a board. With a slicing floorplan, every cut you make goes straight across the board — like slicing a cake, where every cut separates the dessert into two pieces all the way through. With a **non-slicing** floorplan, you can have a tile in the middle, surrounded by tiles on the left, right, top, and bottom — like a **bullseye target**. You cannot remove any piece by a single straight cut because the central tile blocks every line. Modern chip designs look much more like this bullseye — IP cores like CPUs, GPUs, and memory controllers are surrounded by smaller blocks.

### 1.3 Why Non-Slicing Floorplans Matter

| Property | Slicing | Non-Slicing |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| Cut-lines | Straight, full bisection | No requirement |
| Representational complexity | $O(n! \cdot n)$ Polish expressions | Exponential in $n$ |
| Solution space | Smaller | **Larger** |
| Quality achievable | Lower | **Higher** |
| Modern ASIC suitability | Limited | Preferred |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The number of slicing floorplans is approximately $\Theta(8^n / n^{1.5})$ while the number of *all* (non-slicing) floorplans is approximately $\Theta(n!^{2.4})$ — orders of magnitude more solutions exist for the non-slicing case, giving the optimizer a far richer design space.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Floorplan Representation Problem

The KTU syllabus treats the **representation** as the central design choice. A good representation must satisfy three properties:

1. **Bijection / One-to-One Mapping** — Every representation corresponds to *exactly one* floorplan and vice-versa (no two representations give the same packing).
2. **Completeness / Spanning** — It can encode *every* possible floorplan of $n$ modules.
3. **Efficiency** — The encoding size is $O(n)$ and operations (perturbation, evaluation) are polynomial time.

For non-slicing floorplans, the three most prominent representations studied under KTU 2024 are:

- **Sequence Pair (SP)** — Murata et al. (1996)
- **Corner Block List (CBL)** — Hong et al. (2000)
- **B\*-Tree** — Chang et al. (2000)

### 2.2 Sequence Pair (SP)

A sequence pair uses **two permutations** $\Gamma_+$ and $\Gamma_-$ of the $n$ module labels. For any two modules $a$ and $b$, the relative positional constraint is read directly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(a \prec_+ b) \text{ AND } (a \prec_- b) &\;\Rightarrow\; a \text{ is LEFT of } b \\
(a \succ_+ b) \text{ AND } (a \succ_- b) &\;\Rightarrow\; a \text{ is RIGHT of } b \\
(a \prec_+ b) \text{ AND } (a \succ_- b) &\;\Rightarrow\; a \text{ is ABOVE } b \\
(a \succ_+ b) \text{ AND } (a \prec_- b) &\;\Rightarrow\; a \text{ is BELOW } b
\end{aligned}
$$

The pair $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-)$ uniquely determines the relative position of every module pair. Decoding is done by:

1. Building a **Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)** with edges from the $\Gamma_+$ ordering (left-to-right source chain).
2. Building a **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)** with edges from the $\Gamma_-$ ordering.
3. Computing longest paths in the HCG (gives $x$-coordinates) and VCG (gives $y$-coordinates).

### 2.3 Corner Block List (CBL)

A CBL encodes a floorplan as a sequence $(S, T)$ where:
- $S = (s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n)$ is a permutation of $n$ module labels.
- $T = (t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n)$ with $t_i \in \{T, B\}$ indicating whether the block is placed at the **Top** or **Bottom** corner of the previously placed block.

Each new module is "slid" into a corner formed by already-placed blocks and the chip boundary. The CBL is **rotation-aware** — a block has two orientations (swapping $w$ and $h$).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Footprint:** SP uses $2n$ integers, while CBL uses $2n$ symbols but exploits the smaller solution space (roughly $\Theta(n! \cdot 2^{2n-2})$) → CBL produces tighter, faster exploration than SP.

### 2.4 Cost Function for Floorplan Optimization

The combined objective minimized by a Simulated Annealing engine is:

$$
\Phi \;=\; \alpha \cdot \frac{A}{A_{\text{ref}}} \;+\; \beta \cdot \frac{W}{W_{\text{ref}}} \;+\; \gamma \cdot P_{\text{AR}}
$$

where each $P_{\text{AR}}$ is an aspect-ratio penalty applied **module-wise** and the normalization constants are derived from a reference initial floorplan.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Constraint / Range |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| $A$ | Total bounding-rectangle area $W \cdot H$ | Minimize |
| $W$ | Total chip width | $\leq W_{\max}$ |
| $H$ | Total chip height | $\leq H_{\max}$ |
| $\alpha_i = w_i / h_i$ | Aspect ratio of module $i$ | $r_{\min} \leq \alpha_i \leq r_{\max}$ |
| $L_{ij}$ | Manhattan wirelength $\vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$ | Minimize |
| $\Phi$ | Combined cost (area + wire + AR-penalty) | Minimize |
| $T$ | Annealing temperature | Decreases geometrically $T_{k+1} = c \cdot T_k$, $0.8 \leq c \leq 0.99$ |
| $P_{\text{AR},i}$ | Penalty when $\alpha_i \notin [r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$ | $\geq 0$ |
| $L_{\text{HCG}}(v)$ | Longest-path length from source to $v$ in HCG | Equals $x_v$ |
| $L_{\text{VCG}}(v)$ | Longest-path length from source to $v$ in VCG | Equals $y_v$ |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing LaTeX inside markdown tables, **never** use the bare `|` symbol as a delimiter for absolute value. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead — otherwise the table parser treats it as a column separator and breaks the layout.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Non-slicing floorplanning is the **bedrock of placement** in:

- **System-on-Chip (SoC)** layout for smartphones and laptops.
- **GPU compute dies** where a central shader array is surrounded by caches.
- **FPGA floorplanning** tools (Vivado, Quartus).
- **3D-IC stacking** problems where multiple dies need joint optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Decoding a Sequence Pair via Longest-Path

**Given:** Sequence pair $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-) = (\langle 1,2,3,4 \rangle,\ \langle 3,1,4,2 \rangle)$ with module sizes $(w,h)$.

The relative positions are:

- $1 \prec_+ 2$ and $1 \prec_- 2$ ⇒ **1 is LEFT of 2**
- $1 \prec_+ 3$ and $3 \prec_- 1$ ⇒ **1 is BELOW 3**
- $1 \prec_+ 4$ and $1 \prec_- 4$ ⇒ **1 is LEFT of 4**
- $2 \prec_+ 3$ and $3 \prec_- 2$ ⇒ **2 is BELOW 3**
- $2 \prec_+ 4$ and $4 \prec_- 2$ ⇒ **2 is BELOW 4**
- $3 \prec_+ 4$ and $3 \prec_- 4$ ⇒ **3 is LEFT of 4**

**Constructing the HCG (horizontal constraint):**

The HCG encodes left-to-right relations derived from $\Gamma_+$. For each pair $(a,b)$ where $a$ is left of $b$, draw a directed edge $a \rightarrow b$ weighted by $w_a$.

$$
\text{HCG edges:}\quad 1 \rightarrow 2\ (w_1),\quad 1 \rightarrow 4\ (w_1),\quad 3 \rightarrow 4\ (w_3)
$$

**Computing $x$-coordinates** (longest path from a virtual source 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= w_1 \\
x_3 &= w_3 \\
x_2 &= x_1 + w_1 = 2w_1 \\
x_4 &= \max(x_1 + w_1,\ x_3 + w_3) = \max(2w_1,\ w_1 + w_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Constructing the VCG (vertical constraint):**

The VCG encodes below-to-above relations from $\Gamma_-$. Edge $a \rightarrow b$ weighted by $h_a$ means $a$ is below $b$.

$$
\text{VCG edges:}\quad 1 \rightarrow 3\ (h_1),\quad 2 \rightarrow 3\ (h_2),\quad 2 \rightarrow 4\ (h_2)
$$

**Computing $y$-coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= h_1 \\
y_2 &= h_2 \\
y_4 &= y_2 + h_2 = 2h_2 \\
y_3 &= \max(y_1 + h_1,\ y_2 + h_2) = \max(2h_1,\ h_1 + h_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation — Area and Wirelength Cost

**Bounding area** of the chip rectangle:

$$
A = W \cdot H = \left(\max_i x_i + w_i\right) \cdot \left(\max_i y_i + h_i\right)
$$

**Total wirelength** across a net-list $N$ of $K$ nets:

$$
L \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{K}\ \left(\max_{v \in \text{net}_k} x_v - \min_{v \in \text{net}_k} x_v \;+\; \max_{v \in \text{net}_k} y_v - \min_{v \in \text{net}_k} y_v\right)
$$

This is the **Half-Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL)** estimator — the KTU gold-standard for cost evaluation during placement.

### 3.3 Derivation — Aspect-Ratio Penalty

For a soft module $i$ with width $w_i$, height $h_i$:

$$
\alpha_i = \frac{w_i}{h_i}
$$

The penalty function penalizes aspect ratios outside $[r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$:

$$
P_{\text{AR},i} \;=\;
\begin{cases}
0, & r_{\min} \leq \alpha_i \leq r_{\max} \\[4pt]
(\alpha_i - r_{\max})^2, & \alpha_i > r_{\max} \\[4pt]
(r_{\min} - \alpha_i)^2, & \alpha_i < r_{\min}
\end{cases}
$$

Total aspect-ratio penalty:

$$
P_{\text{AR}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{\text{AR},i}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Representations

| Criterion | Sequence Pair | Corner Block List | B\*-Tree |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|----------|
| Encoding size | $2n$ | $2n$ | $n$ |
| Decoding time | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Spanning (covers all floorplans?) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Simulated-Annealing move cost | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Rotation-aware | Via two sequences | Native ($T$ bit) | Native (left/right child) |
| KTU prevalence | Very High | High | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Non-Slicing vs Slicing Floorplan — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Floorplan Input: n rectangular modules])
    slicingCheck{Slicing structure?\\nRecursively bisectable\\nby V/H cut?}
    slicingPath[Slicing Floorplan]
    nonSlicingPath[Non-Slicing Floorplan]
    slicingTree[Represented by\\nPolish Expression + Slicing Tree]
    seqPair[Represented by Sequence Pair]
    cblNode[Represented by Corner Block List]
    bStarTree[Represented by B-star Tree]
    optEngine[Simulated Annealing /\\nEvolutionary Engine]
    finalFloor[Optimized Final Layout]
    startNode --> slicingCheck
    slicingCheck -- Yes --> slicingPath
    slicingCheck -- No --> nonSlicingPath
    slicingPath --> slicingTree
    nonSlicingPath --> seqPair
    nonSlicingPath --> cblNode
    nonSlicingPath --> bStarTree
    slicingTree --> optEngine
    seqPair --> optEngine
    cblNode --> optEngine
    bStarTree --> optEngine
    optEngine --> finalFloor
```

### 4.2 Sequence-Pair Decoding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spInput["Input: Gamma+ and Gamma- sequences"]
    posExtract["Extract pairwise constraints\\nLEFT/RIGHT/ABOVE/BELOW"]
    hcgBuild["Build HCG\\nleft-to-right edges"]
    vcgBuild["Build VCG\\nbelow-to-above edges"]
    longPathH["Longest path in HCG\\n=> x-coordinates"]
    longPathV["Longest path in VCG\\n=> y-coordinates"]
    packOut["Output: x_i, y_i for every module"]
    spInput --> posExtract
    posExtract --> hcgBuild
    posExtract --> vcgBuild
    hcgBuild --> longPathH
    vcgBuild --> longPathV
    longPathH --> packOut
    longPathV --> packOut
```

### 4.3 Simulated Annealing Loop for Floorplan Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init["Initialize: random SP/CBL + high T"]
    perturb["Perturbation:\\nSwap or rotate module"]
    evalCost["Compute cost Phi\\nArea + Wire + AR penalty"]
    delta{"deltaPhi < 0?"}
    accept["Accept move"]
    rejectCheck{"Random < exp(-deltaPhi/T)?"}
    reject["Reject move"]
    cool["T = c * T"]
    stopCheck{"T < T_min ?"}
    done["Best floorplan found"]
    init --> perturb
    perturb --> evalCost
    evalCost --> delta
    delta -- Yes --> accept
    delta -- No --> rejectCheck
    rejectCheck -- Yes --> accept
    rejectCheck -- No --> reject
    accept --> cool
    reject --> cool
    cool --> stopCheck
    stopCheck -- No --> perturb
    stopCheck -- Yes --> done
```

### 4.4 Bullseye vs Sliced-Cake Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Slicing["Slicing: Cake-Cut View"]
        sA["Block 1"]
        sB["Block 2"]
        sC["Block 3"]
        sD["Block 4"]
    end
    subgraph NonSlicing["Non-Slicing: Bullseye View"]
        nCenter["Center IP core"]
        nTop["Top block"]
        nBottom["Bottom block"]
        nLeft["Left block"]
        nRight["Right block"]
    end
    Slicing -.Recursively bisectable.-> nCenter
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
Differentiate between slicing and non-slicing floorplans. Give one example where a non-slicing structure is unavoidable.

**Model Answer:**
A slicing floorplan admits recursive bipartition by vertical or horizontal guillotine cuts, while a non-slicing floorplan does not admit any such recursive cut. A bullseye arrangement (one central block surrounded on all four sides) is the textbook example of a non-slicing structure, common in modern SoCs where an IP core is encircled by smaller memory or I/O blocks.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List any **three** representations for non-slicing floorplans and state one key property of each.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Sequence Pair (SP)** — Encodes floorplan as two permutations; uses HCG/VCG longest paths for decoding.
2. **Corner Block List (CBL)** — Encodes as a permutation plus T/B orientation bits; smaller solution space than SP.
3. **B\*-Tree** — Encodes as a binary tree with $n$ nodes; supports $O(\log n)$ moves and area computation.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Sequence Pair (SP)** representation for non-slicing floorplans. For the sequence pair $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-) = (\langle a, b, c, d \rangle, \langle b, a, d, c \rangle)$, derive the relative positional constraints between every pair of modules.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **simulated annealing algorithm** used to optimize a non-slicing floorplan, including the perturbation operations, cost function, and the cooling schedule.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sequence Pair Explanation & Derivation:**

The Sequence Pair uses two permutations of the $n$ module labels to encode relative positional constraints. The four cases for any pair $(a, b)$ are tabulated in §2.2.

For $(\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-) = (\langle a, b, c, d \rangle, \langle b, a, d, c \rangle)$:

- $a \prec_+ b$ AND $b \prec_- a$ ⇒ **a is BELOW b**
- $a \prec_+ c$ AND $d \prec_- a$ ⇒ **a is ABOVE c** *(read: $a$ is before $c$ in $\Gamma_+$, $a$ is after $d$ in $\Gamma_-$ — but pair-wise check $(a,c)$: $a$ is before $c$ in $\Gamma_+$ ✓, $a$ is after $c$ in $\Gamma_-$ ✓ → a is ABOVE c)*
- $a \prec_+ d$ AND $d \prec_- a$ ⇒ **a is BELOW d**
- $b \prec_+ c$ AND $b \prec_- c$ ⇒ **b is LEFT of c**
- $b \prec_+ d$ AND $d \prec_- b$ ⇒ **b is BELOW d**
- $c \prec_+ d$ AND $d \prec_- c$ ⇒ **c is BELOW d**

The HCG receives edges $b \rightarrow c$. The VCG receives edges $a \rightarrow c$, $a \rightarrow d$, $b \rightarrow d$, $c \rightarrow d$. $x$- and $y$-coordinates are then computed by the longest-path method in §3.1.

**(b) Simulated Annealing for Floorplans:**

Three perturbation operators are applied on the SP:

1. **Swap** two module labels in either $\Gamma_+$ or $\Gamma_-$.
2. **Rotate** a soft module (swap $w_i$ and $h_i$).
3. **Move** a module to a different position in one of the sequences.

The cost function combines area, wirelength, and aspect-ratio penalty:

$$
\Phi = \alpha \cdot \frac{A}{A_{\text{ref}}} + \beta \cdot \frac{W}{W_{\text{ref}}} + \gamma \cdot P_{\text{AR}}
$$

Acceptance criterion: a worsening move with $\Delta \Phi > 0$ is accepted with probability $\exp(-\Delta \Phi / T)$. Temperature is cooled geometrically:

$$
T_{k+1} = c \cdot T_k,\qquad c \in [0.8, 0.99]
$$

The algorithm terminates when $T < T_{\min}$ or when no improving move is found in $M$ consecutive iterations.

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Corner Block List (CBL)** representation. With the help of a neat example, show how a non-slicing floorplan of 4 blocks is encoded.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the cost function $\Phi$ for a non-slicing floorplan with three blocks of dimensions $(w_1, h_1)=(2,3)$, $(w_2, h_2)=(4,2)$, $(w_3, h_3)=(3,2)$ arranged so that block 1 is to the left of blocks 2 and 3, and block 3 is below block 2. Assume $A_{\text{ref}} = 24$, $W_{\text{ref}} = 20$, $\alpha=\beta=\gamma=1$, and aspect-ratio bounds $r_{\min}=0.5$, $r_{\max}=2.0$. (Ignore wirelength.)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Corner Block List Explanation:**

A CBL is a pair $(S, T)$ where:
- $S = (s_1, \dots, s_n)$ is a sequence listing the order of block placement.
- $T = (t_1, \dots, t_n)$ with $t_i \in \{T, B\}$ indicates the corner (Top or Bottom).

The first block $s_1$ sits in the bottom-left corner. Each subsequent block slides into a corner formed by the existing placed blocks and the chip boundary. A block is placed at the **T** corner if the previous block is its lower neighbor (placed above the previous), and **B** if it is the upper neighbor (placed below the previous). This guarantees a valid mosaic non-slicing floorplan.

**Example:** For 4 blocks $\{A,B,C,D\}$, a valid CBL is:
$S = (A, B, C, D)$, $T = (B, B, T, T)$. This places A in the corner, then B at the bottom of A, then C at the top of B, then D at the top of C — yielding a 2×2 mosaic.

**(b) Cost Function Derivation:**

Layout: Block 1 left of {2,3}; Block 3 below Block 2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Width of block 1:}\quad w_1 &= 2 \\
\text{Combined width of blocks 2, 3 stacked:}\quad w_{23} &= \max(w_2, w_3) = \max(4, 3) = 4 \\
\text{Total chip width:}\quad W &= w_1 + w_{23} = 2 + 4 = 6 \\
\text{Height of block 1:}\quad h_1 &= 3 \\
\text{Combined height of blocks 2, 3 stacked:}\quad h_{23} &= h_2 + h_3 = 2 + 2 = 4 \\
\text{Total chip height:}\quad H &= \max(h_1, h_{23}) = \max(3, 4) = 4 \\
\text{Total area:}\quad A &= W \cdot H = 6 \cdot 4 = 24
\end{aligned}
$$

Aspect ratios: $\alpha_1 = 2/3 \approx 0.67$ ✓, $\alpha_2 = 4/2 = 2.0$ ✓, $\alpha_3 = 3/2 = 1.5$ ✓ — all within $[0.5, 2.0]$, so $P_{\text{AR}} = 0$.

Wirelength is ignored per problem statement.

$$
\Phi = 1 \cdot \frac{24}{24} + 1 \cdot \frac{W}{20} + 1 \cdot 0
$$

Plug in $W = 6$:

$$
\Phi = 1 + \frac{6}{20} + 0 = 1 + 0.3 = 1.3
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting rotation costs:** A soft module can be rotated ($w \leftrightarrow h$). Many students assume fixed orientation and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Mishandling the SP rules:** The four pairwise cases are case-sensitive to the order in BOTH sequences. Drawing the HCG/VCG wrongly leads to cascading coordinate errors.
> 3. **Treating all floorplans as slicing:** Saying "all floorplans can be encoded by Polish expression" is **incorrect** — this is the most common 3-mark question trap.
> 4. **Ignoring the bounding rectangle:** Area must be computed on the **outer bounding box**, not on the sum of individual block areas.
> 5. **Confusing SP and CBL solutions spaces:** SP is $\Theta((n!)^2 / 2^{2n})$ whereas CBL is $\Theta(n! \cdot 2^{2n-2})$ — they are **different**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **non-slicing floorplan** admits no recursive vertical/horizontal guillotine cut.
- The **three pillar representations** of KTU 2024 Module 4 are **Sequence Pair**, **Corner Block List**, and **B\*-Tree**.
- **Sequence Pair** = two permutations $\Gamma_+, \Gamma_-$; decoded via longest paths in HCG and VCG.
- **Corner Block List** = permutation $S$ + corner-orientation bits $T$; smaller solution space, faster moves.
- The **combined cost** $\Phi$ adds normalized area, wirelength (HPWL), and aspect-ratio penalty.
- **Simulated annealing** with three perturbation types (swap, rotate, move) and geometric cooling is the standard optimizer.
- Always remember: **absolute values in tables** must use `\vert` or `\mid`, never a bare `|` pipe.
- **Non-slicing floorplans dominate modern SoC** design because of the bullseye/centroid-placement topology.
- HPWL is the KTU-blessed wirelength estimator; Manhattan distance formula is $L = \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert$.
- For 14-mark questions, examiners expect: representation + decoding example + cost-function derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floorplan Representations: Constraint Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Floorplan Representations: Constraint Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Floorplanning** is the critical physical design stage in VLSI where rectangular macro cells (or modules) of varying dimensions are arranged on a chip canvas to optimize area, wirelength, and timing, while satisfying performance and connectivity constraints.

A **Constraint Graph** is a graph-theoretic data structure used to encode the relative placement relationships between rectangular modules in a floorplan. It captures horizontal and vertical adjacency, dimension constraints (width/height), and slicing operations through the use of labeled, directed edges and weighted nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**  
> A *Constraint Graph* is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) used to represent a **slicing floorplan**, where each node represents a module, and each directed edge is labeled with a binary operator (`H` for horizontal cut, `V` for vertical cut) or with a weight indicating the relative position constraint (left, right, above, below).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Furniture Arrangement Blueprint"

Imagine you are arranging furniture in a rectangular room:

- Each piece of furniture is a **module** (e.g., sofa, bed, table).
- You decide that the **sofa must be to the left of the TV** and the **bed must be above the wardrobe**. These are *horizontal* and *vertical* positional constraints.
- A **Constraint Graph** is exactly like a **chessboard with arrows**: each furniture piece is a square (node), and arrows between squares show which piece must lie left/right/above/below another.
- A **slicing floorplan** is one where you can recursively slice the room with a single straight cut (horizontal or vertical) until each piece occupies its own region — much like cutting a cake into smaller and smaller rectangular slices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:**  
> - **Slicing Floorplan** → Can be obtained by recursively bisecting the chip rectangle with straight horizontal or vertical cuts.  
> - **Non-Slicing Floorplan** → Cannot be derived by such recursive cuts. Constraint graphs are *primarily applicable to slicing floorplans*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following key metrics are tracked in floorplanning and constraint graph analysis:

- **Area (A)** — total silicon area occupied (µm²).
- **Aspect Ratio (r)** — height/width of a module.
- **Wirelength (L)** — estimated total interconnection length.
- **Channel Density** — maximum number of nets crossing any horizontal/vertical channel.
- **Dead Space (%)** — unused area ratio = (Unused Area / Bounding Area) × **100**.
- **Module Pin Count (P)** — number of I/O terminals on each module boundary.

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Cue to Remember:** Think of a Constraint Graph as a *family tree* where:
> - The **root** = the whole chip.
> - **Children** = sub-rectangles created by a cut.
> - **Leaves** = individual modules.
> - **Edge labels** = direction of cut (H or V).
> - **Node weights** = module dimensions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Fold Structure of a Constraint Graph

A complete floorplan constraint representation requires **two complementary directed acyclic graphs** working in tandem:

1. **Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)**  
   - Captures relative **left-right (x-direction)** placement of modules.  
   - Directed edge `u → v` means module `u` lies to the **left** of module `v`.  
   - Source node has no incoming edge; sink node has no outgoing edge.  
   - Used to determine the minimum **width** of every row of the slicing structure.

2. **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)**  
   - Captures relative **top-bottom (y-direction)** placement of modules.  
   - Directed edge `u → v` means module `u` lies **above** module `v`.  
   - Used to determine the minimum **height** of every column.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two graphs instead of one?**  
> Because in a 2D slicing structure, both dimensions matter simultaneously. A single graph cannot independently represent the constraints along two orthogonal axes without ambiguity. The HCG governs horizontal packing; the VCG governs vertical packing. They are linked by the same slicing tree.

## 2.2 The Three Operations on Constraint Graphs

When modules are packed, three primary operations manipulate the constraint graphs:

| Operation | Symbol | Description | Graph Effect |
|-----------|--------|-------------|--------------|
| **Horizontal Composition** | `H` | Places two sub-floorplans side by side | Merges roots into single node; adds edge between sub-trees |
| **Vertical Composition** | `V` | Places two sub-floorplans one above the other | Merges roots into single node vertically |
| **Skewed Composition** | `S` (sometimes `H'`/`V'`) | Combines a vertical cut with reversed sub-floorplan orientation | Alters edge direction in one of the constraint graphs |

## 2.3 Labeled vs. Unlabeled Constraint Graphs

> [!NOTE]
> - **Unlabeled Constraint Graph:** Each edge simply represents a positional relationship, without specifying cut direction.  
> - **Labeled Constraint Graph:** Each edge is labeled with `H` or `V` to indicate whether the underlying cut in the slicing tree is horizontal or vertical. This adds information about the *structure* of the slicing.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Total chip area | $A = W_{chip} \times H_{chip}$ | µm² |
| 2 | Dead space % | $D = \frac{A_{chip} - \sum A_i}{A_{chip}} \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | Aspect ratio | $r = h_i / w_i$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Number of slicing trees for *n* modules (upper bound) | $N(n) = \frac{(2n)!}{n! (n+1)!}$ (Catalan number) | count |
| 5 | Horizontal composition width | $W_{H} = w_{left} + w_{right}$ | µm |
| 6 | Vertical composition height | $H_{V} = h_{top} + h_{bottom}$ | µm |
| 7 | Longest path in HCG | $\text{Critical Width} = \max_{(u \to v)} \sum w_{edge}$ | µm |
| 8 | Longest path in VCG | $\text{Critical Height} = \max_{(u \to v)} \sum h_{edge}$ | µm |
| 9 | Wirelength (HPWL approx) | $L = \sum_{i} \left( \vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert \right)$ | µm (use `\vert`) |
| 10 | Baye's bound (memorization) | $B_n = \frac{2(2n-1)}{n+1} B_{n-1}$ | Catalan recursion |

> [!WARNING]
> **In equations, never use the keyboard pipe `|` for absolute value in tables** — it breaks markdown table syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Constraint Graphs are not just academic constructs. In modern EDA tools (Synopsys IC Compiler, Cadence Innovus, OpenROAD), they underpin:

- **Block placement optimization** in hierarchical design.
- **Timing-driven floorplanning** where critical paths are pre-routed as constraints.
- **Mixed-size placement** of macros + standard cells.
- **3D-IC and chiplet floorplanning**, where each die is a node in an inter-die constraint graph.
- **FPGA architecture mapping**, where logic blocks and routing channels are analogous to graph nodes and edges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Catalan Number Count of Slicing Trees

The number of distinct slicing trees for `n` modules (each slicing tree produces a unique labeled constraint graph, ignoring symmetry) is given by the **Catalan number**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_n &= \frac{1}{n+1} \binom{2n}{n} \\
    &= \frac{(2n)!}{n! (n+1)!}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_n &= \frac{(2n)!}{n! \cdot (n+1)!} \\
    &= \frac{2n \cdot (2n-1)!}{n \cdot (n-1)! \cdot (n+1) \cdot n!} \\
    &= \frac{2(2n-1)}{n+1} \cdot \frac{(2n-2)!}{(n-1)! \cdot n!} \\
    &= \frac{2(2n-1)}{n+1} \cdot C_{n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the recursive form: $C_n = \frac{2(2n-1)}{n+1} C_{n-1}$, with $C_0 = 1$.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Constructing Constraint Graph from Slicing Tree

Consider slicing tree:  
`(H (V a b) (V c d))` where `a, b, c, d` are modules.

### Step 1: Identify the binary tree structure

- Root: `H` (horizontal cut)
- Left subtree: `(V a b)` — vertical cut between `a` and `b`
- Right subtree: `(V c d)` — vertical cut between `c` and `d`

### Step 2: Construct the Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)

The top-level `H` cut means the left subtree sits to the **left** of the right subtree.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Root}_L \to \text{Root}_R \quad \text{(label H)} \\
&\text{Inside left subtree: } a \text{ is above } b \Rightarrow a \to b \\
&\text{Inside right subtree: } c \text{ is above } d \Rightarrow c \to d
\end{aligned}
$$

**HCG (as adjacency):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Nodes} &= \{L_{root}, R_{root}, a, b, c, d\} \\
\text{Edges} &= \{L_{root} \to a,\; a \to b,\; R_{root} \to c,\; c \to d,\; L_{root} \to R_{root}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Construct the Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)

The top-level `H` cut creates two horizontal sub-rectangles; the inner `V` cuts divide each horizontally into top and bottom modules.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vertical interpretation:} \quad & b \text{ is below } a \Rightarrow b \to a \\
                                       & d \text{ is below } c \Rightarrow d \to c \\
\text{Cross-subtree:} \quad & \text{no direct vertical edge (they are side by side)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**VCG (as adjacency):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Nodes} &= \{a, b, c, d\} \\
\text{Edges} &= \{b \to a,\; d \to c\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Critical Path (Width) Calculation

If $w_a = 4, w_b = 3, w_c = 5, w_b = 2$ (all in µm), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{left} &= \max(w_a, w_b) = 4 \\
W_{right} &= \max(w_c, w_d) = 5 \\
W_{chip} &= W_{left} + W_{right} = 4 + 5 = 9 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Critical Path (Height) Calculation

If $h_a = 3, h_b = 2, h_c = 4, h_d = 1$ µm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{left} &= h_a + h_b = 3 + 2 = 5 \\
H_{right} &= h_c + h_d = 4 + 1 = 5 \\
H_{chip} &= \max(H_{left}, H_{right}) = \max(5,5) = 5 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Total Area and Dead Space

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{chip} &= W_{chip} \times H_{chip} = 9 \times 5 = 45 \; \mu m^2 \\
A_{modules} &= (4 \times 3) + (3 \times 2) + (5 \times 4) + (2 \times 1) \\
            &= 12 + 6 + 20 + 2 = 40 \; \mu m^2 \\
D &= \frac{45 - 40}{45} \times 100 = 11.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Constraint Graph

| Floorplan Type | Constraint Graph? | Polish Expression? | Tree Size |
|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| Slicing | ✅ Highly suitable | ✅ Yes | $O(n)$ |
| Non-slicing (mosaic) | ⚠️ Extended CG (sequence pair, BSG, O-tree) | ❌ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Fixed-outline | ✅ With pin constraints | ✅ Yes | $O(n)$ |
| 3D / Multi-die | ✅ Multi-layer CG | ✅ Yes | $O(n \cdot d)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Slicing Tree → Constraint Graph

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootH[H - Horizontal Cut]
    rootH --> subV1[V - Vertical Cut]
    rootH --> subV2[V - Vertical Cut]
    subV1 --> modA[Module a]
    subV1 --> modB[Module b]
    subV2 --> modC[Module c]
    subV2 --> modD[Module d]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)

```mermaid
graph LR
    RL[Left Subtree Root] --> A[Module a]
    A --> B[Module b]
    RL --> RR[Right Subtree Root]
    RR --> C[Module c]
    C --> D[Module d]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)

```mermaid
graph LR
    B[Module b] --> A[Module a]
    D[Module d] --> C[Module c]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputFloorplan[Input Slicing Floorplan] --> parser[Polish Expression Parser]
    parser --> buildTree[Slicing Tree Constructor]
    buildTree --> cgGen[Constraint Graph Generator]
    cgGen --> hcg[Horizontal Constraint Graph HCG]
    cgGen --> vcg[Vertical Constraint Graph VCG]
    hcg --> criticalW[Longest Path - Width]
    vcg --> criticalH[Longest Path - Height]
    criticalW --> areaCalc[Area and Dead Space Calculator]
    criticalH --> areaCalc
    areaCalc --> outputLayout[Final Placed Layout]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Composition Operations (H / V / S)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HOP [Horizontal Composition H]
        hL[Left Module Block] --- hR[Right Module Block]
    end
    subgraph VOP [Vertical Composition V]
        vT[Top Module Block] --- vB[Bottom Module Block]
    end
    subgraph SOP [Skewed Composition S]
        sL[Left Reversed Block] --- sR[Right Normal Block]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip:** In all graphs above, the arrow direction `A → B` means **A is constrained to lie on the designated side of B** (left/above for HCG, above for VCG). The slicing tree governs *how* these directional relations are propagated.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a slicing floorplan and a non-slicing floorplan. Can a constraint graph fully represent a non-slicing floorplan?**

**Model Answer:**

A *slicing floorplan* can be obtained by recursively cutting a chip rectangle using straight horizontal or vertical cuts until each sub-rectangle contains exactly one module. A *non-slicing floorplan* cannot be derived by such recursive cuts — it contains "unsliceable" mosaic-style arrangements.

A standard constraint graph (HCG + VCG derived from a slicing tree) **cannot fully represent** a non-slicing floorplan because such floorplans do not possess a unique slicing tree. For non-slicing cases, alternative representations are used: **Sequence Pairs, Bounded-Sliceline Grids (BSG), and O-Trees**.

*(Marks distribution: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the longest path in a constraint graph?**

**Model Answer:**

The longest path (also called the *critical path*) in the Horizontal Constraint Graph determines the **minimum width** required to pack all modules in a row without overlap, by summing module widths along the path. Similarly, the longest path in the Vertical Constraint Graph determines the **minimum height** of the chip. These two values together yield the **minimum bounding area** of the floorplan, which is a key optimization objective in slicing floorplan design.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 + 1.5 = 3)*

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**For a floorplan with 4 modules `a, b, c, d` having dimensions $(w,h)$: `a = (4,3)`, `b = (3,2)`, `c = (5,4)`, `d = (2,1)`, given the slicing tree `(H (V a b) (V c d))`:**

**(a)** Construct the **Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)** and **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)**. Label all edges appropriately. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **chip width, chip height, total area, and dead-space percentage**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The slicing tree `(H (V a b) (V c d))` means:

- The top-level `H` cut divides the chip into **left** and **right** sub-rectangles.
- The left rectangle is further divided by a `V` cut into `a` (top) and `b` (bottom).
- The right rectangle is further divided by a `V` cut into `c` (top) and `d` (bottom).

**Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG):**

Nodes: $\{L, R, a, b, c, d\}$ where $L$ = left-root, $R$ = right-root.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Edges:} \quad & L \xrightarrow{H} R \\
                    & L \to a,\; a \to b \quad \text{(within left subtree)} \\
                    & R \to c,\; c \to d \quad \text{(within right subtree)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG):**

Nodes: $\{a, b, c, d\}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Edges:} \quad & a \xrightarrow{V} b \quad (\text{a is above b}) \\
                    & c \xrightarrow{V} d \quad (\text{c is above d})
\end{aligned}
$$

Edge labels: all internal vertical edges are labeled `V`; the top-level horizontal edge between $L$ and $R$ is labeled `H`.

**Critical path in HCG:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L \to a \to b \quad &\Rightarrow \text{width contribution} = w_a + w_b = 4 + 3 = 7 \; \mu m \\
\text{Or} \quad L \to R \quad &\Rightarrow 0 \; \mu m \text{ (root connector)} \\
R \to c \to d \quad &\Rightarrow w_c + w_d = 5 + 2 = 7 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

But the chip width along the *horizontal axis* is computed by adding the maximum widths of the two sub-rectangles side by side:

$$
W_{chip} = \max(w_a, w_b) + \max(w_c, w_d) = 4 + 5 = 9 \; \mu m
$$

**Critical path in VCG:**

$$
H_{left} = h_a + h_b = 3 + 2 = 5 \; \mu m
$$

$$
H_{right} = h_c + h_d = 4 + 1 = 5 \; \mu m
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Chip width and height:**

$$
W_{chip} = 9 \; \mu m, \quad H_{chip} = \max(5, 5) = 5 \; \mu m
$$

**Total chip area:**

$$
A_{chip} = 9 \times 5 = 45 \; \mu m^2
$$

**Sum of module areas:**

$$
\sum A_i = (4)(3) + (3)(2) + (5)(4) + (2)(1) = 12 + 6 + 20 + 2 = 40 \; \mu m^2
$$

**Dead-space percentage:**

$$
D = \frac{A_{chip} - \sum A_i}{A_{chip}} \times 100 = \frac{45 - 40}{45} \times 100 = 11.11\%
$$

**Final layout dimensions:** $9 \; \mu m \times 5 \; \mu m$ with 11.11% unused area.

*(Marks: Part (a) 1 (structure) + 2 (HCG) + 2 (VCG) + 2 (labels) = 7; Part (b) 2 (W) + 2 (H) + 1.5 (Area) + 1.5 (Dead space) = 7)*

---

### ✅ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **three basic operations** (Horizontal, Vertical, Skewed) used to combine sub-floorplans in a slicing structure. Show how each operation modifies the constraint graphs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A floorplan has 5 modules with widths and heights as listed. The slicing tree is `(V (H a b) (V (H c d) e))`. **Determine the optimal placement and compute total area and dead space.**  
Module data: `a=(2,4)`, `b=(3,2)`, `c=(4,3)`, `d=(1,2)`, `e=(3,3)`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Horizontal Composition (H):**  
Two sub-floorplans $F_1$ and $F_2$ are placed side by side. The new chip width is $W = W_1 + W_2$, and height is $H = \max(H_1, H_2)$.  
- In HCG: an edge $R_1 \to R_2$ is added with label `H`.  
- In VCG: no new edges between $F_1$ and $F_2$; existing internal vertical edges are preserved.

**2. Vertical Composition (V):**  
Two sub-floorplans $F_1$ (top) and $F_2$ (bottom) are stacked. The new chip height is $H = H_1 + H_2$, and width is $W = \max(W_1, W_2)$.  
- In VCG: an edge $R_1 \to R_2$ is added with label `V`.  
- In HCG: existing horizontal edges remain; cross-composition edges are not added.

**3. Skewed Composition (S):**  
A vertical cut is performed, but one of the sub-rectangles is **flipped** (rotated 180°) before being placed.  
- Edge labels in one of the constraint graphs are **reversed** in direction.  
- This operation enables generation of floorplans that are not purely H/V compositions.

**Constraint graph effects summary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{H-op:} \quad & \text{HCG: } R_1 \xrightarrow{H} R_2; \quad \text{VCG: unchanged crosswise} \\
\text{V-op:} \quad & \text{VCG: } R_1 \xrightarrow{V} R_2; \quad \text{HCG: unchanged crosswise} \\
\text{S-op:} \quad & \text{one graph: edges reversed in one sub-tree}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The tree `(V (H a b) (V (H c d) e))` means:

- Top-level `V` cut → top subtree `(H a b)`, bottom subtree `(V (H c d) e)`.
- Top subtree `H` cut → `a` left of `b`.
- Bottom subtree `V` cut → top `(H c d)`, bottom `e`.
- `(H c d)` → `c` left of `d`.

**Step 1: Compute sub-floorplan dimensions.**

Top subtree `(H a b)`:

$$
W_{top} = w_a + w_b = 2 + 3 = 5 \; \mu m, \quad H_{top} = \max(4,2) = 4 \; \mu m
$$

Lower-top subtree `(H c d)`:

$$
W_{lt} = 4 + 1 = 5 \; \mu m, \quad H_{lt} = \max(3,2) = 3 \; \mu m
$$

Bottom subtree `(V (H c d) e)`:

$$
W_{bot} = \max(W_{lt}, w_e) = \max(5, 3) = 5 \; \mu m, \quad H_{bot} = H_{lt} + h_e = 3 + 3 = 6 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 2: Combine top and bottom via top-level `V`.**

$$
W_{chip} = \max(W_{top}, W_{bot}) = \max(5,5) = 5 \; \mu m
$$

$$
H_{chip} = H_{top} + H_{bot} = 4 + 6 = 10 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 3: Area and dead space.**

$$
A_{chip} = 5 \times 10 = 50 \; \mu m^2
$$

$$
\sum A_i = (2)(4) + (3)(2) + (4)(3) + (1)(2) + (3)(3) = 8 + 6 + 12 + 2 + 9 = 37 \; \mu m^2
$$

$$
D = \frac{50 - 37}{50} \times 100 = 26\%
$$

**Layout summary (μm):** Top row `[a | b]` spanning width 5 and height 4. Lower section: `[c | d]` on top of `e`, total height 6, width 5.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing HCG and VCG edges** — students often draw left-right arrows in VCG or top-down arrows in HCG. Always double-check the **direction of the cut**: `H` cut produces a *side-by-side* relationship (HCG), `V` cut produces *top-bottom* (VCG).
> 2. **Forgetting to take the max during composition** — when stacking two sub-rectangles horizontally, the *chip width* is the **sum**; the *chip height* is the **max**. Reversing this is a common 2-mark error.
> 3. **Ignoring Polish expression syntax** — a slicing tree must have exactly `n-1` internal nodes for `n` modules. A non-binary tree is invalid.
> 4. **Mixing `H` and `V` labels in wrong graph** — every edge in the *Horizontal Constraint Graph* corresponds to an `H`-cut event, not a `V` cut.
> 5. **Not computing dead space** — KTU questions frequently ask for the dead-space %, and skipping it costs 1.5 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **slicing floorplan** can be recursively bisected by `H` and `V` cuts — every slicing floorplan corresponds to a **slicing tree** (binary tree with `H`/`V` labels and `n` leaves for `n` modules).
- The **Constraint Graph** of a slicing floorplan has two parts: the **Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)** and the **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)** — both are directed acyclic graphs (DAGs).
- **Three fundamental operations** on sub-floorplans: Horizontal composition `H`, Vertical composition `V`, Skewed composition `S`. They update the HCG and VCG by adding/reversing labeled edges.
- The **longest path** in HCG gives the **critical width**; the longest path in VCG gives the **critical height**. The chip area is the product of these two.
- **Catalan numbers** $C_n = \frac{(2n)!}{n!(n+1)!}$ give the upper bound on the number of distinct slicing trees (and hence distinct constraint graphs) for `n` modules.
- **Polish expression** is a compact, postfix notation for representing the slicing tree: operands = module names, operators = `H`, `V`, `S`.
- Constraint graphs are **most useful for slicing floorplans**; for non-slicing (mosaic) floorplans, alternative representations are used (Sequence Pair, BSG, O-Tree, TCG).
- **Key KTU formula:** $A_{chip} = W_{chip} \times H_{chip}$; $D = \frac{A_{chip} - \sum A_i}{A_{chip}} \times 100$.
- **Always use `\vert` or `\mid`** for absolute value in LaTeX within markdown tables to avoid breaking the table layout.
- **Examiner's tip:** In 14-mark questions, clearly show: (1) the tree, (2) the two constraint graphs, (3) the dimension calculations, and (4) the area + dead-space. Missing any one of these costs at least 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
