topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sequence Pair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 – Sequence Pair: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the VLSI physical design flow, **floorplanning** is the step that decides the positions of macro blocks, I/O pads, and IP cores on the chip canvas *before* detailed placement. One of the most celebrated **topology representations** used during floorplanning is the **Sequence Pair (SP)**, introduced by **Murata, Nakatake, Fujiyoshi, and Kajitani (1995)**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (Board-Examiner Approved)**
> A **Sequence Pair (SP)** for a set of $n$ rectangular modules $\{M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_n\}$ is an **ordered pair of permutations** $(\Gamma^+, \Gamma^-)$ of the module names. The relative positional relationship between any two modules $a$ and $b$ is **uniquely and completely** determined by their order of appearance in these two sequences.

The positional relationships encoded by a Sequence Pair are:

| Order in $\Gamma^+$ | Order in $\Gamma^-$ | Implied Geometric Relation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $a$ before $b$ | $a$ before $b$ | **$a$ is to the LEFT of $b$** |
| $a$ after $b$ | $a$ after $b$ | **$a$ is to the RIGHT of $b$** |
| $a$ before $b$ | $a$ after $b$ | **$a$ is ABOVE $b$** |
| $a$ after $b$ | $a$ before $b$ | **$a$ is BELOW $b$** |

> [!NOTE] **Why Sequence Pair?**
> A Sequence Pair is a **combinational, unambiguous, and one-to-one** representation of the **relative placement topology**. It represents **all 8 mosaic floorplans** (slicing + non-slicing + L-shaped, etc.) without geometric redundancy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Wedding Seating Lists"

Imagine a wedding reception where the host creates **two independent lists of guests**: List-A (north-to-south order) and List-B (west-to-east order).

* If guest **"Anu"** appears **before** guest **"Ben"** in **BOTH** lists → Anu sits on the **left** of Ben.
* If Anu appears **before** Ben in List-A but **after** Ben in List-B → Anu sits **above** Ben.
* If Anu appears **after** Ben in **BOTH** lists → Anu sits on the **right** of Ben.
* If Anu appears **after** Ben in List-A but **before** Ben in List-B → Anu sits **below** Ben.

By cross-referencing the two lists, the exact seating grid of every guest is fixed. **That is precisely what a Sequence Pair does for VLSI modules on a chip canvas.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE] **Key Engineering Metrics**
> * **Number of distinct Sequence Pairs for $n$ modules:** $(n!)^2$
> * **Search space size:** $(n!)^2$ candidates (finite, deterministic, but exponential — hence stochastic search / SA is used).
> * **Constraint Graph Type:** Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) — must be acyclic for a valid floorplan.
> * **Evaluation Complexity:** $O(n^2)$ for a single SP, dominated by longest-path computation in DAGs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Module 4 – Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Sequence Pair Encodes a Floorplan

A Sequence Pair is a **pure topological encoding**. The conversion from $(\Gamma^+, \Gamma^-)$ to a placement proceeds as follows:

1. **Step 1 – Identify Pairs:** For every ordered module pair $(a, b)$, extract their relative order in $\Gamma^+$ and $\Gamma^-$.
2. **Step 2 – Classify Relation:** Apply the 4-case table to determine $a$'s relation to $b$ (left/right/above/below).
3. **Step 3 – Build Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG):**
   * Node for each module + **source** $s_h$ and **sink** $t_h$.
   * Edge $(a, b)$ in HCG iff $a$ is LEFT of $b$ (weight = width of $b$).
   * Edges from $s_h \to$ every module (weight = width of module).
   * Edges from every module $\to t_h$ (weight = **0**).
4. **Step 4 – Build Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG):** Mirror construction with ABOVE/BELOW relations and module **heights**.
5. **Step 5 – Compute Critical Paths:**
   * Width of floorplan $W_{fp}$ = **Longest path in HCG**.
   * Height of floorplan $H_{fp}$ = **Longest path in VCG**.
6. **Step 6 – Recover Coordinates:** The longest path distances in HCG give the $x$-coordinates of the left edges of modules; distances in VCG give $y$-coordinates of the bottom edges.

> [!IMPORTANT] **The "Why" Behind DAG Constraint Graphs**
> A valid floorplan must be **non-overlapping and feasible**. If the HCG/VCG had a cycle, it would imply $a$ is to the left of $b$ AND $b$ to the left of $a$ — a physical impossibility. Hence the acyclicity is a **necessary and sufficient feasibility condition**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE] **⚡ KTU Board Examiner's Formula Table — Memorize Before Exam**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Sequence Pair | $SP = (\Gamma^+, \Gamma^-)$ | Two permutations of $n$ module IDs |
| 2 | Search space size | $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert = (n!)^2$ | For $n$ modules |
| 3 | Number of slicing floorplans | $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\dbinom{2(n-1)}{n-1}$ (Catalan-like) | Less than total SP space |
| 4 | Width of floorplan | $W_{fp} = \text{LongestPath}(HCG)$ | Length in unit grid |
| 5 | Height of floorplan | $H_{fp} = \text{LongestPath}(VCG)$ | Length in unit grid |
| 6 | Edge weight in HCG | $w(a,b) = w_b$ (width of $b$) | Only if $a$ left of $b$ |
| 7 | Edge weight in VCG | $w(a,b) = h_b$ (height of $b$) | Only if $a$ above $b$ |
| 8 | Source edge weight (HCG) | $w(s_h, m_i) = w_i$ | Width of $m_i$ |
| 9 | Sink edge weight (HCG) | $w(m_i, t_h) = 0$ | Termination |
| 10 | Total area penalty | $A = W_{fp} \times H_{fp}$ | To be **minimized** |
| 11 | Topological relation rule | $(\Gamma^+, \Gamma^-)$ cross-order $\Rightarrow$ vertical | Same-order $\Rightarrow$ horizontal |
| 12 | Evaluation time | $O(n^2)$ per SP | Bellman-Ford or topological sort |
| 13 | Constraint graph type | Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) | Cycle = infeasible floorplan |

**⚠️ LATEX SAFETY:** All absolute-value / cardinal operators use `\vert` or `\mid` to protect markdown table parsing (e.g., $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sequence Pair is the **cornerstone representation** for:

* **Macro-cell floorplanning** in standard-cell and mixed-mode ASIC design (e.g., 28nm, 16nm, 7nm SoCs).
* **FPGA architecture mapping** for heterogeneous reconfigurable fabrics.
* **3D-IC stacking optimization** (where SP is extended to **Triple Sequence Pair** or **T-trees**).
* **Multiprocessor System-on-Chip (MPSoC)** floorplanning for block placement.
* Used in industrial-grade tools as a **topology generator** before feeding to a placer (e.g., KraftWerk, Capo, OpenROAD use SP/TCG hybrids).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Module 4 – Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing an SP and Decoding to Floorplan

**Problem (KTU-style):**
Given 4 rectangular modules:
* $A$ — width $w_A = 2$, height $h_A = 4$
* $B$ — width $w_B = 3$, height $h_B = 2$
* $C$ — width $w_C = 4$, height $h_C = 3$
* $D$ — width $w_D = 2$, height $h_D = 5$

with the Sequence Pair:
$$\Gamma^+ = (A, B, C, D)$$
$$\Gamma^- = (C, A, D, B)$$

**Task:** (i) Decode all 6 pairwise relations. (ii) Draw HCG and VCG. (iii) Compute the minimum width and height of the floorplan. (iv) Find $x, y$ coordinates.

### (i) Pairwise Relation Decoding

We apply the **4-case cross-reference rule** to all $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ pairs.

**Step 1 — Look up positions in both sequences:**

| Pair | In $\Gamma^+$ | In $\Gamma^-$ | Same Order? | Relation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(A, B)$ | A before B | A before B | ✅ Yes | **A LEFT of B** |
| $(A, C)$ | A before C | A after C | ❌ Cross | **A BELOW C** |
| $(A, D)$ | A before D | A before D | ✅ Yes | **A LEFT of D** |
| $(B, C)$ | B before C | B after C | ❌ Cross | **B BELOW C** |
| $(B, D)$ | B before D | B after D | ❌ Cross | **B BELOW D** |
| $(C, D)$ | C before D | C before D | ✅ Yes | **C LEFT of D** |

**Step 2 — Verify logical consistency (anti-cycles):**
* $A$ left of $B$ AND $A$ left of $D$ AND $C$ left of $D$: no cycle in horizontal direction.
* $A$ below $C$, $B$ below $C$, $B$ below $D$: no cycle in vertical direction.
* Hence the SP is **feasible**.

### (ii) Building the HCG and VCG

**Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG):** We add an edge $u \to v$ if $u$ is LEFT of $v$, with edge weight = width of $v$.

```
    A (w=2)         C (w=4)         D (w=2)
     │               │               │
  s_h ●───2───►●───3───►●───2───►●───0───► t_h
                B (w=3)
```

**Explicit edge list (with weights):**

$$\begin{aligned}
s_h \xrightarrow{2} A, \quad s_h \xrightarrow{3} B, \quad s_h \xrightarrow{4} C, \quad s_h \xrightarrow{2} D \\
A \xrightarrow{3} B \quad \text{(A left of B, weight } w_B=3) \\
A \xrightarrow{2} D \quad \text{(A left of D, weight } w_D=2) \\
C \xrightarrow{2} D \quad \text{(C left of D, weight } w_D=2) \\
B \xrightarrow{0} t_h, \quad A \xrightarrow{0} t_h, \quad C \xrightarrow{0} t_h, \quad D \xrightarrow{0} t_h
\end{aligned}$$

**Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG):** We add an edge $u \to v$ if $u$ is ABOVE $v$ (equivalently, $v$ below $u$).
* Equivalently: edge $u \to v$ if $u$ is below target — but standard convention: edge from $u$ to $v$ when $u$ is **above** $v$, weight = height of $v$.
* From decoding: $A$ below $C$ → edge $C \to A$, weight $h_A = 4$.
* $B$ below $C$ → edge $C \to B$, weight $h_B = 2$.
* $B$ below $D$ → edge $D \to B$, weight $h_B = 2$.

### (iii) Longest Path Computation — Width $W_{fp}$

**Step 3 — Compute $x$-coordinates using longest path in HCG (topological order: $s_h, A, B, C, D, t_h$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
x(s_h) &= 0 \\
x(A) &= x(s_h) + w(s_h, A) = 0 + 2 = 2 \\
x(B) &= \max\bigl(x(s_h) + 3, \; x(A) + 3\bigr) = \max(3, 5) = 5 \\
x(C) &= x(s_h) + 4 = 0 + 4 = 4 \\
x(D) &= \max\bigl(x(s_h) + 2, \; x(A) + 2, \; x(C) + 2\bigr) = \max(2, 4, 6) = 6 \\
x(t_h) &= \max(x(A), x(B), x(C), x(D)) = 6
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{W_{fp} = x(t_h) = 6}$$

### (iv) Longest Path Computation — Height $H_{fp}$

**Step 4 — Compute $y$-coordinates using longest path in VCG:**

$$\begin{aligned}
y(s_v) &= 0 \\
y(C) &= y(s_v) + 0 = 0 \quad \text{(no incoming from } s_v \text{ except source)} \\
y(D) &= y(s_v) + 0 = 0 \\
y(A) &= y(C) + h_A = 0 + 4 = 4 \\
y(B) &= \max\bigl(y(C) + h_B, \; y(D) + h_B\bigr) = \max(0 + 2, \; 0 + 2) = 2 \\
y(t_v) &= \max(y(A), y(B)) + 0 = \max(4, 2) = 4
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{H_{fp} = y(t_v) = 4}$$

### (v) Final Layout Coordinates (bottom-left origin)

| Module | $x_{\text{left}}$ | $y_{\text{bottom}}$ | Width | Height |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 |
| B | 5 | 2 | 3 | 2 |
| C | 4 | 0 | 4 | 3 |
| D | 6 | 0 | 2 | 5 |

**Total bounding area:**
$$A = W_{fp} \times H_{fp} = 6 \times 4 = \mathbf{24} \text{ grid units}^2$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs

| Criterion | Sequence Pair | Slicing Tree | Corner Block List (CBL) | B*-tree |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Expressiveness** | All 8 mosaic ✅ | Slicing only ❌ | All mosaic ✅ | All mosaic ✅ |
| **Redundancy** | Very high (8x) | Low | Low | Low |
| **Constraint graph** | HCG + VCG | Horizontal/Vertical slices | Corner block lists | Tree traversal |
| **Search space** | $(n!)^2$ | Catalan $S(n)$ | $O(n! \cdot 2^{2n})$ | $O(n! \cdot 2^{2n-2})$ |
| **Decoding time** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Best use case** | Research / optimality | Quick heuristic | Industrial | Place & route hybrid |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Module 4 – Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: End-to-End SP → Floorplan Decoding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT MODULES with w_i, h_i"] --> B["SEQUENCE PAIR GENERATOR Gamma+ Gamma-"]
    B --> C["PAIRWISE RELATION LOOKUP TABLE"]
    C --> D1["HORIZONTAL CONSTRAINT GRAPH HCG"]
    C --> D2["VERTICAL CONSTRAINT GRAPH VCG"]
    D1 --> E1["LONGEST PATH ALGORITHM O n squared"]
    D2 --> E2["LONGEST PATH ALGORITHM O n squared"]
    E1 --> F["COMPUTE x-coordinates Wfp"]
    E2 --> G["COMPUTE y-coordinates Hfp"]
    F --> H["MERGED FLOORPLAN LAYOUT"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["COST FUNCTION A equals Wfp times Hfp"]
    I --> J{"FEASIBLE? DAG ACYCLIC"}
    J -->|YES| K["ACCEPT FLOORPLAN"]
    J -->|NO| L["DISCARD - CYCLE DETECTED"]
    B -.SIMULATED ANNEAL.-> M["NEW SP CANDIDATE"]
    M --> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pairwise Relation Decoder (4-Case Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["INPUT PAIR Module A Module B"] --> Q["LOOKUP posAplus posBplus posAminus posBminus"]
    Q --> R{"A BEFORE B in BOTH sequences?"}
    R -->|YES| S1["A LEFT OF B"]
    R -->|NO| T{"A AFTER B in BOTH sequences?"}
    T -->|YES| S2["A RIGHT OF B"]
    T -->|NO| U{"A BEFORE B in Gamma+ AFTER B in Gamma-?"}
    U -->|YES| S3["A ABOVE B"]
    U -->|NO| S4["A BELOW B"]
    S1 --> OUT["EMIT EDGE to HCG"]
    S2 --> OUT
    S3 --> V1["EMIT EDGE to VCG"]
    S4 --> V1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: HCG/VCG Structure Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SH["SOURCE s_h"] -->|w_A = 2| MA["MODULE A"]
    SH -->|w_B = 3| MB["MODULE B"]
    SH -->|w_C = 4| MC["MODULE C"]
    SH -->|w_D = 2| MD["MODULE D"]
    MA -->|w_B = 3| MB
    MA -->|w_D = 2| MD
    MC -->|w_D = 2| MD
    MA -->|0| TH["SINK t_h"]
    MB -->|0| TH
    MC -->|0| TH
    MD -->|0| TH
```

> [!NOTE] **Why the Sink Edges Have Weight 0**
> Sink edges carry zero weight because the **floorplan width/height is determined entirely by the source-to-sink longest path through module edges**. The sink is a *bookkeeping* node to make the graph a single-source-single-sink (SSSP) DAG suitable for topological sorting.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Module 4 – KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**Define a Sequence Pair. How is the relative position of two modules determined from the pair $(\Gamma^+, \Gamma^-)$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Sequence Pair (SP), proposed by Murata et al. (1995), is a pair of permutations $(\Gamma^+, \Gamma^-)$ of $n$ rectangular module names. The relative position of two modules $a$ and $b$ is decided by their order in the two sequences: (1) if $a$ is before $b$ in **both** $\Gamma^+$ and $\Gamma^-$, $a$ is to the **left** of $b$; (2) if $a$ is after $b$ in both, $a$ is to the **right**; (3) if $a$ is before $b$ in $\Gamma^+$ but after $b$ in $\Gamma^-$, $a$ is **above** $b$; (4) the reverse cross-order implies $a$ is **below** $b$. (1 mark definition + 2 marks for 4-case rule)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is the total number of distinct Sequence Pairs for $n$ modules? Why is the search space considered a limitation in industrial-scale floorplanning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The total number of distinct Sequence Pairs is $(n!)^2$, since each of the two permutations can be any of the $n!$ orderings. For $n = 50$ macros, this is astronomically large ($\approx 10^{64}$), making exhaustive search infeasible. Therefore, heuristic methods like **Simulated Annealing (SA)**, **genetic algorithms**, or **B*-tree search** are used to explore the SP space in industrial tools. (1 mark formula + 2 marks limitation + heuristic mention)

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. *You must attempt EITHER Question A OR Question B.*

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Consider 4 modules with the following dimensions (width × height):**
$$A: 3 \times 4, \quad B: 2 \times 5, \quad C: 4 \times 2, \quad D: 3 \times 3$$

**Given the Sequence Pair:** $\Gamma^+ = (B, A, C, D)$ and $\Gamma^- = (A, C, B, D)$.

#### (a) Decode all 6 pairwise positional relations and build the Horizontal Constraint Graph. Compute the floorplan width. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pairwise decoding (3 marks):**

| Pair | $\Gamma^+$ Order | $\Gamma^-$ Order | Relation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $(A, B)$ | A after B | A before B | **A below B** |
| $(A, C)$ | A before C | A before C | **A left of C** |
| $(A, D)$ | A before D | A before D | **A left of D** |
| $(B, C)$ | B before C | B after C | **B above C** |
| $(B, D)$ | B before D | B before D | **B left of D** |
| $(C, D)$ | C before D | C after D | **C below D** |

**Step 2 — HCG construction (2 marks):** Edges for LEFT relations:
* $A \to C$ (weight $w_C = 4$), $A \to D$ (weight $w_D = 3$)
* $B \to D$ (weight $w_D = 3$)
* $s_h \to A (3), s_h \to B (2), s_h \to C (4), s_h \to D (3)$

**Step 3 — Longest path in HCG (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
x(s_h) &= 0,\ x(A) = 3,\ x(B) = 2,\ x(C) = \max(0+4, 3+4) = 7, \\
x(D) &= \max(0+3, 3+3, 2+3) = 6, \quad W_{fp} = \max(3,2,7,6) = 7
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{W_{fp} = 7 \text{ units}}$$

#### (b) Build the Vertical Constraint Graph, compute the floorplan height, and determine the $(x, y)$ coordinates of each module's bottom-left corner. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — VCG edges for ABOVE relations (3 marks):**
* $B \to A$ (weight $h_A = 4$): A is below B
* $B \to C$ (weight $h_C = 2$): C is below B
* $D \to C$ (weight $h_C = 2$): C is below D

**Step 2 — Longest path in VCG (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
y(s_v) &= 0, \ y(B) = 0, \ y(D) = 0, \\
y(A) &= y(B) + 4 = 4, \ y(C) = \max(y(B)+2, y(D)+2) = 2 \\
H_{fp} &= \max(y(A), y(B), y(C), y(D)) = 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Module coordinates (2 marks):**

| Module | $x_L$ | $y_B$ | Width | Height |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 3 | 4 | 3 | 4 |
| B | 2 | 0 | 2 | 5 |
| C | 7 | 2 | 4 | 2 |
| D | 6 | 0 | 3 | 3 |

**Total bounding area:** $A = 7 \times 4 = \mathbf{28 \text{ units}^2}$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**For a set of 5 modules with the Sequence Pair:** $\Gamma^+ = (E, A, B, D, C)$ and $\Gamma^- = (B, D, A, C, E)$.

**Module dimensions:** $A: 2 \times 3$, $B: 4 \times 2$, $C: 3 \times 4$, $D: 2 \times 3$, $E: 3 \times 2$.

#### (a) Enumerate all 10 pairwise relations. Identify which pairs must be connected in the HCG and VCG. Comment on whether the SP is feasible. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Position lookups (1 mark):**

| Module | Position in $\Gamma^+$ | Position in $\Gamma^-$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 2 | 3 |
| B | 3 | 1 |
| C | 5 | 4 |
| D | 4 | 2 |
| E | 1 | 5 |

**Step 2 — 10 pairwise relations (4 marks):**

| Pair | Same order? | Relation |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| (A, B) | A before B in $\Gamma^+$, A after B in $\Gamma^-$ | **A above B** |
| (A, C) | A before C in both | **A left of C** |
| (A, D) | A before D in $\Gamma^+$, A after D in $\Gamma^-$ | **A above D** |
| (A, E) | A after E in $\Gamma^+$, A before E in $\Gamma^-$ | **A below E** |
| (B, C) | B before C in $\Gamma^+$, B after C in $\Gamma^-$ | **B above C** |
| (B, D) | B before D in $\Gamma^+$, B after D in $\Gamma^-$ | **B above D** |
| (B, E) | B after E in $\Gamma^+$, B before E in $\Gamma^-$ | **B below E** |
| (C, D) | C after D in $\Gamma^+$, C after D in $\Gamma^-$ | **C right of D** |
| (C, E) | C after E in $\Gamma^+$, C before E in $\Gamma^-$ | **C below E** |
| (D, E) | D after E in $\Gamma^+$, D before E in $\Gamma^-$ | **D below E** |

**Step 3 — HCG and VCG edges (1 mark):**
* HCG edges (LEFT relations): $A \to C$.
* VCG edges (ABOVE relations): $A \to B$, $A \to D$, $B \to C$, $B \to D$, $E \to A$, $E \to B$, $E \to C$, $E \to D$.
* (Also "right of" and "below" are mirror — encoded by absence of edge in opposite direction.)

**Step 4 — Feasibility (1 mark):**
* HCG: only one edge $A \to C$ → trivially acyclic.
* VCG: source $E$ dominates all others; no back-edges from children to ancestors → DAG.
* **SP is FEASIBLE.** ✅

#### (b) Compute the minimum width and height of the floorplan, and find the bounding area. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — HCG longest path (3 marks):**
Edges: $s_h \xrightarrow{2} A, s_h \xrightarrow{4} B, s_h \xrightarrow{3} C, s_h \xrightarrow{2} D, s_h \xrightarrow{3} E$, plus $A \xrightarrow{3} C$.

$$\begin{aligned}
x(s_h) &= 0 \\
x(E) &= 0 + 3 = 3, \quad x(A) = 0 + 2 = 2, \quad x(B) = 0 + 4 = 4, \\
x(D) &= 0 + 2 = 2, \quad x(C) = \max(0+3, 2+3) = 5
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{W_{fp} = 5 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 2 — VCG longest path (3 marks):**
Edges: $s_v \to$ all, $A \xrightarrow{2} B$, $A \xrightarrow{3} D$, $B \xrightarrow{4} C$, $B \xrightarrow{3} D$, $E \xrightarrow{3} A$, $E \xrightarrow{2} B$, $E \xrightarrow{4} C$, $E \xrightarrow{3} D$.

$$\begin{aligned}
y(s_v) &= 0,\ y(E) = 0 \\
y(A) &= y(E) + 3 = 3 \\
y(B) &= \max(y(s_v) + 2, y(A) + 2) = \max(2, 5) = 5 \\
y(D) &= \max(y(s_v)+3, y(A)+3, y(B)+3) = \max(3, 6, 8) = 8 \\
y(C) &= \max(y(s_v)+4, y(B)+4) = \max(4, 9) = 9
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{H_{fp} = 9 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 3 — Bounding area (1 mark):**
$$A = W_{fp} \times H_{fp} = 5 \times 9 = \mathbf{45 \text{ units}^2}$$

---

> [!WARNING] **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Cross-order mistake (–2 marks):** Students often confuse "above" and "below" by mixing up the $\Gamma^+$ and $\Gamma^-$ roles. **Trick:** Same order = horizontal (left/right), cross order = vertical (above/below). Lock this in.
> 2. **Edge-weight confusion (–2 marks):** Edge weight in HCG is the width of the **target** module, not the source. Same applies to VCG with height of the target.
> 3. **Forgetting source/sink nodes (–1 mark):** Many students draw only module-to-module edges and forget the **$s$ and $t$** nodes. Without them, you cannot compute the floorplan's bounding dimensions.
> 4. **Not verifying acyclicity:** A question that gives a *cyclic* SP expects you to declare it **infeasible**. Failing to check loses easy marks.
> 5. **Topological-order errors:** For longest path, always process nodes in topological order; doing it ad-hoc gives wrong $W_{fp}/H_{fp}$.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Exam)**
> * ✅ Sequence Pair = **two permutations** $(\Gamma^+, \Gamma^-)$ of $n$ module names.
> * ✅ **Same order** in both → **horizontal** relation (left / right).
> * ✅ **Cross order** between the two → **vertical** relation (above / below).
> * ✅ Total number of SPs = $\mathbf{(n!)^2}$.
> * ✅ Decoding uses **HCG** (width DAG) and **VCG** (height DAG).
> * ✅ $W_{fp} = $ **longest path in HCG**, $H_{fp} = $ **longest path in VCG**.
> * ✅ Edge weight in HCG = **width of destination module**; in VCG = **height of destination module**.
> * ✅ A valid SP must yield **acyclic** HCG and VCG (else infeasible).
> * ✅ Sequence Pair represents **all 8 mosaic floorplans** (slicing + non-slicing).
> * ✅ Time complexity of evaluation: $O(n^2)$ using **topological sort + Bellman-Ford**.
> * ✅ Industrial use: Simulated Annealing over SP space; converted to a placer (e.g., KraftWerk, FastPlace).
> * ✅ Limitations: high redundancy (up to **8×**) and exponential search space → use heuristics.
> * ✅ Competing representations to know for KTU: **Slicing Tree, B\*-Tree, Corner Block List (CBL), TCG**.
> * ✅ Coordinates are recovered as **cumulative longest distances** from source in HCG ($x$) and VCG ($y$).
> * ✅ Total area = $W_{fp} \times H_{fp}$ — to be **minimized** during SA optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Routing: Terminology and Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Global Routing: Terminology and Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Global Routing** is the fundamental planning phase of VLSI physical design routing, in which the chip's routing region is partitioned into a coarse grid of **Global Routing Cells (GCells)**, and a routing solution is constructed that assigns every net to a sequence of GCells connecting its source and sink pins, **without** committing to exact track assignments within each cell. It serves as the critical intermediate stage between **Global Placement** (which determines where cells/modules are placed) and **Detailed Routing** (which assigns wires to specific metal layers and tracks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Global Routing is the problem of determining the geometric path of interconnections between cells without actually committing to specific routing tracks. It addresses the question — "Through which global routing cells should each net pass?" — using a coarser representation of the chip's routing resources.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are a **travel planner** mapping airline routes between 20 Indian cities.

- **Detailed Routing** = deciding which *exact seat* (which metal track) each passenger sits in on every flight.
- **Global Routing** = deciding which *airports* (GCells) each flight path passes through, in what order.
- **Global Routing Cells (GCells)** = the airports on the route map.
- **Capacity** = the number of runways each airport can handle.
- **Congestion** = a "traffic jam" when too many flights try to land at one airport.

Global Routing is therefore the *macro-level "road map"*, while Detailed Routing is the *lane-level "GPS directions"*. A bad Global Routing plan makes Detailed Routing fail (just as a bad air-route plan cannot be fixed by better seat assignments).

### 1.3 Why Global Routing Exists

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Problem:**
> Modern VLSI chips contain **hundreds of millions of nets** spread across **multiple metal layers**, with routing capacities measured in millions of tracks per layer. Solving the *complete* routing problem in one shot is computationally intractable (NP-hard). Global Routing **decomposes** this massive problem into tractable sub-problems by:
> 1. Reducing spatial resolution (chip → GCells).
> 2. Performing topology selection (Steiner trees, path planning).
> 3. Estimating congestion, wire length, and timing **before** committing to detailed geometry.

### 1.4 Position of Global Routing in the Physical Design Flow

The placement of Global Routing in the standard KTU reference flow:

```
Netlist → Partitioning → Floorplanning → Placement → Global Routing → Detailed Routing → DRC/LVS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Inputs to Global Routing:**
> - Post-placement cell locations (`.def` coordinates).
> - Netlist with pin positions.
> - Technology file (metal layers, capacities, design rules).
>
> **Outputs from Global Routing:**
> - Wire length estimates.
> - Congestion map / capacity utilization profile.
> - Feedthrough assignments.
> - Steiner tree topologies per net.
> - Cross-layer via distribution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Routing Concepts

Global Routing terminology forms a *strict hierarchy*. Each higher-level concept builds on lower-level building blocks. Below is the structured breakdown:

#### **Level 1 — Atomic Entities**

| Term | Definition | KTU-Mandated Notation |
|------|------------|------------------------|
| **Pin** | A logical connection terminal of a cell/module, treated as a 0-dimensional point (or small rectangle) in the routing grid. | $p_i = (x_i, y_i)$ |
| **Net** | A set of pins $\{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k\}$ that must be electrically connected. | $n_i$ |
| **Netlist** | The complete set of nets $\{n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_N\}$ defining circuit connectivity. | $\mathcal{N}$ |

#### **Level 2 — Topology Entities**

| Term | Definition | Key Property |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Steiner Tree** | A minimum-cost tree spanning all pins of a net, *possibly* using extra "Steiner" branch points not coincident with pins. | Optimal topology for wire length; NP-hard to compute exactly. |
| **Rectilinear Steiner Tree (RST)** | A Steiner tree constrained to **only horizontal and vertical edges** (Manhattan geometry). | Standard for IC routing due to preferred-direction metal layers. |
| **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** | A minimum-cost tree that spans pins using *only* pin-to-pin direct connections (no Steiner points). | Lower bound: $L_{\text{MST}} \leq L_{\text{RST}}$; ratio $L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}$. |
| **Routing Tree** | The realized topology for a net after global routing. | Often a Steiner tree or MST variant. |
| **Steiner Point** | A degree-3 branch vertex added to reduce total wire length. | $k$-pin net needs at most $(k-2)$ Steiner points. |

#### **Level 3 — Spatial-Grid Entities**

| Term | Definition | KTU Diagram Element |
|------|------------|----------------------|
| **Routing Region** | The 2-D physical area on a chip where routing is permitted. | A rectangle bounded by cell rows / IO pads. |
| **Global Cell (GCell)** | A coarse rectangular sub-region of the routing region, the **basic unit of global routing**. | Numbered $(g_x, g_y)$. |
| **GCell Grid / Routing Graph $G(V,E)$** | A graph whose vertices represent GCells and whose edges represent adjacency (shared boundary). | $|V| = m \cdot n$ for an $m \times n$ grid. |
| **Capacity $c(e)$** | The number of routing tracks (wires) that can physically cross edge $e$. | Integer, technology-dependent. |
| **Demand $d(e)$** | The number of nets assigned to pass through edge $e$. | Computed from routing solution. |
| **Congestion** | The condition $d(e) > c(e)$ (over-demand). | Detected via **Congestion Map**. |
| **Utilization $u(e)$** | The ratio of demand to capacity on edge $e$. | $u(e) = d(e)/c(e)$; must remain $\leq 1$ ideally. |

#### **Level 4 — Cross-Layer and Timing Entities**

| Term | Definition | Used In |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Feedthrough** | A reserved routing channel traversing a cell's interior to allow signals to pass *over* the cell. | Standard-cell row-based design. |
| **Switchbox** | A routing region bounded on **all four sides** by fixed pins (no open edges). | Channel routing / detailed routing. |
| **Channel** | A routing region bounded by **two parallel rows of cells** with open ends. | Predecessor concept of switchbox. |
| **Critical Path** | The signal path with the smallest positive timing slack. | Drives timing-driven routing. |
| **Elmore Delay** | A first-order RC delay model for RC tree networks. | Timing estimation during routing. |
| **Critical Sink** | A sink pin on the critical path requiring tight delay control. | Targeted during routing. |

#### **Level 5 — Cost Function Entities**

| Term | Definition | Expression |
|------|------------|------------|
| **Wire Length Cost $C_W$** | Penalty proportional to total wire length used. | $C_W = \sum_{n \in \mathcal{N}} L(n)$ |
| **Congestion Cost $C_C$** | Penalty when utilization exceeds thresholds. | $C_C = \sum_{e \in E} f(u(e))$ |
| **Timing Cost $C_T$** | Penalty for violating delay constraints on critical paths. | $C_T = \max(0, D_{\text{slack}}^{\text{min}})$ |

The **total routing objective** (for KTU exam problems) is:
$$
\mathcal{O} = \alpha C_W + \beta C_C + \gamma C_T
$$
where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma \geq 0$ are weighting coefficients (usually $\alpha = 1$, $\beta$ increases iteratively).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Definition | Expression | Condition / Unit |
|----------------------|------------|------------------|
| **Steiner tree lower bound (MST)** | $L_{\text{MST}} = \min \sum_{(i,j)\in T} w_{ij}$ | $w_{ij}$ = Manhattan distance |
| **Rectilinear Steiner Tree bound** | $L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}$ | Equality holds for two-pin nets |
| **Steiner-point count for k-pin net** | $\vert S \vert \leq k - 2$ | Non-degenerate tree |
| **GCell grid edge count** | $\vert E \vert = m(n-1) + n(m-1)$ | Rectangular $m \times n$ grid |
| **Wire segment length** | $L_{\text{seg}} = \text{pitch} \times \text{number of GCells crossed}$ | In $\mu m$ |
| **Bin capacity (rectangular bin)** | $c = \min(c_h, c_v)$ | $c_h, c_v$: horizontal & vertical track capacities |
| **Utilization on edge $e$** | $u(e) = d(e)/c(e)$ | Dimensionless; $u > 1$ ⇒ overflow |
| **Elmore delay (tree root to leaf)** | $D_{\text{elmore}} = \sum_{k} R_{ik} C_k$ | $R_{ik}$: shared path resistance |
| **Rent's rule wiring demand** | $T = t \, B^p$ | $T$: external pins, $B$: gates, $p$: Rent exponent |
| **Average wire length (Davis law)** | $\bar{L} = \kappa \cdot (\text{die area})$ | Used in preliminary routing estimation |
| **Routing demand (per GCell edge)** | $d(e) = \sum_{n} \mathbb{1}_{\{e \in \text{path}(n)\}}$ | Sum across nets using $e$ |
| **Total via count (multi-layer)** | $V = \sum_{l=1}^{L-1} \sum_{e_l \in E_l} v(e_l)$ | $v(e_l)$: via demand between layer $l$ and $l+1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-Tested KTU Formulas (3-Mark Quick Recall):**
> 1. **Rectilinear Steiner Tree upper bound:** $L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}$
> 2. **GCell demand definition:** $d(e) = \sum_{n} \mathbb{1}_{\{e \in \text{path}(n)\}}$
> 3. **Rent's rule:** $T = t \cdot B^p$ where $p \in [0.5, 0.75]$ for typical chips.
> 4. **Steiner points:** at most $k - 2$ for a $k$-pin net.
> 5. **Utilization overflow indicator:** $u(e) > 1 \Rightarrow$ infeasible detailed routing.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Rectilinear Steiner Tree Bound

We prove the classical result $L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}$, which is **frequently tested** in KTU 14-mark problems.

**Setup:** Consider a $k$-pin net with pin set $\mathcal{P} = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k\}$ in Manhattan geometry. Let $T_{\text{MST}}$ be the unique minimum spanning tree over $\mathcal{P}$ using Manhattan distances, and $T_{\text{RST}}$ be the optimal rectilinear Steiner tree.

#### **Step 1 — Construct $T_{\text{MST}}$ (Hanan grid property)**

The rectilinear MST lies on the **Hanan grid**, the grid formed by drawing horizontal and vertical lines through every pin in $\mathcal{P}$. The number of Hanan grid intersection points is bounded by:
$$
N_{\text{Hanan}} \leq \binom{k}{2}
$$
since each pair of pins contributes at most one intersection (and Hanan proved the optimal tree is contained in this grid).

#### **Step 2 — Upper bound MST length using Hanan property**

The total length of the MST can be bounded by summing Manhattan distances along tree edges. For a tree on $k$ vertices:
$$
L_{\text{MST}} \leq \sum_{(i,j) \in T_{\text{MST}}} (\vert x_i - x_j \vert + \vert y_i - y_j \vert)
$$

#### **Step 3 — Construct a Steiner tree from $T_{\text{MST}}$**

For each degree-3 (or higher) vertex in $T_{\text{MST}}$ that is **not a pin**, we can re-route edges to introduce a Steiner point, reducing total length. Specifically, for any three edges of Manhattan length $a, b, c$ meeting at a non-pin vertex, the triangle inequality gives:
$$
L_{\text{Steiner local}} = a + b + c \;\;\geq\;\; \text{Steiner length} \;\geq\; a + b + c - \min(a,b,c)
$$
The savings is at most $\min(a,b,c)$ per such vertex.

#### **Step 4 — Summing savings over all Steiner insertions**

The total savings $\Delta$ over all Steiner insertions satisfies:
$$
\Delta \leq \tfrac{1}{2} L_{\text{MST}}
$$
This is a classical result by Hwang (1976). Therefore:
$$
L_{\text{RST}} = L_{\text{MST}} - \Delta \;\geq\; L_{\text{MST}} - \tfrac{1}{2} L_{\text{MST}} = \tfrac{1}{2} L_{\text{MST}}
$$

#### **Step 5 — Upper bound derivation**

Equivalently, rearranging gives:
$$
L_{\text{MST}} - L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{1}{2} L_{\text{MST}}
$$
Adding $L_{\text{RST}}$ to both sides:
$$
L_{\text{MST}} \leq L_{\text{RST}} + \tfrac{1}{2} L_{\text{MST}}
$$
$$
\therefore \;\;\frac{1}{2} L_{\text{MST}} \leq L_{\text{RST}}
$$
$$
\boxed{\,L_{\text{RST}} \geq \tfrac{1}{2} L_{\text{MST}} \;\;\text{ AND }\;\; L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}\,}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Utilization Computation

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A 4×3 GCell grid has the following capacities (tracks) on each edge. Three nets are routed as shown:

| Edge | Capacity $c(e)$ | Net 1 uses | Net 2 uses | Net 3 uses |
|------|:---------------:|:----------:|:----------:|:----------:|
| $e_1$ | 5 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| $e_2$ | 4 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| $e_3$ | 6 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| $e_4$ | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

**Step 1 — Compute demand $d(e)$ on each edge.**
$$
d(e_1) = 1 + 1 + 0 = 2
$$
$$
d(e_2) = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2
$$
$$
d(e_3) = 0 + 1 + 1 = 2
$$
$$
d(e_4) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute utilization $u(e) = d(e)/c(e)$.**
$$
u(e_1) = 2/5 = 0.40 \;\;\;\text{(safe)}
$$
$$
u(e_2) = 2/4 = 0.50 \;\;\;\text{(safe)}
$$
$$
u(e_3) = 2/6 \approx 0.33 \;\;\;\text{(safe)}
$$
$$
u(e_4) = 3/3 = 1.00 \;\;\;\text{(AT CAPACITY — critical edge)}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify congestion.**
Since $u(e_4) = 1.00$ with zero margin, edge $e_4$ is **saturated**. Any additional net using $e_4$ would cause overflow. The global router must reroute one of the nets to free capacity.

### 3.3 Rent's Rule Derivation (Wiring Demand Estimation)

**Problem Statement:** Estimate the number of external I/O pins $T$ for a sub-circuit containing $B$ gates, given Rent exponent $p$ and technology constant $t$.

**Formula:**
$$
T = t \cdot B^{p}
$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Empirical foundation (Rent, 1971).**
Rent observed that for partitioned circuits, the number of external connections of a module grows as a power law of its internal gate count. Empirically $p \approx 0.5$ for memory and $p \approx 0.75$ for random logic.

**Step 2 — Wiring demand prediction.**
The total number of nets in a circuit block is approximately:
$$
T = t \cdot B^p
$$
where $t$ is the average number of pins per gate (typically 2–5).

**Step 3 — Example computation.**
For $B = 100{,}000$ gates, $t = 4$, $p = 0.7$:
$$
T = 4 \cdot (10^{5})^{0.7} = 4 \cdot 10^{3.5} = 4 \cdot 3162.28
$$
$$
\boxed{\,T \approx 12{,}649 \;\text{external pins}\,}
$$

This result is used by global routers to **size channels and feedthroughs** during floorplan-aware global routing.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the following terms with respect to global routing: (i) GCell, (ii) Routing graph $G(V,E)$, (iii) Capacity $c(e)$.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) GCell (Global Cell):** A GCell is the fundamental unit of spatial decomposition used in global routing. The chip's routing region is partitioned into a coarse grid of rectangular sub-regions called GCells, each typically spanning several placement sites. Each GCell is treated as a single vertex $v \in V$ of the routing graph, with the assumption that any wire entering a GCell can be assigned to any available track within it without detailed geometry.

**(ii) Routing Graph $G(V, E)$:** The routing graph is the discrete mathematical representation used by the global router. Its vertex set $V$ corresponds to GCells, and its edge set $E$ corresponds to pairs of GCells that share a common boundary through which a wire may be routed. The graph abstracts away fine geometry, allowing combinatorial optimization.

**(iii) Capacity $c(e)$:** The capacity $c(e)$ of an edge $e \in E$ is the maximum number of routing tracks (wires) that can physically cross the boundary represented by $e$. It is a function of (a) the available metal layers, (b) the minimum pitch of those layers, (c) the width of the GCell edge, and (d) any blockages. Formally:
$$
c(e) = \sum_{l=1}^{L} c_l(e)
$$
where $c_l(e)$ is the per-layer track count and $L$ is the number of available routing layers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**State and explain Rent's rule. What is the typical range of the Rent exponent $p$ for random-logic and memory blocks?**

**Model Answer:**

**Rent's Rule** is an empirical power-law relationship between the number of external I/O pins $T$ of a sub-circuit and the number of gates $B$ contained within it:
$$
T = t \cdot B^{p}
$$
where $t$ is the average number of pins per gate and $p \in (0,1)$ is the **Rent exponent**.

**Typical Range of $p$:**
- For **random-logic** blocks: $p \approx 0.5$ to $0.75$ (commonly $p \approx 0.6$).
- For **memory** blocks (highly regular): $p \approx 0.1$ to $0.5$ (commonly $p \approx 0.12$).

**Engineering Utility:** Rent's rule is used during global routing to (a) predict the **wiring demand** of a block before placement, (b) size channels and feedthroughs during floorplanning, and (c) estimate routing congestion as a function of gate count. A higher $p$ implies a greater wiring demand, which in turn requires greater routing resources.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Define a **Rectilinear Steiner Tree (RST)** for a multi-pin net in Manhattan geometry. Prove that for any $k$-pin net, the optimal RST length satisfies $L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 4-pin net with pin coordinates (in $\mu m$): $p_1 = (0,0)$, $p_2 = (10, 0)$, $p_3 = (10, 10)$, $p_4 = (0, 10)$. Compute:
(i) The Manhattan MST length.
(ii) The optimal Rectilinear Steiner Tree length.
(iii) Verify the bound from part (a). **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Definition and Bound Proof**

**Definition:** A **Rectilinear Steiner Tree (RST)** for a $k$-pin net $\mathcal{P} = \{p_1, \ldots, p_k\}$ is a tree $T_{\text{RST}}$ spanning $\mathcal{P}$ together with additional **Steiner points** $S = \{s_1, \ldots, s_m\}$, such that:
1. Every edge of $T_{\text{RST}}$ is either horizontal or vertical (Manhattan-geometry constraint).
2. The total length $L_{\text{RST}} = \sum_{e \in T_{\text{RST}}} \vert e \vert$ is **minimized** over all such trees.
3. All vertices in $T_{\text{RST}}$ have degree 1 (pins), 3, or 4 (Steiner points); no degree-2 Steiner points are permitted (they are absorbed).

**Proof of $L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}$:**

**Step 1:** The MST $T_{\text{MST}}$ on $\mathcal{P}$ with Manhattan edge weights has length $L_{\text{MST}}$ and lies entirely within the **Hanan grid** of $\mathcal{P}$ (Hanan, 1966).

**Step 2:** Apply **Steiner point insertion** at every non-pin degree-$\geq 3$ vertex of $T_{\text{MST}}$. By Hwang's lemma (1976), the maximum wire-length reduction achievable by introducing a Steiner point at a degree-3 junction of edges of lengths $a, b, c$ is at most $\min(a, b, c)$.

**Step 3:** The total length reduction over the entire tree is bounded:
$$
\Delta = L_{\text{MST}} - L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{1}{2} L_{\text{MST}}
$$
This is because at each Steiner insertion, at most half of the local MST length is removed, and summing across the tree yields the bound.

**Step 4:** Rearranging:
$$
L_{\text{RST}} \leq L_{\text{MST}} - \tfrac{1}{2} L_{\text{MST}} = \tfrac{1}{2} L_{\text{MST}}
$$
The **trivial lower bound** is $L_{\text{RST}} \geq L_{\text{MST}}$ (since MST is the minimum spanning *tree*, while RST is the minimum spanning *Steiner* tree — Steiner trees can be shorter, never longer). Combining:
$$
\boxed{\,L_{\text{MST}} \geq L_{\text{RST}} \;\;\text{ AND }\;\; L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Proof:** Students often confuse the direction of the inequality. Remember: **Steiner trees are SHORTER than MSTs** (they add extra junction points to reduce length). The bound $L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}$ is an **upper bound** on how much longer MST can be, not on RST length.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

**Given:** $p_1 = (0,0)$, $p_2 = (10,0)$, $p_3 = (10,10)$, $p_4 = (0,10)$.

**(i) Manhattan MST length:**

The Manhattan distances between pin pairs are:

| Pair | Manhattan Distance |
|------|:------------------:|
| $p_1 - p_2$ | $10 + 0 = 10$ |
| $p_1 - p_3$ | $10 + 10 = 20$ |
| $p_1 - p_4$ | $0 + 10 = 10$ |
| $p_2 - p_3$ | $0 + 10 = 10$ |
| $p_2 - p_4$ | $10 + 10 = 20$ |
| $p_3 - p_4$ | $10 + 0 = 10$ |

Using Kruskal's algorithm, the smallest edges that connect all 4 pins without cycles are:
- $p_1 - p_2$ (length 10)
- $p_3 - p_4$ (length 10)
- $p_1 - p_4$ (length 10) **or** $p_2 - p_3$ (length 10)

Total MST length:
$$
\boxed{L_{\text{MST}} = 10 + 10 + 10 = 30 \;\mu m}
$$

**(ii) Optimal Rectilinear Steiner Tree length:**

Introduce **one Steiner point** $s = (10, 0)$ at the center — actually a better choice is $s$ such that we form an "H" shape. The optimal RST for a square 4-pin configuration is the **"H-tree" pattern**, with Steiner points at the midpoints.

Place Steiner points at $s_1 = (5, 0)$ and $s_2 = (5, 10)$ (midpoints of horizontal edges). The RST edges are:
- $p_1 \to s_1$: length $5$
- $p_2 \to s_1$: length $5$
- $s_1 \to s_2$: length $10$
- $s_2 \to p_3$: length $5$
- $s_2 \to p_4$: length $5$

Total RST length:
$$
\boxed{L_{\text{RST}} = 5 + 5 + 10 + 5 + 5 = 30 \;\mu m}
$$

(Note: For a perfect rectangle, the H-tree achieves length equal to MST because the MST is already optimal — this is a special case.)

**(iii) Verify the bound:**

The bound states $L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}$.

Computing:
$$
\tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}} = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot 30 = 45 \;\mu m
$$
$$
L_{\text{RST}} = 30 \;\mu m \;\leq\; 45 \;\mu m \;\; \checkmark
$$
The bound is satisfied (in fact, $L_{\text{RST}} = L_{\text{MST}}$ for this symmetric case).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Define the **utilization $u(e)$** of an edge in the global routing graph. Explain how the **congestion map** is computed, and describe what action a global router takes when $u(e) > 1$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3×3 GCell grid has edge capacities (in tracks): all horizontal edges have capacity $c_h = 6$ and all vertical edges have capacity $c_v = 4$. A global router assigns 5 nets, each crossing the grid from left to right. The 5 nets share the central horizontal edge. Compute:
(i) Demand on the central horizontal edge.
(ii) Utilization on that edge.
(iii) Comment on the feasibility for detailed routing. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Utilization, Congestion Map, and Router Action**

**Utilization $u(e)$:** The utilization of an edge $e \in E$ in the global routing graph is the ratio of the **demand** $d(e)$ on that edge (number of nets routed through $e$) to its **capacity** $c(e)$ (maximum number of tracks available):
$$
\boxed{u(e) = \frac{d(e)}{c(e)}}
$$

It is a **dimensionless** quantity with the following interpretation:
- $u(e) < 1$: The edge has spare capacity; routing is safe.
- $u(e) = 1$: The edge is saturated; no further nets can be assigned.
- $u(e) > 1$: The edge is **over-subscribed**; the assignment is infeasible.

**Congestion Map Computation:**

A congestion map is a 2-D heatmap of the GCell grid where each edge $e$ is colored by $u(e)$. It is computed as:

**Step 1:** For each net $n \in \mathcal{N}$, determine its assigned path (sequence of edges) in $G(V,E)$.

**Step 2:** Initialize $d(e) = 0$ for all edges.

**Step 3:** For each net, increment $d(e)$ by 1 for every edge $e$ on its path:
$$
d(e) = \sum_{n \in \mathcal{N}} \mathbb{1}_{\{e \in \text{path}(n)\}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $u(e) = d(e)/c(e)$ for each edge.

**Step 5:** Render the map with colors: green ($u < 0.7$), yellow ($0.7 \leq u < 1.0$), red ($u \geq 1.0$).

**Router Action on Overflow $u(e) > 1$:**

When congestion is detected, the global router takes the following actions:
1. **Identify offending nets**: Find the nets routed through the saturated edge.
2. **Rip-up and reroute**: Remove one or more offending nets from their current path.
3. **Reroute via alternative topology**: Reassign the ripped-up net to a longer but less congested path (cost increases).
4. **Iterate**: Repeat the rip-up-and-reroute procedure (this is called **sequential routing** or **negotiation-based routing**, e.g., PathFinder algorithm).
5. **If persistent overflow**: Apply layer reassignment, vias adjustment, or invoke congestion-aware placement adjustment (post-routing ECO).

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

**Given:** 3×3 GCell grid. Central horizontal edge has $c_h = 6$ tracks. Five nets share this edge.

**(i) Demand on central edge:**
Since all 5 nets are routed through the central horizontal edge:
$$
d_{\text{central}} = 5
$$
$$
\boxed{d_{\text{central}} = 5 \text{ nets}}
$$

**(ii) Utilization on central edge:**
$$
u_{\text{central}} = \frac{d_{\text{central}}}{c_h} = \frac{5}{6}
$$
$$
\boxed{u_{\text{central}} \approx 0.833}
$$

**(iii) Feasibility for Detailed Routing:**

Since $u_{\text{central}} = 0.833 < 1$, the central edge is **NOT congested** and has 1 spare track (capacity 6, used by 5 nets, 1 free). However:

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Concern:** With only **1 spare track** (16.7% margin), this routing solution is at the **boundary of feasibility**. Detailed routing must use the remaining track efficiently, and any cross-layer via conflicts or pin-access congestion could push the route into infeasibility. The recommended margin is $u(e) \leq 0.8$ for **safe** detailed routing. Hence, this assignment is **technically feasible but risky**; the router should consider rerouting at least one net to a longer path to improve the safety margin.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Examiner Notes):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $u(e)$ direction:** Writing $u(e) = c(e)/d(e)$ — this is the *inverse* of utilization. Always **demand over capacity**, never the reverse.
>
> 2. **Steiner vs. MST bound direction:** Stating $L_{\text{MST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{RST}}$ instead of $L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}$. Remember: **Steiner trees are SHORTER or EQUAL to MST**; the $\tfrac{3}{2}$ factor is an upper bound on how much shorter.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Manhattan geometry:** Applying Euclidean Steiner Tree results in IC routing questions — IC routing is **rectilinear** due to preferred-direction metal layers.
>
> 4. **Capacity vs. Demand confusion:** Capacity is a **physical limit** of the edge (technology-defined); Demand is a **routing-decision variable** (router-assigned). They are NOT interchangeable.
>
> 5. **Ignoring layer assignment:** In multi-layer chips, capacity is **summed across layers**. A 2-layer routing has approximately $2\times$ capacity of single-layer.
>
> 6. **Skipping GCell grid reasoning:** The number of edges $|E|$ is not $m \times n$ but $m(n-1) + n(m-1)$ — students often confuse this.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Global Routing Terminology:**

- **GCell**: The atomic unit of routing region; rectangular coarse partition.
- **Routing Graph $G(V,E)$**: Abstract graph whose vertices are GCells and edges are inter-GCell boundaries.
- **Capacity $c(e)$**: Technology-imposed maximum number of wires on edge $e$.
- **Demand $d(e)$**: Number of nets assigned to edge $e$.
- **Utilization $u(e) = d(e)/c(e)$**: Must be $\leq 1$; ideally $\leq 0.8$.
- **Congestion**: Condition $u(e) > 1$; remedied by rip-up-and-reroute.
- **Net**: Set of pins needing electrical connection.
- **Pin**: 0-D terminal of a cell.
- **MST**: Minimum spanning tree (uses only pin-to-pin edges).
- **RST**: Rectilinear Steiner Tree (uses extra Steiner points).
- **Bound**: $L_{\text{RST}} \leq \tfrac{3}{2} L_{\text{MST}}$.
- **Steiner Points**: At most $k - 2$ extra points for a $k$-pin net.
- **Hanan Grid**: Grid of lines through all pins; MST lies within it.
- **Channel**: Open-ended routing region between two cell rows.
- **Switchbox**: Closed (4-sided pin) routing region.
- **Feedthrough**: Reserved path through a cell's interior for routing.
- **Rent's Rule**: $T = t \cdot B^p$; $p \approx 0.5$–$0.75$.
- **Elmore Delay**: First-order RC delay model; $D = \sum_k R_{ik} C_k$.
- **Critical Path**: Path with minimum timing slack.
- **Timing-Driven Routing**: Optimization considering delay, not just length.
- **Congestion-Driven Routing**: Optimization considering capacity limits.
- **Objective Function**: $\mathcal{O} = \alpha C_W + \beta C_C + \gamma C_T$.
- **GCell Grid Edge Count**: $\vert E \vert = m(n-1) + n(m-1)$ for $m \times n$ grid.
- **Routing Region**: 2-D area where routing is permitted.
- **Multi-Layer Capacity**: Sum across layers $c(e) = \sum_l c_l(e)$.
- **Feasibility Margin**: Aim for $u(e) \leq 0.8$ for safe detailed routing.

> **Memory Aid:** *"In Global Routing, we ask the question **WHICH GCells** each net passes through — not **WHICH TRACKS** — that is the job of Detailed Routing."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization Goals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization Goals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Computer-Aided Design (CAD) for VLSI**, *Optimization Goals* are the mathematically formulated, quantitative objectives that a design automation algorithm (partitioning, floorplanning, placement, routing, compaction, or logic synthesis tool) seeks to **minimize** or **satisfy within a constraint bound** during the physical implementation of an integrated circuit. These objectives translate a designer's intent — such as "make the chip small," "make it fast," or "make it low-power" — into **scalar cost functions** or **constraint satisfaction problems** that the underlying engine (graph-based, mathematical-programming, or heuristic) can iteratively improve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Optimization goals in VLSI CAD are the set of conflicting design objectives — primarily area, delay (timing), and power — along with secondary goals such as wirelength, congestion, signal integrity, and manufacturability — that physical design algorithms attempt to optimize, either singly or as a weighted multi-objective function, subject to design rules and constraints."*

Formally, an optimization problem in VLSI CAD is posed as:

$$
\min_{x \in \mathcal{F}} \; \Phi(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot \phi_i(x)
$$

subject to a set of inequality and equality constraints $g_j(x) \le 0$ and $h_m(x) = 0$, where:
- $x$ is the design variable vector (cell positions, net routings, gate sizes, etc.)
- $\mathcal{F}$ is the feasible design space
- $\phi_i(x)$ are individual objective functions
- $w_i$ are user-defined scalar weights with $\sum w_i = 1$

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are an **architect designing a 50-floor apartment building** on a fixed plot of land in downtown Manhattan. You have three competing demands from your client:

1. **Maximum usable floor area** (similar to *Area optimization* in VLSI — packing more logic into less silicon).
2. **Minimum elevator travel time** (similar to *Delay/Timing optimization* — signals should travel quickly across the chip).
3. **Minimum electricity bill for lighting & HVAC** (similar to *Power optimization* — every microwatt saved is significant at chip scale).

You cannot optimize all three simultaneously. If you pack in more rooms (area), the building gets taller and the elevator trip (delay) increases. If you add a fast express elevator (timing), you need extra shaft space (area) and more power for the motor (power). This is the **classic "you cannot have your cake and eat it too"** problem of VLSI design.

> [!NOTE]
> **The VLSI Designer's Iron Triangle:**
> Just like project management has the *Iron Triangle* (Scope, Time, Cost), the VLSI designer faces an **ADP Triangle** — **A**rea, **D**elay, **P**ower. Optimization goals are simply the sides of this triangle made mathematically tractable so that a CAD tool can chase them on a computer.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in Optimization Goals

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Modern Nodes) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chip Area | $A$ | $1 - 700$ | mm² |
| Critical Path Delay | $T_{cp}$ | $0.05 - 5$ | ns |
| Total Wirelength | $L_{WL}$ | $0.01 - 50$ | m (per chip) |
| Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn}$ | $0.1 - 150$ | W |
| Static / Leakage Power | $P_{leak}$ | $0.01 - 30$ | W |
| Routing Congestion | $\rho$ | $0 - 1$ (overflow > 1) | dimensionless |
| Operating Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | $0.6 - 1.2$ | V |
| Threshold Voltage | $V_{th}$ | $0.2 - 0.4$ | V |
| Clock Frequency | $f_{clk}$ | $0.1 - 5$ | GHz |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Whenever a numerical question on optimization appears, the examiner expects students to **state the unit explicitly**. A 1-mark loss in numerical problems often comes from omitting the unit (e.g., writing "2.5" instead of "2.5 mW" or "2.5 GHz").

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Optimization Goals

Optimization goals in VLSI CAD are classified along **three axes**:

### Axis 1 — Primary vs. Secondary Goals
- **Primary Goals** (the "big three"): **Area**, **Delay**, **Power**.
- **Secondary Goals** (KTU frequently tests these): **Wirelength**, **Congestion**, **Signal Integrity**, **Thermal Uniformity**, **Yield / Manufacturability**, **Antenna Effect minimization**.

### Axis 2 — Hard Constraints vs. Soft Objectives
- **Hard Constraints** (must be satisfied; otherwise design is invalid): DRC (Design Rule Check) clean, no open circuits, no shorts, latch-up free.
- **Soft Objectives** (improved but not strictly required): PPA targets, wirelength reduction, congestion smoothing.

### Axis 3 — Single-Objective vs. Multi-Objective
- **Single-Objective**: Optimize one metric (e.g., area-only compaction).
- **Multi-Objective**: Optimize a weighted sum or use Pareto-front techniques (e.g., simultaneous area + delay + power).

---

## 2.2 The Three Primary Optimization Goals — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 AREA Optimization

**Why it matters:** Silicon area directly determines the **cost per die** and **manufacturing yield** ($Y \propto e^{-A \cdot D_0}$, where $D_0$ is the defect density).

**How it is formulated:**

$$
\Phi_{area}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot h_i \cdot b_i
$$

where $h_i$ and $b_i$ are the height and width of cell $i$, and $N$ is the number of cells.

**Sub-goals inside area optimization:**
- Cell area minimization (transistor sizing)
- Dead-space minimization in floorplanning
- Channel routing density reduction
- White-space reduction in placement

---

### 2.2.2 DELAY (Timing) Optimization

**Why it matters:** The clock period $T_{clk}$ must exceed the critical path delay $T_{cp}$; otherwise the chip fails functionally. With modern GHz-scale designs, every picosecond matters.

**How it is formulated — Elmore Delay Model (the KTU favorite):**

For an RC tree network, the Elmore delay from source $s$ to node $i$ is:

$$
T_{ED}(s \to i) = \sum_{e_k \in P(s,i)} R_{e_k} \cdot C_{L_k}
$$

where $P(s,i)$ is the unique path from $s$ to $i$ in the RC tree, $R_{e_k}$ is the resistance of edge $k$, and $C_{L_k}$ is the total capacitance of the **downstream subtree** rooted at edge $k$.

**Sub-goals inside delay optimization:**
- Critical path delay minimization
- Clock skew minimization
- Setup and hold time satisfaction
- Interconnect delay reduction (wire sizing, buffer insertion)

---

### 2.2.3 POWER Optimization

**Why it matters:** Power determines battery life (mobile), packaging cost, and reliability (thermal).

**How it is formulated — Dynamic + Static Power:**

$$
P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} + V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 to 1)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency
- $I_{leak}$ = leakage current (subthreshold + gate)

**Sub-goals inside power optimization:**
- Dynamic power reduction (clock gating, voltage scaling)
- Leakage power reduction (power gating, multi-$V_{th}$)
- Switching activity minimization

---

## 2.3 Secondary Optimization Goals

| Goal | Formulation Sketch | Physical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Wirelength** | $\sum_{e \in E} l_e$ | Less metal → less parasitic R, C, less congestion |
| **Congestion** | $\max_g \rho_g$ where $\rho_g = U_g / C_g$ | Overflow ⇒ unroutable design |
| **Signal Integrity** | minimize $V_{noise} / V_{DD}$ | Crosstalk, IR drop, ground bounce |
| **Thermal** | minimize $\max_{xy} T(x,y)$ | Hot spots reduce lifetime |
| **Yield / DFM** | maximize $Y = e^{-A \cdot D_0}$ | Defects per unit area |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following equations — they appear in 80%+ of KTU ESE numericals on this module.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ | Dynamic power per gate | Power optimization numericals |
| 2 | $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Static power | Leakage-aware design |
| 3 | $T_{ED} = \sum_{e_k \in P(s,i)} R_{e_k} C_{L_k}$ | Elmore delay in RC tree | Delay estimation |
| 4 | $\tau = R_{on} \cdot C_L$ | First-order gate delay | Logical effort |
| 5 | $L_{HP} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D_0 \cdot A_{chip}}$ | Rent's rule — wirelength estimate | Architecture-level |
| 6 | $Y = e^{-A \cdot D_0}$ | Poisson yield model | Manufacturability |
| 7 | $\Phi(x) = \sum w_i \phi_i(x)$ | Weighted cost function | Multi-objective |
| 8 | $\rho_g = U_g / C_g$ | Congestion ratio per bin $g$ | Routing optimization |
| 9 | $T_{cp} \le T_{clk} - T_{skew} - T_{setup}$ | Timing constraint | Performance verification |
| 10 | $E = P \cdot T = C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per transition (per switching event) | Low-power design |

**Boundary Conditions / Limits:**
- $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ (bounded by definition)
- $V_{DD} > V_{th}$ (otherwise the transistor is always off)
- $T_{cp} > 0$ (causality)
- $\sum w_i = 1$ for normalized weighted objectives
- $C_g > U_g$ for a *routable* bin; $\rho_g > 1$ indicates overflow

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, these optimization goals are tackled at **every stage** of the physical design flow:

| Stage | Primary Goal | Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Logic Synthesis | Area + Delay | Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus |
| Partitioning | Cut-size + Delay across cut | hMetis, MLPart |
| Floorplanning | Area + Wirelength | Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2 |
| Placement | Wirelength + Timing + Congestion | Innovus, ICC2, OpenROAD |
| Clock Tree Synthesis | Skew + Latency | CCOpt, CTS in ICC2 |
| Routing | Wirelength + Congestion + SI | NanoRoute, Innovus |
| Compaction | Area (1-D / 2-D) | academic tools, Magic |
| ECO | Timing + Power closure | PrimeTime, Voltus |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dynamic Power from First Principles

The CMOS inverter draws current from $V_{DD}$ to ground **only during switching**, charging and discharging the load capacitance $C_L$.

**Step 1: Energy stored in capacitor per charge cycle**

During one low-to-high transition, charge $Q = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$ is transferred from supply to capacitor.

$$
E_{charge} = \int_0^{T} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 2: Energy dissipated in PMOS during charging**

Half of the energy from the supply is stored in $C_L$ and half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor (independent of $R_{on}$):

$$
E_{dissipated, PMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 3: Energy dissipated during discharge (high-to-low)**

The stored energy in $C_L$ is fully dissipated in the NMOS transistor:

$$
E_{dissipated, NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 4: Total energy dissipated per full switching cycle**

$$
E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 5: Average dynamic power**

If the gate switches $f_{clk}$ times per second and only a fraction $\alpha$ of cycles cause actual switching (the *switching activity*):

$$
P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot E_{cycle} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}}
$$

**Interpretation for optimization:** The power has a **quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$** and a **linear dependence on $C_L$ and $f_{clk}$**. Thus, **voltage scaling** is the most powerful knob (halving $V_{DD}$ reduces power by 4×). This is why *dynamic voltage frequency scaling (DVFS)* is the single most important power-optimization technique in modern SoCs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Elmore Delay for a Simple Two-Node RC Chain

Consider a chain: source node $s$ → resistor $R_1$ → node $i$ → capacitor $C_1$ to ground.

**Step 1: Identify the unique path**

The path from $s$ to $i$ contains exactly one edge: $e_1$ (with resistance $R_1$).

**Step 2: Compute the load capacitance "seen" by $e_1$**

The downstream subtree rooted at $e_1$ contains capacitor $C_1$. So:

$$
C_{L_1} = C_1
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Elmore formula**

$$
T_{ED}(s \to i) = \sum_{e_k \in P(s,i)} R_{e_k} C_{L_k} = R_1 \cdot C_1
$$

**Step 4: Result**

$$
\boxed{T_{ED}(s \to i) = R_1 \cdot C_1}
$$

This is the classical **RC time constant** of a single-pole system and is the 50%-delay point for a step input.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Elmore Delay for an L-Shaped 3-Node RC Tree

Consider a tree with source $s$, branch point $i$, and two leaf nodes $i_1$ and $i_2$:

- $R_1$ between $s$ and $i$
- $R_2$ between $i$ and $i_1$, with capacitance $C_2$ at $i_1$
- $R_3$ between $i$ and $i_2$, with capacitance $C_3$ at $i_2$

**Step 1: Path from $s$ to $i_1$** contains edges $e_1$ and $e_2$.

Load seen by $e_1$ = downstream subtree = $C_2 + C_3$
Load seen by $e_2$ = downstream subtree of $e_2$ = $C_2$

$$
T_{ED}(s \to i_1) = R_1 (C_2 + C_3) + R_2 \cdot C_2
$$

**Step 2: Path from $s$ to $i_2$** contains edges $e_1$ and $e_3$.

$$
T_{ED}(s \to i_2) = R_1 (C_2 + C_3) + R_3 \cdot C_3
$$

**Step 3: Critical path = max of all sink delays**

$$
\boxed{T_{cp} = \max \{ R_1(C_2+C_3) + R_2 C_2, \; R_1(C_2+C_3) + R_3 C_3 \}}
$$

**Optimization insight:** Both sink delays share the common term $R_1 (C_2 + C_3)$. The CAD tool's *timing-driven placement* therefore **minimizes $R_1$** by placing cells $i_1$ and $i_2$ *close* to the driver $s$ — directly tying **wirelength optimization** to **delay optimization**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Multi-Objective Cost Function Assembly

Suppose a placement tool must balance three goals: wirelength $L_{WL}$, congestion penalty $C_p$, and timing penalty $T_p$.

**Step 1: Define each sub-objective as a normalized scalar in $[0, 1]$**

Let:
- $\phi_1 = L_{WL}(x) / L_{WL}^{max}$
- $\phi_2 = C_p(x) / C_p^{max}$
- $\phi_3 = T_p(x) / T_p^{max}$

**Step 2: Assign designer weights** with $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$.

**Step 3: Combine into a single scalar cost**

$$
\Phi(x) = w_1 \phi_1(x) + w_2 \phi_2(x) + w_3 \phi_3(x)
$$

**Step 4: Apply constrained optimization** with $T_{cp} \le T_{budget}$

This is the textbook formulation the ESE examiner expects for any "formulate the optimization problem" question.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Goal Dominates at Which Stage?

| Design Stage | Dominant Goal | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Logic Synthesis | Delay + Area | Early estimate; area sets die cost |
| Partitioning | Cut-size + Cut-delay | Avoid critical paths across cut |
| Floorplanning | Area + Wirelength | Global view; pre-place estimation |
| Placement | Wirelength + Timing + Congestion | Most computationally intensive |
| Clock Tree | Skew + Latency | Determinism is mandatory |
| Global Routing | Congestion + Wirelength | Routability check |
| Detailed Routing | SI + DRC | Sign-off quality |
| Compaction | Area | Final 1-D layout tightening |
| ECO | Timing + Power closure | Last-mile PPA fix |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Strategy:** If a question asks "which goal is most important in placement?", the answer is the **combo of wirelength + timing + congestion**. A single-goal answer is a partial loss; the **interaction** between goals is what earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — The VLSI Optimization-Goals Triad (ADP Triangle)

```mermaid
graph TD
    areaNode["AREA OPTIMIZATION"]
    delayNode["DELAY OPTIMIZATION"]
    powerNode["POWER OPTIMIZATION"]
    
    areaNode -- "Trade off" --> delayNode
    delayNode -- "Trade off" --> powerNode
    powerNode -- "Trade off" --> areaNode
    
    areaNote["Silicon real estate / cost / yield"]
    delayNote["Clock period / critical path"]
    powerNote["Dynamic + leakage / battery life"]
    
    areaNode -.- areaNote
    delayNode -.- delayNote
    powerNode -.- powerNote
    
    note1["Smaller cells -> longer wires -> more delay"]
    note2["Faster gates -> more capacitance -> more power"]
    note3["Lower voltage -> slower transistors -> more delay"]
    
    areaNode -.-> note1
    delayNode -.-> note2
    powerNode -.-> note3
```

**Reading guide:** Each solid arrow denotes a *direct trade-off*. The dashed annotations (`note1`, `note2`, `note3`) describe the *causal mechanism* behind each trade-off — exactly the kind of "explain why" answer KTU expects.

---

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Hierarchical Classification of Optimization Goals

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["VLSI OPTIMIZATION GOALS"]
    
    primaryA["PRIMARY GOALS"]
    secondaryA["SECONDARY GOALS"]

    wireA["Wirelength"]

    siA["Signal Integrity"]

    rootA --> primaryA
    rootA --> secondaryA
    primaryA --> areaA
    primaryA --> delayA
    primaryA --> powerA
    secondaryA --> wireA
    secondaryA --> congA
    secondaryA --> siA
    secondaryA --> thermA
    secondaryA --> yieldA
```

---

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Optimization Flow Inside a CAD Engine (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA["Input: Netlist + Constraints"]
    parserA["Parser / Data Structure Builder"]
    modelA["Cost Function Model Phi of x"]
    solverA["Optimization Engine Iterative"]
    evalA["Evaluator of Area Delay Power"]
    feedbackA["Feedback: Updated x"]
    outputA["Optimized Layout"]
    
    inputA --> parserA
    parserA --> modelA
    modelA --> solverA
    solverA --> evalA
    evalA --> feedbackA
    feedbackA --> solverA
    evalA --> outputA
    
    annotSolverA["Engines: Simulated Annealing / Gradient Descent / ILP / Genetic Algorithm"]
    solverA -.- annotSolverA
    annotEvalA["Metrics: Elmore Delay, Wirelength HPWL, Switching Activity"]
    evalA -.- annotEvalA
```

**Reading guide:** This block diagram represents the **canonical inner loop** of every optimization-based CAD tool. The student's note for the exam: *"The solver proposes, the evaluator scores, the cost function guides."*

---

## 4.4 Figure 4 — Mapping of Optimization Goals to Physical-Design Stages

```mermaid
graph TD
    synthA["Logic Synthesis"]
    partA["Partitioning"]
    floorA["Floorplanning"]

    routeA["Routing"]
    compactA["Compaction"]
    ecoA["Engineering Change Order"]
    
    gA1["Area + Delay"]
    gA2["Cut-size + Cut-delay"]
    gA3["Area + Wirelength"]
    gA4["Wirelength + Timing + Congestion"]
    gA5["Skew + Latency"]
    gA6["Wirelength + Congestion + SI"]
    gA7["Area 1D 2D"]
    gA8["Timing + Power closure"]
    
    synthA -.- gA1
    partA -.- gA2
    floorA -.- gA3
    placeA -.- gA4
    ctsA -.- gA5
    routeA -.- gA6
    compactA -.- gA7
    ecoA -.- gA8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the three primary optimization goals in VLSI physical design. State the fundamental trade-off among them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three primary optimization goals are:

1. **Area ($A$)** — the silicon real estate occupied by the layout. Lower area implies lower cost per die and higher manufacturing yield.
2. **Delay ($D$ / $T_{cp}$)** — the propagation time of signals, bounded by the clock period. Lower delay means higher performance.
3. **Power ($P$)** — the electrical energy consumed per unit time, comprising dynamic and leakage components.

**Trade-off:** They are mutually conflicting. Reducing area by shrinking cell sizes increases wire resistance and capacitance, which *increases* delay and dynamic power. Reducing power by lowering $V_{DD}$ weakens transistor drive, *increasing* delay. Reducing delay by upsizing drivers increases switching capacitance, *increasing* dynamic power. Therefore, the CAD tool must *balance* them via a weighted cost function, not optimize any one in isolation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Write the Elmore delay expression for an RC tree and explain why it is widely used in VLSI timing optimization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a source node $s$ and sink node $i$ in an RC tree, the **Elmore delay** is:

$$
T_{ED}(s \to i) = \sum_{e_k \in P(s,i)} R_{e_k} \cdot C_{L_k}
$$

where $P(s,i)$ is the unique resistive path from $s$ to $i$, $R_{e_k}$ is the resistance of edge $e_k$, and $C_{L_k}$ is the total capacitance in the *downstream subtree* rooted at edge $e_k$.

**Why widely used:**
1. It is **closed-form and additive** — the delay of a path is a sum of $R \cdot C$ products, no differential equations to solve.
2. It is a **conservative upper bound** on the 50%-delay point of a step response, so it is safe for timing sign-off.
3. It is **differentiable and linear** in the resistance/capacitance variables, making it easy to plug into gradient-based optimizers used in placement and wire sizing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS gate. Using the result, explain two techniques by which a CAD tool can reduce the dynamic power of a circuit.** (7 marks)

**(b) For the RC tree shown below, compute the Elmore delay from source $s$ to sink $i_2$.** (7 marks)

```
RC Tree:
   s ---R1--- n ---R2--- i1
                 |
                 +--R3-- i2
                  (with C2 at i1, C3 at i2)
```
Values: $R_1 = 100\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 200\,\Omega$, $R_3 = 300\,\Omega$, $C_2 = 5\,\text{pF}$, $C_3 = 10\,\text{pF}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

In a CMOS inverter switching at frequency $f_{clk}$, the load capacitance $C_L$ is charged to $V_{DD}$ through the PMOS and discharged to ground through the NMOS.

Energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ per charge cycle: $E_{supplied} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$.

Energy stored in $C_L$ after charging: $E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.

Energy dissipated in PMOS during charging: $E_{PMOS} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.

Energy dissipated in NMOS during discharge: $E_{NMOS} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.

Total energy per *full* switching cycle: $E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^2$.

If only a fraction $\alpha$ of clock cycles cause actual transitions (switching activity factor), the average dynamic power is:

$$
\boxed{P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}}
$$

**Two CAD techniques (3 marks — 1.5 each):**

1. **Voltage Scaling (DVFS):** The CAD tool assigns different supply voltages to different functional blocks based on their timing slack. Reducing $V_{DD}$ from $1.0$ V to $0.7$ V reduces dynamic power by $\approx 51\%$, since $P_{dyn} \propto V_{DD}^2$. Tools like Synopsys PrimeTime and Cadence Voltus implement this through voltage islands.

2. **Clock Gating:** The CAD tool inserts gating logic at the clock port of idle registers/blocks, suppressing spurious transitions ($\alpha$ drops to near zero). This reduces the switching activity term $\alpha$ directly. Industry tools such as Synopsys Power Compiler and Cadence Genus synthesize clock-gating cells automatically when an enable signal is detected.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the path from $s$ to $i_2$.
The path contains edges $e_1$ and $e_3$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the downstream capacitance seen by each edge.

- Edge $e_1$ ($R_1$): downstream subtree includes both $C_2$ and $C_3$, so $C_{L_1} = C_2 + C_3 = 5 + 10 = 15\,\text{pF}$.
- Edge $e_3$ ($R_3$): downstream subtree includes only $C_3$, so $C_{L_3} = C_3 = 10\,\text{pF}$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the Elmore formula.

$$
T_{ED}(s \to i_2) = R_1 \cdot C_{L_1} + R_3 \cdot C_{L_3}
$$

$$
T_{ED}(s \to i_2) = (100)(15 \times 10^{-12}) + (300)(10 \times 10^{-12})
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Evaluate numerically.

$$
T_{ED}(s \to i_2) = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} + 3.0 \times 10^{-9} = 4.5 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{ED}(s \to i_2) = 4.5\,\text{ns}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) Define the optimization goal of wirelength minimization as used in VLSI placement. Explain Half-Perimeter Wirelength (HPWL) and its role as a placement cost function.** (7 marks)

**(b) A designer wants to balance area, delay, and power with weights $w_A = 0.5$, $w_D = 0.3$, $w_P = 0.2$. Two candidate placements $X$ and $Y$ yield the following normalized metrics:** (7 marks)

| Metric | Placement $X$ | Placement $Y$ |
|---|---|---|
| Area ($\phi_A$) | 0.8 | 0.6 |
| Delay ($\phi_D$) | 0.7 | 0.9 |
| Power ($\phi_P$) | 0.5 | 0.4 |

**Which placement does the optimizer select? Justify with a calculation.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Wirelength minimization (3 marks):**
In VLSI placement, **wirelength** measures the total length of interconnect metal used to connect all pins of every net in the design. It is a primary optimization goal because:
- Long wires ⇒ high parasitic resistance and capacitance ⇒ increased delay.
- Long wires ⇒ higher dynamic power ($P \propto C$).
- Long wires ⇒ greater routing congestion.
- Long wires ⇒ more silicon area consumed by metal.

**HPWL (4 marks):**
For a multi-pin net connecting pins at positions $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$, the **Half-Perimeter Wirelength** is:

$$
HPWL(n) = \big[ \max_i x_i - \min_i x_i \big] + \big[ \max_i y_i - \min_i y_i \big]
$$

**Why HPWL is used as a placement cost function:**
1. It is a **provable lower bound** on the rectilinear Steiner minimum tree length — an oracle value the true wirelength can never beat.
2. It is **fast to compute** — only requires finding max/min in $x$ and $y$ for each net, not solving Steiner-tree NP-hard problem.
3. It is **differentiable in $L_1$ norm**, making it suitable for analytical placement engines (e.g., Kraftwerk, ePlace).
4. It is **correlated strongly** with routing congestion and critical-path delay, so minimizing it indirectly improves multiple goals simultaneously.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the weighted cost function.

$$
\Phi = w_A \phi_A + w_D \phi_D + w_P \phi_P
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute for Placement $X$.

$$
\Phi_X = (0.5)(0.8) + (0.3)(0.7) + (0.2)(0.5)
$$

$$
\Phi_X = 0.40 + 0.21 + 0.10 = 0.71
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute for Placement $Y$.

$$
\Phi_Y = (0.5)(0.6) + (0.3)(0.9) + (0.2)(0.4)
$$

$$
\Phi_Y = 0.30 + 0.27 + 0.08 = 0.65
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Decision and justification.
