Since $\Phi_Y = 0.65 < \Phi_X = 0.71$, the optimizer **selects Placement $Y$**.

**Justification:** Placement $Y$ saves more area and power (the two highest-weighted goals, $w_A = 0.5$ and $w_P = 0.2$), even though it incurs a delay penalty. Because the designer assigned the highest weight to area, the optimizer correctly trades off some delay for a smaller, cooler chip.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on "Optimization Goals" questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units (–1 mark typical):** Writing "4.5" instead of "4.5 ns" in Elmore-delay numericals. *Always* append the unit.
> 2. **Omitting "switching activity $\alpha$" in dynamic power (–1 mark):** Many students write $P = C V^2 f$ but forget the activity factor $\alpha$. The full expression $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ is mandatory.
> 3. **Mixing up "minimize" and "maximize" (–1 mark):** In a routing-congestion numerical, students may write "minimize $\rho_g$" but compute it incorrectly as the number of *available* tracks rather than *used* tracks.
> 4. **Ignoring hard vs. soft constraints:** The examiner distinguishes between *objective* (optimize) and *constraint* (must satisfy). A 7-mark formulation question that lists "no DRC violations" as an "objective" instead of a *constraint* loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing HPWL with Steiner length:** HPWL is a *bound*, not the actual routed wirelength. A student claiming "HPWL is the minimum wirelength achievable for the net" is *almost* right but loses a mark for missing the word "bound."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Optimization Goals (Module 4)**
>
> ☐ **Primary Triad:** Area + Delay + Power — the three pillars of any VLSI optimization.
>
> ☐ **Dynamic Power Formula:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ — *quadratic* in voltage.
>
> ☐ **Static Power Formula:** $P_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ — dominant in nanometer nodes.
>
> ☐ **Elmore Delay:** $T_{ED} = \sum R_{e_k} C_{L_k}$ — closed-form, additive, conservative.
>
> ☐ **HPWL:** $HPWL = (\max x - \min x) + (\max y - \min y)$ — fast, differentiable lower bound on Steiner length.
>
> ☐ **Congestion Ratio:** $\rho_g = U_g / C_g$; overflow when $\rho_g > 1$.
>
> ☐ **Yield (Poisson):** $Y = e^{-A D_0}$ — link between area and manufacturability.
>
> ☐ **Weighted Cost Function:** $\Phi = \sum w_i \phi_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$ for multi-objective optimization.
>
> ☐ **Constraints vs. Objectives:** Hard constraints (DRC, no shorts) are *not* weighted into $\Phi$.
>
> ☐ **Critical Path:** $T_{cp} \le T_{clk} - T_{skew} - T_{setup}$ — the timing-bound equation.
>
> ☐ **Stage-wise goals:** Synthesis → Area+Delay; Placement → WL+Timing+Congestion; Routing → WL+Congestion+SI; Compaction → Area.
>
> ☐ **Real-world levers:** Voltage scaling, clock gating, buffer insertion, gate sizing, placement density-aware, congestion-driven routing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation of Routing Regions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Representation of Routing Regions ## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Routing Regions In the **Physical Design** phase of VLSI design automation, once **Placement** of standard cells/macros is completed, the next critical step is **Routing**, which establishes the geometric wire connections between the pins of interconnected modules. However, before any routing algorithm can execute, the continuous, two-dimensional silicon plane must be discretized into a finite, well-defined computational model. This discretized abstraction is known as the **Representation of Routing Regions**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > A *Routing Region* is a bounded portion of the routing plane (defined by one or more metal layers and the available routing tracks) where a set of signal nets must be connected. It is represented as a graph-theoretic structure — most commonly a **Grid Graph** $G(V, E)$ — in which vertices $V$ model the candidate wiring coordinates and edges $E$ model the legal wire segments between adjacent coordinates. A *Net* is a set of electrically equivalent pins that must be shorted together by a single connected wire tree, respecting the design rules (min-width, min-spacing, no-overlap). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The City Road Network Imagine you are a **city planner** who must build roads connecting several houses in a new township. | VLSI Concept | City-Planning Analogy | |---|---| | Silicon die | The empty land | | Cell pins | House doorways | | Routing layer (Metal-1 / Metal-2) | Different elevation levels of flyovers | | Grid graph | Paved road-grid laid on the land | | A *Net* | A postal-delivery route visiting several houses | | Design rules (min-spacing) | Minimum gap between two parallel roads | | Via | An interchange/ramp connecting flyover levels | > [!NOTE] > The **grid graph** is like laying down a uniform rectangular road grid before deciding *which* roads belong to which delivery route. Each intersection is a *vertex*; each road segment is an *edge*. Routing algorithms then "drive" wires through this fixed grid. ### 1.3 Categories of Routing Regions Routing regions are broadly classified into **three** geometric abstractions based on where the cell pins lie along the region boundary: 1. **Channel** — Pins lie on **two opposite** parallel boundaries (typically the top and bottom rows). This is the simplest and historically most-studied topology. 2. **Switchbox** — Pins lie on **all four** sides of a rectangular region. More general and harder than a channel. 3. **General/Over-the-cell (OTC) region** — Pins are scattered throughout the interior of the cell row; wires may pass **over** the active cells (using unused metal layers above the cell). > [!TIP] > KTU students should remember the **2 → 4 → scattered** progression: *Channel (2 sides)* → *Switchbox (4 sides)* → *General region (interior)*. Each step up in generality causes a sharp increase in algorithmic complexity. ### 1.4 Key Metrics Used by KTU Examiners When evaluating a routing-region representation, the following engineering metrics are routinely tested: - **Track density (horizontal/vertical):** $d_h, d_v$ — number of horizontal/vertical routing tracks crossing a vertical/horizontal cut-line. - **Channel density $\rho$:** Maximum $d_h$ over all vertical cut-lines — gives the **lower bound** on the number of horizontal tracks required. - **Channel capacity:** Number of tracks actually allocated to the channel. - **Net length / Wirelength:** Sum of Manhattan distances in the chosen grid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Channel Density** is the single most tested metric in KTU Module-4 questions. A channel is *routable* iff **channel capacity $\geq$ channel density**. --- ## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Grid-Graph Representation The routing plane is divided into a uniform array of horizontal and vertical grid lines. Formally: $$ G = (V, E) $$ where: - $V = \{(x_i, y_j) \mid 1 \le i \le m,\; 1 \le j \le n\}$ — the set of grid intersections (vertices). - $E = E_h \cup E_v$ — the set of allowed horizontal edges $E_h$ and vertical edges $E_v$. $$\begin{aligned} E_h &= \{((x_i, y_j),(x_{i+1}, y_j)) \mid \text{track } j \text{ is free on row } i\} \\ E_v &= \{((x_i, y_j),(x_i, y_{j+1})) \mid \text{track } i \text{ is free on column } j\} \end{aligned}$$ Each edge has an associated **cost** $w(e) \ge 1$ (commonly $w=1$ for unit cost, or $w>1$ to discourage vias/crossings). ### 2.2 Channel Routing — The Canonical Region A **channel** $C$ is a rectangular region of height $H$ (number of horizontal tracks) and width $W$ (the span between the two cell rows). **Anatomy of a Channel (top-down):** 1. **Top row of cells** with pins reaching down into the channel (top-edge pins). 2. **Horizontal tracks** $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_H$ — wires running left-to-right. 3. **Vertical tracks** (columns) — short vertical segments (often called *trunks* or *stems*) carrying signals up/down. 4. **Bottom row of cells** with pins reaching up into the channel. A pin of net $n_k$ on the top edge lies at some column $c$; on the bottom edge at some other column(s). Net $n_k$ must occupy one or more horizontal tracks such that all its pins become electrically connected. ### 2.3 The Two Fundamental Constraint Graphs For channel routing (especially in the **Left-Edge** and **Dogleg** algorithms), two directed graphs encode the *forbidden* ordering of nets on adjacent tracks: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)** — models *same-track* conflicts. > Nodes = nets. Directed edge $n_i \rightarrow n_j$ exists iff net $n_i$ has a pin **to the left of** a pin of $n_j$ on the **same horizontal track**, meaning $n_i$ must be placed **left of** $n_j$ if they share a track. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)** — models *cross-track* conflicts. > Directed edge $n_i \rightarrow n_j$ exists iff the horizontal span of $n_i$ *vertically overlaps* the horizontal span of $n_j$. Then $n_j$ **cannot** be placed on a track **above** $n_i$. - A **cycle** in the VCG implies that the channel is **non-routable** with that net ordering (requires a dogleg or extra track). - The length of the longest path in the VCG equals the minimum number of horizontal tracks needed — another lower bound. ### 2.4 Switchbox Routing A switchbox is a rectangular region $W \times H$ whose **four** sides are pinned. Switchboxes arise: - Between four adjacent cell rows. - Around macro blocks (RAM/ROM/IP cores). - At irregular placements where channels cannot be cleanly separated. The number of possible two-layer routing solutions grows **exponentially** with switchbox size; KTU examiners love contrasting this with the linear-time complexity of channel routing. ### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---| | Channel density $\rho$ | $\rho = \max_{c \in \text{cut-lines}} d(c)$ | Lower bound on number of horizontal tracks | | Cut-line density $d(c)$ | $d(c) = \vert \{n_k \mid \text{pin of } n_k \text{ crosses cut } c\}\vert$ | Number of nets crossing a vertical cut-line | | Channel capacity $C$ | Number of horizontal tracks allocated | Upper bound; need $C \ge \rho$ | | Total wirelength $L$ | $L = \sum_{e \in \text{tree}} w(e)$ | Sum of edge weights along routed trees | | Manhattan distance $d_M$ | $d_M = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$ | Length between two grid points | | VCG longest path | $\ell_{VCG}$ | Lower bound on number of routing tracks | | HCG cycle | cycle present ⇒ forbidden same-track ordering | Forces doglegs or extra tracks | | Switchbox complexity | $\mathcal{O}(W \cdot H)$ grid, exponentially many orderings | Why switchbox routing is NP-hard | > [!WARNING] > **Notation safeguard:** Avoid writing $\vert x\vert$ inside a markdown table. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mathrm{abs}(x)$. The vertical pipe `|` breaks markdown table parsing. --- ## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation : Computing the Channel Density Lower Bound We prove the foundational inequality **Capacity $\ge$ Density**. **Step 1 — Define a cut-line.** A *vertical cut-line* $c$ is an imaginary vertical line drawn between two adjacent grid columns. Every net whose pin-interval spans this line must cross it. **Step 2 — Define cut-line density.** $$d(c) = \bigl\vert\{\, n_k \in N \;\big|\; \text{pin-interval of } n_k \text{ spans cut } c \,\}\bigr\vert$$ **Step 3 — Lower bound argument.** Consider any routing solution using $H$ horizontal tracks. Each track is a single horizontal line that can carry **at most one** wire at any $x$-coordinate. Therefore, the number of tracks $H$ must be **at least** the maximum number of nets that need to cross *any* cut-line, otherwise two nets would collide on the same track at the same column: $$H \;\ge\; \max_{c} d(c) \;=\; \rho$$ **Step 4 — Conclusion.** The **channel density $\rho$** is a proven *lower bound* on the number of horizontal tracks required. Hence the routability condition: $$\boxed{\; C \;=\; H_{\text{allocated}} \;\ge\; \rho \;=\; \max_c d(c)\;}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Channel Density Computation Consider a channel with **5 nets** having the following pin intervals (left column → right column) along the top and bottom edges: | Net | Top-edge pins | Bottom-edge pins | Combined span | |---|---|---|---| | $n_1$ | columns 1, 4 | — | [1, 4] | | $n_2$ | — | column 2 | [2, 2] | | $n_3$ | column 6 | column 8 | [6, 8] | | $n_4$ | column 3 | column 5, 9 | [3, 9] | | $n_5$ | column 7 | — | [7, 7] | We compute $d(c)$ for every integer cut $c \in \{1.5, 2.5, 3.5, \dots, 8.5\}$ (a cut *between* columns $c$ and $c+1$). | Cut-line $c$ | Nets whose span contains $c$ | $d(c)$ | |---|---|---| | 1.5 | $n_1$ | 1 | | 2.5 | $n_1, n_2$ | 2 | | 3.5 | $n_1, n_4$ | 2 | | 4.5 | $n_4$ | 1 | | 5.5 | $n_3, n_4$ | 2 | | 6.5 | $n_3, n_4, n_5$ | **3** | | 7.5 | $n_3, n_4$ | 2 | | 8.5 | $n_4$ | 1 | $$\rho = \max_c d(c) = d(6.5) = 3$$ So **at least 3 horizontal tracks** are required. A capacity of 2 would be **infeasible**; a capacity of 3 is the *minimum*; $\ge 4$ is wasteful but routable. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Building the VCG From the same example, identify vertical overlaps (nests that occupy overlapping horizontal columns): | Overlap pair | Overlap region | VCG edge | |---|---|---| | $n_1$ & $n_2$ | column 2 | $n_1 \to n_2$ (or $n_2 \to n_1$) — pin to right | | $n_1$ & $n_4$ | columns 3–4 | $n_4 \to n_1$ (since $n_4$ has pin at 3 < $n_1$'s pin at 4, on a *shared* track) | | $n_3$ & $n_5$ | column 7 | $n_5 \to n_3$ (pin at 7 < pin at 8) | The longest directed path: $n_4 \to n_1 \to n_2$ (length 3) and $n_5 \to n_3$ (length 2). The **VCG longest path** $=3$, agreeing with $\rho = 3$ — a self-consistent lower bound. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Channel | Switchbox | General / OTC | |---|---|---|---| | Pin sides | 2 (top & bottom) | 4 (all sides) | Interior + sides | | Algorithm complexity | Polynomial (linear, e.g. Left-Edge) | NP-hard in general | NP-hard | | Canonical algorithm | Left-Edge, Yoshimura-Kuh, Dogleg | Greedy / rip-up-and-reroute | Multi-layer OTC router | | Density bound applies | Yes ($\rho$ on vertical cut) | Yes (4-directional) | Approximate | | Layer model | Usually 2-layer | 2 or 3 layers | 3+ layers | | KTU exam weight | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | > [!NOTE] > In KTU 2024 scheme Module-4, the examiner expects students to (i) draw the channel schematic, (ii) compute density, (iii) draw HCG & VCG, and (iv) apply Left-Edge or Yoshimura-Kuh algorithm. --- ## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Routing-Region Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Placement Output
Cell Rows and Macros] --> B[Region Identification
Detect empty rectangular gaps] B --> C{Region Type?} C -- 2-sided pin row --> D[CHANNEL
Top + Bottom pins] C -- 4-sided pin row --> E[SWITCHBOX
All four sides] C -- interior pins --> F[GENERAL / OTC
Over-the-cell region] D --> G[Grid Graph Construction
Vertices V, Edges E] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Constraint Graph Build
HCG and VCG] H --> I[Density Computation
rho = max cut-line] I --> J{Routability Check
Capacity >= rho ?} J -- Yes --> K[Assign Tracks
Left-Edge / YK / Dogleg] J -- No --> L[Route Failure
Trigger rip-up-and-reroute] K --> M[Routed Layout
Output to Verification] L --> K ``` ### 4.2 Channel Routing Schematic (Pin Layout) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TopRow[Top cell row - pins project downward] T1((n1)) --- T2((n2)) --- T3((n4)) --- T4((n3)) end subgraph Chan[Channel region - 3 horizontal tracks] direction LR H1[Track 1] --- H2[Track 2] --- H3[Track 3] end subgraph BotRow[Bottom cell row - pins project upward] B1((n4)) --- B2((n2)) --- B3((n1)) --- B4((n5)) --- B5((n3)) end TopRow --> Chan Chan --> BotRow ``` ### 4.3 Constraint Graph Visualisation ```mermaid flowchart LR n1((n1)) -- pin to right --> n2((n2)) n4((n4)) -- pin to right --> n1((n1)) n5((n5)) -- pin to right --> n3((n3)) n1((n1)) -. shared-track conflict .-> n4((n4)) ``` > [!TIP] > In the rendered diagram above, **solid arrows** depict Vertical-Constraint edges (different tracks, overlapping spans). **Dotted edges** depict Horizontal-Constraint edges (same-track, left-of relations). KTU examiners frequently award partial credit for clearly distinguishing these. ### 4.4 Grid-Graph Data-Structure Abstraction ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Routing Plane - continuous 2D] -->|discretize| G[Grid Graph G of V and E] G --> G1[Vertices V at grid intersections] G --> G2[Edges E split into Eh and Ev] G1 --> V1[(x_i, y_j) - any grid crossing] G2 --> Eh1[Horizontal edges - along x] G2 --> Ev1[Vertical edges - along y] Eh1 --> RoutedA[Maze Search Lee algorithm] Ev1 --> RoutedA ``` --- ## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]** **Q1. Define a routing region. Differentiate between a channel and a switchbox.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** A *routing region* is a bounded sub-area of the routing plane that has been discretized into a grid graph $G(V, E)$ in which signal nets must be electrically connected, respecting design rules. | Property | Channel | Switchbox | |---|---|---| | Pin sides | Two opposite (top & bottom) | All four sides | | Complexity | Polynomial-time solvable | NP-hard in general | | Canonical algorithm | Left-Edge / YK | Greedy / rip-up-and-reroute | | Density bound | Single direction (vertical cut) | Two directions combined | **(Examiner's note:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for comparison.) --- **Q2. What is channel density? State the routability condition.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** *Channel density* $\rho$ is the maximum number of nets whose pin-intervals cross any single vertical cut-line of the channel: $$\rho = \max_{c} d(c), \qquad d(c) = \bigl|\{n_k : c \in \text{span}(n_k)\}\bigr|$$ A channel is *routable* if and only if the **allocated channel capacity** (number of horizontal tracks) $C$ satisfies: $$C \;\ge\; \rho$$ If $C < \rho$, at least two nets must collide on some track, making routing **infeasible**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Internal Choice, Dec 2024 Pattern]** --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** **(a)** Draw the architecture of a grid-graph representation for a routing region. Explain vertices, edges, and edge costs. *(7 marks)* **(b)** For the channel with pin-intervals given below, compute the channel density, draw the Horizontal-Constraint Graph (HCG) and Vertical-Constraint Graph (VCG), and state the minimum number of horizontal tracks required. *(7 marks)* | Net | Leftmost pin column | Rightmost pin column | |---|---|---| | A | 1 | 5 | | B | 2 | 3 | | C | 4 | 8 | | D | 6 | 7 | | E | 1 | 8 | **Model Solution:** **(a) Grid-graph architecture:** *(7 marks)* The routing plane is divided into a uniform rectangular grid. Each grid intersection is a **vertex** $v_{i,j}=(x_i, y_j)$; each segment between adjacent vertices is an **edge**. - **Vertex set:** $V = \{(x_i, y_j) \mid 1 \le i \le m, 1 \le j \le n\}$ - **Horizontal edges:** $E_h = \{((x_i,y_j),(x_{i+1},y_j))\}$ - **Vertical edges:** $E_v = \{((x_i,y_j),(x_i,y_{j+1}))\}$ - **Edge cost:** $w(e) \ge 1$. Unit cost $w=1$ for shortest-path; $w>1$ to penalise vias, bends, or congested regions. - Maze-routing algorithms (Lee's algorithm) operate directly on this graph: BFS wavefront expands from source until target vertex is reached. The architecture delivers: (i) finite search space, (ii) simple cost accumulation, (iii) easy design-rule checks per edge. **(b) Density, HCG, VCG computation:** *(7 marks)* Compute cut-line densities for every integer cut between columns $c$ and $c+1$: | Cut $c$ | Nets spanning $c$ | $d(c)$ | |---|---|---| | 1.5 | A, E | 2 | | 2.5 | A, B, E | 3 | | 3.5 | A, E | 2 | | 4.5 | A, C, E | 3 | | 5.5 | C, E | 2 | | 6.5 | C, D, E | 3 | | 7.5 | C, E | 2 | $$\boxed{\rho = \max_c d(c) = 3}$$ **HCG (same-track left-of relations):** Only nets whose pin-sets interleave on a *candidate shared track* form edges. - $B$ has pin at 2, $A$ has pin at 5 → $B \to A$ (same track: $B$ must be left of $A$) - $D$ has pin at 6–7 inside $C$'s span 4–8 → $C \to D$ (same track: $C$ must be left of $D$) - $E$ spans 1–8 — touches every other net → multiple edges to/from $E$. **VCG (vertical-overlap, cross-track):** An edge $n_i \to n_j$ exists iff $n_i$ has a pin *left of* a pin of $n_j$ and their spans overlap. - $A$ & $B$ overlap at column 2–3 → $B \to A$ - $C$ & $D$ overlap at 6–7 → $D \to C$ (D pin at 6 < C pin at 8) - $E$ overlaps with all nets → $E \to A, B, C, D$ **Longest VCG path:** $E \to A \to (\text{no further})$ (length 2), $E \to C \to (\text{length 2})$. Longest path = 2. Density bound (3) is *tighter* than VCG bound (2) in this case. **Minimum number of horizontal tracks $\ge \rho = 3$.** --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)** **(a)** Explain the Yoshimura-Kuh channel-routing algorithm. How does it differ from the simple Left-Edge algorithm? *(7 marks)* **(b)** Consider a switchbox of size $4 \times 3$ (4 wide × 3 high) with pins on all four sides. List the difficulties that make switchbox routing fundamentally harder than channel routing, and propose two techniques commonly used to handle them. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Yoshimura-Kuh algorithm:** *(7 marks)* The **Left-Edge algorithm** (Hashimoto & Stevens) is a *greedy, single-pass* algorithm that assigns nets to the lowest available horizontal track, ordered by their leftmost pin column. It works perfectly for **2-sided channels without doglegs** but fails when nets require doglegs (multi-track segments for the same net). The **Yoshimura-Kuh algorithm** (1982) extends this by: 1. **Partitioning** each multi-pin net into *two-pin sub-nets* (an interval tree / Steiner decomposition). 2. Building the **Vertical Constraint Graph** on sub-nets. 3. Performing a **topological sort** to assign a track index $t(n)$ to each sub-net. 4. Assigning actual horizontal positions via a modified Left-Edge sweep, allowing **doglegs** to break vertical constraints. 5. Handling **cycles** in VCG by introducing extra tracks or doglegs. **Key differences:** | Aspect | Left-Edge | Yoshimura-Kuh | |---|---|---| | Multi-pin nets | Restricted | Decomposed into segments | | Dogleg support | None | Native | | VCG handling | Ignored | Topologically sorted | | Track optimality | Near-optimal | Often achieves $\rho$ | | Cycle recovery | Not possible | Possible via dogleg | **(b) Switchbox difficulties & techniques:** *(7 marks)* **Difficulties (any 4 for full marks):** 1. Pins on **all four sides** — a net may enter from any combination of sides, giving exponentially many topology choices. 2. The classical **density lower bound** is no longer tight — a single cut-line in only one direction is insufficient. 3. **VCG cycles** are common and cannot be broken solely by extra tracks; vertical wires on the sides restrict track usage. 4. **Pin ordering on left and right sides** imposes *both* horizontal and vertical constraints simultaneously. 5. No clear "input" and "output" sides — algorithms cannot exploit unidirectional flow. **Common techniques:** 1. **Rip-up-and-reroute** — Route greedily; on failure, rip-up conflicting nets and reroute with updated costs. 2. **Maze routing (Lee's algorithm)** restricted to two-pin nets within the switchbox, with iterative refinement. 3. **Integer Linear Programming (ILP)** formulation for small switchboxes to obtain optimal solutions. 4. **Greedy channel-style** extension with side-pin pre-routing — first commit boundary pins to specific tracks, then route the resulting residual as a channel. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common mistake 1:** Confusing **HCG** (same-track) with **VCG** (cross-track). Losing 1–2 marks per occurrence. > - **Common mistake 2:** Computing density from *all* cut-lines but forgetting that the maximum is the lower bound, not the sum. > - **Common mistake 3:** Drawing the VCG with **undirected** edges. The VCG is a *directed* graph encoding "must-be-below" relations. > - **Common mistake 4:** Stating $\rho = $ number of tracks needed *exactly*. The correct statement is $\rho \le $ tracks required. Equality holds only when the VCG is cycle-free and a perfect track assignment exists. > - **Common mistake 5:** In switchbox questions, students often apply channel-only density. Switchboxes need **both** horizontal *and* vertical density checks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Representation of Routing Regions** - ☐ **Routing region** = discretized sub-plane represented as a **grid graph** $G(V, E)$. - ☐ Three region types: **Channel (2 sides)**, **Switchbox (4 sides)**, **General / OTC (interior)**. - ☐ **Channel density** $\rho = \max_c d(c)$ is a *lower bound* on required horizontal tracks. - ☐ **Routability condition:** $C_{\text{capacity}} \ge \rho$. - ☐ **HCG** captures same-track ordering; a cycle in HCG ⇒ conflict on shared track. - ☐ **VCG** captures cross-track ordering; a cycle in VCG ⇒ requires dogleg or extra track. - ☐ **VCG longest path** = second lower bound on track count. - ☐ **Left-Edge** algorithm: greedy, single-pass, no doglegs. - ☐ **Yoshimura-Kuh** algorithm: handles doglegs, decomposes multi-pin nets. - ☐ **Lee's maze algorithm** is the canonical grid-graph shortest-path search. - ☐ Switchbox routing is **NP-hard** in general; uses rip-up-and-reroute or ILP. - ☐ Always state the *direction* of every constraint-graph edge — examiners deduct for undirected drawings. - ☐ In LaTeX tables, write $\lvert x \rvert$, never $\vert x \vert$, to preserve markdown rendering. > **End of Notes — Module 4 : Representation of Routing Regions**

topics.title: Maze Routing Algorithms: Lee's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Maze Routing Algorithms: Lee's Algorithm ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Lee's Algorithm (1961)** is a **guaranteed, optimal maze-routing algorithm** used in VLSI physical design automation to find the **shortest (minimum cost) path** between a *source* terminal and a *target* terminal on a **discrete rectangular routing grid** that may contain obstacles (pre-routed nets, blocked regions, macro cells). In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Lee's Algorithm is classified as a **graph-search-based, sequential, single-net, grid-based global router** that uses an exhaustive **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** wavefront expansion strategy. | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Routing Grid** | Discretized 2-D plane where each cell represents a possible wire location. | | **Source (S)** | Starting pin/terminal of the net to be routed. | | **Target (T)** | Destination pin/terminal of the net to be routed. | | **Obstacle / Block** | A grid cell already occupied or forbidden for routing. | | **Wavefront** | The set of all grid cells at the same BFS distance from the source. | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Water in a Maze" Imagine pouring colored water at the **source cell** of a maze. The water spreads **outward equally in all four (or eight) directions at the same speed**, one square per second, but it **cannot pass through walls (obstacles)**. - The water keeps expanding **level by level**, like ripples on a pond. - The moment the wave reaches the **target cell**, you know the **shortest number of steps** required. - Now you simply trace back from the target to the source by following the **descending water levels** (i.e., cells with progressively smaller labels). > [!NOTE] > This analogy perfectly captures the two phases of Lee's Algorithm: > 1. **Wave Expansion / Labeling** (water spreading outward). > 2. **Backtracing / Retrace** (walking back from target following the gradient). ### 1.3 Why Lee's Algorithm Matters in VLSI - **Optimality**: Guarantees the shortest path (minimum number of grid cells), assuming uniform cost. - **Generality**: Works on any planar grid with arbitrary obstacle geometry. - **Determinism**: Always finds a path **if one exists**; reports failure clearly when none does. - **Simplicity**: Conceptually clean, easy to implement in HDL/EDA tooling. > [!WARNING] > **Trade-off**: Lee's Algorithm has a memory and time complexity of **O(N × M)** per wave step, which makes it **memory-intensive** for large industrial grids (millions of cells). It is therefore used as a *conceptual baseline* and is the foundation for many accelerated variants (A*, Hadlock, Soukup, etc.). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Routing Grid Model The chip routing area is discretized into a 2-D matrix of size **N rows × M columns**. - **Cost of each free cell** = 1 unit (uniform). - **Blocked cells** = infinity cost (impassable). - Allowed moves: **4-directional (Manhattan)** by default — Up, Down, Left, Right. - *Optional*: 8-directional (including diagonals) when preferred routing directions exist. ### 2.2 The Two-Phase Operational Model #### Phase I — Wave Propagation (Labeling / Forward Search) 1. Set the source cell `S` with label **1**. 2. For each integer `k = 1, 2, 3, ...`, label every **unblocked, unlabeled** 4-neighbor of any cell labeled `k-1` with label `k`. 3. Stop when either: - The target `T` is reached → path exists. - No new cell can be labeled → **no path exists (failure)**. #### Phase II — Backtracing (Retrace / Reverse Search) 1. Begin at the target cell `T` with its assigned label `L_T`. 2. Move to any 4-neighbor with label `L_T - 1`. 3. Repeat until the source cell with label **1** is reached. 4. The traversed cells form the **shortest routing path**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | $N \times M$ | Grid dimensions (rows × columns) | $10^{3} \text{ to } 10^{6}$ cells | | $d_{min}$ | Minimum path length (Manhattan lower bound) | $\vert r_T - r_S \vert + \vert c_T - c_S \vert$ cells | | $L_{path}$ | Actual length found by Lee's algorithm | $L_{path} \geq d_{min}$ | | $\Theta(NM)$ | Memory complexity per wave step | bytes / cells | | $O(N^{2}M^{2})$ | Worst-case time complexity | operations | | $L_T$ | Label value at the target | integer (steps from S) | | $k$ | Current wavefront label index | $\geq 1$ | | $N_c$ | Total number of free + blocked cells | $\leq NM$ | ### 2.4 Key Engineering Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Routing Channel Density**, **Critical Path Delay**, and **Wire Congestion** are the three metrics Lee's algorithm implicitly minimizes when used iteratively — it always picks the *shortest geometric path*, which generally reduces wirelength and hence signal delay. ### 2.5 Advantages vs. Limitations (Board-Exam Favourite) | Advantages | Limitations | |---|---| | Guaranteed optimal path | High memory usage $O(NM)$ | | Guaranteed to find path if one exists | Slow on industrial-scale grids | | Handles arbitrary obstacle layouts | Doesn't consider net ordering / congestion | | Simple, deterministic, easy to verify | Single-net only (not multi-net aware) | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example: Routing a Net on a 5×6 Grid **Given:** Source $S$ at $(0,0)$, Target $T$ at $(4,5)$. `#` denotes a **blocked cell**, `.` denotes a **free cell**. **Initial Routing Grid:** $$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline S & . & . & \# & . & . \\ \hline . & \# & . & \# & . & . \\ \hline . & . & . & . & \# & . \\ \hline \# & \# & \# & . & . & . \\ \hline . & . & . & . & . & T \\ \hline \end{array} $$ ### 3.2 Phase I — Wave Expansion (Step-by-Step Derivation) > Each cell value represents the **label assigned** during wave propagation. The label equals the minimum number of steps from $S$. **Step 0 — Initialization:** Assign label $\mathbf{1}$ to source cell $(0,0)$. **Step 1 — Wave $k=2$:** Label all unlabeled 4-neighbors of label-1 cells. - $(0,1)$: free → label **2** - $(1,0)$: free → label **2** **Step 2 — Wave $k=3$:** From all cells labeled 2. - $(0,2)$: free → label **3** - $(2,0)$: free → label **3** - $(1,1)$: blocked → skip **Step 3 — Wave $k=4$:** - $(1,2)$: free → label **4** - $(2,1)$: free → label **4** **Step 4 — Wave $k=5$:** - $(2,2)$: free → label **5** **Step 5 — Wave $k=6$:** - $(2,3)$: free → label **6** **Step 6 — Wave $k=7$:** - $(3,3)$: free → label **7** **Step 7 — Wave $k=8$:** - $(3,4)$: free → label **8** - $(4,3)$: free → label **8** **Step 8 — Wave $k=9$:** - $(3,5)$: free → label **9** - $(4,2)$: free → label **9** - $(4,4)$: free → label **9** **Step 9 — Wave $k=10$:** - $(2,5)$: free → label **10** - $(4,1)$: free → label **10** - $(4,5)$: **TARGET reached!** → label **10** **Fully Labeled Grid:** $$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline 1 & 2 & 3 & \# & 8 & 9 \\ \hline 2 & \# & 4 & \# & 9 & 10 \\ \hline 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & \# & 10 \\ \hline \# & \# & \# & 7 & 8 & 9 \\ \hline 10 & 10 & 9 & 8 & 9 & 10 \\ \hline \end{array} $$ ### 3.3 Phase II — Backtracing (Reverse Path) Starting from $T = (4,5)$ with label **10**, walk to any 4-neighbor with label **9**, then 8, then 7, ... down to 1. **Selected Retrace Path:** $$ \begin{aligned} (4,5)_{L=10} &\rightarrow (4,4)_{L=9} \rightarrow (4,3)_{L=8} \rightarrow (3,3)_{L=7} \\ &\rightarrow (2,3)_{L=6} \rightarrow (2,2)_{L=5} \rightarrow (1,2)_{L=4} \\ &\rightarrow (0,2)_{L=3} \rightarrow (0,1)_{L=2} \rightarrow (0,0)_{L=1} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Verification of Optimality The **Manhattan lower bound** is: $$ d_{min} = \vert r_T - r_S \vert + \vert c_T - c_S \vert $$ Substituting: $$ d_{min} = \vert 4 - 0 \vert + \vert 5 - 0 \vert = 4 + 5 = 9 \text{ edges} $$ The obtained path length is: $$ L_{path} = 10 - 1 = 9 \text{ edges} $$ Since $L_{path} = d_{min}$, the algorithm has produced the **shortest possible path**. ✅ ### 3.5 Complexity Derivation **Memory:** Each of the $NM$ grid cells stores an integer label. $$ \text{Memory} = N \cdot M \cdot \text{sizeof}(\text{int}) = \Theta(NM) $$ **Time:** In the worst case, every cell is visited in each of the $O(NM)$ waves. $$ \text{Time} = O(NM \cdot NM) = O(N^{2}M^{2}) $$ > [!NOTE] > For an $N \times N$ square grid, this simplifies to $O(N^{4})$ — a critical KTU board-exam number. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of Lee's Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Input: Grid N×M, Source S, Target T, Obstacles"] --> B["Initialize all cells to UNLABELED"] B --> C["Mark S with label 1"] C --> D{"Current wavefront
k = 1, 2, 3 ..."} D --> E["Find all 4-neighbors of
cells labeled k-1"] E --> F{"Neighbor is free AND
unlabeled?"} F -- Yes --> G["Assign label k"] F -- No --> H["Skip cell"] G --> I{"Target T reached?"} H --> I I -- Yes --> J["Phase II: Backtrace"] I -- No --> K{"Any cell labeled
this wave?"} K -- Yes --> D K -- No --> L["Report FAILURE:
No path exists"] J --> M["Walk from T to S
using labels L-1, L-2, ..., 1"] M --> N["Output: Shortest Path"] ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Phase-I (Wave Expansion) Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Start Wave k"] --> P2["Create list Wk
of new frontier cells"] P2 --> P3["Pop cell X from Wk"] P3 --> P4["Examine 4 neighbors of X"] P4 --> P5{"Neighbor Y is free
AND label Y = 0?"} P5 -- Yes --> P6["Set label Y = k"] P6 --> P7["Add Y to next wave Wk+1"] P5 -- No --> P8["Skip Y"] P7 --> P9{"More cells in Wk?"} P8 --> P9 P9 -- Yes --> P3 P9 -- No --> P10{"Target T in Wk+1?"} P10 -- Yes --> P11["Stop expansion:
Proceed to retrace"] P10 -- No --> P12{"Wk+1 empty?"} P12 -- Yes --> P13["Return NO PATH"] P12 -- No --> P1 ``` ### 4.3 Backtracing (Phase II) Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD R1["Start at Target T
with label LT"] --> R2["Add T to path list"] R2 --> R3["Examine 4 neighbors of current cell"] R3 --> R4{"Any neighbor has
label = current - 1?"} R4 -- Yes --> R5["Move to that neighbor
Append to path"] R4 -- No --> R6["Multiple candidates:
apply tie-break rule"] R6 --> R5 R5 --> R7{"Reached Source S
with label 1?"} R7 -- Yes --> R8["Reverse path list
Output final route"] R7 -- No --> R3 ``` ### 4.4 Cell-Type Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR src["SOURCE CELL
S = 1"] --> w2["WAVE 2
distance = 2"] w2 --> w3["WAVE 3
distance = 3"] w3 --> w4["WAVE k
distance = k"] w4 --> wN["WAVE LT
target reached"] wN --> bk["BACKTRACE
LT → LT-1 → ... → 1"] bk --> out["FINAL ROUTE
shortest path"] ``` > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Examiners often award marks for a **clearly labeled flowchart** showing the two-phase architecture. Use the diagrams above as a template and adapt labels to your specific routing problem. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define Lee's maze routing algorithm. List its two main phases.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Lee's Algorithm is a **grid-based shortest-path routing algorithm** introduced by C. Y. Lee (1961) used in VLSI physical design to find the minimum-cost path between a source pin and a target pin on a discretized 2-D routing grid containing obstacles. It works in **two main phases**: 1. **Wave Propagation / Labeling Phase:** A breadth-first wavefront expands outward from the source cell, labeling each reachable free cell with its Manhattan distance from the source. 2. **Backtracing / Retrace Phase:** Once the target is reached, the path is recovered by walking from the target back to the source through cells whose labels decrease by exactly one at each step, guaranteeing the **shortest path**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State any **three limitations** of Lee's algorithm.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **High memory consumption:** Requires storage of $O(NM)$ labels for an $N \times M$ grid. 2. **High time complexity:** Worst-case time is $O(N^{2}M^{2})$, which becomes infeasible for modern chips. 3. **Single-net limitation:** It routes only one net at a time and does not inherently handle **congestion**, **net ordering**, or **crosstalk**. --- ### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]** *For the following 5×6 routing grid, route the net from source $S(0,0)$ to target $T(4,5)$ using **Lee's algorithm**. Show the complete wave propagation (labeling) phase and the final backtraced path. Verify optimality using the Manhattan lower bound.* $$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline S & . & . & X & . & . \\ \hline . & X & . & X & . & . \\ \hline . & . & . & . & X & . \\ \hline X & X & X & . & . & . \\ \hline . & . & . & . & . & T \\ \hline \end{array} $$ **(a) Show all wavefront labels clearly and explain the wave-expansion logic. — 7 marks** **Model Solution:** Assign label **1** to source $S$ at $(0,0)$. Iterate waves $k = 2, 3, \dots$ labelling every free, unlabeled 4-neighbor of any cell with label $k-1$. The final labeled grid is: $$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline 1 & 2 & 3 & X & 8 & 9 \\ \hline 2 & X & 4 & X & 9 & 10 \\ \hline 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 & X & 10 \\ \hline X & X & X & 7 & 8 & 9 \\ \hline 10 & 10 & 9 & 8 & 9 & 10 \\ \hline \end{array} $$ The target $T$ receives label **10**, so $L_T = 10$. The expansion took **9 wavefronts** beyond the source. **(b) Trace the shortest path back from $T$ to $S$ and prove it is optimal. — 7 marks** **Model Solution:** **Backtrace Path:** $$ (0,0) \rightarrow (0,1) \rightarrow (0,2) \rightarrow (1,2) \rightarrow (2,2) \rightarrow (2,3) \rightarrow (3,3) \rightarrow (4,3) \rightarrow (4,4) \rightarrow (4,5) $$ **Path Length:** $$ L_{path} = L_T - 1 = 10 - 1 = 9 \text{ edges} $$ **Manhattan Lower Bound:** $$ d_{min} = \vert 4 - 0 \vert + \vert 5 - 0 \vert = 4 + 5 = 9 \text{ cells} $$ Since $L_{path} = d_{min} = 9$, the path is **provably optimal**. ✅ --- #### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]** **(a) With a neat flowchart, describe the **wave propagation phase** of Lee's maze routing algorithm. Mention the data structure used and the stopping condition. — 7 marks** **Model Solution:** The wave propagation phase uses a **FIFO queue** (BFS frontier) initialized with the source cell. **Flowchart (reproduced):** ``` START ↓ [Initialize label[i][j] = 0 for all cells] [Set label[S] = 1] [Push S into queue Q] ↓ WHILE Q is not empty: X = dequeue(Q) IF X == T: GOTO backtrace_phase FOR each 4-neighbor Y of X: IF label[Y] == 0 AND Y is not blocked: label[Y] = label[X] + 1 enqueue(Y) END IF END FOR END WHILE ↓ REPORT "NO PATH EXISTS" STOP ``` **Stopping Conditions:** 1. **Success:** Target cell $T$ is dequeued and labeled. 2. **Failure:** Queue becomes empty without reaching $T$. **(b) Explain the **backtracing phase**. Why does this phase guarantee the shortest path? — 7 marks** **Model Solution:** Once $T$ is labeled with $L_T$, backtracing begins at $T$. The algorithm iteratively selects a 4-neighbor of the current cell whose label equals `current_label - 1`, moving to it and appending it to the path list. This continues until the source (label **1**) is reached. The path list is then reversed to obtain the source-to-target route. **Why this guarantees optimality:** Because BFS visits cells in **strictly non-decreasing order of distance** from $S$, every cell's label equals its **true shortest Manhattan distance** from $S$. The target label $L_T$ therefore equals the **shortest path length + 1** (since source is labeled 1). Backtracing along decreasing labels reconstructs one of the shortest paths, and since BFS explores all equidistant cells before moving to the next wavefront, no shorter path can exist. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in Lee's Algorithm questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to label the source as 1** (not 0) — costs 1 mark immediately. > 2. **Off-by-one errors** in path length: Path length = $L_T - 1$, **not** $L_T$. > 3. **Skipping blocked cells properly** — students often re-label them. > 4. **Not showing intermediate wavefronts** — examiners require **all wave labels**, not just the final grid. > 5. **Confusing 4-connectivity vs 8-connectivity** — always state the assumption. > 6. **Omitting the Manhattan lower-bound verification** — this alone fetches 1–2 marks. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ Lee's Algorithm = **BFS wavefront expansion + gradient backtrace**. - ✅ It is a **guaranteed shortest-path, single-net, grid-based** router. - ✅ The **source is labeled 1**, and **labels represent distance-from-source**. - ✅ **Backtrace** walks from $T$ to $S$ along labels decreasing by exactly 1. - ✅ **Optimality check:** $L_{path} = L_T - 1 = d_{min} = \vert \Delta r \vert + \vert \Delta c \vert$. - ✅ **Memory:** $\Theta(NM)$; **Worst-case time:** $O(N^{2}M^{2})$ or $O(N^{4})$ for square grids. - ✅ **Phase I = Forward BFS labeling; Phase II = Reverse retrace.** - ✅ **FIFO queue** is the standard data structure for the frontier. - ✅ **Failure condition:** Queue empties before reaching $T$ → no path exists. - ✅ **Industrial limitation:** Memory-intensive → inspired accelerated variants like **A\***, **Hadlock's algorithm**, and **Soukup's algorithm**. - ✅ Always **state 4- vs 8-connectivity** assumption explicitly in your answer. - ✅ Always **verify optimality** with the Manhattan lower-bound formula — a KTU board-exam favourite.

topics.title: Detailed Routing: Horizontal and Vertical Constraint Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Detailed Routing: Horizontal and Vertical Constraint Graph ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Detailed Routing** is the second major phase of VLSI physical design, executed immediately after Global Routing. While global routing assigns every net to a *coarse region* (a routing region or "g-cel"), **detailed routing** decides the **exact geometric path, layer assignment, and via placement** of every wire segment within that region. The classic formulation of detailed routing is **Channel Routing**, where the routing region is a rectangular channel with all terminals pre-fixed on the top and bottom edges. For the **two-layer HVH (Horizontal–Vertical–Horizontal) routing model**, the routing problem reduces to assigning each net to a horizontal track subject to two structural constraints captured by **constraint graphs**: - **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)** — captures ordering constraints along the column direction (which net must lie above which). - **Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)** — captures non-overlapping constraints along the row direction (which nets cannot share the same horizontal track). > [!IMPORTANT] > **VCG (Vertical Constraint Graph)** > A directed graph $G_v = (V, E_v)$ where each vertex $v_i \in V$ represents a net $i$, and a directed edge $(i, j) \in E_v$ exists if and only if there is a column $c$ where net $i$ has a terminal on the **top edge** of the channel and net $j$ has a terminal on the **bottom edge** of the same column. The edge denotes the constraint that **net $i$ must be placed on a track strictly above net $j$** in that column. > [!IMPORTANT] > **HCG (Horizontal Constraint Graph)** > An undirected graph $G_h = (V, E_h)$ where each vertex $v_i \in V$ represents a net $i$, and an undirected edge $\{i, j\} \in E_h$ exists if and only if the horizontal spans of net $i$ and net $j$ **overlap** in at least one column. The edge denotes that **net $i$ and net $j$ cannot be assigned to the same horizontal track** (because their horizontal segments on the same layer would short). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "A Two-Story Parking Lot with One-Way Ramps" Imagine a **two-story parking lot**: - The **lower story** (Layer 1) has horizontal lanes (cars drive left–right). - The **upper story** (Layer 2) has vertical ramps (cars move up–down). - Each car (a net) must enter at its entry gate and exit at its exit gate. The **VCG is like the rule book for ramps**: if Car A's exit is directly above Car B's entry at the same vertical shaft, Car A must use an upper ramp (be above Car B). The **HCG is like the lane reservation system**: if two cars want to travel in the same horizontal lane through the same stretch at the same time, they cannot share the lane. If the **VCG ever forms a directed cycle** (A above B, B above C, C above A), the system is **logically impossible** — like three cars needing to simultaneously be above, below, and beside each other in a one-way spiral ramp. ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted) - **Channel Density ($d$)**: the maximum, over all columns, of the number of nets whose horizontal span covers that column. **The minimum number of horizontal tracks required is bounded below by $d$.** - **Track Count ($t$)**: the actual number of horizontal tracks used by the final routing. - **Critical Net**: a net that lies on a longest path of the VCG; it determines the vertical depth of the routing. - **VCG-Acyclic Condition**: a necessary condition for *dogleg-free* two-layer routing. > [!NOTE] > **Key Highlight for KTU 2024 Examinations:** > A channel is **routable without doglegs** in the two-layer HVH model **if and only if** its Vertical Constraint Graph is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. If the VCG contains a cycle, either doglegs must be introduced or extra layers must be used. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Decomposition — How a Detailed Router Works The constraint-graph-based detailed routing pipeline follows five rigorous logical steps: 1. **Net Enumeration & Span Computation** - For every net $i$, identify the set of columns $C_i = \{c : \text{net } i \text{ has a terminal in column } c\}$. - Compute the **horizontal span** $\sigma_i = [\min(C_i), \max(C_i)]$. 2. **VCG Construction** - Iterate over every column $c$. - If a net $i$ occupies the top of column $c$ *and* a net $j$ (with $i \ne j$) occupies the bottom of column $c$, insert a directed edge $(i, j)$ into $E_v$. 3. **VCG Cycle Check (DAG Test)** - Run a topological sort on $G_v$. If it fails, the channel needs doglegs or extra layers. 4. **HCG Construction** - For every pair of nets $(i, j)$, if $\sigma_i \cap \sigma_j \ne \emptyset$, insert an undirected edge $\{i, j\}$ into $E_h$. 5. **Track Assignment (Left-Edge Algorithm)** - Topologically order the nets using VCG. - Sweep columns left → right; assign each net to the **lowest-numbered track** that is free in its span and does not violate HCG. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Logic - **Why VCG uses top-vs-bottom pairing in the same column?** Because in the HVH model, the horizontal segment of a net must reach its terminal at the top or bottom edge. If two nets share a column and one is on top while the other is on bottom, their horizontal segments on the same layer cannot cross — the top net's track must be vertically above the bottom net's track. - **Why HCG checks span overlap?** Two horizontal segments on the same layer (Layer 1) that overlap in any column would create an electrical short. Hence they must occupy distinct tracks. - **Why is the VCG acyclicity only a *necessary* condition?** Even without VCG cycles, the channel might still require more tracks than the density bound, especially when HCG edges force separation. The minimum track count $t^*$ equals: $$ t^* \;=\; \text{chromatic index of the HCG, subject to VCG topological order} $$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Domain | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Horizontal span of net $i$ | $\sigma_i = [\min(C_i), \max(C_i)]$ | column indices (integer) | | 2 | Channel density | $d = \max\limits_{c \in \text{columns}} \left\vert \{\, i \,:\, c \in \sigma_i \,\} \right\vert$ | tracks (integer) | | 3 | Lower bound on tracks | $t \geq d$ | tracks | | 4 | VCG edge condition | $(i, j) \in E_v \iff \exists\, c$ with top-terminal$(i,c)$ and bottom-terminal$(j,c)$ | boolean | | 5 | HCG edge condition | $\{i, j\} \in E_h \iff \sigma_i \cap \sigma_j \ne \emptyset$ | boolean | | 6 | Routability (dogleg-free, 2-layer HVH) | $G_v$ is a DAG | necessary & sufficient in HVH | | 7 | Critical path length | $L = \text{length of longest path in } G_v$ | track-depth metric | | 8 | Zone reduction | $z_i = \max(i_{\text{left span}})$ | integer index | | 9 | Total wire length | $W = \sum_i \vert \sigma_i \vert + \sum_i \vert V_i \vert$ | $\lambda$ (lambda, layout unit) | | 10 | For Japanese / dogleg-free Manhattan | each net has at most one horizontal segment | structural constraint | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pipes inside math are escaped**: in row #2 above, the absolute value of a set is written using `\vert \dots \vert` (NOT the bare pipe `|`) to keep the markdown table well-formed. Likewise, row #9 uses `\vert \sigma_i \vert`. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Constraint-graph channel routing underpins the placement–routing engines used by **Synopsys IC Compiler, Cadence Innovus, and open-source tools like qRouter**. In modern silicon, channel routing has been generalized to **switchbox routing** and **global-track assignment**, but the **VCG/HCG abstraction persists** as the analytical engine for **congestion estimation**, **track assignment in FinFET/GCAA patterns**, and **routability-driven placement** (RDP) — where pre-routing congestion maps are derived from constraint-graph analogues computed on a coarse grid. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Build VCG and HCG for a 6-Net Channel #### 3.1.1 Channel Specification Consider a 6-column channel with the following terminal layout (numbers are net IDs, blanks mean no terminal): | Column $c$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Top terminals** | 1 | — | 2 | 2 | — | 3 | | **Bottom terminals** | — | 5 | 5 | — | 6 | 4 | Nets and their column sets $C_i$: - Net 1: $C_1 = \{1\} \Rightarrow \sigma_1 = [1, 1]$ - Net 2: $C_2 = \{3, 4\} \Rightarrow \sigma_2 = [3, 4]$ - Net 3: $C_3 = \{6\} \Rightarrow \sigma_3 = [6, 6]$ - Net 4: $C_4 = \{6\} \Rightarrow \sigma_4 = [6, 6]$ - Net 5: $C_5 = \{2, 3\} \Rightarrow \sigma_5 = [2, 3]$ - Net 6: $C_6 = \{5\} \Rightarrow \sigma_6 = [5, 5]$ #### 3.1.2 Build the Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG) Scan column-by-column for top–bottom co-occurrence: - **Column 1**: top = net 1, bottom = none → **no edge** - **Column 2**: top = none, bottom = net 5 → **no edge** - **Column 3**: top = net 2, bottom = net 5 → **edge (2 → 5)**, meaning net 2 must be above net 5 - **Column 4**: top = net 2, bottom = none → **no edge** - **Column 5**: top = none, bottom = net 6 → **no edge** - **Column 6**: top = net 3, bottom = net 4 → **edge (3 → 4)** Final VCG edges: $$ E_v = \{\, (2, 5),\ (3, 4) \,\} $$ The VCG is a **DAG** with two independent edges. The channel is **routable without doglegs** in the two-layer HVH model. #### 3.1.3 Build the Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG) Check every pair of nets for span overlap: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_1 \cap \sigma_2 &= [1,1] \cap [3,4] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_1 \cap \sigma_3 &= [1,1] \cap [6,6] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_1 \cap \sigma_4 &= [1,1] \cap [6,6] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_1 \cap \sigma_5 &= [1,1] \cap [2,3] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_1 \cap \sigma_6 &= [1,1] \cap [5,5] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_2 \cap \sigma_3 &= [3,4] \cap [6,6] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_2 \cap \sigma_4 &= [3,4] \cap [6,6] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_2 \cap \sigma_5 &= [3,4] \cap [2,3] = \{3\} \Rightarrow \text{edge} \\ \sigma_2 \cap \sigma_6 &= [3,4] \cap [5,5] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_3 \cap \sigma_4 &= [6,6] \cap [6,6] = \{6\} \Rightarrow \text{edge} \\ \sigma_3 \cap \sigma_5 &= [6,6] \cap [2,3] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_3 \cap \sigma_6 &= [6,6] \cap [5,5] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_4 \cap \sigma_5 &= [6,6] \cap [2,3] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_4 \cap \sigma_6 &= [6,6] \cap [5,5] = \emptyset \\ \sigma_5 \cap \sigma_6 &= [2,3] \cap [5,5] = \emptyset \end{aligned} $$ Final HCG edges: $$ E_h = \{\, \{2, 5\},\ \{3, 4\} \,\} $$ ### 3.2 Compute Channel Density $d$ Count nets covering each column: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Col } 1 &: \{\text{Net 1}\} \Rightarrow d_1 = 1 \\ \text{Col } 2 &: \{\text{Net 5}\} \Rightarrow d_2 = 1 \\ \text{Col } 3 &: \{\text{Net 2, Net 5}\} \Rightarrow d_3 = 2 \\ \text{Col } 4 &: \{\text{Net 2}\} \Rightarrow d_4 = 1 \\ \text{Col } 5 &: \{\text{Net 6}\} \Rightarrow d_5 = 1 \\ \text{Col } 6 &: \{\text{Net 3, Net 4}\} \Rightarrow d_6 = 2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ d = \max_c d_c = \max(1, 1, 2, 1, 1, 2) = 2 $$ Therefore the routing requires **at least 2 horizontal tracks**. ### 3.3 Apply the Left-Edge Algorithm to Assign Tracks **Step 1 — Sort by left-endpoint and break ties by VCG topological order:** Nets sorted by left endpoint (smaller is processed first), ties broken by VCG constraints: | Order | Net | Span | VCG-in-neighbors | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1 | [1,1] | none | | 2 | 5 | [2,3] | {2} (so 2 before 5) | | 3 | 2 | [3,4] | none | | 4 | 6 | [5,5] | none | | 5 | 3 | [6,6] | none | | 6 | 4 | [6,6] | {3} (so 3 before 4) | **Step 2 — Sweep left to right, assigning lowest free track:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Track 1: Net 1 at col 1, Net 5 at cols 2–3, Net 6 at col 5, Net 3 at col 6} \\ \text{Track 2: Net 2 at cols 3–4, Net 4 at col 6} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification of constraints:** - Track 1 vs Net 2 (cols 3–4): at col 3, track 1 is occupied by Net 5 (which overlaps with Net 2 per HCG). Correctly rejected. - Net 3 at col 6 on track 1; Net 4 at col 6 on track 2; both fine because HCG requires them to be on different tracks (already satisfied). **Final track count:** $$ t = 2 \quad (\text{matches density lower bound } \Rightarrow \text{optimal}) $$ ### 3.4 Critical-Path (VCG Depth) Calculation The longest directed path in the VCG has length 1 (each edge is its own path). Therefore the minimum vertical depth needed is: $$ L_{\text{depth}} = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ tracks} $$ This agrees with our assignment. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Channel Routing — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of constraint-graph-based detailed routing, showing how VCG and HCG interact with the channel specification and the left-edge algorithm. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Channel Specification
Top and Bottom Terminals] --> B[Net Enumeration
and Span Computation sigma_i] B --> C1[Build Vertical
Constraint Graph VCG] B --> C2[Build Horizontal
Constraint Graph HCG] C1 --> D1{VCG has
Directed Cycle?} D1 -- Yes --> E1[Dogleg Routing
or Extra Layer] D1 -- No --> F[Topological Sort
of VCG] F --> G[Left Edge Algorithm
Track Assignment] C2 --> G B --> H[Compute Channel
Density d] H --> G G --> I[Final Track
Assignment Output] E1 --> I I --> J[Verify Constraints
and Report Wirelength] ``` ### 4.2 Constraint Graph Visualisation for the Worked Example The diagram below shows the **VCG and HCG** for the 6-net example derived in Section 3. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VCG["Vertical Constraint Graph VCG"] v2((Net 2)) -- arrow Net2 above Net5 --> v5((Net 5)) v3((Net 3)) -- arrow Net3 above Net4 --> v4((Net 4)) end subgraph HCG["Horizontal Constraint Graph HCG"] h2((Net 2)) --- h5((Net 5)) h3((Net 3)) --- h4((Net 4)) end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** In the VCG block, each arrow represents a "must-be-above" ordering. In the HCG block, each undirected line represents "cannot share the same horizontal track." Notice that the **same pair (2, 5)** and **the same pair (3, 4)** appear in both graphs — but they encode **different physical constraints**. ### 4.3 Schematic of the Routed Channel (Block Layout) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CHN[Channel with 6 Columns] direction LR T1[Col1 Top Net1]:::topnode T2[Col3 Top Net2]:::topnode T3[Col4 Top Net2]:::topnode T4[Col6 Top Net3]:::topnode B1[Col2 Bot Net5]:::botnode B2[Col3 Bot Net5]:::botnode B3[Col5 Bot Net6]:::botnode B4[Col6 Bot Net4]:::botnode end subgraph TR1[Track 1 - Layer H] direction LR N1A[Net1 col1]:::tr1 N5A[Net5 col2-3]:::tr1 N6A[Net6 col5]:::tr1 N3A[Net3 col6]:::tr1 end subgraph TR2[Track 2 - Layer H] direction LR N2A[Net2 col3-4]:::tr2 N4A[Net4 col6]:::tr2 end classDef topnode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A06000,color:#000 classDef botnode fill:#A0D0FF,stroke:#003060,color:#000 classDef tr1 fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#006020,color:#000 classDef tr2 fill:#FFE8E8,stroke:#600020,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid Node-Identifier Safeguard:** Every node ID above (e.g., `T1`, `v2`, `N1A`, `CHN`) is purely **alphanumeric** and prefixed with letters. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`, `classDef`) are used as node names. Labels inside double quotes are kept as **plain uppercase alphanumeric** without bold or HTML tags, per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering rules. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** *Define the Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG) and Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG) used in two-layer channel routing. State the condition for a channel to be dogleg-free routable.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **VCG** (1 mark): A directed graph where each node is a net and a directed edge $(i, j)$ exists if at some column $c$, net $i$ has a top terminal and net $j$ has a bottom terminal, implying net $i$ must lie above net $j$. - **HCG** (1 mark): An undirected graph where each node is a net and an edge $\{i, j\}$ exists if the horizontal spans $\sigma_i$ and $\sigma_j$ overlap, implying $i$ and $j$ cannot share the same horizontal track. - **Routability condition** (1 mark): For two-layer HVH dogleg-free routing, the VCG must be a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is channel density? Compute the density of a 5-column channel whose top terminals are $\{1, 2, 3\}$ at columns 1, 3, 5 and bottom terminals are $\{4, 5\}$ at columns 2, 4 respectively.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition** (1 mark): Channel density is the maximum, over all columns, of the number of nets whose horizontal span covers that column. It gives a lower bound on the number of tracks required. - **Computation** (1.5 marks): Spans: $\sigma_1=[1,1]$, $\sigma_2=[3,3]$, $\sigma_3=[5,5]$, $\sigma_4=[2,2]$, $\sigma_5=[4,4]$. Column-wise count: $d_1=1$, $d_2=1$, $d_3=1$, $d_4=1$, $d_5=1$. - **Result** (0.5 marks): $d = \max(1,1,1,1,1) = 1$, so **at least 1 track** is required. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) #### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]` **(a)** *For the channel specification below, construct the Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG) and Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG). Determine whether the channel is dogleg-free routable in the two-layer HVH model.* **(7 marks)** | Column $c$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Top terminals** | 1 | 1 | 2 | — | 3 | — | 4 | | **Bottom terminals** | — | 5 | — | 6 | 6 | 7 | — | **(b)** *Compute the channel density and apply the Left-Edge Algorithm to determine the optimal track assignment. Show all intermediate steps.* **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) Construction of VCG and HCG (7 marks)** *Step 1 — Net spans* (1 mark): - Net 1: $C_1 = \{1, 2\} \Rightarrow \sigma_1 = [1, 2]$ - Net 2: $C_2 = \{3\} \Rightarrow \sigma_2 = [3, 3]$ - Net 3: $C_3 = \{5\} \Rightarrow \sigma_3 = [5, 5]$ - Net 4: $C_4 = \{7\} \Rightarrow \sigma_4 = [7, 7]$ - Net 5: $C_5 = \{2\} \Rightarrow \sigma_5 = [2, 2]$ - Net 6: $C_6 = \{4, 5\} \Rightarrow \sigma_6 = [4, 5]$ - Net 7: $C_7 = \{6\} \Rightarrow \sigma_7 = [6, 6]$ *Step 2 — VCG edges* (3 marks): Scan columns for top–bottom pairs: - Col 1: top = 1, bottom = none → no edge - Col 2: top = 1, bottom = 5 → edge $(1 \to 5)$ - Col 3: top = 2, bottom = none → no edge - Col 4: top = none, bottom = 6 → no edge - Col 5: top = 3, bottom = 6 → edge $(3 \to 6)$ - Col 6: top = none, bottom = 7 → no edge - Col 7: top = 4, bottom = none → no edge $$ E_v = \{\, (1, 5),\ (3, 6) \,\} $$ **The VCG is acyclic**, hence the channel is **dogleg-free routable** (1 mark). *Step 3 — HCG edges* (2 marks): Check span overlaps: - $\sigma_1 = [1,2]$ overlaps with $\sigma_5 = [2,2]$ at column 2 → edge $\{1, 5\}$ - $\sigma_6 = [4,5]$ overlaps with $\sigma_3 = [5,5]$ at column 5 → edge $\{3, 6\}$ - $\sigma_6 = [4,5]$ overlaps with $\sigma_7 = [6,6]$? No - All other pairs are non-overlapping $$ E_h = \{\, \{1, 5\},\ \{3, 6\} \,\} $$ --- **(b) Density and Left-Edge Track Assignment (7 marks)** *Step 1 — Density* (2 marks): Column-wise counts: $$ d_1 = 1,\ d_2 = 2,\ d_3 = 1,\ d_4 = 1,\ d_5 = 2,\ d_6 = 1,\ d_7 = 1 $$ $$ d = \max_c d_c = 2 \Rightarrow t \geq 2 \text{ tracks} $$ *Step 2 — Sort nets by left endpoint, breaking ties by VCG order* (1 mark): Sorted order: 1, 5, 2, 6, 3, 7, 4 (respecting VCG: 1 before 5; 3 before 6) *Step 3 — Track assignment via Left-Edge* (4 marks): | Net | Span | Assigned Track | Reasoning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | [1,2] | Track 1 | First net, lowest free track | | 5 | [2,2] | Track 2 | Track 1 occupied at col 2 (HCG conflict with net 1) | | 2 | [3,3] | Track 1 | Track 1 free from col 3 onward | | 6 | [4,5] | Track 1 | Track 1 free | | 3 | [5,5] | Track 2 | Track 1 occupied at col 5 (HCG conflict with net 6) | | 7 | [6,6] | Track 1 | Track 1 free | | 4 | [7,7] | Track 1 | Track 1 free | **Final routing uses $t = 2$ tracks, matching density ⇒ optimal.** --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** *Explain the Left-Edge Algorithm for track assignment in detail. How does it use the VCG and HCG?* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Consider the channel below. Compute the VCG, HCG, channel density, and apply the Left-Edge Algorithm. Comment on whether the channel is routable without doglegs.* **(7 marks)** | Column $c$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Top terminals** | 1 | 2 | — | 3 | 3 | — | | **Bottom terminals** | — | — | 1 | 4 | — | 2 | --- **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) Left-Edge Algorithm Explanation (7 marks)** The Left-Edge Algorithm (Hashimoto & Stevens, 1971) assigns nets to horizontal tracks in polynomial time $O(n \log n)$, where $n$ is the number of nets. The procedure is as follows (4 marks): 1. **Pre-process** all nets: sort by **left endpoint** of horizontal span; if tied, sort by **right endpoint** ascending. 2. Maintain a list of tracks, each storing the **rightmost occupied column** (initially empty). 3. For each net $i$ in sorted order: - Find the **lowest-numbered track** whose rightmost occupied column is **strictly less than** the left endpoint of net $i$. - If no such track exists, **open a new track** at the bottom. - **Assign** net $i$ to that track and update the track's rightmost occupied column to the right endpoint of net $i$. The algorithm **uses the VCG** (1 mark) to impose a topological ordering among nets that have vertical constraints — nets higher in the VCG must be assigned earlier (or to a higher track). It **uses the HCG** (1 mark) implicitly through the "lowest free track" rule: two nets with an HCG edge cannot be placed on the same track because their spans overlap. **Advantages** (0.5 mark): Simple, $O(n \log n)$, optimal for the HVH model when VCG is acyclic. **Limitation** (0.5 mark): Not optimal when VCG has cycles; doglegs or extra layers are needed. --- **(b) Worked Computation (7 marks)** *Spans* (1 mark): - Net 1: $C_1 = \{1, 3\} \Rightarrow \sigma_1 = [1, 3]$ - Net 2: $C_2 = \{2, 6\} \Rightarrow \sigma_2 = [2, 6]$ - Net 3: $C_3 = \{4, 5\} \Rightarrow \sigma_3 = [4, 5]$ - Net 4: $C_4 = \{4\} \Rightarrow \sigma_4 = [4, 4]$ *VCG* (2 marks): Column scan for top–bottom pairs: - Col 1: top = 1, bottom = none → no edge - Col 2: top = 2, bottom = none → no edge - Col 3: top = none, bottom = 1 → no edge - Col 4: top = 3, bottom = 4 → edge $(3 \to 4)$ - Col 5: top = 3, bottom = none → no edge - Col 6: top = none, bottom = 2 → no edge $$ E_v = \{\, (3, 4) \,\} \Rightarrow \text{acyclic, dogleg-free routable} $$ *HCG* (1 mark): Span overlaps: - $\sigma_1=[1,3] \cap \sigma_2=[2,6] = \{2,3\} \Rightarrow$ edge $\{1,2\}$ - $\sigma_2=[2,6] \cap \sigma_3=[4,5] = \{4,5\} \Rightarrow$ edge $\{2,3\}$ - $\sigma_3=[4,5] \cap \sigma_4=[4,4] = \{4\} \Rightarrow$ edge $\{3,4\}$ $$ E_h = \{\, \{1,2\},\ \{2,3\},\ \{3,4\} \,\} $$ *Density* (1 mark): $d_1=1, d_2=2, d_3=2, d_4=3, d_5=2, d_6=1 \Rightarrow d = 3$. *Left-Edge assignment* (2 marks): | Net | Span | Track | |---|---|---| | 1 | [1,3] | Track 1 | | 2 | [2,6] | Track 3 (because spans 1 and 2 conflict, then 2 conflicts with later nets; need careful placement) | | 3 | [4,5] | Track 2 | | 4 | [4,4] | Track 1 (Track 1 free at col 4) | A valid 3-track routing is achievable. **Channel density = 3 ⇒ optimal track count is 3.** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing VCG and HCG.** VCG encodes *vertical ordering* (above/below), HCG encodes *horizontal non-overlap*. Drawing them with reversed semantics is a 2-mark penalty on a 7-mark sub-question. > 2. **Forgetting the VCG acyclicity test.** If you skip checking for cycles, the algorithm may produce an infeasible assignment — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for not validating routability. > 3. **Ignoring tie-breaking in Left-Edge.** When two nets share a left endpoint, the **VCG topological order** must dictate which goes first. Ignoring this gives a wrong answer. > 4. **Off-by-one column errors.** Spans are *closed intervals* $[\min, \max]$. A common slip is using open or half-open intervals, which corrupts the HCG. > 5. **Miscounting density.** Density must be computed column-wise over the **entire channel**, including columns with no terminals at the channel boundary. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **VCG** = directed graph encoding **vertical (above/below) constraints**; edges are placed where a top-terminal and a bottom-terminal meet in the same column. - **HCG** = undirected graph encoding **horizontal non-overlap constraints**; edges are placed where two nets' horizontal spans overlap. - **Channel Density $d$** = max column-wise net count = **lower bound on tracks**. - **Routability (HVH, dogleg-free)** ⟺ VCG is a **DAG**. - **Left-Edge Algorithm**: sort nets by left endpoint, assign each to the lowest free track respecting both VCG (topological) and HCG (non-overlap) — runs in $O(n \log n)$. - **Critical path length in VCG** sets the **vertical depth** needed. - **If VCG has a cycle**, doglegs or extra routing layers are required. - **For modern designs**, VCG/HCG abstractions extend to switchbox routing, congestion maps, and track-assignment in advanced nodes (e.g., 7 nm and below with GCAA — Gridded-Constrained routing). - **Two-layer HVH** is the canonical model; **three-layer HVH or VH-V** offers more flexibility at higher cost. - **Examiners love**: column-wise scans, span computations, cycle tests, and the explicit track-assignment table. --- > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Detailed Routing: Horizontal and Vertical Constraint Graph.** > Continue to: *Module 4 — Topic: Channel Routing Algorithms (Yoshimura & Deutsch)* and *Switchbox Routing*.

topics.title: Channel Routing Algorithms: Left-Edge Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT524 - CAD FOR VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Channel Routing Algorithms: Left-Edge Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Channel Routing

In VLSI physical design automation, **Channel Routing** is the problem of assigning geometric paths (consisting of horizontal and vertical wire segments) to a predefined set of nets within a rectangular routing region called a **channel**. The channel is bounded by two parallel rows of logic cells, with its **top edge** and **bottom edge** containing fixed **terminals** (connection pins) that belong to specific nets.

Formally, given:
- A channel of width $W$ and length $L$
- A set of terminals $T_{top}$ on the top boundary and $T_{bot}$ on the bottom boundary
- A netlist $N = \{n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k\}$ specifying which terminals must be electrically connected

The objective is to **minimize the number of horizontal tracks** $h_{min}$ required to complete all connections, subject to the constraint that no two distinct nets may overlap on the same layer (no design-rule violations, no electrical shorts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *channel* is the rectangular free-space routing region between two abutting cell rows in standard-cell layout style. The routing problem is reduced to a 1-D track-assignment problem along the horizontal axis.

### 1.2 The Left-Edge Algorithm (LEA) — Overview

The **Left-Edge Algorithm**, introduced by **Hashimoto and Stevens (1971)**, is the **first and most fundamental** greedy channel-routing algorithm in VLSI CAD history. It is a **track-by-track, left-to-right** assignment algorithm that operates on the **horizontal intervals** of each net.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Treat each net as a horizontal line segment spanning from its leftmost terminal to its rightmost terminal. Sweep along the channel horizontally, placing nets into the lowest available track such that they do not horizontally overlap any net already placed in that track.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Packing Books on a Shelf"

Imagine you are a librarian organizing books onto the thinnest possible number of shelves:
- Each **book** = a **net** (with a fixed left edge and right edge)
- Each **shelf** = a horizontal **track** in the channel
- The goal: stack books so no two overlap on the same shelf

Your strategy (the Left-Edge rule):
1. Sort all books by their **leftmost starting position**.
2. Walk along the shelves from left to right.
3. On each shelf, pick the **leftmost book** that fits without overlapping any book already on that shelf.
4. Move to the next shelf when no more books fit at the current horizontal position.

This is *exactly* what LEA does in silicon — except the "books" are wires and the "shelves" are metal-layer tracks.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board Definition Bank)

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Net** | A set of electrically equivalent terminals to be wired together |
| **Terminal** | A fixed pin on the top/bottom boundary of the channel |
| **Track** | A horizontal routing row on a given metal layer |
| **Horizontal Interval** | The closed segment $[\,x_{left}, x_{right}\,]$ covered by a net |
| **Channel Density** $d$ | Maximum, over all vertical cut-lines, of the number of nets crossing that line |
| **Vertical Constraint** | A topological demand that one net must be routed *above* another |
| **Horizontal Constraint** | A demand that two nets cannot share the same track |
| **Dogleg** | A net that bends into multiple tracks using vias (LEA does not use doglegs) |

### 1.5 Why LEA Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Study LEA?** It is the **theoretical foundation** of all later channel routers (YACR, Mighty, Chameleon, Box Router). Every KTU board question on channel routing starts from LEA. Examiners *expect* mastery of LEA before tackling multilayer or constraint-graph-based routers.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Inputs and Outputs of the LEA Algorithm

**Inputs:**
1. A list of $k$ nets: $N = \{n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k\}$
2. For each net $n_i$, its horizontal span: $[L_i, R_i]$, where $L_i$ = leftmost column index, $R_i$ = rightmost column index
3. A vertical constraint graph $G_V = (N, E_V)$ specifying that if $(n_i \to n_j) \in E_V$, then net $n_i$ must be placed in a track **strictly above** net $n_j$

**Outputs:**
1. An assignment $T: N \to \{1, 2, \ldots, h\}$ mapping each net to a track number
2. Total number of tracks used $h$

### 2.2 The LEA Algorithm — Operational Steps

The procedure can be decomposed into the following structured logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Preprocessing (Interval Computation):** For every net $n_i$, compute the horizontal interval $[L_i, R_i]$. $L_i$ is the minimum column index of any terminal belonging to $n_i$, and $R_i$ is the maximum column index.

- **Step 2 — Sort by Left Edge:** Sort all nets in **non-decreasing order** of $L_i$. If two nets share the same $L_i$, the tie is broken arbitrarily (or by choosing the one with the larger $R_i$ first to reduce future conflicts).

- **Step 3 — Initialize Tracks:** Start with track number $t = 1$. Maintain a list `placed_in_track_t` initially empty.

- **Step 4 — Greedy Placement:** For each net $n_i$ in sorted order, attempt to insert it into track $t$. A net $n_i$ **can** be inserted into track $t$ if and only if **two conditions hold simultaneously**:
  - **Horizontal Condition:** $\forall\, n_j \in \text{placed\_in\_track\_t},\ R_j < L_i$ (i.e., $n_i$'s interval begins strictly after every previously placed net's right edge)
  - **Vertical Condition:** $\forall\, n_j \in \text{placed\_in\_track\_t}$, the vertical constraint relation $n_i \to n_j$ does NOT exist (i.e., no placed net requires $n_i$ to be above it)

- **Step 5 — Track Overflow:** If $n_i$ cannot be placed in track $t$, increment $t \leftarrow t+1$ and create a new empty track. Place $n_i$ there.

- **Step 6 — Termination:** Stop when all nets are assigned. The final track number is the **channel height** $h$.

### 2.3 Vertical and Horizontal Constraint Graphs — Why They Exist

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we cannot just "stack by interval":** Imagine two nets that overlap horizontally. Even if their columns overlap, sometimes one MUST be above the other because of where their terminals sit on the top and bottom edges. These are *physical* constraints imposed by the layout.

**Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG) Construction Rule:**
For two nets $n_i$ and $n_j$, draw a directed edge $n_i \to n_j$ if:
- $n_i$ has a terminal on the **top** edge to the **left** of a top terminal of $n_j$, AND
- $n_i$ has a terminal on the **bottom** edge to the **right** of a bottom terminal of $n_j$

This means $n_i$ *must be routed above* $n_j$. The VCG must be **acyclic** for the channel to be routable on a single layer without doglegs.

**Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG) Construction Rule:**
For two nets $n_i$ and $n_j$, draw an undirected edge if their horizontal intervals $[L_i, R_i]$ and $[L_j, R_j]$ **overlap**, i.e., $L_i \le R_j$ and $L_j \le R_i$. Such nets cannot share a track.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Application in LEA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $L_i = \min_{p \in n_i} x(p)$ | Left edge (column index) of net $n_i$ | Used for sorting |
| $R_i = \max_{p \in n_i} x(p)$ | Right edge (column index) of net $n_i$ | Used for overlap check |
| $d = \max_{c=1}^{L} \vert\{n_i : L_i \le c \le R_i\}\vert$ | **Channel density** (lower bound on tracks) | LEA's result $\ge d$ |
| $T(n_i) = t$ | Track assignment of net $n_i$ | Output of LEA |
| $h_{LEA}$ | Number of tracks produced by LEA | Final answer |
| $n_i \perp n_j$ | Two nets with overlapping intervals (cannot share track) | Horizontal constraint |
| $n_i \succ n_j$ | $n_i$ must lie strictly above $n_j$ | Vertical constraint |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Channel routing underpins the **standard-cell design style** used in virtually every commercial ASIC and SoC layout flow (Synopsys ICC, Cadence Innovus). LEA is still taught because:
- It is the **fastest possible** $O(k \log k)$ channel router
- It serves as the **initial lower-bound estimator** before invoking heavier constraint-graph routers
- The *concept of constraint graphs* introduced here generalizes directly to **global routing** in modern 3-D ICs

### 2.6 Limitations of LEA (Important for KTU Theory)

- **Non-optimality:** LEA does **not** always achieve the channel density $d$; it can use more tracks than necessary.
- **No doglegs:** Original LEA cannot break a net into segments, limiting routability of complex channels.
- **Single-layer assumption:** The classical LEA assumes all horizontal segments are on one layer; extensions handle multiple layers.
- **Local view:** Greedy placement may lock the router into a poor configuration that a global view could avoid.
