## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — The Reference LEA Problem

**Given Channel Specification** (terminal columns 1 through 8):

| Net | Top Terminals | Bottom Terminals | Interval $[L_i, R_i]$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $n_1$ | columns 1, 4 | column 1 | $[1, 4]$ |
| $n_2$ | column 2 | columns 2, 6 | $[2, 6]$ |
| $n_3$ | column 3 | column 5 | $[3, 5]$ |
| $n_4$ | column 5 | columns 5, 7 | $[5, 7]$ |
| $n_5$ | column 7 | column 7, 8 | $[7, 8]$ |

### 3.2 Step A — Sort Nets by Left Edge

Applying $L_i$ ordering (with $L_1 = 1, L_2 = 2, L_3 = 3, L_4 = 5, L_5 = 7$):

$$\text{Sorted order} = \{n_1, n_2, n_3, n_4, n_5\}$$

### 3.3 Step B — Construct Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)

Apply the VCG rule to every pair. Inspecting the terminal positions:

- $n_1$ has top terminal at column 1; $n_2$ has top terminal at column 2. Bottom terminals: $n_1$ at 1, $n_2$ at 2, 6. **No VCG edge** (no opposite-direction terminal).
- $n_2$ vs $n_3$: $n_2$ top at 2 (left of $n_3$ top at 3) AND $n_2$ bottom extends to 6 (right of $n_3$ bottom at 5). Therefore:

$$n_2 \to n_3 \quad (\text{so } n_2 \text{ must be above } n_3)$$

- $n_2$ vs $n_4$: $n_2$ top at 2 (left of $n_4$ top at 5) AND $n_2$ bottom at 6 (right of $n_4$ bottom at 5). Therefore:

$$n_2 \to n_4$$

- $n_3$ vs $n_4$: $n_3$ top at 3 (left of $n_4$ top at 5) AND $n_3$ bottom at 5 (right of $n_4$ bottom at 5). **Tie — no strict inequality**, so no edge.
- All other pairs: no vertical conflict.

$$\boxed{G_V: \quad n_2 \to n_3, \quad n_2 \to n_4}$$

### 3.4 Step C — Track-by-Track Greedy Placement

**Track 1 ($t=1$):**

| Candidate | Check | Decision |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $n_1$ | Track empty; vertical OK | **Place** $n_1$ at $[1,4]$ |
| $n_2$ | $R_1 = 4 \ge L_2 = 2$ → overlap | Skip (cannot fit) |
| $n_3$ | $R_1 = 4 \ge L_3 = 3$ → overlap | Skip |
| $n_4$ | $R_1 = 4 < L_4 = 5$ ✓ AND no $n_1 \to n_4$ in VCG | **Place** $n_4$ at $[5,7]$ |
| $n_5$ | $R_4 = 7 = L_5 = 7$ → overlap (closed interval) | Skip |

Track 1 contents: $\{n_1, n_4\}$.

**Track 2 ($t=2$):**

| Candidate | Check | Decision |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $n_2$ | Track empty; vertical OK | **Place** $n_2$ at $[2,6]$ |
| $n_3$ | $R_2 = 6 \ge L_3 = 3$ → overlap | Skip |
| $n_4$ | $R_2 = 6 \ge L_4 = 5$ → overlap | Skip |
| $n_5$ | $R_2 = 6 < L_5 = 7$ ✓ AND no $n_2 \to n_5$ in VCG | **Place** $n_5$ at $[7,8]$ |

Track 2 contents: $\{n_2, n_5\}$.

**Track 3 ($t=3$):**

| Candidate | Check | Decision |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $n_3$ | Track empty; vertical OK | **Place** $n_3$ at $[3,5]$ |

Track 3 contents: $\{n_3\}$.

### 3.5 Step D — Final Result and Verification

$$\boxed{h_{LEA} = 3 \text{ tracks}}$$

**Track Assignment Mapping:**

$$T(n_1) = 1, \quad T(n_2) = 2, \quad T(n_3) = 3, \quad T(n_4) = 1, \quad T(n_5) = 2$$

**Verification of Vertical Constraint:** The VCG requires $n_2$ above $n_3$ and $n_2$ above $n_4$. Indeed:

$$T(n_2) = 2 < T(n_3) = 3 \quad \text{✓}$$
$$T(n_2) = 2 < T(n_4) = 1 \quad \text{✗ (Violation!)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Hold on — re-check the VCG!** The convention $T=1$ = *topmost* track vs $T=1$ = *bottommost* track matters. Most KTU texts define **Track 1 = TOP**, so smaller number = higher position. Under this convention $T(n_2)=2 > T(n_3)=3$ means $n_2$ is **below** $n_3$ — which **violates** $n_2 \to n_3$ (must be above). 

**Correction (Top = Track 1):** The LEA must enforce $T(n_2) < T(n_3)$. Our greedy placement was incorrect. Re-run with vertical enforcement:

- Track 1: place $n_2$ at $[2,6]$ (top). 
- Track 2: $n_1$ cannot go above $n_2$ (no constraint requires it), $n_1 = [1,4]$ fits before $n_2$ starts at 2? No — $R_1=4 \ge L_2=2$ overlap. $n_3$ requires $n_2$ above, so $T(n_3) > T(n_2) = 1$. $n_3 = [3,5]$ → Track 2? But $n_1$ also wants Track 2. $R_1=4 < L_3=3$? No, $4 \ge 3$ overlap. So $n_1$ → Track 3: $R_1=4 < L_4=5$, place $n_4$ → Track 3. $n_5$ → Track 2 ($R_3=5 < 7$).

**Corrected Final Result:**

$$T(n_2)=1, \ T(n_3)=2, \ T(n_1)=3, \ T(n_4)=3, \ T(n_5)=2$$

**Verification:** $T(n_2)=1 < T(n_3)=2$ ✓; $T(n_2)=1 < T(n_4)=3$ ✓. **Routed in 3 tracks.**

### 3.6 Channel Density Computation (Lower Bound Check)

Compute the number of nets active at each column:

$$d(1) = 1\ (n_1),\ d(2) = 2\ (n_1, n_2),\ d(3) = 3\ (n_1, n_2, n_3)$$
$$d(4) = 3\ (n_1, n_2, n_3),\ d(5) = 3\ (n_2, n_3, n_4),\ d(6) = 2\ (n_2, n_4)$$
$$d(7) = 2\ (n_4, n_5),\ d(8) = 1\ (n_5)$$

$$d_{channel} = \max_c d(c) = 3$$

Hence $h_{LEA} = d_{channel} = 3$. **Optimal** in this case.

### 3.7 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Metric | Value | KTU Note |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Channel density $d$ | **3** | Theoretical lower bound |
| LEA tracks $h_{LEA}$ | **3** | Achieves bound → optimal here |
| VC edges | **2** | $n_2 \to n_3$, $n_2 \to n_4$ |
| HC edges | **6** | All overlapping pairs |
| VCG cycles | **0** | Acyclic → routable |
| Doglegs used | **0** | Pure LEA |
| Time complexity | $O(k \log k)$ | Dominated by sort |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow of the Left-Edge Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Channel Routing Problem] --> B[Read Net List and Terminal Positions]
    B --> C[Compute Horizontal Interval Li Ri for Each Net]
    C --> D[Construct Vertical Constraint Graph Gv]
    D --> E[Sort All Nets by Left Edge Li in Ascending Order]
    E --> F[Initialize Track Counter t = 1]
    F --> G[Select Next Net ni from Sorted List]
    G --> H{Does ni Fit in Track t?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Assign ni to Track t]
    I --> J{More Nets Remaining?}
    H -- No --> K[Increment t by 1]
    K --> G
    J -- Yes --> G
    J -- No --> L[Output Track Assignment T and Total Tracks h]
    L --> M[End]
```

### 4.2 Constraint Graph Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph VCG[Vertical Constraint Graph Gv]
        direction LR
        v2[Net n2] -->|must be above| v3[Net n3]
        v2 -->|must be above| v4[Net n4]
    end

    subgraph HCG[Horizontal Constraint Graph Gh]
        direction TB
        h12[n1] --- h22[n2]
        h12[n1] --- h32[n3]
        h22[n2] --- h32[n3]
        h22[n2] --- h42[n4]
        h32[n3] --- h42[n4]
        h42[n4] --- h52[n5]
    end

    subgraph TRACK[LEA Track Assignment]
        direction LR
        t1row[Track 1 TOP] --> t1a[Net n2]
        t2row[Track 2] --> t2a[Net n3 and Net n5]
        t3row[Track 3 BOTTOM] --> t3a[Net n1 and Net n4]
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of Channel Routing Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input: Netlist + Terminal Map] --> PRE[Preprocessor]
    PRE --> INT[Interval Calculator Li Ri]
    PRE --> VCG2[VCG Builder]
    PRE --> HCG2[HCG Builder]
    INT --> SORT[Sorter: Sort by Li]
    VCG2 --> SORT
    HCG2 --> SORT
    SORT --> LEA[Left-Edge Assignor]
    LEA --> ASSIGN[Track Assignment T: N to Tracks]
    ASSIGN --> OUT[Output: Routing Pattern and Track Count h]
```

### 4.4 Routing Channel Cross-Section (Schematic)

```
   TOP CELL ROW (terminals on bottom of upper cells)
   ┌──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──┐
   │ T1│ T2│ T3│  │ T5│  │ T7│  │  ← Top terminals
   ├──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼──┤
   │  │  │  │  │  │  │  │  │
   │    n2 horizontal segment [2,6]    │  ← Track 1
   │  │  │  │  │  │  │  │  │
   │      n3 [3,5]    n5 [7,8]   │  ← Track 2
   │  │  │  │  │  │  │  │  │
   │  n1 [1,4]      n4 [5,7]      │  ← Track 3
   │  │  │  │  │  │  │  │  │
   ├──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼──┼──┤
   │ B1│ B2│  │ B5│ B6│ B7│ B8│  │  ← Bottom terminals
   └──┴──┴──┴──┴──┴──┴──┴──┘
   BOTTOM CELL ROW (terminals on top of lower cells)
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define channel routing. What is meant by channel density? How does it relate to the minimum number of tracks required?**

**Model Answer:**
Channel routing is the problem of assigning wires to nets within a rectangular routing region (channel) bounded by two parallel rows of standard cells. Terminals lie on the top and bottom edges; horizontal segments occupy tracks and vertical segments occupy columns.
Channel density $d$ is defined as the maximum number of nets crossing any vertical cut-line in the channel:

$$d = \max_{1 \le c \le L} \left\vert\{n_i : L_i \le c \le R_i\}\right\vert$$

It provides the **theoretical lower bound** on the number of tracks required by any router. Any valid routing must satisfy $h \ge d$, since at column $c$ at least $d$ distinct horizontal tracks are needed to accommodate the $d$ active nets.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between vertical constraints and horizontal constraints in channel routing. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Vertical Constraint | Horizontal Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Defines the *relative vertical ordering* of nets | Defines which nets *cannot share* a track |
| **Origin** | Opposite-side terminals of two nets | Overlapping horizontal intervals |
| **Graph Type** | Directed (acyclic VCG) | Undirected (HCG) |
| **Example** | $n_i$ top terminal left of $n_j$ top terminal, AND $n_i$ bottom terminal right of $n_j$ bottom terminal → $n_i$ must be above $n_j$ | Two nets spanning columns $[2,6]$ and $[4,8]$ overlap → cannot share a track |

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full LEA Trace Problem

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the **Left-Edge Algorithm** for channel routing in detail. State its input, output, complexity, and clearly state the conditions under which a net can be assigned to the current track.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Consider a channel with the following nets (top terminal columns / bottom terminal columns):

- $n_A$: top $\{1, 5\}$, bottom $\{1\}$
- $n_B$: top $\{2\}$, bottom $\{2, 6\}$
- $n_C$: top $\{3\}$, bottom $\{4\}$
- $n_D$: top $\{4, 7\}$, bottom $\{5\}$
- $n_E$: top $\{6\}$, bottom $\{7\}$
- $n_F$: top $\{8\}$, bottom $\{8, 9\}$

Apply the **Left-Edge Algorithm** and determine the track assignment. Compute the channel density and verify whether LEA achieved the lower bound.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Explanation — refer to Section 2.2 of these notes. Key points:
- Input: net list with horizontal intervals; Output: track assignment $T: N \to \mathbb{Z}^+$
- Complexity: $O(k \log k)$ due to sorting
- **Placement condition** for net $n_i$ into track $t$ (Track 1 = TOP):
  1. **Horizontal:** $L_i > \max_{n_j \in T_t} R_j$
  2. **Vertical:** $\forall\, n_j \in T_t,\ T(n_j) \ne 1$ when $n_j \to n_i$ exists in VCG (i.e., $n_i$ is not required by VCG to lie above $n_j$, so $T(n_i) \ge T(n_j)$ in bottom-numbered systems, or $T(n_i) \le T(n_j)$ in top-numbered systems)

**(b)** Computation:

Compute intervals:

$$L_A=1,\ R_A=5;\ L_B=2,\ R_B=6;\ L_C=3,\ R_C=4;\ L_D=4,\ R_D=7;\ L_E=6,\ R_E=7;\ L_F=8,\ R_F=9$$

Sort by $L_i$: $n_A(1),\ n_B(2),\ n_C(3),\ n_D(4),\ n_E(6),\ n_F(8)$.

Vertical constraint analysis:
- $n_B$ vs $n_C$: $n_B$ top at 2 (left of $n_C$ top at 3); $n_B$ bottom extends to 6 (right of $n_C$ bottom at 4). → $n_B \to n_C$
- $n_B$ vs $n_D$: $n_B$ top at 2 (left of $n_D$ top at 4); $n_B$ bottom at 6 (right of $n_D$ bottom at 5). → $n_B \to n_D$
- $n_C$ vs $n_D$: top $n_C=3 < 4=n_D$ top; bottom $n_C=4 < 5=n_D$ bottom. Same direction → no edge.

VCG: $n_B \to n_C$, $n_B \to n_D$.

LEA track placement (Track 1 = TOP):

**Track 1:** place $n_B = [2,6]$ first (must be topmost due to outgoing VCG edges — placing it first guarantees no conflict).
- $n_A = [1,5]$: $L_A = 1 < L_B = 2$, cannot be placed (must come before $n_B$ but interval overlaps).
- $n_C = [3,4]$: $L_C = 3 < R_B = 6$, overlap → cannot fit.
- $n_D = [4,7]$: $L_D = 4 < 6$, overlap → cannot fit.
- $n_E = [6,7]$: $L_E = 6 \le R_B = 6$, overlap (closed interval) → cannot fit.
- $n_F = [8,9]$: $L_F = 8 > R_B = 6$ ✓ AND no VCG conflict. **Place $n_F$.**

Track 1: $\{n_B, n_F\}$.

**Track 2:** place $n_A = [1,5]$. VCG: no incoming constraints requiring $n_A$ above; no outgoing edges problematic with $n_B$ above. ✓
- $n_C = [3,4]$: $R_A = 5 > L_C = 3$, overlap.
- $n_D = [4,7]$: $R_A = 5 > L_D = 4$, overlap.
- $n_E = [6,7]$: $R_A = 5 < L_E = 6$ ✓. Check VCG: $n_E$ has no relation to $n_A$. **Place $n_E$.**

Track 2: $\{n_A, n_E\}$.

**Track 3:** place $n_C = [3,4]$. VCG: requires $n_B$ above, currently $T(n_B)=1 < T(n_C)=3$ ✓.
- $n_D = [4,7]$: $R_C = 4 = L_D = 4$, overlap.

Track 3: $\{n_C\}$.

**Track 4:** place $n_D = [4,7]$. VCG: requires $n_B$ above, $T(n_B)=1 < 4$ ✓.

Track 4: $\{n_D\}$.

$$\boxed{h_{LEA} = 4}$$

**Channel Density:**

$$d(1)=1,\ d(2)=2,\ d(3)=3,\ d(4)=4,\ d(5)=4,\ d(6)=3,\ d(7)=2,\ d(8)=1,\ d(9)=1$$

$$d_{channel} = 4$$

$$\boxed{h_{LEA} = d_{channel} = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{OPTIMAL}}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Constraint Graph and Limitations

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Define the **Vertical Constraint Graph (VCG)** and **Horizontal Constraint Graph (HCG)** formally. Construct both graphs for the channel routing instance given below:

- $n_1$: top $\{1\}$, bottom $\{1, 4\}$
- $n_2$: top $\{2, 5\}$, bottom $\{3\}$
- $n_3$: top $\{3\}$, bottom $\{2, 6\}$
- $n_4$: top $\{4\}$, bottom $\{5\}$

Explain why the VCG must be acyclic for routability.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Discuss the **limitations** of the Left-Edge Algorithm. With a small counter-example, show that LEA does not always achieve the channel density lower bound. How can these limitations be addressed?

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Definitions and construction:

**VCG definition:** Directed graph $G_V = (N, E_V)$. Edge $(n_i \to n_j) \in E_V$ iff there exists a column $c$ with $n_i$'s top terminal left of $n_j$'s top terminal AND $n_i$'s bottom terminal right of $n_j$'s bottom terminal. Interpretation: $n_i$ must lie in a track above $n_j$.

**HCG definition:** Undirected graph $G_H = (N, E_H)$. Edge $(n_i, n_j) \in E_H$ iff $[L_i, R_i] \cap [L_j, R_j] \ne \emptyset$.

Construction:

Intervals:
- $n_1$: $L=1,\ R=4$ (bottom dominates)
- $n_2$: $L=2,\ R=5$ (top dominates)
- $n_3$: $L=2,\ R=6$ (bottom dominates)
- $n_4$: $L=4,\ R=5$ (both)

VCG edges (apply rule):
- $n_2$ vs $n_3$: $n_2$ top at 2 (left of $n_3$ top at 3); $n_2$ bottom at 3 (right of $n_3$ bottom at 2)? Wait — $n_3$ bottom terminals at 2 and 6, so its leftmost is 2. $n_2$ bottom at 3 > 2. AND $n_2$ top leftmost at 2 < 3 = $n_3$ top leftmost. ✓ → $n_2 \to n_3$.
- $n_2$ vs $n_4$: $n_2$ top leftmost 2 < $n_4$ top at 4; $n_2$ bottom at 3 < $n_4$ bottom at 5. Same direction → no edge.
- $n_3$ vs $n_4$: $n_3$ top at 3 < $n_4$ top at 4; $n_3$ bottom leftmost 2 < $n_4$ bottom at 5. Same direction → no edge.
- $n_1$ vs $n_2$: $n_1$ top at 1 < $n_2$ top at 2; $n_1$ bottom at 4 > $n_2$ bottom at 3. → $n_1 \to n_2$.

$$\boxed{G_V: n_1 \to n_2, \quad n_2 \to n_3}$$

HCG edges (interval overlaps):
- $[1,4] \cap [2,5] \ne \emptyset$ → $n_1 - n_2$
- $[1,4] \cap [2,6] \ne \emptyset$ → $n_1 - n_3$
- $[1,4] \cap [4,5] \ne \emptyset$ → $n_1 - n_4$
- $[2,5] \cap [2,6] \ne \emptyset$ → $n_2 - n_3$
- $[2,5] \cap [4,5] \ne \emptyset$ → $n_2 - n_4$
- $[2,6] \cap [4,5] \ne \emptyset$ → $n_3 - n_4$

$$\boxed{G_H: \text{complete graph } K_4}$$

**Acyclicity of VCG:** If $G_V$ contains a cycle $n_{i_1} \to n_{i_2} \to \cdots \to n_{i_1}$, then each net must be above its successor, which transitively forces $n_{i_1}$ to be above itself — a contradiction. Hence a channel is single-layer routable without doglegs **iff** $G_V$ is acyclic.

**(b)** Limitations of LEA:

1. **Sub-optimality:** LEA can use more tracks than the channel density lower bound.
2. **No doglegs:** Cannot split a single net across tracks via vertical jogs.
3. **Greedy local decisions:** Once placed, a net is never reconsidered, leading to poor global choices.
4. **Single-layer assumption:** Original LEA does not leverage multiple metal layers.

**Counter-example for sub-optimality:**

Consider:
- $n_1 = [1, 3]$, $n_2 = [1, 3]$, $n_3 = [3, 5]$, $n_4 = [3, 5]$, $n_5 = [5, 7]$, $n_6 = [5, 7]$.

After sorting: $n_1, n_2, n_3, n_4, n_5, n_6$. All intervals pair up:
- Track 1: $n_1, n_3, n_5$
- Track 2: $n_2, n_4, n_6$

Channel density $d = 2$. LEA achieves 2 tracks → optimal in this case.

A better counter-example:
- $n_1 = [1, 4]$, $n_2 = [2, 5]$, $n_3 = [3, 6]$, $n_4 = [4, 7]$.

Sorted: $n_1, n_2, n_3, n_4$.
- Track 1: $n_1$ → check $n_2$: $R_1 = 4 \ge L_2 = 2$ overlap → skip. $n_3$: $R_1 = 4 \ge L_3 = 3$ overlap → skip. $n_4$: $R_1 = 4 = L_4 = 4$ overlap → skip.
- Track 2: $n_2$ placed. $n_3$ overlap ($R_2=5 \ge 3$). $n_4$: $R_2=5 < L_4=4$? No $5 \ge 4$ overlap.
- Track 3: $n_3$ placed. $n_4$: $R_3 = 6 \ge 4$ overlap.
- Track 4: $n_4$ placed.

$$\boxed{h_{LEA} = 4}$$

But channel density $d(4) = 3$ (nets $n_1, n_2, n_3$ all active). So LEA used **4 tracks when 3 suffice** — sub-optimal!

**How to address:** Use constraint-graph-based routers such as **YACR (Yet Another Channel Router)** or algorithms that incorporate doglegs to break nets, allowing denser packing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the vertical constraint check** — many students only check horizontal overlap, producing invalid routings. Always enforce VCG.
> 2. **Track-numbering convention confusion** — Track 1 can mean either topmost or bottommost. The KTU answer script must declare the convention explicitly.
> 3. **Closed vs open interval overlap** — intervals $[L, R]$ are CLOSED. So $[2,4]$ and $[4,6]$ overlap at column 4; do not treat as non-overlapping.
> 4. **Forgetting density verification** — always compute $d$ at the end and state whether LEA achieved the bound.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Channel Routing:** Routing inside a rectangular region between two cell rows; terminals on top and bottom edges.
- [x] **Net:** Set of electrically connected terminals.
- [x] **Horizontal Interval:** $[L_i, R_i]$ for net $n_i$.
- [x] **Channel Density $d$:** Maximum active nets over any vertical cut-line → **lower bound on tracks**.
- [x] **VCG (Vertical Constraint Graph):** Directed; edge $n_i \to n_j$ means $n_i$ above $n_j$; must be **acyclic** for single-layer routability.
- [x] **HCG (Horizontal Constraint Graph):** Undirected; edge means overlapping intervals → cannot share a track.
- [x] **Left-Edge Algorithm (Hashimoto-Stevens, 1971):** Sort by left edge; sweep left-to-right; greedily assign to lowest available track satisfying BOTH horizontal and vertical constraints.
- [x] **Placement Condition (track $t$, Track 1 = TOP):** Net $n_i$ fits if (a) $L_i > \max_{n_j \in T_t} R_j$ AND (b) no VCG edge forces $n_i$ above any net already in $T_t$.
- [x] **Time Complexity:** $O(k \log k)$ dominated by sorting.
- [x] **Optimality:** Achieves $h = d$ when no vertical constraints exist; **sub-optimal** in general.
- [x] **No Doglegs:** Pure LEA does not split nets across tracks.
- [x] **Improvements:** YACR, dogleg routers, multi-layer channel routers (MCR, Chameleon).
- [x] **Exam Trick:** Always compute and report channel density; always verify VCG acyclicity; always declare track-numbering convention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Mantra:** *"Sort by left edge. Sweep track by track. Check horizontal overlap AND vertical constraints. Verify channel density. Declare your track convention."*
